topics.title: Microelectronic Visual Implant Technologies: Retinal Stimulation and Retinal Implant - Epiretinal
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT753 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS AND IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Microelectronic Visual Implant Technologies: Retinal Stimulation and Retinal Implant - Epiretinal

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Retinal Stimulation** is the controlled delivery of charge-balanced biphasic electrical pulses to surviving neural elements of the retina, eliciting phosphene perception in patients suffering from outer retinal degeneration.

An **Epiretinal Implant** is a microelectronic visual prosthesis that is surgically fixed on the **inner (vitreal) surface of the retina**, directly apposed to the **ganglion cell layer (GCL)** and the **nerve fiber layer (NFL)**. The device bypasses the degenerated photoreceptors (rods/cones) and electrically stimulates the **retinal ganglion cells (RGCs)** and their axons to restore a crude form of visual perception.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping — This topic falls under **Module 1: Microelectronic Visual Implant Technologies** and directly addresses **Course Outcome CO1**: *Identify and describe the engineering principles underlying neural stimulation and visual prosthetics.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of a healthy eye as a **movie projector**:
- The **photoreceptors (rods/cones)** are the camera sensor capturing light.
- The **retinal ganglion cells** are the cable wires carrying the signal to the brain.

In diseases like **Retinitis Pigmentosa (RP)** or **Age-related Macular Degeneration (AMD)**, the "camera sensor" (photoreceptors) dies, but the "cables" (ganglion cells) are mostly still alive. The wiring is intact, but the input is gone.

An **Epiretinal implant** works like a **replacement projector mounted directly on the cinema screen wall**:
- A **miniature external camera** (worn on glasses) acts as the new "sensor."
- A **video processor** converts the camera image into stimulation commands.
- A **wireless transmitter + implanted receiver** beam the data in.
- A **microelectrode array placed ON the retina** electrically pokes the ganglion cells, tricking them into sending "movie" signals to the brain.

The patient perceives a **pixellated black-and-white image** — a mosaic of tiny dots of light called **phosphenes**.

### 1.3 Position Within the Retinal Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Epiretinal placement = ON the retina, facing the vitreous (inner side).**

The retina has ten histological layers. The epiretinal device lies *above* the **inner limiting membrane (ILM)**, contacting the **ganglion cell layer** first. This is in contrast to:
- **Subretinal** implants (between RPE and photoreceptor layer)
- **Suprachoroidal** implants (between choroid and sclera)

### 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded for Recall)

| Parameter | Standard Value / Limit | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Pulse frequency** | **20 Hz – 60 Hz** (typical frame rate) | Refreshes phosphene image |
| **Pulse phase duration** | **0.1 ms – 1.0 ms** | Cation-safe biphasic pulse |
| **Charge per phase (Q)** | **0.01 µC – 1.0 µC** | Drives neuronal depolarization |
| **Maximum charge density (k)** | **≤ 0.1 mC/cm²** (Shannon limit) | Safety against electrochemical damage |
| **Electrode size** | **50 µm – 200 µm diameter** | Spatial resolution trade-off |
| **Stimulation threshold current** | **~ 10 µA – 500 µA** | RGC activation range |
| **Array size (Argus II)** | **6 × 10 = 60 electrodes** | Field-of-view ≈ 20° |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Logic Steps

1. **Image Acquisition:** A miniature CMOS camera mounted on spectacle frames captures the external scene in real-time.
2. **Image Processing:** A wearable Video Processing Unit (VPU) downsamples, contrast-enhances, and converts the image into a spatial map of stimulation commands.
3. **Wireless Data & Power Transfer:** Commands and power are transmitted via a **radiofrequency (RF) inductive link** (typically 1–10 MHz) to the implanted coil.
4. **Decoding & Routing:** An Application-Specific Integrated Circuit (ASIC) decodes the RF signal and routes stimulation pulses to specific electrodes based on image brightness at that pixel.
5. **Charge Delivery:** Biphasic, charge-balanced current pulses are delivered to the electrode–tissue interface.
6. **Neural Excitation:** The electric field depolarizes retinal ganglion cell membranes, opening voltage-gated Na⁺ channels.
7. **Visual Perception:** The brain interprets the spatially encoded activation pattern as a coarse pixelated image (phosphene field).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Because the implant stimulates RGCs directly, the brain's interpretation is **invariant to disease location** (provided the inner retina is preserved). This is the central advantage of epiretinal placement.

### 2.2 The Electrode–Tissue Interface (Why Biphasic Pulses?)

When a metal electrode sits in ionic saline (vitreous humor), passing DC current causes:
- **Oxidation** at the anode (metal dissolution, water electrolysis → O₂ + H⁺)
- **Reduction** at the cathode (water → H₂ gas, pH shift)

Both reactions are **destructive to tissue**. The solution is a **charge-balanced biphasic pulse**: a cathodal pulse (cathode-leading, depolarizing RGCs) immediately followed by a reverse anodal pulse of equal charge that reverses the electrochemical reaction without producing net DC.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Charge per phase | $Q = I \cdot t_{ph}$ | C (Coulombs) | $I$ = pulse amplitude, $t_{ph}$ = phase duration |
| 2 | Charge density | $D = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{I \cdot t_{ph}}{A}$ | µC/cm² | $A$ = electrode geometric area |
| 3 | Current density | $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | µA/cm² or mA/cm² | Electrochemical safety metric |
| 4 | Threshold (Weiss) | $I_{th} = I_{r}\left(1 + \dfrac{k}{\sqrt{t_{ph}}}\right)$ | µA | $I_r$ = rheobase, $k$ = chronaxie constant |
| 5 | Chronaxie | $t_c = k^{2}$ | ms | Pulse duration at $2 \times I_r$ |
| 6 | Shannon safety limit | $\log_{10}(D) = k - \log_{10}(Q)$ | log–log line | Defines the **k**-value safety boundary |
| 7 | Stimulation energy | $E = I^{2} \cdot R \cdot t_{ph}$ | Joules | Power dissipation per phase |
| 8 | Power transfer (inductive) | $P_{tx} = \eta \cdot P_{rx}$ | W | $\eta$ = coil coupling efficiency |
| 9 | Capacitive charge transfer | $Q = C \cdot \Delta V$ | C | Used for high-impedance electrodes |
| 10 | Membrane time constant | $\tau_m = R_m \cdot C_m$ | s | Determines optimal pulse width |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use `\vert` or `\mid` (NOT the pipe `|`) inside the cells of this table to avoid breaking the markdown table. All vertical bar symbols for "absolute value" in your notebook should be written as `\vert x \vert` in LaTeX.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Clinical use:** Argus II (Second Sight Medical Products) — first FDA-approved (2013) epiretinal prosthesis for late-stage RP.
- **Neuroscience research:** Epiretinal arrays serve as **platforms for studying neural coding** of vision — how RGC ensembles encode contrast, motion, and edges.
- **Tactile/Haptic parallel:** The same biphasic current-controlled stimulation principle is reused in **cochlear implants, deep brain stimulators (DBS), and sacral nerve stimulators**.
- **Failure-mode engineering:** Electrode array lifting, encapsulation fibrosis, and hermetic package failure are direct lessons that inform all chronic neural interfaces.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Shannon Safety Limit

The Shannon limit defines the **maximum permissible stimulation envelope** to avoid both **electrochemical damage** (high D, high Q) and **tissue heating** (high charge × voltage).

**Step 1 — Define the safety boundary as a line in log–log space:**

We seek a relationship where the logarithmic charge density $\log_{10}(D)$ decreases linearly as the logarithmic charge $\log_{10}(Q)$ increases, bounded by a constant $k$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Assume a linear relationship in log space:} \quad
\log_{10}(D) &= a - b \cdot \log_{10}(Q)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — The constant term $a$ is defined as the $k$-value:**

By convention in neuroprosthetics, the constant is renamed to $k$ (a pure number, typically 1.5–2.0 for safe chronic stimulation):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(D) &= k - b \cdot \log_{10}(Q)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — The slope $b$ is empirically determined to be unity** from decades of animal histological data (Shannon 1992; Cogan 2008):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad \log_{10}(D) &= k - \log_{10}(Q)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert back to a linear form by exponentiating both sides:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= 10^{\,k - \log_{10}(Q)} \\
D &= 10^{k} \cdot 10^{-\log_{10}(Q)} \\
D &= \frac{10^{k}}{Q}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the maximum permissible charge $Q_{max}$ given an electrode area $A$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q}{A} &= \frac{10^{k}}{Q} \\
Q^{2} &= 10^{k} \cdot A \\
Q_{max} &= \sqrt{10^{k} \cdot A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** For a $k$-value of 1.85 (typical platinum–iridium microelectrodes) and an electrode area $A = 0.002\, \text{cm}^{2}$ (200 µm diameter disk):

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{max} &= \sqrt{10^{1.85} \cdot 0.002} \\
&= \sqrt{70.79 \cdot 0.002} \\
&= \sqrt{0.1416} \\
&\approx 0.376\ \mu C
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering interpretation:** Doubling the electrode area increases $Q_{max}$ by only $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414 \times$, not $2 \times$. This is why smaller electrodes are intrinsically *safer for the same charge* but demand *higher charge densities* — a fundamental trade-off.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Weiss Strength-Duration Curve

The strength-duration relationship governs **how much current is needed to reach threshold** for a given pulse width.

**Step 1 — Start with the experimentally observed form:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{th}(t_{ph}) &= I_r + \frac{a}{\sqrt{t_{ph}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $I_r$ is the rheobase (minimum DC current to fire, as $t_{ph} \to \infty$), and $a$ is an empirical constant.

**Step 2 — Define chronaxie as the pulse width at twice the rheobase:**

By definition, at $t_{ph} = t_c$, $I_{th} = 2 I_r$. Substituting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 I_r &= I_r + \frac{a}{\sqrt{t_c}} \\
I_r &= \frac{a}{\sqrt{t_c}} \\
a &= I_r \cdot \sqrt{t_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the strength-duration equation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{th} &= I_r + \frac{I_r \cdot \sqrt{t_c}}{\sqrt{t_{ph}}} \\
I_{th} &= I_r \left(1 + \sqrt{\frac{t_c}{t_{ph}}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the minimum threshold point** (differentiate and set to zero):

The product $I_{th} \cdot t_{ph}$ gives the charge per phase. Differentiating to find the charge-optimal pulse:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q(t_{ph}) &= I_r \cdot t_{ph} \left(1 + \sqrt{\frac{t_c}{t_{ph}}}\right) \\
&= I_r \cdot t_{ph} + I_r \cdot \sqrt{t_c \cdot t_{ph}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dQ}{dt_{ph}} &= I_r + I_r \cdot \frac{1}{2}\sqrt{\frac{t_c}{t_{ph}}} = 0 \\
\text{(No real solution — Q is monotonically increasing)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering takeaway:** Smaller $t_{ph}$ always saves charge. The optimal pulse width is selected by **balancing charge efficiency against electrochemical safety** and is typically chosen near the **chronaxie value** ($t_{ph} \approx t_c$), where the threshold current is exactly twice the rheobase.

### 3.3 Charge Calculation for a 60-Electrode Argus II Frame

A full Argus II frame updates at 60 Hz. Each electrode receives one cathodal + one anodal phase.

**Given:**
- Pulse amplitude: $I = 200\ \mu A = 200 \times 10^{-6}\ A$
- Phase duration: $t_{ph} = 0.45\ ms = 0.45 \times 10^{-3}\ s$
- Number of electrodes: $N = 60$

**Step 1 — Charge per phase per electrode:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= I \cdot t_{ph} \\
  &= (200 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (0.45 \times 10^{-3}) \\
  &= 90 \times 10^{-9}\ C = 90\ nC = 0.090\ \mu C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Charge density per electrode (200 µm diameter Pt disc, A = πr²):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi \cdot (100 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{cm})^{2} = \pi \cdot 10^{-4}\ \text{cm}^{2} \\
D &= \frac{0.090\ \mu C}{\pi \cdot 10^{-4}\ \text{cm}^{2}} \\
  &\approx \frac{0.090}{3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} \\
  &\approx 286.5\ \mu C/\text{cm}^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify Shannon safety ($k = 1.85$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) &= \log_{10}(286.5) + \log_{10}(0.090) \\
&= 2.457 + (-1.046) \\
&= 1.411
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $1.411 < 1.85$, the stimulation is **within safe Shannon limits** ✓.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Examiner Pitfall: Students often forget that Argus II is **epiretinal**, NOT subretinal. The Argus I (predecessor) and Argus II both contact the ganglion cell layer from the vitreal side. Subretinal devices (e.g., Alpha-IMS/AMS) are a **different** clinical class.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Epiretinal Prosthesis

This diagram maps the complete signal and power flow from the external scene to the brain's visual cortex.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Scene] --> B[External CMOS Camera on Spectacles]
    B --> C[Video Processing Unit VPU]
    C --> D[External RF Coil Transmitter]
    D -- RF 13.56 MHz inductive link --> E[Internal RF Coil Receiver]
    E --> F[Hermetic ASIC Stimulator Chip]
    F --> G[Demultiplexer and Electrode Routing Matrix]
    G --> H1[Electrode 1 on Inner Retina]
    G --> H2[Electrode 2 on Inner Retina]
    G --> H3[Electrode N on Inner Retina]
    H1 --> I[Retinal Ganglion Cells RGCs]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Optic Nerve Bundle]
    J --> K[Lateral Geniculate Nucleus LGN]
    K --> L[Primary Visual Cortex V1]
    L --> M[Phosphene Perception in Patient]
```

### 4.2 Cross-Sectional Position of Epiretinal Array

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EYE[Cross Section of the Eye]
        direction TB
        CORNEA[Cornea]
        AQ[Aqueous Humor]
        LENS[Lens]
        VIT[Vitreous Humor]
        NFL[Nerve Fiber Layer]
        GCL[Ganglion Cell Layer]
        IPL[Inner Plexiform Layer]
        INL[Inner Nuclear Layer]
        OPL[Outer Plexiform Layer]
        ONL[Outer Nuclear Layer Photoreceptors DEGENERATED]
        RPE[Retinal Pigment Epithelium]
        CHO[Choroid]
        SCLE[Sclera]
        EPI[EPIRETINAL ELECTRODE ARRAY]
    end
    EPI -. Sits on inner retinal surface .-> NFL
    ONL ~~~ DEG[Marked with dashed border to indicate loss of rods and cones]
```

### 4.3 Stimulation Pulse Waveform (Charge-Balanced Biphasic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[T0 equals 0 ms Start of cycle] --> P1[T1 Cathodal Phase Negative current]
    P1 --> P2[T2 equals T1 plus 0.45 ms Zero current interphase gap]
    P2 --> P3[T3 Anodal Phase Positive current equal charge reversed]
    P3 --> P4[T4 End of cycle pulse repeats at frame rate 20 to 60 Hz]
```

### 4.4 Stimulation Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Frame captured by camera] --> PROC[Image processed by VPU]
    PROC --> MAP{Per pixel brightness decision}
    MAP -- pixel above threshold --> ACT[Activate corresponding electrode with biphasic pulse]
    MAP -- pixel below threshold --> SKIP[Skip stimulation to save charge]
    ACT --> CHECK[Check Shannon safety log D plus log Q less than k value]
    CHECK -- within limits --> SAFE[Deliver to RGCs]
    CHECK -- exceeds limit --> ABORT[Abort pulse and flag fault]
    SKIP --> NEXT[Move to next frame]
    SAFE --> NEXT
    ABORT --> NEXT
```

> [!NOTE]
> All node IDs above are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `P0`, `NFL`, `MAP`, `SAFE`) and **never** use reserved Mermaid keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) as standalone identifiers. All node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text without embedded markdown formatting tags.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

**Q1. (3 Marks) Define an epiretinal implant. Where is it surgically placed?**

**Model Answer:**
An epiretinal implant is a microelectronic visual prosthesis designed to restore a crude form of vision in patients with degenerative retinal diseases such as retinitis pigmentosa (RP). It is surgically placed on the **inner (vitreal) surface of the retina**, directly apposed to the **ganglion cell layer (GCL)** and the **nerve fiber layer (NFL)**. The device bypasses the degenerated photoreceptors and electrically stimulates the surviving retinal ganglion cells (RGCs), whose axons form the optic nerve, to produce phosphene perception in the brain. The most clinically significant example is the **Argus II Retinal Prosthesis System**.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**Q2. (3 Marks) Why are biphasic charge-balanced pulses used in epiretinal stimulation?**

**Model Answer:**
Biphasic charge-balanced pulses are used to **prevent electrochemical damage** at the electrode–tissue interface. A monophasic DC current causes irreversible faradaic reactions — oxidation at the anode and reduction at the cathode — leading to electrode dissolution, pH shifts, gas evolution (H₂, O₂), and tissue injury. In a biphasic pulse, the cathodal (depolarizing) phase is immediately followed by a reverse anodal phase of **equal and opposite charge**, so the **net DC transfer is zero**. This restores the electrochemical equilibrium at the interface, enabling safe chronic stimulation of the retinal tissue.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks)

> **ESE (End Semester Evaluation) — KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern: Internal choice between Question A and Question B, each carrying 14 marks, divided into 7 + 7 sub-parts.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of an epiretinal visual prosthesis. List the major components and state the function of each.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Derive the Shannon safety limit equation and calculate the maximum allowable charge per phase for a 200 µm diameter platinum electrode using $k = 1.85$. Comment on the safety of a stimulation pulse of $Q = 0.2\ \mu C$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain the strength–duration relationship for neural stimulation. Define rheobase and chronaxie. Derive the relationship $I_{th} = I_r(1 + \sqrt{t_c/t_{ph}})$.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Compare epiretinal, subretinal, and suprachoroidal retinal implant approaches in terms of (i) surgical placement, (ii) target cell layer, (iii) surgical complexity, (iv) visual field coverage, and (v) clinical examples.

---

### 5.3 Model Solutions for Question A and Question B

#### Question A — Model Solution

**(a) (7 Marks) Block Diagram & Functional Architecture:**

| Block # | Component | Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | External CMOS Camera (on spectacles) | Captures real-time visual scene |
| 2 | Video Processing Unit (VPU) | Downsamples image, enhances contrast, encodes into stimulation map |
| 3 | External RF coil + transmitter | Sends data and power wirelessly to implant |
| 4 | Internal RF coil (receiver) | Receives power and data via inductive coupling |
| 5 | Hermetic ASIC | Decodes telemetry, generates biphasic current pulses |
| 6 | Demultiplexer | Routes each pulse to the correct electrode |
| 7 | Microelectrode array (60 electrodes, Argus II) | Delivers current to retinal ganglion cells |
| 8 | Retinal ganglion cells → Optic nerve → Visual cortex | Transmits perceived phosphene pattern to brain |

**Marks Distribution (as per KTU valuation key):**
- Correct block diagram — 3 marks
- Listing 5+ components — 2 marks
- Functions of each — 2 marks

**(b) (7 Marks) Shannon Limit Derivation and Safety Calculation:**

**Derivation (4 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1, the Shannon limit is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(D) &= k - \log_{10}(Q) \\
\Rightarrow D &= \frac{10^{k}}{Q} \\
\Rightarrow Q_{max} &= \sqrt{10^{k} \cdot A}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical calculation (3 marks):**

Given $k = 1.85$ and electrode diameter $= 200\ \mu m$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi r^{2} = \pi \cdot (0.01\ \text{cm})^{2} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{cm}^{2} \\
Q_{max} &= \sqrt{10^{1.85} \cdot 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= \sqrt{70.79 \cdot 3.1416 \times 10^{-4}} \\
&= \sqrt{2.224 \times 10^{-2}} \\
&\approx 0.149\ \mu C
\end{aligned}
$$

**Safety comment:** The applied pulse $Q = 0.2\ \mu C$ **exceeds** the maximum allowable $Q_{max} = 0.149\ \mu C$. This stimulation would fall **outside** the Shannon safety envelope (k-value boundary) and is **NOT safe** for chronic application at this electrode size. The examiner should reduce the phase duration or current amplitude to restore safety.

---

#### Question B — Model Solution

**(a) (7 Marks) Strength–Duration Relationship:**

**Theoretical explanation (4 marks):**

The strength–duration curve describes how the threshold current required to excite a neuron varies with the duration of the stimulating pulse. Two key parameters characterize the curve:

- **Rheobase ($I_r$):** The minimum DC current amplitude that, when applied for a very long duration, can just barely excite the neuron. (Units: µA or mA)
- **Chronaxie ($t_c$):** The minimum pulse duration required to excite the neuron at a current amplitude equal to **twice** the rheobase. (Units: ms or µs)

**Derivation (3 marks):** The Weiss-Lafordyce equation is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{th}(t_{ph}) &= I_r + \frac{a}{\sqrt{t_{ph}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply the chronaxie condition $I_{th}(t_c) = 2I_r$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2I_r &= I_r + \frac{a}{\sqrt{t_c}} \\
a &= I_r \cdot \sqrt{t_c}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{th} &= I_r + \frac{I_r \cdot \sqrt{t_c}}{\sqrt{t_{ph}}} \\
\boxed{\;I_{th} &= I_r \left(1 + \sqrt{\dfrac{t_c}{t_{ph}}}\right)\;}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) (7 Marks) Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Epiretinal | Subretinal | Suprachoroidal |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Surgical placement** | On inner retinal surface (vitreal side) | Between RPE & photoreceptor layer | Between choroid and sclera |
| **Target cell layer** | Ganglion cells (RGCs) | Bipolar cells (replaces photoreceptors) | Bipolar / RGCs (trans-retinal) |
| **Surgical complexity** | High (vitrectomy + tack fixation) | High (subretinal space access) | Moderate (ab externo approach) |
| **Visual field** | 20° (Argus II, 60 ch) | 11° (Alpha-IMS, 1500 px) | Wide potential |
| **Clinical examples** | Argus II (Second Sight) | Alpha-IMS / AMS (Retina Implant AG) | Bionic Vision Australia (suprachoroidal) |

**Marks Distribution:** Table completeness (5 marks) + clinical examples (1 mark) + concluding remark (1 mark).

---

### 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes costing marks in retinal implant questions:**
> 1. **Confusing epiretinal and subretinal placement** — this alone can lose 2–3 marks. Always state *which surface* of the retina the array contacts.
> 2. **Forgetting charge-balanced biphasic requirement** — many students write only the cathodal phase. The reverse anodal phase is **non-negotiable** for chronic implants.
> 3. **Wrong units in Shannon calculation** — Charge must be in **Coulombs (not µC)** and area in **cm² (not mm²)** when using the standard k-value definition.
> 4. **Missing the rheobase/chronaxie distinction** — these are two separate parameters, not interchangeable.
> 5. **Calling the optic nerve a "wire"** — it is a bundle of ~1 million RGC axons. Precision in anatomical terminology earns additional examiner goodwill.
> 6. **Ignoring encapsulation** — chronic implants trigger a foreign-body response (gliosis). Discussing it shows engineering maturity and scores above-average marks.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Epiretinal Implant — Final 60-Second Recap:**
>
> - ⭐ **Placement:** Inner (vitreal) surface of retina, contacting the ganglion cell layer.
> - ⭐ **Target cell:** Retinal ganglion cells (RGCs), bypassing degenerated rods/cones.
> - ⭐ **Energy delivery:** Charge-balanced biphasic current pulses — ALWAYS.
> - ⭐ **Key equation #1:** $Q = I \cdot t_{ph}$
> - ⭐ **Key equation #2:** $\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) \leq k$  (Shannon safety, $k \approx 1.85$)
> - ⭐ **Key equation #3:** $I_{th} = I_r \left(1 + \sqrt{t_c/t_{ph}}\right)$  (Weiss strength-duration)
> - ⭐ **Clinical gold standard:** Argus II (60 electrodes, FDA-approved 2013).
> - ⭐ **Trade-off:** Smaller electrodes → better resolution, but charge density rises.
> - ⭐ **Failure modes:** Encapsulation fibrosis, electrode lift-off, hermetic seal failure.
> - ⭐ **KTU buzzwords to memorize:** phosphene, charge-balanced, biphasic, rheobase, chronaxie, Shannon limit, inductive telemetry, vitrectomy, tack fixation.
> - ⭐ **Distinguish clearly:** Epiretinal (RGC-side) vs. Subretinal (bipolar-side) vs. Suprachoroidal (ab externo) — examiners **love** asking this comparison.
> - ⭐ **Pipeline:** Scene → Camera → VPU → RF transmit → Coil receive → ASIC → Electrode → RGC → Optic nerve → V1 → Phosphene.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Subretinal and Extraocular Implants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT753 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS AND IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Subretinal and Extraocular Implants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Subretinal Implants — Formal KTU Definition

A **subretinal implant** (also called a *subretinal microphotodiode array* or *sub-retinal prosthetic*) is a visual neuroprosthetic device that is surgically positioned **between the retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and the degenerated photoreceptor layer (outer nuclear layer)** of the retina. Its primary function is to electrically stimulate the surviving **inner retinal neurons** (bipolar and horizontal cells) by replacing the phototransduction function lost due to photoreceptor degeneration in diseases such as **Retinitis Pigmentosa (RP)** and **Age-related Macular Degeneration (AMD)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Subretinal implants operate on the principle that the *inner retinal neurons* (bipolar cells, ganglion cells) are largely preserved in RP and AMD, while only the photoreceptors die. Therefore, replacing the photoreceptor function at its anatomical location is feasible.

**Key Anatomical Reference Layers (from inner to outer retina):**
1. Inner Limiting Membrane (ILM)
2. Nerve Fiber Layer (NFL)
3. Ganglion Cell Layer (GCL)
4. Inner Plexiform Layer (IPL)
5. Inner Nuclear Layer (INL) — *Bipolar, Horizontal, Amacrine cells*
6. Outer Plexiform Layer (OPL)
7. **Outer Nuclear Layer (ONL) — Photoreceptors (target site for replacement)**
8. **Subretinal Space — Implant Location**
9. Retinal Pigment Epithelium (RPE)
10. Choroid → Sclera

### 1.2 Extraocular Implants — Formal KTU Definition

**Extraocular implants** are visual/neural prosthetics that are positioned **outside the eyeball (extra-ocular space)** — either on the **scleral surface (episcleral)**, within the **orbital cavity**, or attached to **extraocular muscles**. Unlike intraocular retinal prosthetics, these devices do **not penetrate the vitreous or retinal layers** for stimulation. They are primarily designed for:
- Restoration of ocular motility (eye movement) in patients with paralytic strabismus or ophthalmoplegia
- Cortical/surface-based visual stimulation via extraocular pathways in select experimental paradigms

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Distinction:** "Extraocular" must NOT be confused with "Epiretinal" (which is *inside* the eye, on the inner retinal surface). Extraocular strictly implies *outside the globe of the eye*.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Subretinal Implant Analogy — "Solar Panel Under the Shade":**
Imagine a greenhouse where the solar panels (photoreceptors) on the roof have been destroyed by a storm, but the wiring inside the greenhouse (bipolar and ganglion cells) is still perfectly intact. Instead of rebuilding the entire roof, we slip a **flexible micro-solar-panel array directly beneath the broken panels** (in the subretinal space). When sunlight enters through the broken roof, the new panel captures it, generates tiny electrical currents, and feeds those currents directly into the intact wiring — turning on the lights inside without ever rewiring the building.

**Extraocular Implant Analogy — "External Pacemaker for Eye Muscles":**
Think of a patient whose eye muscles have lost their nerve supply (like a heart with blocked electrical pathways). A traditional cardiac pacemaker is placed *outside* the heart and delivers controlled pulses to the muscle. Similarly, an extraocular implant sits *outside the eyeball* and delivers controlled electrical pulses to the **extraocular muscles** (lateral rectus, medial rectus, etc.) via surface electrodes, restoring coordinated eye movement without invading the globe itself.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Anchor for Exam:** *Subretinal = Sun (light) directly replaced under the retina; Extraocular = External muscle pacer outside the globe.*

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|-----------|----------------|------|
| Retinal stimulation threshold current | **10 – 100** | µA |
| Charge per phase (safe limit) | **< 0.36** | µC/cm² |
| Subretinal electrode diameter | **20 – 50** | µm |
| Microphotodiode size | **20 × 20** | µm² |
| Number of pixels (typical) | **~5000** | electrodes |
| Operating wavelength sensitivity | **500 – 700** | nm |
| Stimulation pulse duration | **0.1 – 2** | ms |
| Frequency of stimulation | **20 – 60** | Hz |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Subretinal Implant — Operational Architecture

A subretinal implant functions through the following sequential logic chain:

**Step 1 — Incident Light Capture**
Incoming photons (wavelength 500–700 nm) pass through the transparent inner retinal layers and reach the subretinal microphotodiode array (MPDA) located in the subretinal space.

**Step 2 — Photovoltaic Conversion**
Each microphotodiode (typically a silicon PIN photodiode) generates a **photocurrent** proportional to the local light intensity:
$$I_{ph} = \eta \cdot q \cdot \Phi_{ph}$$
where $\eta$ is the quantum efficiency, $q$ is the electron charge, and $\Phi_{ph}$ is the photon flux.

**Step 3 — Direct Stimulation of Bipolar Cells**
The generated current flows through the surrounding electrolyte (extracellular fluid) and depolarizes the **bipolar cell membrane** in the overlying INL. This bypasses the dead photoreceptors entirely.

**Step 4 — Signal Propagation Through Intact Retina**
Depolarization → neurotransmitter release at the bipolar cell axon terminal → ganglion cell activation → action potential travels via optic nerve → visual cortex.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 'Why' Behind Subretinal Design:** The subretinal location is preferred because:
> - It uses **natural image projection** from the eye's optics (cornea + lens), so no external camera is needed in the simplest designs.
> - It stimulates bipolar cells at an **earlier stage** of the visual pathway, preserving more natural retinotopic mapping.
> - The RPE provides **immunological privilege** and structural support to the device.

### 2.2 Extraocular Implant — Operational Architecture

**Step 1 — External Power and Signal Source**
A control unit (worn externally) generates stimulation pulses based on inputs from sensors (e.g., eye position encoders, EMG sensors, or cortical command signals).

**Step 2 — Transcutaneous/Inductive Power Transfer**
Power is delivered to the implant via **inductive coupling** (similar to cochlear implants) using paired RF coils.

**Step 3 — Surface Electrode Stimulation**
Sutured or screwed electrodes on the sclera or extraocular muscle surface deliver biphasic charge-balanced pulses:
- Pulse amplitude: 0.5 – 5 mA
- Pulse width: 100 – 500 µs
- Pulse rate: 20 – 50 Hz per channel

**Step 4 — Muscle Recruitment**
Stimulated extraocular muscles (e.g., lateral rectus) contract, producing controlled eye rotation. Multiple channels allow coordinated multi-muscle activation.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $I_{ph} = \eta \cdot q \cdot \Phi_{ph}$ | Photocurrent from microphotodiode | A |
| 2 | $P_{stim} = V_{th} \cdot I_{stim}$ | Stimulation power per electrode | W |
| 3 | $Q = I \cdot t$ | Charge per phase | C |
| 4 | $D = \frac{Q}{A}$ | Charge density (safety metric) | C/cm² |
| 5 | $\rho = \frac{V}{I}$ | Electrode-tissue interfacial resistance | Ω |
| 6 | $E = \frac{V_{stim}}{d}$ | Electric field in tissue | V/m |
| 7 | $f_{stim} = \frac{1}{T_{pulse}}$ | Stimulation frequency | Hz |
| 8 | $R_{retina} = \frac{\rho_{tissue} \cdot L}{A}$ | Effective retinal tissue resistance | Ω |
| 9 | $\Phi_{retinal} = \frac{N_{photons}}{A \cdot t}$ | Retinal photon flux | photons/(m²·s) |
| 10 | $\eta_{stim} = \frac{N_{activated}}{N_{total}}$ | Neural recruitment efficiency | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Safety Equation — KTU Exam Favorite:** The **Shannon safety limit** for chronic neural stimulation:
> $$\log(D) + k \cdot \log(Q/\text{phase}) < 1.85$$
> where $D$ is charge density and $k \approx 1.5$ for platinum electrodes. Exceeding this damages tissue.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Subretinal implants** represent the only class of visual prosthetics that preserve the natural optical pathway (cornea → lens → retina), giving them a unique advantage in retinotopic accuracy.
- **Extraocular implants** avoid intraocular surgical risks (vitreous hemorrhage, retinal detachment, endophthalmitis), making them attractive for high-risk patient cohorts.
- Both technologies drive advances in **biocompatible encapsulation** (e.g., parylene-C, titanium hermetic packages), **flexible thin-film electronics**, and **wireless power transfer** — techniques transferable to cochlear, cortical, and deep brain stimulators.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Photocurrent Generated by a Single Subretinal Microphotodiode

We derive the steady-state photocurrent delivered by a single silicon PIN photodiode embedded in the subretinal space, illuminated by ambient light transmitted through the ocular media.

**Given:**
- Photon flux incident on retina: $\Phi_{ph} = 10^{17}$ photons/(m²·s)
- Quantum efficiency of silicon at 550 nm: $\eta = 0.6$
- Active diode area: $A_{d} = 20\,\mu m \times 20\,\mu m = 4 \times 10^{-10}\,m^2$
- Electron charge: $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,C$

**Step 1 — Total photons absorbed per second on the active area:**
$$N_{ph} = \Phi_{ph} \cdot A_{d}$$

Substituting values:
$$N_{ph} = 10^{17} \times 4 \times 10^{-10}$$
$$N_{ph} = 4 \times 10^{7}\;\text{photons/s}$$

**Step 2 — Number of electron-hole pairs generated (using quantum efficiency):**
$$N_{e} = \eta \cdot N_{ph} = 0.6 \times 4 \times 10^{7}$$
$$N_{e} = 2.4 \times 10^{7}\;\text{carriers/s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert carrier generation rate to photocurrent:**
$$I_{ph} = N_{e} \cdot q$$

Substituting:
$$I_{ph} = 2.4 \times 10^{7} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$$
$$I_{ph} = 3.8448 \times 10^{-12}\;A$$
$$I_{ph} \approx 3.84\;pA$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A single 20×20 µm² photodiode under typical indoor illumination produces only ~3.84 pA — far below the ~10 µA threshold needed to stimulate a bipolar cell. This is why subretinal implants use **external power amplification** (ASR device) or **near-infrared (NIR) boosting illumination** (PRIMA, Pixium Vision systems).

### 3.2 Derivation: Charge Density Safety Check for Subretinal Electrode

We verify whether a proposed subretinal stimulation protocol falls within the safe charge-density limit to avoid electrochemical tissue damage.

**Given:**
- Stimulation current amplitude: $I_{stim} = 50\,\mu A = 50 \times 10^{-6}\,A$
- Pulse width per phase: $t_{pulse} = 0.5\,ms = 5 \times 10^{-4}\,s$
- Electrode diameter: $d_{e} = 50\,\mu m \Rightarrow r_{e} = 25\,\mu m$

**Step 1 — Charge per phase:**
$$Q = I_{stim} \cdot t_{pulse}$$
$$Q = 50 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$Q = 2.5 \times 10^{-8}\;C = 25\;nC$$

**Step 2 — Electrode surface area (assuming planar disk):**
$$A_{e} = \pi r_{e}^{2} = \pi \times (25 \times 10^{-6})^{2}$$
$$A_{e} = \pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-10}$$
$$A_{e} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-9}\;m^{2}$$
$$A_{e} = 1.9635 \times 10^{-5}\;cm^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Charge density:**
$$D = \frac{Q}{A_{e}} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-8}}{1.9635 \times 10^{-5}}$$
$$D = 1.273 \times 10^{-3}\;C/cm^{2} = 1.273\;mC/cm^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Compare to Shannon limit:**
$$\log(D) + k\log(Q) = \log(1.273 \times 10^{-3}) + 1.5 \times \log(2.5 \times 10^{-8})$$
$$= -2.895 + 1.5 \times (-7.602)$$
$$= -2.895 + (-11.403)$$
$$= -14.298$$

Since $-14.298 < 1.85$, the protocol is **deeply within safe limits**. ✅

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Subretinal vs. Extraocular Implants

| Parameter | Subretinal Implant | Extraocular Implant |
|-----------|---------------------|----------------------|
| Anatomical Location | Between RPE & photoreceptors | On sclera / orbital surface |
| Target Tissue | Bipolar & horizontal cells | Extraocular muscles |
| Surgical Risk | High (intraocular) | Moderate (extraocular) |
| Retinotopic Accuracy | Excellent (natural optics) | Not applicable (motor restoration) |
| Need for External Camera | Optional (natural image) | Always required |
| Power Source | Photovoltaic + external IR boost | Inductive RF coupling |
| Resolution Capability | High (up to ~1500 electrodes) | Limited by muscle recruitment |
| Major Indications | RP, AMD | Ophthalmoplegia, strabismus |
| Representative Devices | ASR (Optobionics), PRIMA (Pixium), Boston Retinal Implant | POEMS (Pulsed Orbital Extraocular Muscle Stimulator) |
| Chronic Stability | Limited by RPE changes | Good (outside globe) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Subretinal Implant System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ambient Light Entry via Cornea and Lens] --> B[Transparent Inner Retinal Layers]
    B --> C[Subretinal Space MPDA Array]
    C --> D{Microphotodiode Pixel}
    D --> E[Photovoltaic Conversion to Current]
    E --> F[Current Injection into Bipolar Cells]
    F --> G[Signal Relay through Ganglion Cells]
    G --> H[Optic Nerve Transmission]
    H --> I[Visual Cortex Perception]

    J[External NIR Booster Projection Optional] --> C

    subgraph PowerManagementUnit
        K[Hermetic Ti Package] --> L[Biocompatible Parylene C Encapsulation]
    end

    C -. embedded in .-> PowerManagementUnit
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Extraocular Implant System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[External Command Processor Worn on Glasses] --> B[RF Inductive Link Coils]
    B --> C[Implantable Pulse Generator IPG]
    C --> D[Multichannel Output Stage]
    D --> E[Scleral Surface Electrode Array]
    E --> F[Extraocular Muscle Recruitment]
    F --> G[Controlled Eye Rotation]
    G --> H[Restored Ocular Motility]

    subgraph FeedbackLoop
        I[Eye Position Sensor] --> A
    end

    subgraph SafetyUnit
        J[Charge Balanced Biphasic Stimulus]
        K[Impedance Monitoring]
    end

    C --> SafetyUnit
```

### 4.3 Comparative Anatomical Placement Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Cornea] --> B[Lens]
    B --> C[Vitreous Humor]
    C --> D[Ganglion Cell Layer]
    D --> E[Inner Nuclear Layer]
    E --> F[Outer Nuclear Layer Photoreceptors]
    F --> G[Subretinal Implant Location]
    G --> H[RPE]
    H --> I[Choroid]
    I --> J[Sclera]
    J --> K[Extraocular Implant Location]
    K --> L[Extraocular Muscles]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a subretinal implant. Mention the anatomical location and the type of retinal cells it primarily stimulates.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A subretinal implant is a visual neuroprosthetic device surgically placed in the **subretinal space**, i.e., between the **retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) and the outer nuclear layer** of the retina. It is used to replace the function of degenerated photoreceptors in diseases like retinitis pigmentosa and age-related macular degeneration.

**(1 mark)** – Definition of subretinal implant.
**(1 mark)** – Anatomical location: between RPE and photoreceptor layer (ONL).
**(1 mark)** – Stimulates bipolar and horizontal cells of the inner nuclear layer (INL), whose axons lead to ganglion cells.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*Differentiate between 'epiretinal' and 'extraocular' visual implants.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Epiretinal | Extraocular |
|---------|------------|-------------|
| Location | Inner retinal surface (on NFL/GCL) | Outside the eyeball (sclera/orbit) |
| Target | Ganglion cells directly | Extraocular muscles |
| Surgical Access | Vitreoretinal surgery | Extraocular muscle surgery |
| Indication | RP, AMD | Ophthalmoplegia, ocular motility disorders |

**(1 mark)** – Epiretinal: inner retinal surface, stimulates ganglion cells.
**(1 mark)** – Extraocular: outside the globe, stimulates extraocular muscles.
**(1 mark)** – Clear distinction in target cells and indication.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

(a) *With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a subretinal implant. Mention the diseases it aims to treat.* **(7 marks)**

(b) *A subretinal microphotodiode of area $25 \times 25 \,\mu m^2$ receives a photon flux of $5 \times 10^{17}$ photons/(m²·s). The quantum efficiency is 0.7. Calculate the photocurrent generated. Given $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\,C$.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Working Principle (7 marks):**

1. **(2 marks)** A subretinal implant consists of a microphotodiode array (MPDA) surgically implanted in the subretinal space, between the RPE and the degenerated photoreceptor layer.
2. **(2 marks)** Ambient light enters the eye through the cornea and lens, passes through transparent inner retinal layers, and strikes the MPDA. Each microphotodiode converts incident photons into a small photocurrent proportional to local light intensity.
3. **(1 mark)** This photocurrent flows through the extracellular medium and depolarizes the overlying bipolar and horizontal cells in the INL, bypassing the dead photoreceptors.
4. **(1 mark)** The signal then propagates via normal retinal circuitry: bipolar → ganglion cells → optic nerve → visual cortex.
5. **(1 mark)** Diseases treated: **Retinitis Pigmentosa (RP)** and **Age-related Macular Degeneration (AMD)**, where photoreceptors degenerate but inner retinal neurons are preserved.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Active area
$$A_{d} = (25 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-10}\;m^2$$

**Step 2:** Photons absorbed per second
$$N_{ph} = \Phi_{ph} \cdot A_{d} = 5 \times 10^{17} \times 6.25 \times 10^{-10} = 3.125 \times 10^{8}\;\text{photons/s}$$

**Step 3:** Electron-hole pairs generated
$$N_{e} = \eta \cdot N_{ph} = 0.7 \times 3.125 \times 10^{8} = 2.1875 \times 10^{8}\;\text{carriers/s}$$

**Step 4:** Photocurrent
$$I_{ph} = N_{e} \cdot q = 2.1875 \times 10^{8} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$$
$$I_{ph} = 3.5 \times 10^{-11}\;A = 35\;pA$$

**(1 mark)** Final answer: **$I_{ph} = 35\,pA$**

---

**Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1]**

(a) *Describe the architecture and working of an extraocular implant system. Mention its clinical indications.* **(7 marks)**

(b) *An extraocular stimulator delivers biphasic pulses of amplitude $2\,mA$ with pulse width $200\,\mu s$ per phase through a disk electrode of radius $1\,mm$. Calculate (i) the charge per phase, and (ii) the charge density. Comment on its safety using the Shannon limit.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Extraocular Implant Architecture (7 marks):**

1. **(2 marks)** An extraocular implant system consists of an **external command processor**, an **inductive RF link**, an **implantable pulse generator (IPG)**, and **scleral-surface electrodes** sutured onto the extraocular muscles.
2. **(1 mark)** The external unit processes input signals (e.g., from eye position sensors or EMG) and encodes them into stimulus patterns.
3. **(1 mark)** Power and data are transmitted transcutaneously via inductively coupled coils (primary on spectacle frame, secondary implanted).
4. **(1 mark)** The IPG generates **charge-balanced biphasic pulses** that are routed to selected muscle electrodes.
5. **(1 mark)** Stimulated muscles contract, producing controlled eye rotation and restoring ocular motility.
6. **(1 mark)** Clinical indications: **chronic ophthalmoplegia**, **paralytic strabismus**, **congenital cranial dysinnervation disorders**, and select cases of **nystagmus**.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $I_{stim} = 2\,mA = 2 \times 10^{-3}\,A$
- $t_{pulse} = 200\,\mu s = 2 \times 10^{-4}\,s$
- $r_{e} = 1\,mm = 10^{-3}\,m$

**Step (i) — Charge per phase:**
$$Q = I_{stim} \cdot t_{pulse} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-4}$$
$$Q = 4 \times 10^{-7}\;C = 0.4\,\mu C$$

**Step (ii) — Electrode area:**
$$A_{e} = \pi r_{e}^{2} = \pi \times (10^{-3})^{2} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-6}\;m^{2} = 3.1416 \times 10^{-2}\;cm^{2}$$

**Charge density:**
$$D = \frac{Q}{A_{e}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-7}}{3.1416 \times 10^{-2}} = 1.273 \times 10^{-5}\;C/cm^{2} = 12.73\,\mu C/cm^{2}$$

**Step (iii) — Shannon limit check:**
$$\log(D) + 1.5 \log(Q) = \log(1.273 \times 10^{-5}) + 1.5 \log(4 \times 10^{-7})$$
$$= -4.895 + 1.5 \times (-6.398) = -4.895 - 9.597 = -14.49$$

Since $-14.49 \ll 1.85$, the protocol is **well within safe limits**. ✅

**(1 mark)** Final verdict: Stimulation protocol is safe with large safety margin.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing subretinal with epiretinal** — many students write that subretinal implants stimulate ganglion cells. **WRONG.** They stimulate **bipolar cells** (since ganglion cells are on the inner retinal surface, far from the subretinal space). **−2 marks.**
> 2. **Forgetting to specify the disease indication** (RP and AMD) when discussing subretinal implants. Examiners deduct 1 mark for incomplete clinical context.
> 3. **In numerical problems, students forget unit conversions** — particularly $\mu m^2 \to m^2$ and $\mu A \to A$. Always show the conversion step explicitly. **−1 to −2 marks.**
> 4. **Not mentioning charge-balanced biphasic pulses** when describing extraocular or any neural stimulator. This is a KTU mandatory safety term. **−1 mark.**
> 5. **Confusing extraocular with intraocular** — extraocular means *outside the globe*. If you write "intraocular extraocular implant", expect full sub-part deduction.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Subretinal Implant** → placed between RPE and ONL; replaces dead photoreceptors; stimulates **bipolar cells**; uses **microphotodiode arrays**; treats **RP and AMD**.
- **Extraocular Implant** → placed outside the eyeball on sclera/orbit; stimulates **extraocular muscles**; restores **ocular motility**; uses **inductive RF power** and **charge-balanced biphasic pulses**.
- **Photocurrent formula:** $I_{ph} = \eta \cdot q \cdot \Phi_{ph} \cdot A_{d}$
- **Charge safety equation:** $Q = I \cdot t$, with Shannon limit $\log(D) + 1.5\log(Q) < 1.85$.
- **Stimulation thresholds:** ~10–100 µA; pulse width 0.1–2 ms; frequency 20–60 Hz.
- **Photodiode size:** ~20×20 µm²; typical array has ~5000 pixels.
- **Key clinical keywords:** Retinitis Pigmentosa, AMD, Ophthalmoplegia, Retinotopic mapping, Biphasic stimulation.
- **Examiner focus areas:** Anatomical placement, target cell type, working principle, device examples (ASR, PRIMA, Boston Retinal Implant, POEMS), and safety calculations.
- **Biocompatibility materials to remember:** Parylene-C encapsulation, titanium hermetic packages, platinum-iridium electrodes.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Visual Stimulation in the Brain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT753 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS AND IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Visual Stimulation in the Brain

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Visual Stimulation in the Brain** is defined as the controlled application of electrical, optical, or chemical energy to excitable neural tissue within the visual pathway (retina → optic nerve → Lateral Geniculate Nucleus (LGN) → primary visual cortex / V1) for the purpose of eliciting, restoring, or augmenting the sensation of light (a *phosphene*). In the domain of **Neural Prosthesis and Implants**, this concept underpins the design of **Visual Neuroprostheses** — devices such as retinal implants, optic-nerve cuffs, and **cortical visual prostheses (CVPs)** that bypass damaged photoreceptors to artificially recreate vision in patients suffering from retinitis pigmentosa (RP), age-related macular degeneration (AMD), or traumatic blindness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition:** A *phosphene* is the elementary visual percept (a spot of light, edge, or geometric shape) evoked by direct stimulation of visual neurons — it is the "pixel" of any artificial vision system.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of the visual cortex (V1) as a giant **chessboard painted on the back of your head**, and each square contains thousands of neurons. Normally, the eyes (the camera) send signals up through cables (optic nerves) to light up the right squares to form an image. Now imagine the camera is broken — you can still "tap" each chessboard square directly with a tiny electrode. Each tap makes that neuron cluster report a dot of light (phosphene) to your conscious mind. By tapping thousands of squares in the correct pattern, the brain "sees" an image even though no photons ever hit the eye. **The electrode array is a synthetic retina, and the cortex is the display.**

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following engineering metrics govern every visual neuroprosthesis and are **frequently tested** in KTU exams:

- **Typical safe cortical stimulation amplitude:** 1 mA – 10 mA
- **Pulse Width (PW):** 100 µs – 500 µs (charge-balanced biphasic)
- **Pulse Frequency:** 50 Hz – 200 Hz
- **Phosphene size:** ~ 0.5° to 5° of visual angle
- **Shannon's safety limit:** log(D) + log(Q) ≤ 1.85 (where D in kA/m², Q in µC/cm²)
- **Chronaxie of cortical neurons:** ≈ 100 µs – 400 µs

> [!NOTE]
> All KTU numerical problems on this topic will revolve around the **Strength-Duration curve (Weiss-Lapicque equation)**, **Charge per Phase**, and **Shannon's safety envelope**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Visual Pathway — Why Stimulation Must Happen Here

Information from the world travels through a well-defined hierarchy:

1. **Photoreceptors (rods & cones)** → transduce photons to neural spikes.
2. **Retinal Ganglion Cells (RGCs)** → encode contrast, motion, colour.
3. **Optic Nerve (CN II)** → cable bundle of ~1 million RGC axons.
4. **Lateral Geniculate Nucleus (LGN)** → thalamic relay, modulates saliency.
5. **Optic Radiations (Meyer's & Baum's loops)** → cable to cortex.
6. **Primary Visual Cortex (V1, Brodmann area 17, striate cortex)** → first cortical map; located in the occipital pole along the calcarine sulcus.

Damage at **any** stage produces blindness. A visual neuroprosthesis is therefore designed to stimulate the **most downstream intact node**:
- Damage ≤ RGCs → **Retinal implant** (epiretinal/subretinal).
- Damage at optic nerve → **Optic-nerve cuff** (C-SPI project).
- Damage beyond LGN → **Cortical Visual Prosthesis** (Dobelle, Brindley, Utah Array, Second Sight Orion).

## 2.2 Why Biphasic, Charge-Balanced Pulses?

Any charge imbalance causes **net DC** flow → electrochemical reduction of water → generation of toxic species (H₂, O₂, Cl₂) and irreversible electrode corrosion. Hence:
- **Cathodic phase** (negative): depolarises the neuron → triggers action potential.
- **Anodic phase** (positive): repolarises / balances charge → no net DC.

This is a fundamental design constraint **always asked in KTU Part A**.

## 2.3 Phosphene Characteristics

A phosphene is not a "real" pixel. Its engineering properties are:

- **Size**: grows with stimulation current (recruitment radius).
- **Brightness**: increases logarithmically with pulse amplitude.
- **Shape**: dot, bar, wedge, or curved arc depending on cortical column orientation.
- **Colour**: usually white/yellow, rarely coloured.
- **Location**: retinotopically mapped — stimulating occipital pole produces foveal percepts; stimulating anterior calcarine produces peripheral percepts.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The table below contains every formula you need for Module 1 visual stimulation questions. Note: the pipe `\|` is rendered as `\vert` to keep markdown tables valid.

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Application |
|---|---------|---------|----------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Charge per phase | $Q = I \cdot PW$ | I = pulse current, PW = pulse width | Coulombs (C) | Charge injection budget |
| 2 | Charge density | $D = Q \vert A$ | A = electrode geometric area | C/cm² | Shannon safety |
| 3 | Current density | $J = I \vert A$ | — | A/m² | Electrode heating |
| 4 | Weiss-Lapicque strength-duration | $I = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau}}$ | I_rh = rheobase, τ = membrane time constant | A | Threshold vs PW |
| 5 | Linearised form | $I \cdot t = I_{rh} \cdot t + Q_{min}$ | Q_min = minimum injected charge | C·A | Long-pulse regime |
| 6 | Chronaxie | $t_{ch} = \tau \cdot \ln 2$ | — | s | Tissue excitability index |
| 7 | Shannon envelope | $\log(D) + \log(Q) \le 1.85$ | D in kA/m², Q in µC/cm² | — | Safe stimulation region |
| 8 | Phosphene visual angle | $\theta \approx \arctan(d \vert L)$ | d = cortical activation radius, L = cortical magnification | rad / deg | Resolution estimation |
| 9 | Charge per stimulation train | $Q_{tot} = I \cdot PW \cdot N$ | N = number of pulses in train | C | Long-term dose |
| 10 | Stimulation frequency | $f_{stim} = 1 \vert T_{period}$ | T_period = pulse repetition interval | Hz | Refresh rate of artificial vision |

## 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World

This framework is not abstract — it directly determines the design of every commercial and clinical visual prosthesis:

- **Argus II (Second Sight)**: 60-electrode epiretinal array, 20° FOV.
- **Alpha-IMS (Retina Implant AG)**: 1500-photodiode subretinal subretinal array.
- **PRIMA (Pixium Vision)**: photovoltaic subretinal, 378 pixels.
- **Orion (Second Sight)**: 60-electrode cortical array — *currently the only cortical prosthesis in human trials*.
- **Dobelle's early cortical patient (1978)**: enabled a blind man to drive slowly in a parking lot.

Every one of these is bounded by the **Shannon limit**, **rheobase**, and **chronaxie** discussed above.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Weiss-Lapicque Strength-Duration Equation

We begin from the **cable equation** of a neuron's membrane under a stimulating electrode. The membrane is modelled as a parallel RC circuit. When a rectangular cathodic pulse of amplitude $I$ and duration $t$ is applied, the membrane voltage $V_m(t)$ rises exponentially toward a steady-state value $V_\infty$ determined by the rheobase.

**Step 1 — Membrane voltage response under a step current:**

The passive membrane obeys the first-order ODE:

$$
\tau \frac{dV_m}{dt} + V_m = V_\infty
$$

where $\tau = R_m C_m$ is the membrane time constant and $V_\infty = I \cdot R_m$ is the asymptotic depolarisation.

**Step 2 — Solve the ODE for $V_m(t)$:**

Starting from $V_m(0) = 0$ (resting potential), the solution is:

$$
V_m(t) = V_\infty \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)
$$

**Step 3 — Threshold criterion:**

An action potential is fired when $V_m(t)$ reaches the **critical firing threshold** $V_{th}$. Setting $V_m(t_{th}) = V_{th}$:

$$
V_{th} = V_\infty \left( 1 - e^{-t_{th}/\tau} \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of rheobase $I_{rh}$:**

Rheobase is defined as the **minimum current** that can fire the neuron when $t \to \infty$. Hence $V_\infty^{rh} = V_{th}$, giving $I_{rh} = V_{th}/R_m$. Replacing $V_\infty = I \cdot R_m$ and dividing:

$$
\frac{I \cdot R_m}{I_{rh} \cdot R_m} = \frac{1}{1 - e^{-t_{th}/\tau}}
$$

**Step 5 — Final Weiss-Lapicque equation:**

$$
\boxed{\, I = \frac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau}} \,}
$$

This is the **canonical strength-duration curve** and the most-tested KTU formula.

**Step 6 — Two asymptotic cases (essential for KTU problems):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(a) Long pulse } (t \gg \tau): \quad & I \to I_{rh} \\
\text{(b) Short pulse } (t \ll \tau): \quad & I \approx \dfrac{I_{rh} \cdot \tau}{t}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Chronaxie definition:**

Chronaxie $t_{ch}$ is the pulse width at which threshold current is **twice the rheobase**. Substitute $I = 2 I_{rh}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
2 I_{rh} &= \frac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t_{ch}/\tau}} \\
2 \left(1 - e^{-t_{ch}/\tau}\right) &= 1 \\
e^{-t_{ch}/\tau} &= \frac{1}{2} \\
t_{ch} &= \tau \cdot \ln 2
\end{aligned}
$$

Thus $\boxed{t_{ch} = 0.693 \, \tau}$.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A cortical neuron has rheobase $I_{rh} = 0.5$ mA and membrane time constant $\tau = 200$ µs. Find the threshold current for a pulse of width $t = 400$ µs. Also compute the chronaxie.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau}} = \frac{0.5 \text{ mA}}{1 - e^{-400/200}} = \frac{0.5}{1 - e^{-2}} \\
&= \frac{0.5}{1 - 0.1353} = \frac{0.5}{0.8647} \\
&\approx 0.578 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
t_{ch} = \tau \cdot \ln 2 = 200 \cdot 0.693 = 138.6 \text{ µs}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Shannon's Safety Limit

Robert Shannon (1992) compiled hundreds of microstimulation studies and found the empirical envelope below which neural damage is **not observed**:

$$
\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) \le 1.85
$$

where $D$ is in **kA/m²** and $Q$ in **µC/cm²**. Equivalently:

$$
D \cdot Q \le 10^{1.85} \approx 70.79 \;\; \text{(in mixed units)}
$$

This is the **single most-cited safety constraint** in neural implant design.

**Step-by-step interpretation:**

- $\log_{10}(D)$ is the **horizontal axis** of the *Shannon plot* (kA/m²).
- $\log_{10}(Q)$ is the **vertical axis** (µC/cm²).
- The line $\log(D) + \log(Q) = 1.85$ separates "safe" from "unsafe" combinations of charge density and charge per phase.

**Example check (KTU-style):** An electrode injects $Q = 1$ µC/cm² per phase. Compute the maximum allowable current density $D_{max}$:

$$
\log(D_{max}) = 1.85 - \log(1) = 1.85 \Rightarrow D_{max} = 10^{1.85} \approx 70.79 \text{ kA/m}^2
$$

## 3.4 Phosphene Size Estimation

Phosphene size scales with the **volume of neural tissue activated**. For cortical stimulation, the activated radius $r$ follows:

$$
r \approx k \cdot \sqrt{I}
$$

and the visual angle subtended is:

$$
\theta = \arctan\!\left( \frac{r}{L} \right)
$$

where $L$ is the **cortical magnification factor** (~ 15 mm / degree at the fovea, falling to ~ 3 mm / degree in periphery).

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define the term "phosphene" in the context of visual neuroprostheses. List two of its clinically observed characteristics.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **phosphene** is the elementary visual percept — typically a spot, bar, or geometric figure of light — that is elicited by direct electrical stimulation of neurons in the retina, optic nerve, lateral geniculate nucleus, or visual cortex, in the absence of any external photonic input. It is the smallest perceptual "pixel" of any artificial vision system.
*(2 marks for definition)*

Two clinically observed characteristics:
1. **Size grows with stimulation amplitude** — larger currents recruit more neurons radially, producing larger phosphenes. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Location is retinotopic** — stimulating the occipital pole produces foveal (central) percepts; stimulating anterior calcarine cortex produces peripheral percepts. *(0.5 mark)*

*(Bonus: brightness is roughly log-linear with current; colour is uncommon — usually white/yellow.)*

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Why are biphasic, charge-balanced pulses mandatory in any visual stimulation protocol?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A biphasic pulse consists of a cathodic (negative) phase followed by an anodic (positive) phase of equal charge magnitude. The cathodic phase depolarises the neuronal membrane and triggers an action potential that generates the phosphene, while the anodic phase **completely neutralises** the injected charge.
1. This prevents any **net DC component** from flowing through the electrode–tissue interface. *(1 mark)*
2. Without charge balance, electrolysis of extracellular fluid occurs, producing **toxic species (H₂, O₂, Cl₂, metal ions)** and causing **irreversible electrode corrosion**. *(1 mark)*
3. The result is **neural damage and device failure**, violating **Shannon's safety envelope**. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **ESE Module Question:** *Explain in detail the principles of electrical stimulation of the visual cortex for restoring vision in blind individuals. Discuss the design of cortical visual prostheses, stimulation parameters, the strength-duration relationship, and the major engineering challenges.*

### QUESTION A — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 - Modified]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Describe the architecture of a Cortical Visual Prosthesis (CVP). With a neat block diagram, explain the function of each block from the external camera to phosphene perception.*

**Model Solution:**

A Cortical Visual Prosthesis is a class of neural implant that **bypasses the entire sub-cortical visual pathway** by directly stimulating the **primary visual cortex (V1)**. Its architecture is divided into **external** and **implanted** sections:

1. **External Camera Module** *(0.5 mark)* — A miniature CMOS camera mounted on spectacle frames captures the scene in front of the patient at standard video rates (30–60 fps).

2. **Video Processing Unit (VPU)** *(1 mark)* — A belt-worn DSP performs image preprocessing: contrast enhancement, edge detection, down-sampling to the prosthesis resolution (e.g., 60 × 60), and extraction of salient features to reduce data bandwidth.

3. **Transcutaneous Telemetry Coil** *(1 mark)* — Power and encoded stimulation commands are transmitted wirelessly via an inductive RF link (typically 10–50 MHz), eliminating percutaneous wires and infection risk.

4. **Internal Receiver Coil & ASIC Decoder** *(1 mark)* — Implanted beneath the scalp, this block demodulates the RF signal, rectifies power, and recovers the stimulation command frame for each electrode.

5. **Stimulus Generator** *(1 mark)* — A current-steering DAC produces **charge-balanced biphasic constant-current pulses** with independently programmable amplitude (1–10 mA), pulse width (100–500 µs), and frequency (50–200 Hz) per channel.

6. **Utah / Michigan Electrode Array** *(1 mark)* — A 10 × 10 silicon-based microelectrode array (Brindley-Dobelle or Second Sight Orion) is inserted into the calcarine cortex. Each shank is ~1.5 mm long and delivers stimulation to a localised neuronal cluster, producing one phosphene per active channel.

7. **Phosphene Map & Patient Adaptation** *(1 mark)* — Through post-operative training (weeks to months), the patient learns to interpret the spatio-temporal pattern of phosphenes as meaningful visual information (edges, motion, large print).

8. **Conclusion** *(0.5 mark)* — Unlike retinal implants, CVPs can help patients with **damage anywhere from the optic nerve onwards**, including glaucoma, optic atrophy, and traumatic cortical injury — a major clinical advantage.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Derive the Weiss-Lapicque strength-duration equation. A cortical neuron has rheobase $I_{rh}$ = 0.8 mA and membrane time constant $\tau$ = 250 µs. Compute (i) the threshold current for a 500 µs pulse, (ii) the chronaxie time, and (iii) the charge per phase if the electrode area is 0.01 cm².*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Derivation of Weiss-Lapicque equation (3 marks):**

Starting from the passive membrane ODE:

$$
\tau \frac{dV_m}{dt} + V_m = V_\infty
$$

Solving with $V_m(0) = 0$:

$$
V_m(t) = V_\infty (1 - e^{-t/\tau})
$$

Setting $V_m(t_{th}) = V_{th}$ and substituting $V_\infty = I R_m$ and $V_{th} = I_{rh} R_m$:

$$
I = \frac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau}}
$$

**(ii) Numerical calculation (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \frac{0.8 \text{ mA}}{1 - e^{-500/250}} = \frac{0.8}{1 - e^{-2}} \\
  &= \frac{0.8}{1 - 0.1353} = \frac{0.8}{0.8647} \\
  &\approx 0.925 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Chronaxie (1 mark):**

$$
t_{ch} = \tau \ln 2 = 250 \cdot 0.693 = 173.25 \text{ µs}
$$

**(iv) Charge per phase & density (1 mark):**

$$
Q = I \cdot PW = 0.925 \times 10^{-3} \times 500 \times 10^{-6} = 4.625 \times 10^{-7} \text{ C} = 0.4625 \text{ µC}
$$

$$
D = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.4625 \text{ µC}}{0.01 \text{ cm}^2} = 46.25 \text{ µC/cm}^2
$$

Check Shannon: $\log(0.04625 \text{ kA/m}^2... )$ — within safe envelope. *(Conclusion: stimulation is safe.)*

---

### QUESTION B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 - Modified]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Compare and contrast retinal, optic-nerve, and cortical visual prostheses. Mention at least three clinical advantages and three limitations of the cortical approach.*

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table** (constructed within the answer):

| Feature | Retinal Implant | Optic-Nerve Cuff | Cortical Prosthesis |
|---------|----------------|------------------|----------------------|
| Stimulation site | RGC layer / subretinal | CN II | V1 calcarine cortex |
| Indication | RP, AMD | Optic nerve trauma | Anything beyond LGN |
| Electrode count | 60–1500 | 4–8 cuff contacts | 60–100 (Orion) |
| Surgical risk | Low (intraocular) | Medium | High (craniotomy) |
| FOV | 20°–40° | Wide but low res | Up to ~60° |
| Visual acuity | 20/1200 – 20/200 | Very coarse | Comparable to retinal |
| Biostability | Mature | Experimental | Improving |

**Clinical advantages of cortical approach (3 marks):**
1. **Universality** — works irrespective of cause (RP, glaucoma, trauma, optic atrophy).
2. **Large cortical real estate** — V1 has millions of neurons; high-density arrays possible.
3. **No intraocular surgery** — avoids risks of retinal detachment, infection, vitreous haemorrhage.

**Limitations of cortical approach (3 marks):**
1. **Craniotomy required** — risk of infection, seizures, cortical damage.
2. **Limited spatial resolution** — phosphenes are larger and less focal than retinal percepts.
3. **Training-intensive** — patients require months of rehabilitation.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Explain Shannon's safety limit. An electrode of area 0.005 cm² injects a charge of 0.5 µC per phase. Find the maximum safe current density. State what happens if the limit is exceeded.*

**Model Solution:**

Shannon (1992) empirically determined the **safe stimulation envelope**:

$$
\log(D) + \log(Q) \le 1.85
$$

where $D$ is in kA/m² and $Q$ in µC/cm². Stimulation parameters within this envelope have **not been observed** to cause histological neural damage in animal or human studies.

**Calculation:**

$$
Q = 0.5 \text{ µC/cm}^2
$$

$$
\log(D_{max}) = 1.85 - \log(0.5) = 1.85 - (-0.301) = 2.151
$$

$$
D_{max} = 10^{2.151} \approx 141.6 \text{ kA/m}^2
$$

Alternatively, $D = I/A$. With $A = 0.005 \text{ cm}^2 = 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m}^2$:

$$
I_{max} = D_{max} \cdot A = 141.6 \times 10^3 \times 5 \times 10^{-7} \approx 0.0708 \text{ A} = 70.8 \text{ mA}
$$

**Consequences of exceeding the limit (3 marks):**
1. **Electrochemical tissue damage** — irreversible electrolytic breakdown of extracellular fluid.
2. **Electrode corrosion** — metal ions (Pt, Ir) dissolve, damaging both tissue and device.
3. **Gliosis** — encapsulation by astrocytes and microglia, raising thresholds over time.
4. **Seizure risk** — particularly in cortical stimulation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Confusing charge density (Q/A) with charge per phase (Q)** — students often interchange units. Remember: Q is per phase in µC, D is in µC/cm².
> 2. **Forgetting the rheobase is the asymptote of the strength-duration curve** — not the threshold at typical pulse widths.
> 3. **Writing "phosphene = pixel" without explaining retinotopy** — KTU examiners specifically look for the retinotopic mapping mention.
> 4. **Forgetting to mention charge balance** when describing biphasic pulses — partial credit is deducted.
> 5. **Skipping the units** in numerical answers — even a correct formula loses 1 mark if units are absent.
> 6. **Mixing up Argus II (epiretinal) and PRIMA (subretinal)** — these are *favourite* KTU one-liners.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Visual Stimulation in the Brain**

- ✅ **Phosphene** = artificially-evoked visual percept (the "pixel" of any visual prosthesis).
- ✅ Visual pathway order: **Photoreceptors → RGC → Optic nerve → LGN → V1 cortex**.
- ✅ **Biphasic, charge-balanced pulses** are mandatory — no net DC allowed.
- ✅ **Cathodic phase** triggers the action potential; **anodic phase** restores charge balance.
- ✅ **Weiss-Lapicque equation**: $I = \dfrac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau}}$.
- ✅ **Rheobase** = minimum current at very long pulse widths (asymptote).
- ✅ **Chronaxie** $t_{ch} = \tau \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \tau$ — the pulse width at $2 \times$ rheobase.
- ✅ **Charge per phase** $Q = I \cdot PW$ ; **Charge density** $D = Q/A$.
- ✅ **Shannon's safety envelope**: $\log(D) + \log(Q) \le 1.85$ — never violate.
- ✅ **Cortical prosthesis** = bypasses everything upstream of V1 → widest patient applicability but highest surgical risk.
- ✅ **Retinal implants** (Argus II, PRIMA, Alpha-IMS) need intact RGCs — useless for optic-nerve disease.
- ✅ **Phosphene size ∝ √(stimulus current)**; **brightness ∝ log(I)**.
- ✅ **Retinotopic mapping** — foveal cortex is at occipital pole, peripheral cortex is anterior calcarine.
- ✅ **Typical CVP parameters**: 1–10 mA, 100–500 µs, 50–200 Hz, charge-balanced biphasic.
- ✅ **Utah Array** = 10×10 silicon microelectrode array, gold standard for cortical stimulation research.
- ✅ **Dobelle (1978)** demonstrated the first clinically meaningful cortical visual prosthesis in a human.
