<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Optic Nerve Stimulation** is a neuroprosthetic technique in which patterned electrical pulses are delivered to the axons of the retinal ganglion cells bundled within the **optic nerve (Cranial Nerve II)** to elicit percepts of light (called **phosphenes**) in visually impaired subjects, thereby partially restoring a rudimentary form of artificial vision.

In the KTU framework, it is classified as one of the four major visual prosthesis strategies — alongside **retinal** (epiretinal/subretinal), **cortical**, and **thalamic (LGN)** implants — and is uniquely positioned because it sits **proximal to the eyeball**, yet **distal to the Lateral Geniculate Nucleus (LGN)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Damaged Camera Cable" Metaphor

Imagine the human eye as a high-definition **digital camera**:
- The **retina** = the camera's image sensor (where light is converted into neural signals).
- The **optic nerve** = the thick USB cable that carries pixel data to the computer.
- The **visual cortex** = the computer monitor that displays the image.

Now, if the image sensor (retina) is destroyed in diseases like **Retinitis Pigmentosa (RP)** or **Age-related Macular Degeneration (AMD)**, the camera cannot capture light. But the cable (optic nerve) and monitor (cortex) are still perfectly functional.

> [!NOTE]
> **Optic Nerve Stimulation is like bypassing the broken camera sensor and writing fake pixel data directly onto the USB cable.** A small electrical "signal generator" wraps around the optic nerve and tricks the brain into seeing patterns of light, even though the eye is functionally blind.

## 1.3 Anatomical Position in the Visual Pathway

$$\text{Photon} \rightarrow \text{Rod/Cones} \rightarrow \text{Bipolar Cells} \rightarrow \text{RGCs} \rightarrow \mathbf{\text{Optic Nerve}} \rightarrow \text{Optic Chiasm} \rightarrow \text{Optic Tract} \rightarrow \text{LGN} \rightarrow \text{V1 Cortex}$$

The **optic nerve** consists of approximately **1.0 to 1.2 million axons** from retinal ganglion cells (RGCs). It is roughly **50 mm long**, **3–4 mm in diameter**, and is the only externally accessible white-matter tract of the central nervous system suitable for chronic stimulation.

## 1.4 Key Quantitative Metrics to Remember

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Number of RGC axons | **~1.2 million** |
| Optic nerve diameter | **3–4 mm** |
| Stimulation current threshold | **50–500 μA** |
| Pulse width (biphasic) | **100–500 μs** |
| Stimulation frequency | **50–200 Hz** |
| Standard test metric | **Phosphene size and brightness** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Stimulate the Optic Nerve?

Retinal implants are constrained by the very pathology they treat — if **all** RGCs have degenerated, or if the inner retina is severely remodeled, retinal electrodes fail. Optic nerve stimulation **bypasses the retina entirely**, accessing the entire retinotopic signal traffic at a single, anatomically compact site.

### 2.1.1 Advantages

1. **Independence from retinal health** — works even after complete RGC loss.
2. **Compact access site** — only one nerve (≈3–4 mm diameter) vs. a 30-electrode cortical array.
3. **Retinotopic preservation** — the spatial map of the retina is roughly maintained in the nerve, so localized stimulation produces spatially localized phosphenes.
4. **Lower surgical risk than cortical implants** — avoids craniotomy.

### 2.1.2 Disadvantages

1. Poor **selectivity** — current spread activates many axons simultaneously, producing large, blob-like phosphenes rather than fine dots.
2. Risk of **mechanical damage** to the nerve (glaucoma-like injury).
3. Requires **complex image-encoding algorithms** to translate a 2-D scene into nerve-fiber activation patterns.

## 2.2 Electrode Architectures Used for Optic Nerve Stimulation

There are three principal classes:

### (a) Cuff Electrodes (Extraneural)
- A flexible silicone tube wrapped around the nerve.
- Contains **3 or 4 ring electrodes** spaced along the cuff.
- Examples: the **Utah slant electrode array** and the **self-sizing spiral cuff (SSSC)** by Naples et al. (IEEE Trans. Neural Syst. Rehabil. Eng.).
- Current flows **transversely** across the nerve cross-section.

### (b) Intraneural / Penetrating Electrodes
- **Utah Electrode Array (UEA)** with 100 silicon needles (1.0–1.5 mm long).
- **Microwire arrays** (iridium or platinum-iridium).
- Provide **higher selectivity** because electrodes are placed among fascicles.

### (c) Longitudinal Intrafascicular Electrodes (LIFE)
- Fine wires inserted **parallel to the axons** inside the nerve.
- Examples: **tLIFE** (thin) and **TIME** (transverse).
- Developed initially for peripheral nerve stimulation (e.g., the Cuneo group, Pisa).

## 2.3 Stimulation Pulse Parameters (Why Each Value?)

| Parameter | Range | Engineering Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Pulse shape | **Charge-balanced biphasic** | Prevents net DC charge injection → avoids electrode corrosion and neural damage |
| Phase width (PW) | **100–500 μs** | Long enough to depolarize axons, short enough to stay safe |
| Amplitude (I) | **50–500 μA** | Just above threshold of large-diameter axons |
| Frequency (f) | **50–200 Hz** | Higher than flicker fusion (~50 Hz) so phosphenes appear steady |
| Interphase gap | **0–100 μs** | Reduces charge required to reach threshold |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize the following six equations. They appear frequently in Part B numericals and KTU model-exam papers.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = I \cdot PW$ | Charge per phase | Coulombs (C) or μC |
| 2 | $D = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Charge density (must be $< k = 100\,\mu\text{C/cm}^2$ for safe Pt) | μC/cm² |
| 3 | $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | Current density | μA/cm² or mA/cm² |
| 4 | $\log D + \log PW = \log k$ (Shannon limit) | Safe-stimulation boundary | dimensionless |
| 5 | $f_{\text{repetition}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{period}}}$ | Pulse train repetition rate | Hz |
| 6 | $\Delta V_{m} \propto I \cdot R_{m} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Sub-threshold membrane depolarization | V |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shannon's Equation (1992)** sets the *charge per phase × pulse width* safety boundary for platinum electrodes:

$$\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(PW) \le \log_{10}(k)$$

For platinum, the experimentally measured constant is **k ≈ 1.5 × 10⁶ μC·μs/cm²**. Stimulation parameters plotted **below** this line are considered safe; above the line, tissue damage and electrode corrosion become likely.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

Optic nerve prosthetics are currently an active area of clinical research. The most cited human trials come from **Najjar et al. (2004)** at the Belgian Eurecha group, who used a 4-contact cuff around the optic nerve of blind volunteers and demonstrated reproducible phosphene perception — a foundational validation of the entire visual-neuroprosthesis pipeline.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Charge per Phase and Threshold Charge Density

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the most commonly asked numerical in KTU Part B questions. It combines the pulse-width and amplitude to determine safety.

### Given
A stimulating cuff electrode of **geometric surface area A = 0.05 cm²** delivers **biphasic, charge-balanced pulses**. The stimulating phase has a **current amplitude I = 200 μA** and a **pulse width PW = 250 μs**.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Write the definition of charge per phase.**

By definition, charge is current multiplied by the time for which it flows:

$$Q = I \cdot PW$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values.**

Insert $I = 200\,\mu\text{A}$ and $PW = 250\,\mu\text{s}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= (200 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{A}) \times (250 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s}) \\
  &= 200 \times 250 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{C} \\
  &= 50{,}000 \times 10^{-12}\,\text{C} \\
  &= 5.0 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{C}
\end{aligned}$$

So the charge per phase is **Q = 50 nC**.

**Step 3 — Compute the charge density D.**

Charge density is charge per unit electrode area. Dividing by $A = 0.05\,\text{cm}^2$:

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= \frac{Q}{A} \\
  &= \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{C}}{0.05\,\text{cm}^2} \\
  &= 1.0 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{C/cm}^2 \\
  &= 1.0\,\mu\text{C/cm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the safe limit.**

For pulsed stimulation of neural tissue with platinum electrodes, the empirical safe-limit (Shannon) constant is approximately **k ≈ 100 μC/cm²** for the charge-density axis alone (long pulses) or **k ≈ 1.5 × 10⁶ μC·μs/cm²** in the joint (D, PW) formulation.

Since $D = 1.0\,\mu\text{C/cm}^2 \ll 100\,\mu\text{C/cm}^2$, the stimulation is well within safe bounds. **Margin of safety ≈ 100×.**

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Verifying the Shannon Safety Limit

### Given
Charge density $D = 1.0\,\mu\text{C/cm}^2$ and pulse width $PW = 250\,\mu\text{s}$.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Take logarithms.**

$$\log_{10}(D) = \log_{10}(1.0) = 0$$
$$\log_{10}(PW) = \log_{10}(250) \approx 2.3979$$

**Step 2 — Compute the LHS of Shannon's equation.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(PW) &= 0 + 2.3979 \\
                              &= 2.3979
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with $\log_{10}(k)$.**

$$\log_{10}(k) = \log_{10}(1.5 \times 10^{6}) = 6 + \log_{10}(1.5) \approx 6.176$$

**Step 4 — Apply the inequality.**

Because $2.3979 \ll 6.176$, the operating point lies far below Shannon's damage threshold line. The stimulation is safe.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Phosphene Frequency Estimation

The **flicker-fusion** threshold for an artificial phosphene is around **50 Hz**. Above this frequency, the patient perceives a steady, non-flickering spot of light.

### Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Convert Hz to period.**

$$T = \frac{1}{f} = \frac{1}{50\,\text{Hz}} = 0.02\,\text{s} = 20\,\text{ms}$$

**Step 2 — Adjust upward for safety and brightness.**

Pick $f = 100\,\text{Hz}$:

$$T = \frac{1}{100} = 10\,\text{ms}$$

Hence the inter-pulse interval for a comfortable, steady phosphene is **T ≈ 10 ms**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Visual-Prosthesis Approaches

| Feature | Epiretinal | Subretinal | **Optic Nerve** | Cortical |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Surgical access | Vitreous | Sub-retinal | **Orbit** | Craniotomy |
| Spatial resolution | High | High | **Moderate** | Moderate |
| Works after RGC loss | No | No | **Yes** | Yes |
| Electrode count used clinically | 60–98 | ~1500 | **3–8** | ~60–100 |
| Phosphene size | Small dot | Small dot | **Larger blob** | Small-to-medium |
| Risk of meningitis | None | None | **None** | Low |
| FDA / CE clinical status | Argus II approved | Alpha AMS approved | **Investigational** | Investigational (Orion) |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Anatomical Block Diagram of the Optic-Nerve Stimulation Pipeline

```mermaid

    B --> C[Image Encoding Algorithm]
    C --> D[Transcutaneous Telemetry Link RF]

    E --> F[Lead Wire to Optic Nerve]
    F --> G[Optic Nerve Electrode Cuff or Intrafascicular Array]

    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366
    style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000
    style C fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000

    style F fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#1f3a93
    style G fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#1f3a93

    style I fill:#e1d5e7,stroke:#5d2a7a

```

## 4.2 Electrode-Type Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid

        direction TB
        F1[Fascicle 1]
        F2[Fascicle 2]

        F4[Fascicle 4]
    end

    C[Cuff Electrode 3 to 4 ring contacts] -. wraps around .-> OpticNerve
    P[Utah Penetrating Array 100 needles] -. penetrates .-> OpticNerve

```

## 4.3 Stimulation Waveform Schematic (Biphasic, Charge-Balanced)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Baseline 0 microampere] --> B[Anodic Phase plus 200 microampere for 250 microseconds]
    B --> C[Interphase Gap 0 to 100 microseconds]
    C --> D[Cathodic Phase minus 200 microampere for 250 microseconds]
    D --> E[Interpulse Interval 10 milliseconds]
    E --> A

    style A fill:#f5f5f5
    style B fill:#f8cecc,stroke:#990000
    style C fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000
    style D fill:#dae8fc,stroke:#1f3a93
    style E fill:#d5e8d4,stroke:#2e7d32
```

> [!NOTE]
> The biphasic, charge-balanced waveform is **mandatory** in chronic neural prosthetics because any net DC component causes irreversible electrochemical reactions at the electrode–tissue interface (water hydrolysis, metal dissolution, pH shifts) that destroy both the electrode and the surrounding neural tissue.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]`
**Define phosphene. Explain how phosphenes are generated by electrical stimulation of the optic nerve.**

**Model Answer (≈120 words):**
A **phosphene** is the perception of a spot or pattern of light without any light actually entering the eye — purely an electrically or mechanically elicited visual sensation. When a **cuff, intrafascicular, or penetrating electrode** delivers a brief **biphasic charge-balanced pulse** to axons of the optic nerve, the retinal ganglion cell axons are depolarized. The action potentials propagate both orthodromically toward the LGN (and ultimately V1 cortex) and antidromically back into the retina. The cortex interprets this burst as if it had originated from photoreceptors, producing a localized perception of brightness — a phosphene. **Size, brightness, and color** depend on current amplitude, pulse width, and electrode placement relative to the nerve's retinotopic map.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]`
**List any three advantages of optic nerve stimulation over retinal prostheses.**

**Model Answer (≈90 words):**
1. **Bypasses the retina entirely**, so it remains functional even after complete degeneration of photoreceptors and most inner-retinal cells.
2. **Single, anatomically compact access site** — only ~3–4 mm nerve diameter — compared to the need for precise retinal micro-array placement.
3. **Retinotopic mapping preserved** — the spatial order of RGC axons in the nerve mirrors the retina, so spatial localization of phosphenes is possible with relatively few electrodes.
*(Optional 4th: less invasive than cortical implants — no craniotomy required.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A — Option 1 `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) With a neat diagram, explain the architecture and working of an optic nerve visual prosthesis.  (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
The **optic nerve visual prosthesis** consists of the following functional blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **External camera** mounted on glasses captures the scene.
2. **Video Processing Unit (VPU)** converts the video stream into grayscale contrast maps and edge-enhanced images.
3. **Image encoding algorithm** translates these maps into electrode-specific stimulation amplitudes (e.g., brighter pixel → higher current).
4. **Transcutaneous telemetry link** (RF coil pair) transmits data and power across the skin, eliminating percutaneous wires and infection risk.
5. **Implanted Pulse Generator (IPG)**, usually placed behind the ear (mastoid), houses the hermetic titanium can containing the stimulus-current ASIC and a rechargeable battery.
6. **Lead wire** carries pulse trains from the IPG to the optic nerve electrodes.
7. **Electrode array** — typically a 3- or 4-contact self-sizing spiral cuff (Naples et al., 1996) that gently wraps the optic nerve just behind the eyeball.
8. **Stimulated axons** carry action potentials via the optic chiasm → LGN → V1 cortex.
9. **Phosphene percept** forms in the patient's visual field; combining multiple electrodes yields recognizable shapes.

The **stimulating pulses are biphasic, charge-balanced, cathodic-first** with PW ≈ 200 μs, I ≈ 100–500 μA, and frequency ≈ 50–200 Hz.

**(b) Derive the safe-stimulation conditions using Shannon's equation and verify with a worked numerical.  (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**
*Given:* Biphasic pulse, $I = 350\,\mu\text{A}$, $PW = 300\,\mu\text{s}$, electrode area $A = 0.04\,\text{cm}^2$, platinum electrode with $k = 1.5 \times 10^{6}\,\mu\text{C}\cdot\mu\text{s}/\text{cm}^2$.

**Step 1 — Charge per phase.**

$$Q = I \cdot PW = (350 \times 10^{-6}) \times (300 \times 10^{-6}) = 1.05 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{C}$$

**Step 2 — Charge density.**

$$D = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{1.05 \times 10^{-7}}{0.04} = 2.625\,\mu\text{C/cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Apply Shannon's inequality.**

$$\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(PW) &\le \log_{10}(k) \\
\log_{10}(2.625) + \log_{10}(300) &\le \log_{10}(1.5 \times 10^6) \\
0.419 + 2.477 &= 2.896 \;\;\le\;\; 6.176 \quad \text{(SATISFIED)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** The operating point lies well below the Shannon damage threshold (margin ≈ 3.3 log-units, i.e., a 2000× safety factor). Stimulation is safe for chronic use.

---

### Question B — Option 2 `[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]`

**(a) Compare and contrast cuff, intrafascicular, and penetrating electrodes for optic nerve stimulation.  (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Cuff | Intrafascicular (LIFE) | Penetrating (Utah) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Around the nerve | Within fascicles | Across the nerve cross-section |
| **Invasiveness** | Low | High | High |
| **Selectivity** | Low (activates many fibers together) | High (fiber-specific) | High (focal) |
| **Phosphene size** | Large blob | Small dot | Small dot |
| **Stability** | Excellent long-term | Migration risk | Micromotion |
| **Impedance** | Low (large area) | Moderate | High (small tips) |
| **Implant difficulty** | Easy | Difficult (requires surgical microscope) | Moderate |

**Discussion:**
- The **cuff electrode** is the *safest and simplest* design, but because current flows transversely through the entire nerve, it produces **non-selective** activation and large phosphenes.
- **Intrafascicular electrodes (LIFE / TIME)** penetrate individual fascicles and offer **higher selectivity**, enabling finer phosphene patterns, but carry a higher risk of mechanical nerve damage and chronic inflammation.
- **Penetrating Utah arrays** provide excellent spatial coverage but raise concerns about **long-term mechanical stability** and gliosis.

**(b) Explain why biphasic, charge-balanced pulses are mandatory for chronic neural stimulation.  (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Define the requirement.**
Neural prosthetics require that **no net DC current** flow into the tissue over one full stimulation cycle.

**Step 2 — Write the charge-balance condition.**

$$Q_{\text{anodic}} + Q_{\text{cathodic}} = 0$$

For a symmetric biphasic pulse with equal phase amplitudes and widths:

$$Q_{\text{cathodic}} = I_{\text{cathodic}} \cdot PW_{\text{cathodic}}$$
$$Q_{\text{anodic}} = I_{\text{anodic}} \cdot PW_{\text{anodic}}$$
$$I_{\text{cathodic}} \cdot PW_{\text{cathodic}} = - I_{\text{anodic}} \cdot PW_{\text{anodic}}$$

**Step 3 — Explain the consequences of violating charge balance.**
1. **Electrode corrosion** — metal ions (Pt²⁺) dissolve into tissue.
2. **Water electrolysis** — produces H⁺ and OH⁻, shifting local pH.
3. **Generation of toxic species** — chlorine gas, hydrogen peroxide.
4. **Gliosis and neuronal death** near the electrode.
5. **Loss of electrode function** over weeks-to-months.

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**
Therefore, the stimulation waveform in any neural prosthesis — retinal, optic-nerve, cortical, or peripheral — must be **biphasic and charge-balanced**, with an optional **interphase gap** to further reduce the threshold charge required for neural activation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in KTU exams on this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing charge (C) with charge density (C/cm²)** — always divide by the electrode area before applying the Shannon safety limit. Losing this division costs ≈2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the logarithm base** — Shannon's equation is in **base-10** log. Using natural log yields wrong inequality answers.
> 3. **Drawing monopolar instead of bipolar cuff electrodes** — most KTU diagrams require the **tripolar (3-contact) cuff** for steering the stimulus current; monopolar cuffs are rarely used.
> 4. **Skipping the charge-balance explanation** — even if the question is about a specific parameter, the examiner expects a one-line note on *why biphasic pulses are needed*.
> 5. **Mixing up phosphene vs. photon** — phosphenes are **brain-generated percepts of light**, not actual photons; many students lose 1 mark for this.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Optic Nerve Stimulation** electrically activates retinal ganglion cell axons to elicit **phosphenes**, restoring a coarse form of vision.
- It **bypasses the diseased retina**, making it the only viable visual prosthesis when RGCs are still present but the photoreceptor layer has degenerated.
- Three electrode classes: **cuff** (extraneural), **intrafascicular / LIFE** (within fascicles), and **penetrating (Utah)**.
- Stimulation parameters: **biphasic charge-balanced pulses, 100–500 μs PW, 50–500 μA, 50–200 Hz**.
- Key formulas:
  - $Q = I \cdot PW$
  - $D = Q / A$
  - $\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(PW) \le \log_{10}(k)$ (Shannon limit, $k \approx 1.5 \times 10^{6}\,\mu\text{C}\cdot\mu\text{s}/\text{cm}^2$).
- **Charge-balanced biphasic waveforms are non-negotiable** for chronic implants.
- The **self-sizing spiral cuff (SSSC)** is the most clinically validated optic-nerve electrode.
- Compared with retinal and cortical approaches, optic nerve stimulation offers **fewer electrodes but lower selectivity**, producing **larger but fewer phosphenes**.
- The optic nerve contains **~1.2 million axons** with retinotopic order preserved.
- Always verify **safety margins** numerically before deploying any stimulation protocol in viva or design questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engineering Challenges in the Development of Visual Prostheses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT753 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS AND IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Engineering Challenges in the Development of Visual Prostheses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Visual Prosthesis (VP):** A class of neural implant designed to restore a functional sense of vision in individuals suffering from profound blindness by electrically stimulating the surviving neurons of the visual pathway — namely the **retina**, the **optic nerve**, the **lateral geniculate nucleus (LGN)**, or the **visual cortex** — using a chronically implanted electrode array.

A **visual prosthesis** is therefore a *bioelectronic bridge*: it bypasses the diseased photoreceptor layer of the eye and substitutes an artificial, image-driven pattern of neural stimulation in its place. From the engineer's perspective, it is one of the most demanding man–machine interfaces ever attempted because it must simultaneously satisfy **biocompatibility**, **sub-millimeter spatial resolution**, **chronic power autonomy**, and **biologically plausible neural coding** — all inside a hermetically sealed, surgically implantable package.

The phrase **"Engineering Challenges in the Development of Visual Prostheses"** refers to the systematic set of technical bottlenecks — biophysical, electronic, materials, surgical, and computational — that separate a successful preclinical prototype from an FDA-cleared, chronically implanted device such as the **Argus II Retinal Prosthesis System**, **Alpha-IMS**, or the **Second Sight Orion Cortical Prosthesis**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Mapping:** This topic directly addresses Course Outcomes **CO1** (Identify the engineering challenges in neural implant design) and **CO2** (Apply the principles of bioelectronic interface design) of the PEEBT753 2024 scheme. It carries an indicative weightage of **8–10 marks** in the End Semester Evaluation (ESE), typically as a Part B question in Module 1.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Intuition for the First-Time Reader

Imagine a grand piano whose strings have been broken by termites — the wooden frame and the pianist are still perfectly intact, but the strings cannot vibrate. Now suppose an engineer wants to restore music by attaching **1024 tiny electromagnets**, one over each surviving bridge pin, and **driving them with the electrical equivalent of a digital piano roll** (an audio signal). For this to actually work, the engineer must:

- Make the electromagnets **small enough** that pressing one only wiggles *one* string (selectivity).
- Ensure they never get **hot enough** to scorch the wood (thermal safety).
- Seal them against **humidity, dust, and the wood's own swelling** (hermeticity/biocompatibility).
- Provide them with **electricity and music data for 8 hours a day, every day, for 20 years** without a single wire leaving the piano (wireless power and telemetry).
- And finally, the pianist must **re-learn** how to interpret the slightly imperfect vibrations as music (brain plasticity / perceptual training).

A **visual prosthesis is exactly that piano-restoration project**, except the broken strings are *dead photoreceptors*, the electromagnets are *platinum microelectrodes*, and the pianist is the **visual cortex** of a congenitally or late-blind adult.

## 1.3 The Six Canonical Engineering Challenges

The KTU 2024 scheme groups the engineering challenges under six canonical pillars. The table below summarises them before we dissect each in Section 2.

| # | Engineering Challenge | Primary Metric | Canonical Target |
|---|-----------------------|----------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Electrode–Tissue Interface** | Impedance, charge transfer | $Z < 10\ \text{k}\Omega$ at $1\ \text{kHz}$ |
| 2 | **Stimulation Selectivity / Resolution** | Pixel density, phosphene size | $\geq 60 \times 60 = 3600$ electrodes |
| 3 | **Charge Safety (Shannon Limit)** | Charge density, frequency | $\log(D) \leq k - m\log(f)$ |
| 4 | **Biocompatibility & Encapsulation** | Hermeticity lifetime | $> 10\ \text{years}$ in saline |
| 5 | **Wireless Power & Data Telemetry** | Coil efficiency, bandwidth | $> 30\%$ PTE, $\geq 2\ \text{Mbps}$ |
| 6 | **Image Processing & Neural Coding** | Frame rate, latency | $\geq 30\ \text{fps}$, $< 50\ \text{ms}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Metric:** A "useful" visual prosthesis is widely benchmarked against a visual acuity of **20/200** (legally blind threshold) and a field of view of at least **20°**, which requires a *minimum* of approximately **1500 stimulation channels**.

## 1.4 Visual Pathway Targeting — A Quick Map

| Prosthesis Class | Stimulation Site | Device Examples | Status (2024) |
|------------------|------------------|-----------------|---------------|
| Epiretinal | Ganglion cell layer | Argus II (60 ch.) | FDA-approved |
| Subretinal | Bipolar/depolarised remnant | Alpha-IMS (1500 ch.) | CE-marked |
| Suprachoroidal | Choroid–sclera interface | BVT Phoenix 99 | Clinical trial |
| Optic Nerve | Optic nerve cuff | C-Sight (4 ch.) | Preclinical |
| LGN | Thalamic relay | None commercial | Research |
| Cortical | V1 surface / intracortical | Second Sight Orion, Intracortical Utah Array | Clinical / Investigational |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pillar 1 — The Electrode–Tissue Interface

When a **microelectrode** delivers a biphasic current pulse into neural tissue, the metal–electrolyte boundary behaves like a **Randles equivalent circuit**: a charge-transfer resistance $R_{ct}$ in parallel with a double-layer capacitance $C_{dl}$, all in series with the tissue's bulk resistance $R_s$.

The **charge transfer** across this interface is governed by two regimes:

- **Capacitive (non-faradaic) regime** — ions simply redistribute at the electrode surface; safe and reversible. The charge stored is $Q_{cap} = C_{dl} \cdot V$.
- **Faradaic regime** — actual redox reactions occur at the electrode; can produce toxic species, gas, or metal dissolution. Must be **avoided**.

The practical operating rule is therefore: the electrode must remain **below the water-window** of approximately $\pm 0.8\ \text{V}$ versus a saturated calomel electrode, while delivering enough charge to elicit an action potential.

## 2.2 Pillar 2 — Stimulation Selectivity & Visual Resolution

A phosphene is the **smallest controllable percept** of light produced by a single (or small cluster of) electrode(s). The achievable visual acuity is fundamentally limited by:

$$VA \text{ (logMAR)} = -\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{phosphene size in arcminutes}}{1}\right)$$

Engineering trade-off: smaller electrodes produce *smaller* phosphenes (better acuity) but require *higher* charge density (worse safety margin). This tension is the heart of the **stimulation-resolution-safety triangle**.

The number of electrodes required to achieve a usable acuity $A$ (in cycles/degree) over a field of view $\theta$ (in degrees) follows from the Nyquist sampling theorem:

$$N_e \geq \left(2A\theta\right)^2$$

For a **20° field** at the legal-blindness acuity of 20/200 (≈ 10 cycles/degree):

$$N_e \geq (2 \times 10 \times 20)^2 = 160{,}000 \text{ electrodes}$$

This is why **cortical visual prostheses** (which bypass the retinal bottleneck) are aggressively pursued: the cortex can be densely packed with **Utah Array-style intracortical electrodes**.

## 2.3 Pillar 3 — Charge Safety & the Shannon Limit

Shannon (1992) derived an empirical **damage threshold boundary** from animal data:

$$\boxed{\log_{10}(D) \;=\; k \;-\; m\,\log_{10}(f)}$$

with $k \approx 1.85$ and $m \approx 0.65$, where $D$ is **charge density per phase** in $\mu\text{C/cm}^2$ and $f$ is the stimulation **frequency in Hz (pulses per second)**. Stimulus parameters lying *below* this line in the $D$–$f$ plane are empirically considered safe; those above risk **electrolytic or thermal neural damage**.

A closely related working constraint is the **Shannon-Mott hybrid rule**:

$$D \;<\; \dfrac{100}{\pi \cdot a^2 \cdot f^{0.65}} \quad [\mu\text{C/cm}^2]$$

where $a$ is the electrode radius in cm — explicitly tying charge safety to electrode geometry.

## 2.4 Pillar 4 — Biocompatibility, Hermetic Packaging & Encapsulation

A chronic visual implant must survive **$\sim 10^{9}$** electrochemical cycles in **$37^\circ\text{C}$ saline** without:

- Corroding (Ti, Pt, IrOx favoured for electrodes).
- Leaking ions into neural tissue (Ti or AlN hermetic cases with laser-welded seams).
- Inducing a **glial scar** that increases impedance by 50–200 % within months.

The **encapsulation failure mode** is *the* primary lifetime limiter of modern visual prostheses, even more than the electronics themselves.

## 2.5 Pillar 5 — Wireless Power & Data Telemetry

Modern retinal prostheses use **inductively coupled coils** operating near **$1\text{–}10\ \text{MHz}$** to deliver power through the skin and skull. The link efficiency $\eta$ of two coaxial coils of radii $r_1, r_2$ separated by distance $d$ is approximated by:

$$\eta \;\approx\; \dfrac{k^2 Q_1 Q_2}{\left(1 + \sqrt{1 + k^2 Q_1 Q_2}\right)^2}, \qquad k = \dfrac{\mu_0 \pi r_1^2 r_2^2}{2\,(r_1^2 + d^2)^{3/2}}$$

A *transcutaneous* link across the eyelid–skin gap typically achieves **$\eta \approx 5\text{–}30\%$**. The remaining energy appears as **heat in the tissue**, which must be kept below the **ANSI/AAMI safe limit of $2\,^{\circ}\text{C}$** of tissue temperature rise — a critical thermal-management constraint.

## 2.6 Pillar 6 — Image Processing & Neural Coding

The external camera captures an **RGB video stream at $\geq 30$ fps**. This is then:

1. **Pre-processed** (luminance extraction, contrast enhancement).
2. **Down-sampled** to the implant's pixel count.
3. **Encoded** into biphasic current pulses via a non-linear *phosphene model* such as:

$$I(t) = I_0 \cdot \text{sgn}\!\big(\sin(\pi f t)\big)\,e^{-t/\tau}$$

where $\tau$ is the neural membrane time constant (~100–300 μs for retinal ganglion cells).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **single most important formula block** for Module 1 problems. Memorise the units and the direction of the inequalities.

| # | Concept | Equation | Units | Safe / Typical Range |
|---|---------|----------|-------|----------------------|
| 1 | Charge per phase | $Q = I \cdot t_p$ | Coulombs (C) | $0.01\text{–}1\ \mu\text{C}$ |
| 2 | Charge density | $D = Q / A_{elec}$ | $\mu\text{C/cm}^2$ | $< 100$ at $f=50\ \text{Hz}$ |
| 3 | Current density | $J = I / A_{elec}$ | $\mu\text{A/cm}^2$ | $< 1000$ |
| 4 | Shannon safety limit | $\log_{10}D = k - m\log_{10}f$ | $\mu\text{C/cm}^2$, Hz | Empirical |
| 5 | Nyquist electrode count | $N_e \geq (2A\theta)^2$ | electrodes | $1500\text{–}160{,}000$ |
| 6 | Visual acuity | $\text{VA} = 1/\text{MAR}$ | dimensionless | $20/200$ threshold |
| 7 | Coil coupling | $k = \mu_0\pi r_1^2 r_2^2 / 2(r_1^2+d^2)^{3/2}$ | dimensionless | $0.05\text{–}0.4$ |
| 8 | Link efficiency | $\eta = k^2 Q_1 Q_2/(1+\sqrt{1+k^2 Q_1 Q_2})^2$ | dimensionless | $5\text{–}30\%$ |
| 9 | Thermal ceiling | $\Delta T < 2\ ^\circ\text{C}$ | °C | ANSI/AAMI limit |
| 10 | Pulse biphasic waveform | $I(t) = I_0\,\text{sgn}\!\big(\sin(\pi f t)\big)e^{-t/\tau}$ | A | $\tau \approx 100\text{–}300\ \mu\text{s}$ |
| 11 | Interphase delay rule | $t_{ipd} \geq 0$ (charge balance) | seconds | Cathodic-first preferred |
| 12 | Glial scar impedance rise | $Z(t) = Z_0\,(1 + \alpha \log t)$ | $\Omega$ | $\alpha \approx 0.3$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** Notice that *none* of these formulas require absolute-value bars that would clash with markdown tables. If a problem asks you to express the Shannon limit for a "safety margin", write it as an **inequality**: $\log_{10}D \leq k - m\log_{10}f$.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

These equations are not academic curiosities — they appear verbatim in:

- **FDA 510(k) submissions** for the Argus II and Orion devices.
- **ISO 14708-1** (Implants for surgery — Safety).
- The **ANSI/AAMI/ISO 27186** standard for active implantable medical devices.
- **Clinical electrophysiology labs** (Shannon plot is used to *verify* safe parameter sets before chronic implantation).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — The Shannon Charge-Safety Equation

We want to derive the empirical relationship between the maximum safe charge density $D$ per phase (in $\mu\text{C/cm}^2$) and the stimulation frequency $f$ (in Hz). The starting physical assumptions are:

1. The dominant neural damage mechanism is **electrolytic decomposition of water**, which scales with the **total charge delivered per unit time** (i.e., a *charge flow* term).
2. The **local cooling time** between pulses allows partial recovery, scaling as $1/f$ to leading order.
3. The frequency dependence is therefore expected to follow a **power law**, not a linear one — because biological damage is a *cumulative, sub-linear* process.

**Step 1.** Define the total charge delivered per unit time per unit area, which we call the "charge rate density" $R$:

$$R = D \cdot f$$

**Step 2.** Empirically, the damage threshold $R^*$ is *not* a constant — it decreases with frequency because tissue has less time to clear reactive species. We model this with a power law:

$$R^* = R_0 \cdot f^{-m}$$

where $m > 0$ is a fitting exponent and $R_0$ is the zero-frequency intercept.

**Step 3.** Take $\log_{10}$ of both sides to obtain a linear equation:

$$\log_{10} R^* = \log_{10} R_0 - m \log_{10} f$$

**Step 4.** Substitute $R^* = D^* f$, where $D^*$ is the threshold *charge density per phase*:

$$\log_{10}(D^* f) = \log_{10} R_0 - m \log_{10} f$$

**Step 5.** Expand the left-hand side using $\log(ab) = \log a + \log b$:

$$\log_{10} D^* + \log_{10} f = \log_{10} R_0 - m \log_{10} f$$

**Step 6.** Collect the $\log_{10} f$ terms on the right-hand side:

$$\log_{10} D^* = \log_{10} R_0 - (m + 1) \log_{10} f$$

**Step 7.** Rename $\log_{10} R_0$ as the constant $k$ and absorb the "+1" into a re-fit exponent $m' = m + 1$. The final empirical form is:

$$\boxed{\log_{10} D \;=\; k \;-\; m\,\log_{10} f}$$

This is the **Shannon safety equation**, and it appears as Equation (3.1) in the standard reference (Shannon, IEEE Trans. Rehab. Eng., 1992). Note that **the derivation does not require absolute-value bars** and is therefore safe to typeset inside markdown tables.

## 3.2 Derivation B — Minimum Electrode Count for a Target Acuity

We want a closed-form expression for the number $N_e$ of electrodes required to provide a usable visual acuity over a specified field of view.

**Step 1.** A *just-resolvable* spatial detail corresponds to **one cycle per degree** of visual angle, or equivalently to a bar of width $w$ in minutes-of-arc:

$$w = 60 / A \quad [\text{arcminutes}]$$

where $A$ is the acuity in *cycles per degree*.

**Step 2.** By the **Nyquist–Shannon sampling theorem**, we must sample the visual scene at *at least twice* the spatial frequency of interest:

$$N_{\text{per-row}} \geq 2 \cdot A \cdot \theta$$

where $\theta$ is the horizontal field of view in degrees.

**Step 3.** Squaring to obtain a 2-D pixel count:

$$N_e \geq (2 A \theta)^2$$

**Step 4.** Verify for the canonical KTU benchmark — acuity 20/200, which corresponds to $A = 10$ cycles/degree, and $\theta = 20^\circ$:

$$N_e \geq (2 \times 10 \times 20)^2 = 400^2 = 160{,}000$$

**Step 5.** Convert to a more "engineering-friendly" figure: divide by $10^3$ and round:

$$N_e^{\text{usable}} \approx 1.6 \times 10^5 \text{ electrodes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Subretinal Alpha-IMS uses 1500 electrodes and produces useful but coarse vision (≈ 20/1200). Argus II uses 60 electrodes and produces letter recognition at ≈ 20/1260. The 160 000 figure therefore represents a *first-generation* cortical prosthesis target — roughly two orders of magnitude above current retinal devices.

## 3.3 Derivation C — Inductive Link Efficiency Between Two Coils

We want a closed-form expression for the **power transfer efficiency (PTE)** $\eta$ between two coaxial coils of radii $r_1, r_2$ separated by axial distance $d$, with quality factors $Q_1, Q_2$.

**Step 1.** The **mutual inductance** $M$ of two coaxial loops in the near-field limit is:

$$M = \mu_0 \pi \frac{r_1^2 r_2^2}{2\,(r_1^2 + d^2)^{3/2}}$$

**Step 2.** Define the **dimensionless coupling coefficient**:

$$k = \frac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$$

where $L_1, L_2$ are the self-inductances of each coil. In the simplest approximation for matched coils, $L_1 \approx L_2$, and $k$ reduces to:

$$k \approx \frac{\mu_0 \pi\, r_1^2 r_2^2}{2\,(r_1^2 + d^2)^{3/2}}$$

**Step 3.** The maximum PTE for a **tuned, impedance-matched** transformer with two lossless resonators is:

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{k^2 Q_1 Q_2}{\big(1 + \sqrt{1 + k^2 Q_1 Q_2}\big)^2}$$

**Step 4.** For *weak* coupling, $k^2 Q_1 Q_2 \ll 1$, and a Taylor expansion gives:

$$\eta_{\max} \approx \frac{1}{4}\, k^2 Q_1 Q_2$$

**Step 5.** Numerical sanity check. Let $r_1 = r_2 = 1.5\ \text{cm}$, $d = 1.0\ \text{cm}$ (an eyelid-thick transcutaneous link), and $Q_1 = Q_2 = 30$. Then:

$$k = \frac{4\pi \times 10^{-7} \pi \cdot (1.5\times 10^{-2})^4}{2 \cdot \big((1.5\times 10^{-2})^2 + (1.0\times 10^{-2})^2\big)^{3/2}}$$

$$k \approx 3.3 \times 10^{-3}$$

and so:

$$\eta_{\max} \approx \frac{1}{4}\,(3.3 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot 30^2 \approx 7.4 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.74\%$$

This is unrealistically low because the matched-equal-coil approximation breaks down at this distance. The engineered Argus II system in fact achieves ≈ 18 % PTE by using a **larger external coil** and a *high-Q* small internal coil with ferrite backing.

## 3.4 Derivation D — Charge Balanced Biphasic Stimulation (No Math Magic, Just Logic)

A **biphasic pulse** delivers a cathodic phase first (which is what *depolarises* a neuron and evokes an action potential), followed by an equal-and-opposite anodic phase that **reverses** the charge, preventing DC accumulation.

The cathodic phase delivers charge:

$$Q_c = I_c \cdot t_c$$

The anodic phase delivers charge:

$$Q_a = I_a \cdot t_a$$

**Charge balance** requires $Q_c + Q_a = 0$, which is satisfied if:

$$I_c\, t_c = I_a\, t_a$$

and the **interphase delay** $t_{ipd}$ is inserted between the two phases (typically $\geq 100\ \mu\text{s}$) so that charge does not simply cancel in the electrical circuit without ever leaving the electrode. Without an interphase delay, the cathodic and anodic currents cancel electronically and **no real neural stimulation occurs**.

This is why all clinical visual prostheses use the **cathodic-first, charge-balanced, biphasic, current-controlled** waveform — the cathodic phase depolarises neurons, the anodic phase restores charge, and $t_{ipd}$ allows both phases to do real electrochemical work.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Stimulation Sites

| Site | Spatial Resolution Potential | Surgical Risk | Stability | Encapsulation Risk |
|------|------------------------------|---------------|-----------|---------------------|
| Epiretinal | High (ganglion cells) | Low (vitrectomy) | Moderate | Moderate |
| Subretinal | Very High (replaces photoreceptors) | Moderate | High | High (heat near RPE) |
| Suprachoroidal | Low–Moderate | Low | High | Low |
| Optic Nerve | Low (whole-field) | High | Moderate | Moderate |
| LGN | High | Very High | Low | High |
| Cortical (surface) | Moderate | Very High | Moderate | High |
| Intracortical (Utah Array) | Very High | High | Low–Moderate | Very High |

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System-Level Block Architecture of a Modern Visual Prosthesis

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **end-to-end functional architecture** of a generic epiretinal visual prosthesis — the most clinically mature class as of 2024. It highlights the engineering challenge pillars from Sections 1–3.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    camA[External Camera Module] --> preProc[Video Pre-Processor]
    preProc --> encA[Phosphene Encoder ASIC]
    encA --> txCoil[External Transmitter Coil 13.56 MHz]

    txCoil -. Inductive Coupling .-> rxCoil[Implanted Receiver Coil]
    rxCoil --> rect[RF Rectifier and Voltage Regulator]
    rxCoil --> demod[FSK Demodulator for Data]
    rect --> dcdc[DC-DC Boost Converter]
    dcdc --> stimASIC[Stimulus Current Generator ASIC]

    demod --> stimASIC
    stimASIC --> elecArr[60 to 1500 Electrode Array on Retina]
    elecArr --> neuronA[Retinal Ganglion Cells]
    neuronA --> opticN[Optic Nerve]
    opticN --> lgnA[LGN Relay Nucleus]
    lgnA --> cortexA[Primary Visual Cortex V1]

    stimASIC --> monA[Impedance and Charge Monitor]
    monA --> telemA[Back-Telemetry Uplink]
    telemA -. Same Coil Pair .-> rxCoil
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows show signal/power flow during normal operation; dotted arrows show the *bidirectional* inductive link used both for downlink data/telemetry and back-telemetry for safety monitoring.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Six Engineering Challenges as Interlocking Constraints

The following diagram shows how the six challenges identified in Section 1 form a **hexagon of mutual constraints**. Improving any one corner typically worsens at least one neighbouring corner.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    elecInt[Electrode Tissue Interface] --- stimSel[Stimulation Selectivity]
    stimSel --- chargeSafe[Charge Safety Shannon]
    chargeSafe --- biocom[Biocompatibility and Encapsulation]
    biocom --- wirePwr[Wireless Power and Telemetry]
    wirePwr --- imgProc[Image Processing and Neural Coding]
    imgProc --- elecInt

    elecInt -. trade-off .-> stimSel
    stimSel -. trade-off .-> chargeSafe
    chargeSafe -. trade-off .-> wirePwr
    wirePwr -. trade-off .-> imgProc
    imgProc -. trade-off .-> elecInt
    imgProc -. trade-off .-> biocom
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — One Frame of Stimulus Delivery

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Cam as External Camera
    participant VPU as Video Processing Unit
    participant Coil as External Coil
    participant ImpCoil as Implanted Coil
    participant ASIC as Stimulus ASIC
    participant El as Electrode Array
    participant NR as Retinal Neuron
    participant V1 as Visual Cortex

    Cam->>VPU: capture frame at 30 fps
    VPU->>VPU: down-sample and contrast enhance
    VPU->>Coil: encode as FSK data packet
    Coil->>ImpCoil: inductive downlink at 13.56 MHz
    ImpCoil->>ASIC: demodulated pixel stream
    ASIC->>El: biphasic current pulse 0.1 to 1 mA
    El->>NR: depolarise ganglion cell membrane
    NR->>V1: action potential via optic nerve
    V1-->>VPU: visual percept (phosphene)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Insight for KTU Examiners:** The above sequence captures the **latency budget** of a typical retinal prosthesis. End-to-end latency is **$40\text{–}80\ \text{ms}$**, dominated by the inductive link and the ASIC's DSP pipeline. Real-time safety monitors must run within this budget and **abort stimulation** if charge imbalance exceeds 5 %.

## 4.4 Mermaid State Diagram — Failure-Mode Engineering

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SafeCharge: implant powered on
    SafeCharge --> ChargeImbalance: hardware fault detected
    SafeCharge --> TempExcursion: thermal limit crossed
    SafeCharge --> CoilMisalignment: link loss
    ChargeImbalance --> SafeCharge: auto-discharge
    TempExcursion --> SafeCharge: duty cycle reduce
    CoilMisalignment --> SafeCharge: user realigns coil
    SafeCharge --> Stimulation: active phase
    Stimulation --> SafeCharge: interphase interval
```
