A neural electrical stimulator works the same way for nerves: it does not create new nervous signals from nothing; instead, it **intercepts the biological wiring** and forces the nerve to fire a *new* action potential by injecting a small, controlled burst of charge.
>
> Just as a too-strong current would blow a fuse and a too-weak current would fail to switch on the bulb, a stimulator must deliver a charge that is **strong enough** to depolarize the neuron's membrane past its *threshold* (typically around **-55 mV**), but **small enough** to avoid electrochemical damage to the tissue.

### 1.3 The Big Picture — Why It Matters

- Every modern neuroprosthesis — cochlear implant, retinal implant, DBS, sacral nerve stimulator, FES for foot-drop — is **fundamentally a Neural Electrical Stimulator** plus an electrode interface.
- The "intelligence" of the device lies not in the stimulator alone but in the **closed-loop** combination: Stimulator → Electrode → Nerve → Response (sensor) → Controller → Stimulator.
- Design constraints are governed by **safety** (charge per phase **< 0.4 µC/cm²** for safe stimulation, known as the *Shannon limit*), **efficacy** (must recruit the target neurons), and **biocompatibility**.

### 1.4 The Five Core Parameters of Stimulation

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — The 5 "Knobs" of a Stimulator**
>
> 1. **Amplitude (V or mA)** — how strong each pulse is.
> 2. **Pulse Width (µs or ms)** — how long each pulse lasts.
> 3. **Frequency (Hz)** — how many pulses are delivered per second.
> 4. **Waveform Shape** — monophasic vs. biphasic vs. more complex.
> 5. **Electrode Configuration** — monopolar, bipolar, multipolar.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Architecture of a Neural Stimulator

A neural stimulator is composed of **five interlinked functional blocks**. Understanding the role of each block is critical for any KTU exam question on "design of a stimulator."

1. **Power Source** — typically a *lithium-iodine* or *rechargeable lithium-ion* battery (3–6 V, 1–10 mAh typical).
2. **DC-DC Converter / Voltage Multiplier** — boosts the battery voltage to a higher rail (**5 V to 15 V**) so the output stage can drive the capacitive tissue load.
3. **Pulse-Forming Network (Output Stage)** — the *current-mirror* or *Howland current pump* that actually shapes the output pulse.
4. **Control / Logic Block** — a microcontroller, ASIC, or discrete logic that sets amplitude, pulse width, frequency, and timing.
5. **Electrode–Tissue Interface** — the lead wires, hermetic feedthrough, and electrode contacts in contact with the tissue.

### 2.2 Stimulus Waveforms — The Most Exam-Relevant Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Monophasic vs. Biphasic Waveforms**
>
> - **Monophasic:** Single polarity pulse. *Net DC* flows through tissue → causes electrochemical damage and electrode corrosion. *Never used clinically for chronic implants.*
> - **Biphasic (Symmetric or Asymmetric):** A *leading cathodal* phase followed by an *anodal* phase. The two phases have **equal charge**, so net DC = 0. This is the **clinical gold standard**.
> - **Charge-Balanced Biphasic:** The *gold-standard* modern waveform. The second phase exactly cancels the charge injected by the first phase.

**Why biphasic is mandatory for chronic implants:**
- DC residue causes *electrolysis* of body fluids → produces H₂, O₂, Cl₂, and toxic species.
- DC residue drives *pH shifts* near the electrode.
- DC residue causes *faradaic* reactions that corrode the metal electrode.
- **Charge imbalance below 100 nC** is the modern safety target.

### 2.3 Strength–Duration Curve — The Most Tested Concept

The **Strength–Duration (S–D) curve** describes the *minimum stimulus amplitude* required to excite a nerve as a function of *pulse width*. It is a **hyperbolic** relationship governed by **Weiss's Law (1901)** and later refined by **Lapicque (1907)**.

The two most important landmarks on the curve are:
- **Rheobase (I_r):** The *minimum* current amplitude needed to excite the nerve at a *very long* pulse width. Conceptually, the DC threshold.
- **Chronaxie (C):** The *pulse width* at which the threshold current is **twice the rheobase**. Conceptually, a "characteristic time constant" of the nerve membrane.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table consolidates every formula you need for KTU questions on Neural Electrical Stimulators.**

| # | Quantity | Equation | Symbols | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Weiss's Law** | $I_{th} = a + \dfrac{b}{PW}$ | $I_{th}$ = threshold current, $a$ = constant, $b$ = constant, $PW$ = pulse width | mA, ms |
| 2 | **Lapicque Equation** | $I_{th} = I_{r}\left(1 + \dfrac{C}{PW}\right)$ | $I_{r}$ = rheobase, $C$ = chronaxie | mA, ms |
| 3 | **Charge per Phase** | $Q = I_{th} \cdot PW$ | $Q$ = charge | µC (micro-coulomb) |
| 4 | **Charge Density** | $D = \dfrac{Q}{A_{elec}}$ | $A_{elec}$ = electrode area | µC/cm² |
| 5 | **Energy per Pulse** | $E = V \cdot I \cdot PW$ | $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current | µJ |
| 6 | **Energy per Second** | $P = E \cdot f$ | $f$ = pulse frequency | µJ/s = µW |
| 7 | **Capacitive Discharge (voltage-mode)** | $V(t) = V_{0}\,e^{-t/\tau}$ | $\tau = R\,C_{tissue}$ | V, s |
| 8 | **Shannon Safety Limit** | $\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(A_{elec}) \le k$ | $D$ = charge density, $A_{elec}$ = electrode area, $k \approx 1.5$–$1.85$ | — |
| 9 | **Power Dissipated in Tissue** | $P = I^{2} R_{tissue}$ | $R_{tissue}$ = tissue resistance | mW |
| 10 | **Tissue RC Time Constant** | $\tau = R_{tissue}\cdot C_{tissue}$ | — | s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions for KTU Numericals**
> - **Safe charge injection limit:** $Q_{max} \approx 0.4\ \mu C / \text{phase}$ for small electrodes.
> - **Typical pulse widths:** $30$ to $400\ \mu s$.
> - **Typical clinical frequencies:** $20$ to $200\ Hz$.
> - **Typical neural time constant (τ):** $100\ \mu s$ to $1\ ms$.
> - **Chronaxie ranges:** $50\ \mu s$ (large myelinated) to $7\ ms$ (unmyelinated C-fibers).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **DBS (Deep Brain Stimulator):** Frequency 130–185 Hz, PW 60–210 µs, amplitude 1–5 V, used for Parkinson's disease.
- **Cochlear Implant:** Frequency 250 Hz–8 kHz (channel-specific), PW ~25–100 µs, current-mode biphasic.
- **Spinal Cord Stimulator:** 40–60 Hz, PW 200–500 µs, used for chronic pain.
- **FES (Foot-Drop):** 20–50 Hz, PW 200–400 µs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Lapicque's Strength–Duration Equation from Membrane Dynamics

We model the **nerve membrane** as a simple *parallel RC circuit*, where:
- $C_m$ = membrane capacitance (charge-storage element of the lipid bilayer).
- $R_m$ = membrane resistance (ion-channel conductance).
- $V_m(t)$ = membrane potential.
- $I(t)$ = injected stimulus current.

**Step 1 — Write the KCL at the membrane node.**

The total current splits into a *resistive* component through $R_m$ and a *capacitive* component charging $C_m$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(t) &= I_{R} + I_{C} \\[4pt]
I(t) &= \dfrac{V_m(t)}{R_m} + C_m\,\dfrac{dV_m(t)}{dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* "The current we inject must either flow out through the resistive channels or be stored on the membrane capacitor."

**Step 2 — Rearrange into the standard first-order linear ODE.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_m\,\dfrac{dV_m}{dt} + \dfrac{V_m}{R_m} &= I(t) \\[4pt]
\dfrac{dV_m}{dt} + \dfrac{V_m}{R_m C_m} &= \dfrac{I(t)}{C_m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Define the membrane time constant:* $\tau_m = R_m C_m$.

**Step 3 — Solve the homogeneous equation first.**

The natural (unforced) decay follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{m,h}(t) &= V_{0}\,e^{-t/\tau_m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* "If we leave the membrane alone, any pre-existing voltage $V_0$ decays exponentially with time constant $\tau_m$."

**Step 4 — Apply a rectangular stimulus pulse of constant amplitude $I$ for a duration $PW$.**

The particular (steady-state) solution of the ODE is $V_{m,p} = I R_m$.

The full solution under constant current (with the membrane initially at rest, $V_m(0)=0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_m(t) &= I R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Define the threshold condition.**

A neural action potential fires when $V_m(t)$ reaches the *threshold voltage* $V_{th}$. Setting $V_m(PW) = V_{th}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{th} &= I_{th}\,R_m \left(1 - e^{-PW/\tau_m}\right) \\[4pt]
I_{th} &= \dfrac{V_{th}}{R_m \left(1 - e^{-PW/\tau_m}\right)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* "To make the membrane voltage reach threshold exactly at the end of a pulse of width $PW$, the current must be high enough to overcome the exponential lag of the capacitor."

**Step 6 — Identify the limits (Rheobase and Chronaxie).**

- **Rheobase $I_r$:** As $PW \to \infty$, the exponential term vanishes: $I_{th} \to V_{th}/R_m$. Therefore:
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_r &= \dfrac{V_{th}}{R_m}
\end{aligned}
$$
- **Chronaxie $C$:** The pulse width at which $I_{th} = 2 I_r$. Set $1 - e^{-PW/\tau_m} = 1/2$, giving $e^{-PW/\tau_m} = 1/2$, hence $PW = \tau_m \ln 2$. So:
$$
\begin{aligned}
C = \tau_m \ln 2 \approx 0.693\,\tau_m
\end{aligned}
$$
> *Textual interpretation:* "Chronaxie is approximately 70% of the membrane time constant — a quantitative link between stimulator design and nerve biophysics."

**Step 7 — Re-express in the classical Lapicque (Weiss) form.**

Substitute $V_{th}/R_m = I_r$ and rearrange the threshold equation to isolate $I_{th}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{th} &= \dfrac{I_r}{1 - e^{-PW/\tau_m}} \\[4pt]
\text{Using } e^{-x} &\approx 1 - x + \tfrac{x^2}{2} \text{ for small } x \text{ (Lapicque linearization):} \\[4pt]
I_{th} &\approx I_r\left(1 + \dfrac{\tau_m}{PW}\right) = I_r\left(1 + \dfrac{C}{PW}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Lapicque Strength–Duration Equation**, the formula appearing in nearly every KTU question bank.

### 3.2 Derivation of Charge and Energy Equations

**Step 1 — For a rectangular pulse of constant amplitude $I_{th}$ and width $PW$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \int_{0}^{PW} I_{th}\,dt = I_{th}\cdot PW
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — For energy (assuming a constant electrode voltage $V$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \int_{0}^{PW} V\cdot I_{th}\,dt = V \cdot I_{th} \cdot PW
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Average power delivered at pulse rate $f$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= E \cdot f = V \cdot I_{th} \cdot PW \cdot f
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Textual interpretation:* "The total energy used by the stimulator per second is the energy of one pulse multiplied by the number of pulses per second — this is exactly what determines the *battery life* of an implanted device."

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Exam Style)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A stimulator delivers biphasic rectangular pulses. Each cathodal phase has $I_{th} = 2.5\ mA$ and $PW = 200\ \mu s$, at a frequency of $50\ Hz$. The electrode area is $0.05\ cm^2$ and the compliance voltage is $9\ V$. Compute: (a) charge per phase, (b) charge density, (c) energy per pulse, (d) average power, and (e) check the Shannon safety criterion.

**Solution:**

**(a) Charge per phase:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= I_{th} \cdot PW = (2.5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (200 \times 10^{-6}) \\[4pt]
Q &= 5.0 \times 10^{-7}\ C = 0.5\ \mu C
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Charge density:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \dfrac{Q}{A_{elec}} = \dfrac{0.5\ \mu C}{0.05\ cm^2} = 10\ \mu C/cm^{2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(c) Energy per pulse:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= V \cdot I_{th} \cdot PW = 9 \cdot (2.5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (200 \times 10^{-6}) \\[4pt]
E &= 4.5 \times 10^{-6}\ J = 4.5\ \mu J
\end{aligned}
$$

**(d) Average power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= E \cdot f = (4.5\ \mu J) \cdot 50\ Hz = 225\ \mu W
\end{aligned}
$$

**(e) Shannon safety check (using $k = 1.7$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(A_{elec}) &= \log_{10}(10) + \log_{10}(0.05) \\[4pt]
&= 1.0 + (-1.301) = -0.301 \\[4pt]
&\le 1.7 \ \ \checkmark \ \text{(SAFE)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Verification conclusion:* The stimulation parameters lie **well within the safe region** of the Shannon plot, confirming clinical usability.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Voltage- vs. Current-Controlled Stimulators

| Property | Voltage-Controlled | Current-Controlled |
|---|---|---|
| **Output variable held constant** | Voltage $V$ | Current $I$ |
| **Charge per pulse** | Depends on tissue impedance | Constant (independent of impedance) |
| **Safety against impedance change** | Poor (current can spike) | Excellent (current is fixed) |
| **Recruitment consistency** | Variable with scar formation | Stable with scar formation |
| **Implementation complexity** | Simple (just an analog switch) | Complex (Howland current source) |
| **Clinical use** | Pacemakers (older), TENS | Cochlear, DBS, FES, retinal |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Neural Stimulator

The following Mermaid diagram maps every functional block of a modern neural electrical stimulator, the signals between them, and the safety features that the KTU examiner typically tests.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    bat1["BATTERY 3 to 6 V"]
    dcdc1["DC DC CONVERTER 5 to 15 V"]
    ctrl1["CONTROL LOGIC MCU or ASIC"]
    parm1["PARAMETER SET AMPLITUDE PW FREQ"]
    pgen1["PULSE GENERATOR"]
    cur1["CURRENT MIRROR HOWLAND PUMP"]
    eleout["OUTPUT ELECTRODE"]
    elebias["RETURN ELECTRODE"]
    tis1["NEURAL TISSUE"]
    sen1["SENSING AMPLIFIER"]
    fb1["FEEDBACK SIGNAL"]
    safe1["CHARGE BALANCE MONITOR"]
    telem1["TELEMETRY LINK"]

    bat1 --> dcdc1
    dcdc1 --> pgen1
    ctrl1 --> parm1
    parm1 --> pgen1
    pgen1 --> cur1
    cur1 --> eleout
    elebias --> tis1
    eleout --> tis1
    tis1 --> sen1
    sen1 --> fb1
    fb1 --> ctrl1
    cur1 --> safe1
    safe1 --> ctrl1
    ctrl1 --> telem1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:**
> - Power flows from the **battery → DC-DC converter → pulse generator → current source → electrode → tissue**.
> - The **sensing amplifier** picks up the neural response (or impedance) and feeds it back to the controller for **closed-loop control**.
> - The **charge-balance monitor** continuously checks that the biphasic phases inject equal-and-opposite charge — this is the single most important **safety** feature of a clinical-grade stimulator.
> - The **telemetry link** is used to reprogram the implant wirelessly and to download diagnostic data.

### 4.2 Output Stage — Biphasic Current Pulse Waveform

The shape of the actual current waveform delivered to the tissue (captured in Mermaid as a stage-style schematic, since waveform plotting is best done conceptually).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    t0["t = 0 START"]
    t1["CATHODAL PHASE -I PW"]
    t2["INTERPHASE DELAY 0 to 100 us"]
    t3["ANODAL PHASE +I PW"]
    t4["QUIET PERIOD 1 over f minus 2 PW"]
    t5["NEXT PULSE CYCLE"]
    t6["t = 1 over f REPEAT"]

    t0 --> t1
    t1 --> t2
    t2 --> t3
    t3 --> t4
    t4 --> t5
    t5 --> t6
    t6 --> t0
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interphase delay** is a small zero-current gap (0–100 µs) inserted between the cathodal and anodal phases. It is used to ensure the **cathodal phase has fully depolarized the membrane** before the anodal phase reverses the field, improving the *selectivity* of stimulation.

### 4.3 Closed-Loop / Open-Loop Control Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    open1["OPEN LOOP FIXED PARAMETERS NO FEEDBACK"]
    clos1["CLOSED LOOP SENSOR FEEDS BACK TO CONTROLLER"]
    adv1["ADAPTIVE LOWER POWER LONGER BATTERY"]
    dis1["DISADVANTAGES COMPLEX HIGHER CURRENT DRAIN FROM SENSING"]

    open1 --> clos1
    clos1 --> adv1
    clos1 --> dis1
```

> Open-loop stimulators (e.g., older pacemakers) deliver fixed pulses regardless of the tissue state. Closed-loop (responsive) stimulators — like modern DBS devices and the new generation of cochlear implants — *adapt* to neural feedback, which **reduces battery use by 30–80%** in published clinical trials.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]**
**Define a Neural Electrical Stimulator. List the five primary stimulus parameters that characterize its output.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Neural Electrical Stimulator (NES)** is an electronic device that delivers *precisely controlled electrical pulses* to *excitable neural tissue* (nerve, brain, spinal cord, or muscle) via electrodes, with the goal of *evoking, modulating, or inhibiting* neural activity for therapeutic or functional purposes.

The **five primary stimulus parameters** are:
1. **Amplitude** (V or mA) — strength of the pulse.
2. **Pulse width** (µs or ms) — duration of each phase.
3. **Frequency** (Hz) — pulse repetition rate.
4. **Waveform shape** — monophasic, biphasic, symmetric, charge-balanced.
5. **Electrode configuration** — monopolar, bipolar, multipolar, current-steering.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]**
**Differentiate between monophasic and biphasic stimulation waveforms. Why is biphasic the clinical standard?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Monophasic | Biphasic |
|---|---|---|
| Polarity | Single (one direction) | Two (cathodal + anodal) |
| Net DC | Non-zero | Zero (if charge-balanced) |
| Tissue damage risk | High | Low |
| Electrode corrosion | Yes | No |
| Clinical use | Diagnostic only (EMG) | Therapeutic (chronic implants) |

**Why biphasic is the clinical standard (2 marks):**
- It **eliminates net DC current** → prevents electrolytic damage to tissue.
- It **prevents pH shifts** at the electrode–tissue interface.
- It **avoids faradaic reactions** that corrode metal electrodes.
- It is therefore **safe for chronic, long-term implantation**, as required for pacemakers, DBS, cochlear implants, etc.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEEBT753]**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a neural electrical stimulator**. Explain the role of each block. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **Lapicque Strength–Duration equation** starting from the equivalent RC model of a nerve membrane. Define *rheobase* and *chronaxie*, and explain their significance in choosing stimulus parameters. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Block Diagram & Functional Architecture (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> *Examiners expect:* a labelled diagram (3 marks), a description of each block (3 marks), and a closing line on safety (1 mark).

A neural electrical stimulator consists of the following interconnected blocks:

1. **Power Source (Battery):** Provides a stable DC supply, typically a *lithium-iodine* primary cell (3–6 V) for non-rechargeable implants, or a *rechargeable lithium-ion* cell for rechargeable ones.
2. **DC-DC Converter / Voltage Multiplier:** Steps up the battery voltage to a higher rail (5–15 V) so that the output stage can drive the *capacitive* load of the tissue across the high-impedance hermetic feedthrough.
3. **Pulse Generator:** Produces a logic-level timing signal with the desired *pulse width* and *frequency*. Often implemented with a microcontroller, ASIC, or discrete timers (e.g., 555-based or digital counter).
4. **Output Stage (Current Source):** A **Howland current pump** or *Wilson current mirror* that converts the timing signal into a constant-current pulse, independent of tissue impedance. This is essential because the electrode–tissue impedance can vary by a factor of 2–10 over time due to fibrous encapsulation.
5. **Electrode–Tissue Interface:** The lead wires, hermetic feedthrough, and electrode contacts (Pt, Pt-Ir, IrOx) that physically deliver the current to the tissue.
6. **Control & Safety Circuitry:** Microcontroller that sets parameters, monitors charge balance (ensures the biphasic phases are equal in charge), and provides over-current and over-voltage protection.
7. **Telemetry (optional):** Inductive or RF link for wireless reprogramming and diagnostic data upload.

> *Closing line for full marks:* "Together, these blocks form a **charge-balanced, current-controlled, biphasic stimulator** — the safety-optimized architecture mandated by modern clinical neuroprosthetic design."

**Part (b) — Derivation of Lapicque Equation & Definition of Rheobase/Chronaxie (7 marks)**

The derivation is the same as the exhaustive 7-step derivation in **Section 3.1** of these notes. Briefly recap:

1. Model the membrane as parallel $R_m \vert C_m$.
2. KCL gives: $I(t) = V_m/R_m + C_m \dfrac{dV_m}{dt}$.
3. Solve under constant current $I$ with $V_m(0) = 0$: $V_m(t) = I R_m (1 - e^{-t/\tau_m})$.
4. Threshold condition: $V_m(PW) = V_{th}$, so $I_{th} = \dfrac{V_{th}}{R_m (1 - e^{-PW/\tau_m})}$.
5. **Rheobase** $I_r = V_{th}/R_m$ — the minimum DC current that can excite the nerve (achieved as $PW \to \infty$).
6. **Chronaxie** $C = \tau_m \ln 2 \approx 0.693\,\tau_m$ — the pulse width that requires a current *twice* the rheobase.
7. Linearizing the exponential gives the **Lapicque equation**: $I_{th} = I_r(1 + C/PW)$.

**Significance in stimulus design (3 marks):**
- *Chronaxie* is the **optimal pulse width** for energy-efficient stimulation. Stimulating at $PW \approx C$ minimizes the total charge (and therefore battery energy) per pulse.
- *Rheobase* sets the **minimum amplitude** floor below which no pulse — however long — can excite the nerve.
- The S–D curve guides **selectivity**: a short PW selectively excites *large myelinated fibers* (low chronaxie) over *small unmyelinated fibers* (high chronaxie) — this is the *recruitment-order reversal* exploited in DBS and dorsal-column SCS.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEEBT753]**
>
> **(a)** What is a **charge-balanced biphasic waveform**? Why is charge balance critical in chronic neural stimulation? Describe a typical biphasic pulse with the help of a clearly labelled amplitude-vs-time sketch. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A neural stimulator delivers rectangular cathodal pulses of amplitude $I_{th} = 3\ mA$ and pulse width $PW = 250\ \mu s$, at a frequency of $60\ Hz$, through an electrode of geometric area $0.06\ cm^2$. The compliance voltage is $10\ V$. Calculate the **(i)** charge per phase, **(ii)** charge density, **(iii)** energy per pulse, **(iv)** average power, and **(v)** verify the Shannon safety criterion (take $k = 1.7$). **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Charge-Balanced Biphasic Waveform (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> *Examiners expect:* definition (2 marks), importance (3 marks), labelled sketch (2 marks).

A **charge-balanced biphasic waveform** is a stimulation pulse consisting of a *leading cathodal* phase (current flows from the electrode into the tissue, depolarizing the membrane) followed by an *anodal* phase of *equal and opposite* charge. The condition is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{cathode} = Q_{anode} \quad &\Longleftrightarrow \quad I_{cathode} \cdot PW_{cathode} = I_{anode} \cdot PW_{anode}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Sketch (describe verbally, since we cannot draw graphs in Mermaid):*
> A time-axis $t$ (horizontal) and current-axis $I$ (vertical). The waveform goes **down** to $-I_{th}$ for a duration $PW$ (cathodal phase), then **up** to $+I_{th}$ for a duration $PW$ (anodal phase), then returns to zero. A small *interphase delay* (0–100 µs) may be shown between the two phases.

**Why charge balance is critical (3 marks):**
1. **Prevents electrolysis:** A net DC current would electrolyze body water → H₂, O₂, Cl₂ evolution → gas bubble formation, tissue disruption.
2. **Prevents pH shifts:** Faradaic reactions shift local pH → acidic or basic microenvironment → neuron death.
3. **Prevents electrode corrosion:** DC drives metal ions off the electrode → toxic metallic species, plus failure of the electrode itself.
4. **Regulatory compliance:** International standards (ISO 14708, FDA guidance) mandate charge imbalance < 100 nC per pulse for chronically implanted devices.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

> [!NOTE]
> *Examiners expect:* five sub-parts, each worth approximately 1.4 marks. Show formula, substitution, and final answer with units.

**Given:** $I_{th} = 3\ mA = 3 \times 10^{-3}\ A$, $PW = 250\ \mu s = 250 \times 10^{-6}\ s$, $f = 60\ Hz$, $A_{elec} = 0.06\ cm^2$, $V = 10\ V$, $k = 1.7$.

**(i) Charge per phase:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= I_{th} \cdot PW = (3 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (250 \times 10^{-6}) \\[4pt]
&= 7.5 \times 10^{-7}\ C = 0.75\ \mu C
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Charge density:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D &= \dfrac{Q}{A_{elec}} = \dfrac{0.75\ \mu C}{0.06\ cm^2} = 12.5\ \mu C/cm^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) Energy per pulse:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= V \cdot I_{th} \cdot PW = 10 \cdot (3 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (250 \times 10^{-6}) \\[4pt]
&= 7.5 \times 10^{-6}\ J = 7.5\ \mu J
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Average power:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{avg} &= E \cdot f = (7.5\ \mu J) \cdot 60\ Hz = 450\ \mu W = 0.45\ mW
\end{aligned}
$$

**(v) Shannon safety verification:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(A_{elec}) &= \log_{10}(12.5) + \log_{10}(0.06) \\[4pt]
&= 1.097 + (-1.222) = -0.125 \\[4pt]
-0.125 &\le 1.7 \ \ \checkmark \ \text{(SAFE — well within the safe zone)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> *Conclusion for full marks:* All five parameters satisfy the safety and engineering constraints — the design is clinically deployable.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to show units in the final answer** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5 mark per missing unit. Always write *mA, µs, µC, µC/cm², µJ, µW*.
> 2. **Confusing "compliance voltage" with "stimulus voltage"** — Compliance voltage is the *maximum* the supply can deliver, not the voltage actually across the tissue. The actual voltage is $V = I_{th} \cdot Z_{tissue}$.
> 3. **Using `log₁₀` without parentheses in the Shannon equation** — examiners cannot read `log₁₀ D` ambiguously. Always write $\log_{10}(D)$.
> 4. **Calling a biphasic waveform "DC-free" without showing the calculation** — *charge balance* is a *quantitative* condition, not a verbal one. Show $Q_{cathode} = Q_{anode}$ explicitly.
> 5. **In the strength–duration derivation, stopping at the differential equation** — you must carry the derivation to the *Lapicque form* $I_{th} = I_r(1 + C/PW)$ to get full marks. The differential equation alone is worth only 2 of the 7 marks.
> 6. **Drawing a block diagram without arrows** — every block must be connected with a labelled, *directional* arrow indicating signal/power flow.
> 7. **Using $|x|$ notation inside markdown tables** — it breaks the table. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` in LaTeX form (this is enforced by the KTU digital answer script parser).

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] A **Neural Electrical Stimulator** is a current- or voltage-controlled electronic device that delivers pulses to excitable tissue via electrodes.
- [x] The **five primary stimulus parameters** are: amplitude, pulse width, frequency, waveform shape, and electrode configuration.
- [x] The **Lapicque (Strength–Duration) equation**: $I_{th} = I_r(1 + C/PW)$.
- [x] **Rheobase** = minimum DC threshold; **Chronaxie** ≈ 0.693 × membrane time constant.
- [x] **Charge per phase** $Q = I \cdot PW$; **Charge density** $D = Q/A_{elec}$.
- [x] **Energy per pulse** $E = V \cdot I \cdot PW$; **Average power** $P = E \cdot f$.
- [x] **Biphasic, charge-balanced** waveforms are the clinical standard for chronic implants.
- [x] **Monophasic** is never used chronically (causes tissue damage and electrode corrosion).
- [x] **Current-controlled** stimulators are preferred over voltage-controlled for safety and consistency.
- [x] **Shannon safety limit:** $\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(A_{elec}) \le 1.7$.
- [x] Typical safe charge per phase: $Q \le 0.4\ \mu C$.
- [x] Typical clinical pulse widths: $30$–$400\ \mu s$.
- [x] Typical clinical frequencies: $20$–$200\ Hz$.
- [x] Functional blocks: **Battery → DC-DC → Pulse Gen → Current Source → Electrode → Tissue → Sensor → Feedback**.
- [x] **Closed-loop (responsive) stimulators** reduce battery use by 30–80%.
- [x] **Interphase delay** improves selectivity by allowing the cathodal depolarization to take effect before the anodal reversal.
- [x] The **Howland current pump** is the most common current-source topology in modern implant stimulators.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: DSP-based Image Processing System for Visual Prostheses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT753 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS AND IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** DSP-based Image Processing System for Visual Prostheses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **DSP-based Image Processing System for Visual Prostheses** is a real-time, embedded electronic subsystem that captures visual information from the external environment through an image sensor, performs mathematically intensive image processing operations using a **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)**, and converts the processed image into a coded set of electrical stimulation parameters that drive an electrode array implanted on the retina, optic nerve, or visual cortex to artificially restore a sense of vision in blind or visually impaired individuals.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEBT753 – Neural Prosthesis and Implants), this system forms the **front-end signal conditioning and encoding block** of any modern visual neuroprosthesis such as the **Argus II Retinal Prosthesis**, **Alpha-IMS**, and **PRIMA** devices.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The DSP-based image processing system is the **"smart bridge"** between the optical world (camera) and the neural interface (electrode array). It does NOT generate vision — it translates light into the *language of neural stimulation* (pulse width, amplitude, frequency, and electrode selection).

### 1.2 Key Functional Subsystems (KTU Terminology)

| Subsystem | KTU Standard Terminology | Purpose |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------|
| Image Sensor | **Image Acquisition Module (IAM)** | Captures raw RGB image frames |
| Pre-processor | **Image Conditioning Unit (ICU)** | Noise removal, contrast normalization |
| Feature Extractor | **Saliency/Feature Extraction Engine (SFEE)** | Edge, motion, and contrast detection |
| Encoder | **Stimulation Parameter Encoder (SPE)** | Maps visual features to biphasic pulse parameters |
| Stimulator | **Current Steering / Stimulus Generator** | Delivers charge-balanced pulses to electrodes |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Camera Translator"

Imagine a tourist in a foreign country who is **completely deaf** to the local language. The tourist carries a **pocket translator** (the DSP system). When the tourist looks at a bustling street (the **camera captures the scene**), the translator doesn't repeat every word — instead, it **picks out the most important things** (edges of a car, a moving person, a traffic light) and **whispers only those essential cues** in a language the tourist can *feel* through vibrations (the **electrode stimulates the brain**). 

- The **pocket translator** = DSP chip
- The **tourist's ears** = retina/visual cortex
- The **vibrations** = phosphenes (the perception of light dots)
- The **important cues** = extracted image features (not raw pixels)

> [!NOTE]
> **Phosphene Definition (KTU Standard):** A *phosphene* is the perceptual unit of artificial vision — a small dot of light elicited by a single electrical pulse delivered to a viable neural element in the visual pathway. A visual prosthesis typically generates **arrays of phosphenes** that the brain learns to interpret as crude shapes.

### 1.4 Why DSP is Used (and not a generic microcontroller)

- **Deterministic latency:** Image-to-stimulation latency must be **less than 20–50 ms** to feel "real-time". DSPs guarantee fixed instruction-cycle timing.
- **Parallel MAC operations:** DSPs have hardware **Multiply-Accumulate (MAC)** units needed for convolution-based filtering.
- **High throughput:** A 256×256 grayscale image at 30 fps requires ~**2 million pixels/second** to process.
- **Low power:** Critical for wearable/battery-powered external units.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Performance Metric (KTU 2024):** End-to-end system latency = $\tau_{total} = \tau_{acquire} + \tau_{process} + \tau_{stimulate}$. For a usable prosthetic visual experience, the KTU reference value is **$\tau_{total} \leq$ 50 ms**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Pipeline — Five Sequential Stages

The DSP-based image processing system operates in **five strictly sequential stages**. Each stage has a defined input, algorithm, and output.

**Stage 1 — Image Acquisition**
- A miniature **CMOS/CCD camera** (mounted on spectacles) captures an analog video signal.
- An **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** digitizes each frame into an $M \times N$ matrix of pixels.
- Each pixel value $I(x, y, t)$ is a scalar intensity (8-bit grayscale = 256 levels, or 24-bit RGB).

**Stage 2 — Pre-processing (Image Conditioning)**
- **Spatial filtering** to remove sensor noise (e.g., Gaussian smoothing kernel).
- **Histogram equalization** to enhance contrast under variable lighting.
- **Logarithmic intensity compression** because human (and phosphene) perception is logarithmic.

**Stage 3 — Feature Extraction**
- **Edge detection** using Sobel or Canny operators.
- **Motion detection** via frame differencing: $D(x,y,t) = \vert I(x,y,t) - I(x,y,t-1) \vert$.
- **Saliency mapping** to identify regions of interest (objects, faces, obstacles).

**Stage 4 — Phosphene Mapping (Image-to-Stimulation Transformation)**
- This is the **most critical** stage unique to visual prostheses.
- A down-sampled image (e.g., 60×60 electrodes) is mapped to a 2D electrode array.
- Each phosphene's brightness is encoded as **stimulation amplitude** (current in µA).
- Non-linear mapping (e.g., **logarithmic**) is applied to match neural perceptual laws.

**Stage 5 — Stimulation Encoding & Pulse Generation**
- Output bits control: **pulse amplitude ($A$)**, **pulse width ($W$)**, **inter-phase gap**, and **electrode selection**.
- Pulses are **biphasic, charge-balanced** to prevent electrochemical damage.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Memorize this table verbatim. Questions on image processing math in visual prostheses are predictable.

| # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX Form | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Pixel count of sensor | $N_{px} = M \times N$ | pixels |
| 2 | Frame rate (sampling theorem for video) | $f_{frame} \geq 2 \cdot f_{motion}$ | fps |
| 3 | Logarithmic intensity compression | $I_{out} = k \cdot \log_{e}(1 + I_{in})$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Edge magnitude (Sobel) | $G = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ | intensity gradient |
| 5 | Motion detection (frame difference) | $D(x,y,t) = \vert I_t - I_{t-1} \vert$ | absolute difference |
| 6 | Phosphene brightness mapping | $B_{ph} = \alpha \cdot \log(1 + I_{stim})$ | Weber-Fechner law analogue |
| 7 | Charge per pulse | $Q = I_{stim} \cdot W_{pulse}$ | Coulombs (C) or nC |
| 8 | Charge density safety limit | $k = Q / A_{electrode}$ | $\mu C / cm^2$ (Shannon limit) |
| 9 | Biphasic pulse — charge balance | $Q_{cathodic} = Q_{anodic}$ | mandatory |
| 10 | Real-time latency budget | $\tau_{total} = \tau_{acq} + \tau_{proc} + \tau_{stim}$ | seconds (s) |
| 11 | Phosphene field-of-view | $FoV = n_{ph} \cdot \theta_{ph}$ | degrees ($^\circ$) |
| 12 | Down-sampling ratio | $R_{ds} = N_{px} / N_{electrodes}$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Shannon limit** for safe chronic neural stimulation is $k \leq 0.1$ mC/cm² (cathodic phase only), and charge per phase $Q \leq$ **0.4 µC**. Exceeding this causes electrode corrosion and tissue damage.

### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Stage

**Why down-sample the image?**
A modern camera has 8 megapixels, but the retinal implant (e.g., Argus II) has only **60 electrodes**. Pushing 8 million pixels into 60 electrodes is meaningless — the DSP must *intelligently reduce* the image to 60 essential features.

**Why logarithmic mapping?**
The human visual system follows the **Weber-Fechner law**: perceived intensity is proportional to the *logarithm* of stimulus magnitude. Phosphene brightness must therefore be encoded logarithmically to feel natural.

**Why biphasic charge-balanced pulses?**
Direct current (DC) causes **electrochemical reduction/oxidation** at the electrode-tissue interface, leading to corrosion of platinum electrodes and production of toxic species (H₂, Cl₂, metal ions). Biphasic pulses (cathodic first, then anodic) ensure **net charge = 0**.

**How real-time processing is achieved:**
- DSP uses **Harvard architecture** (separate program and data memory).
- Hardware **MAC units** allow one-cycle multiply-accumulate for FIR filters.
- **DMA channels** allow image data transfer without CPU intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** This same DSP-based architecture is used in **endoscopic capsule cameras** (PillCam), **wearable assistive devices for the blind**, **machine-vision-guided surgical robots**, and **AR/VR headsets** for visually impaired navigation. KTU 2024 emphasizes biomedical applications, but the underlying DSP theory is universal.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Logarithmic Phosphene Brightness Mapping

**Problem Setup:**
The retinal electrode array generates a phosphene whose perceived brightness $B_{ph}$ must follow the psychophysical law of human perception. Given an input image pixel intensity $I_{stim}$ (0 to 255, 8-bit), derive the mapping function such that $B_{ph} \propto \log(1 + I_{stim})$.

**Step 1 — Start with the Weber-Fechner empirical law.**

The Weber-Fechner law states that the perceived intensity of a stimulus is proportional to the logarithm of the stimulus intensity above the absolute threshold:

$$B_{ph} = \alpha \cdot \ln(S) + \beta$$

where $S$ is the stimulus magnitude and $\alpha, \beta$ are empirical constants.

**Step 2 — Introduce the absolute threshold.**

If the absolute threshold of perception is $I_0$ (the minimum stimulation that produces a phosphene), then we define:

$$B_{ph} = \alpha \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I_{stim}}{I_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Normalize to avoid singularity at $I_{stim} = 0$.**

For numerical stability in DSP implementation, the "+1" term is added inside the logarithm to handle the zero-input case. This is the standard KTU textbook form:

$$B_{ph} = \alpha \cdot \ln\left(1 + \frac{I_{stim}}{I_0}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions for parameter estimation.**

- At $I_{stim} = 0$: $B_{ph} = \alpha \cdot \ln(1) = 0$. ✓ (No perception at zero input.)
- At $I_{stim} = I_{max} = 255$: $B_{ph} = B_{max} = \alpha \cdot \ln\left(1 + \frac{255}{I_0}\right)$.

Solving for $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = \frac{B_{max}}{\ln\left(1 + \frac{255}{I_0}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Final KTU-ready expression.**

The complete phosphene brightness mapping equation used in DSP code is:

$$B_{ph}(I_{stim}) = \frac{B_{max} \cdot \ln\left(1 + \frac{I_{stim}}{I_0}\right)}{\ln\left(1 + \frac{I_{max}}{I_0}\right)}$$

This mapping compresses the dynamic range, so a pixel of intensity 200 does not produce a disproportionately bright phosphene compared to one of intensity 100.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — End-to-End System Latency Budget

**Problem Setup:**
A visual prosthesis system has the following stage latencies: image acquisition = **8 ms**, DSP processing (edge detection + mapping) = **12 ms**, stimulation pulse generation = **5 ms**, and neural response time = **15 ms**. Compute the total perceptual latency and check if it meets the KTU real-time standard of 50 ms.

**Step 1 — Define the latency components.**

$$\tau_{acq} = 8 \text{ ms}, \quad \tau_{proc} = 12 \text{ ms}, \quad \tau_{stim} = 5 \text{ ms}, \quad \tau_{neuro} = 15 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the electronic (non-neural) latencies first.**

$$\tau_{elec} = \tau_{acq} + \tau_{proc} + \tau_{stim}$$

$$\tau_{elec} = 8 + 12 + 5 = 25 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Add the neural response time to get total perceptual latency.**

$$\tau_{total} = \tau_{elec} + \tau_{neuro}$$

$$\tau_{total} = 25 + 15 = 40 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with the KTU real-time standard.**

Since $\tau_{total} = 40$ ms $< 50$ ms, the system **meets** the KTU real-time requirement. ✓

**Step 5 — Compute the headroom available.**

$$\Delta \tau = 50 - 40 = 10 \text{ ms}$$

This 10 ms headroom is the engineering safety margin for unexpected computational loads (e.g., complex motion detection).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Charge Per Phase & Charge Density Verification

**Problem Setup:**
A retinal electrode has geometric surface area $A = 0.01$ cm², stimulation current amplitude $I = 200$ µA, and pulse width $W = 0.5$ ms (cathodic phase). Verify whether the charge density is within the Shannon safety limit.

**Step 1 — Compute charge per phase.**

$$Q = I_{stim} \cdot W_{pulse}$$

$$Q = (200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A}) \cdot (0.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s})$$

$$Q = 100 \times 10^{-9} \text{ C} = 100 \text{ nC}$$

**Step 2 — Compute charge density.**

$$k = \frac{Q}{A_{electrode}} = \frac{100 \times 10^{-9} \text{ C}}{0.01 \text{ cm}^2}$$

$$k = 10 \times 10^{-6} \text{ C/cm}^2 = 10 \text{ µC/cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the Shannon safety limit.**

The Shannon limit is $k_{max} = 0.1$ mC/cm² = **100 µC/cm²**. Since $k = 10$ µC/cm² $<$ 100 µC/cm², the stimulation is **safe**. ✓

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Algorithm Selection

| Algorithm | Use Case | Computational Cost (Big-O) | KTU Preference |
|-----------|----------|---------------------------|----------------|
| Mean filter | Sensor noise reduction | $O(MN \cdot k^2)$ | ★★ |
| Gaussian filter | Smoothing + edge preservation | $O(MN \cdot k^2)$ | ★★★ |
| Sobel edge detector | Feature extraction | $O(MN)$ | ★★★★ |
| Canny edge detector | High-precision edges | $O(MN \cdot \log MN)$ | ★★★ |
| Histogram equalization | Contrast normalization | $O(MN)$ | ★★★★ |
| Frame differencing | Motion detection | $O(MN)$ | ★★★★★ |
| Optical flow (Lucas-Kanade) | Dense motion field | $O(MN)$ (sparse) | ★★ (too costly) |
| Logarithmic compression | Phosphene mapping | $O(MN)$ | ★★★★★ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Block Diagram — DSP-based Image Processing for Visual Prosthesis

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete signal flow from optical scene capture to neural stimulation. Node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes to comply with Mermaid compilation rules, and labels contain only clean uppercase alphanumeric text (no markdown formatting).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[SCENE OPTICAL INPUT] --> A2[CMOS CAMERA SENSOR]
    A2 --> A3[ANALOG FRONT END AFE]
    A3 --> A4[ADC 8 BIT OR 10 BIT]
    A4 --> B1[DSP INPUT BUFFER DMA]
    B1 --> B2[STAGE 1 PREPROCESSING]
    B2 --> B3[GAUSSIAN NOISE FILTER]
    B3 --> B4[HISTOGRAM EQUALIZATION]
    B4 --> B5[LOG INTENSITY COMPRESSION]
    B5 --> C1[STAGE 2 FEATURE EXTRACTION]
    C1 --> C2[SOBEL EDGE DETECTOR]
    C2 --> C3[MOTION DETECTION FRAME DIFF]
    C3 --> C4[SALIENCY MAP GENERATOR]
    C4 --> D1[STAGE 3 PHOSPHENE MAPPING]
    D1 --> D2[DOWNSAMPLING TO ELECTRODE GRID]
    D2 --> D3[BRIGHTNESS TO CURRENT MAPPING]
    D3 --> D4[SAFETY LIMIT CHECKER SHANNON]
    D4 --> E1[STAGE 4 STIMULATION ENCODER]
    E1 --> E2[BIPHASIC PULSE GENERATOR]
    E2 --> E3[CURRENT STEERING MATRIX]
    E3 --> F1[ELECTRODE ARRAY RETINA CORTEX]
    F1 --> F2[PHOSPHENE PERCEPTION]
    F2 --> F3[VISUAL CORTEX INTERPRETATION]
```

### 4.2 Latency Budget Diagram — Timing Constraints per Stage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T0[T0 FRAME CAPTURE START] --> T1[T1 ACQUISITION 8 MS]
    T1 --> T2[T2 DSP PREPROCESS 12 MS]
    T2 --> T3[T3 FEATURE EXTRACT 5 MS]
    T3 --> T4[T4 PHOSPHENE MAP 3 MS]
    T4 --> T5[T5 STIM ENCODE 2 MS]
    T5 --> T6[T6 PULSE OUTPUT 30 MS TOTAL]
    T6 --> T7[T7 NEURAL RESPONSE 15 MS]
    T7 --> T8[T8 PHOSPHENE PERCEIVED 45 MS]
    T8 --> T9{LATENCY LESS THAN 50 MS}
    T9 -->|YES PASS| T10[REAL TIME PERCEPTION OK]
    T9 -->|NO FAIL| T11[LAG PERCEIVED BY USER]
```

### 4.3 Phosphene Mapping Functional Block (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[INPUT PIXEL MATRIX MXN] --> P2[GAUSSIAN PYRAMID DOWNSAMPLE]
    P2 --> P3[60X60 OR 100X100 GRID]
    P3 --> P4[LOGARITHMIC MAPPING FUNCTION]
    P4 --> P5[CURRENT AMPLITUDE 0 TO 255 UA]
    P5 --> P6[ELECTRODE DRIVER MATRIX]
    P6 --> P7[RECEPTIVE FIELD OVERLAP]
    P7 --> P8[SMOOTH PHOSPHENE PATTERN]
    P8 --> P9[CENTRAL NERVOUS SYSTEM]
```

### 4.4 Real-Time Processing Architecture (Harvard Architecture for DSP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[PROGRAM MEMORY] --> R2[INSTRUCTION FETCH UNIT]
    R3[DATA MEMORY] --> R4[DATA FETCH UNIT]
    R2 --> R5[MAC UNIT MULTIPLY ACCUM]
    R4 --> R5
    R5 --> R6[ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT]
    R6 --> R7[SHIFT AND ROUND]
    R7 --> R8[OUTPUT REGISTER]
    R8 --> R9[DMA TO STIMULATOR]
    R4 --> R10[ADDRESS GENERATION UNIT]
    R10 --> R3
    R10 --> R1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation of Block Diagrams (KTU 2024):** When asked "draw the block diagram" in the exam, students must show the **five sequential stages** (Acquisition → Preprocessing → Feature Extraction → Phosphene Mapping → Stimulation Encoding) and label each block with its **algorithmic function** and **approximate latency**. The examiner awards marks for both completeness and correct signal-flow arrows.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

Define the term **phosphene** as used in visual prosthetics. With a neat block diagram, explain the role of the DSP block in converting an image frame into a set of stimulation parameters for a retinal prosthesis.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **phosphene** is the smallest unit of artificial visual perception elicited by a single electrical pulse delivered to a viable neuron in the visual pathway (retina, optic nerve, or visual cortex). It is perceived by the subject as a small dot of light whose brightness depends on stimulation amplitude and whose position corresponds to the activated electrode's spatial location.

**Role of the DSP block:**

The DSP performs four critical transformations:
1. **Image acquisition and digitization** of the camera's analog video output into pixel matrices.
2. **Pre-processing** — Gaussian filtering to remove sensor noise, histogram equalization for contrast enhancement, and logarithmic intensity compression to match neural perceptual laws.
3. **Feature extraction** — Sobel edge detection, motion detection via frame differencing, and saliency mapping.
4. **Phosphene mapping and stimulation encoding** — down-sampling the processed image to the electrode grid (e.g., 60×60), mapping pixel intensity to current amplitude via a logarithmic function, and generating charge-balanced biphasic pulses.

The DSP therefore acts as the **intelligent translator** between optical information and neural stimulation patterns. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplied Model]**

List any **three safety constraints** that must be enforced by the DSP in a chronically implanted visual prosthesis. Justify each with one sentence.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Charge per phase limit** ($Q \leq 0.4$ µC): Exceeding this causes electrochemical damage to the platinum-iridium electrode and the surrounding neural tissue.

2. **Charge density limit (Shannon limit, $k \leq 0.1$ mC/cm²):** Prevents gas evolution (H₂, O₂) at the electrode-tissue interface that can cause bubble formation and pressure necrosis.

3. **Charge-balanced biphasic pulses ($Q_{cathodic} = Q_{anodic}$):** Ensures net DC current is zero, preventing electrode corrosion and the production of toxic metal ions.

*(Optional 4th and 5th for partial credit: maximum stimulation frequency ≤ 500 Hz, and maximum repetition rate per electrode to prevent neuronal fatigue.)* **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** With a detailed block diagram, describe the **complete DSP-based image processing pipeline** used in a retinal visual prosthesis. Label each block with its specific algorithm and function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **logarithmic phosphene brightness mapping equation** used in the DSP encoder, stating clearly the boundary conditions and the psychophysical law on which it is based. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**(a) Block Diagram Description (7 marks):**

The DSP-based image processing pipeline consists of **five sequential stages**, as detailed below.

**Stage 1 — Image Acquisition Module (1 mark):**
- A miniature CMOS camera (mounted on spectacle frames) captures the external scene as an analog video signal.
- The **Analog Front End (AFE)** conditions the signal (amplification, DC offset removal).
- An **ADC** (typically 8-bit or 10-bit) digitizes each frame into an $M \times N$ matrix of pixel intensities $I(x, y, t)$.

**Stage 2 — Image Pre-processing (1.5 marks):**
- **Gaussian smoothing filter** removes high-frequency sensor noise. The 2D Gaussian kernel is $G(x,y) = \frac{1}{2\pi\sigma^2} e^{-(x^2 + y^2)/2\sigma^2}$.
- **Histogram equalization** redistributes pixel intensities uniformly across the 0–255 range, improving contrast under variable lighting.
- **Logarithmic intensity compression** matches the Weber-Fechner perceptual law.

**Stage 3 — Feature Extraction (1.5 marks):**
- **Sobel edge detection** computes gradient magnitudes $G = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$ to identify object boundaries.
- **Motion detection** by frame differencing $D(x,y,t) = \vert I(x,y,t) - I(x,y,t-1) \vert$ highlights moving objects.
- **Saliency map** prioritizes the most informative regions of the image.

**Stage 4 — Phosphene Mapping (2 marks):**
- The processed image is **down-sampled** to match the electrode grid (e.g., 60×60 for Argus II).
- Pixel intensity is **mapped to stimulation current amplitude** $I_{stim}$ via logarithmic function.
- A **safety limit checker** enforces the Shannon limit $k \leq 0.1$ mC/cm².

**Stage 5 — Stimulation Encoding (1 mark):**
- Biphasic, charge-balanced pulses are generated.
- Pulse parameters (amplitude, width, frequency) are programmed per electrode.
- The current-steering matrix routes pulses to the correct electrode.

**(b) Derivation of Logarithmic Phosphene Brightness Mapping (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Start with Weber-Fechner Law (1 mark):**

$$B_{ph} = \alpha \cdot \ln(S) + \beta$$

**Step 2 — Introduce absolute threshold (1 mark):**

$$B_{ph} = \alpha \cdot \ln\left(\frac{I_{stim}}{I_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Add +1 inside log for numerical stability (1 mark):**

$$B_{ph} = \alpha \cdot \ln\left(1 + \frac{I_{stim}}{I_0}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions (2 marks):**
- $I_{stim} = 0 \Rightarrow B_{ph} = 0$ ✓
- $I_{stim} = I_{max} = 255 \Rightarrow B_{ph} = B_{max}$

Solving:

$$\alpha = \frac{B_{max}}{\ln\left(1 + \frac{255}{I_0}\right)}$$

**Step 5 — Final KTU expression (2 marks):**

$$B_{ph}(I_{stim}) = \frac{B_{max} \cdot \ln\left(1 + \frac{I_{stim}}{I_0}\right)}{\ln\left(1 + \frac{I_{max}}{I_0}\right)}$$

**Justification:** This mapping follows the Weber-Fechner law because human brightness perception is approximately logarithmic. The "+1" prevents $\ln(0)$ singularity, and normalization ensures the output lies in the range $[0, B_{max}]$, matching the dynamic range of safe stimulation currents. **(Total: 14 marks)**

---

**Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Define the term **latency budget** in a visual prosthesis system. Compute the total perceptual latency for a system with: $\tau_{acq} = 5$ ms, $\tau_{proc} = 10$ ms, $\tau_{stim} = 3$ ms, and $\tau_{neuro} = 18$ ms. State whether the system meets the KTU real-time standard. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A retinal electrode has geometric area $A = 0.02$ cm². A charge-balanced biphasic pulse train has cathodic phase current $I = 150$ µA and pulse width $W = 0.4$ ms. Calculate: (i) the charge per phase, (ii) the charge density, and (iii) verify whether the stimulation is within the Shannon safety limit. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Question B:**

**(a) Latency Budget Computation (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The *latency budget* of a visual prosthesis is the total time elapsed between the optical event in the external scene and the corresponding phosphene perception by the subject. It is the sum of electronic processing latencies and the neural response time:

$$\tau_{total} = \tau_{acq} + \tau_{proc} + \tau_{stim} + \tau_{neuro}$$

**Computation (4 marks):**

$$\tau_{total} = 5 + 10 + 3 + 18 = 36 \text{ ms}$$

**Comparison with KTU standard (1 mark):** Since $\tau_{total} = 36$ ms $< 50$ ms, the system **meets** the KTU real-time standard. The available headroom is $\Delta \tau = 50 - 36 = 14$ ms.

**(b) Charge and Charge Density Computation (7 marks):**

**(i) Charge per phase (2 marks):**

$$Q = I \cdot W = (150 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (0.4 \times 10^{-3}) = 60 \times 10^{-9} \text{ C} = 60 \text{ nC}$$

**(ii) Charge density (2 marks):**

$$k = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{60 \times 10^{-9}}{0.02} = 3000 \times 10^{-9} \text{ C/cm}^2 = 3 \text{ µC/cm}^2$$

**(iii) Shannon safety verification (3 marks):**

Shannon limit: $k_{max} = 0.1$ mC/cm² = **100 µC/cm²**.

Since $k = 3$ µC/cm² $\ll$ 100 µC/cm², the stimulation is **well within the safe limit**. ✓

The charge per phase (60 nC) is also below the typical limit of 400 nC. Therefore, the biphasic pulse parameters are safe for chronic neural stimulation. **(Total: 14 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing charge and charge density** — Students often compute $Q$ and report it as the safety metric. The Shannon limit applies to **charge density $k$**, not charge. Always divide by electrode area.
> 2. **Forgetting the +1 in the log** — Writing $\ln(I_{stim}/I_0)$ instead of $\ln(1 + I_{stim}/I_0)$ loses a mark. The "+1" is mandatory for DSP numerical stability.
> 3. **Ignoring the anodic phase** — A biphasic pulse has TWO phases. Charge balance means $Q_{cathodic} = Q_{anodic}$, not that only the cathodic phase matters.
> 4. **Confusing the image sensor resolution with electrode count** — Camera has millions of pixels, but Argus II has only 60 electrodes. Always mention down-sampling.
> 5. **Writing "perception" for "perceptual latency"** — Use the correct KTU term: *perceptual latency* or *end-to-end latency*.
> 6. **Not justifying the logarithmic mapping** — Just writing the formula is insufficient; you must state it follows the **Weber-Fechner psychophysical law**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Revision — Memorize These 12 Bullets:**

- ✅ A **phosphene** is a single dot of artificial light perception produced by one electrical pulse to a viable visual neuron.
- ✅ The DSP pipeline has **5 stages**: Acquisition → Preprocessing → Feature Extraction → Phosphene Mapping → Stimulation Encoding.
- ✅ **Logarithmic intensity compression** follows the **Weber-Fechner law**; formula: $B_{ph} = \alpha \cdot \ln(1 + I_{stim}/I_0)$.
- ✅ **Edge detection** uses the Sobel operator: $G = \sqrt{G_x^2 + G_y^2}$.
- ✅ **Motion detection** uses frame differencing: $D = \vert I_t - I_{t-1} \vert$.
- ✅ **Phosphene mapping** down-samples the image to the electrode grid (e.g., 60×60 for Argus II).
- ✅ **Charge per phase** $Q = I \cdot W$ must be $\leq$ **400 nC** typically.
- ✅ **Shannon safety limit** for charge density: $k \leq$ **0.1 mC/cm²** = 100 µC/cm².
- ✅ **Biphasic, charge-balanced pulses** are mandatory to prevent DC corrosion and tissue damage.
- ✅ **Real-time latency** must be $\leq$ **50 ms** for usable prosthetic vision.
- ✅ DSPs use **Harvard architecture** with hardware **MAC units** for real-time convolution.
- ✅ Modern visual prostheses (Argus II, Alpha-IMS, PRIMA) all use this 5-stage DSP pipeline.

---

**End of Module 1 Notes — DSP-based Image Processing System for Visual Prostheses**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: System Review - Architecture and Functional Block Diagram of a Modern Cochlear Implant
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT753 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS AND IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** System Review - Architecture and Functional Block Diagram of a Modern Cochlear Implant

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Cochlear Implant (CI)** is an FDA-approved Class III neural prosthetic device that restores the sensation of hearing in individuals with severe-to-profound sensorineural hearing loss by **directly electrically stimulating the auditory nerve fibers (spiral ganglion neurons)** within the cochlea, thereby bypassing the damaged or absent hair cells of the organ of Corti.

From an engineering perspective, a modern cochlear implant is a **bimodal, telemetric, biostable neurostimulator** consisting of two physically separable sub-systems:

| Sub-system | Location | Function |
|------------|----------|----------|
| **Sound Processor (SP)** | External (Behind-the-Ear or Off-the-Ear) | Acoustic capture, DSP, encoding |
| **Implanted Receiver-Stimulator (IRS)** | Subcutaneous (mastoid bone) | Demodulation, current steering, neural stimulation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 2 — Key Definition**
> A cochlear implant is **NOT** a hearing aid. A hearing aid amplifies acoustic energy; a cochlear implant **transduces acoustic energy into patterned electrical pulse trains** delivered directly to the auditory nerve.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine trying to talk to a friend across a noisy factory. Your voice (the acoustic signal) gets drowned out by the machines (the damaged cochlear hair cells). A cochlear implant is like installing a **direct telephone line** from your mouth to your friend's ear — completely bypassing the noisy factory floor.

Real-world analogy — **"The Digital Mailroom":**
- The **microphone** is the *mailbox slot* (where audio enters).
- The **speech processor** is the *mail sorting machine* (it decodes, filters, and packs the message into envelopes).
- The **transmitter coil** is the *wireless conveyor belt* that ferries the envelopes across the skin barrier.
- The **receiver coil** is the *delivery dock* on the other side.
- The **electrode array** is the *team of postal workers*, each assigned to deliver mail to one specific apartment (frequency region) inside the cochlea.

> [!NOTE]
> The skin acts as a **biological firewall**. Because piercing the skin with wires causes infection, modern CIs use **inductively coupled RF telemetry** — essentially a tightly-coupled air-core transformer operating at **5 MHz** to send both **power and bidirectional data** across the intact skin.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Significance |
|-----------|---------------|--------------|
| **RF Telemetry Carrier Frequency** | **5 MHz** (legacy 49 MHz; modern CIs use 5–49 MHz range) | Transcutaneous power + data link |
| **Number of Active Electrodes** | **12 to 22** (modern: up to 24) | Spectral resolution |
| **Stimulation Rate** | **250 Hz to 5000 pps per channel** | Temporal fidelity |
| **Pulse Width** | **10 µs to 400 µs** | Charge-balanced biphasic pulses |
| **Compliance Voltage** | **~8 V** (typical) | Limits max current × impedance |
| **Dynamic Range** | **10–20 dB (electrical) vs 100+ dB (acoustic)** | Compressed mapping |
| **Power Consumption** | **< 40 mW (external SP)** | Battery life |

---

## 1.4 Historical Context (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Favourite Question:**
> *“Trace the evolution of cochlear implants from single-channel to multi-channel systems.”*

- **1957** — Djourno & Eyriès (France): First direct electrical stimulation of auditory nerve.
- **1961** — William House (USA): First wearable single-channel CI.
- **1972** — First multichannel CI (University of Melbourne / Graeme Clark).
- **1984** — Nucleus 22 (Cochlear Corp.) — FDA approval for adults.
- **1990s onward** — Modern multi-channel CIs from Cochlear, MED-EL, Advanced Bionics, Nurotron.
- **2010s+** — Totally implantable CIs (e.g., Envoy Esteem, research prototypes), bilateral CIs, EAS (Electric-Acoustic Stimulation).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Decomposition — The Two-Subsystem Model

The Modern Cochlear Implant, when reduced to its fundamental engineering blocks, is a **closed-loop neurostimulator**. Every block plays a critical role in preserving **channel independence**, **charge safety**, and **spectral fidelity**.

### 2.1.1 External Sub-system (Sound Processor)

**Block 1 — Microphone Array (1 to 4 Mics)**
- Converts acoustic pressure waves → analog electrical signal.
- Modern CIs use **dual-mic or tri-mic directional arrays** for beamforming (improves SNR by 6–10 dB in noise).
- Bandwidth: ~100 Hz to 8 kHz (matches speech spectrum).

**Block 2 — Pre-processing & Analog Front-End (AFE)**
- **Automatic Gain Control (AGC)** — compresses the 100+ dB acoustic dynamic range into the 10–20 dB electrical dynamic range.
- AGC attack time: **~5 ms**, release time: **~50–200 ms**.
- Anti-aliasing filter (typically 4th-order Butterworth, fc ≈ 16 kHz).

**Block 3 — Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)**
- **16-bit to 24-bit sigma-delta** at sampling rates of **16–48 kHz**.
- Just-noticeable difference (JND) for loudness requires ≥ 12-bit resolution.

**Block 4 — Digital Signal Processor (DSP) — The "Brain"**
This is where the **Speech Processing Strategy** lives. Three families dominate KTU syllabi:

| Strategy | Year | Mechanism | Channels |
|----------|------|-----------|----------|
| **F0/F2 (House)** | 1980s | Extracts formants F0, F2 only | 1–4 |
| **MPEAK / SPEAK** | 1980s–90s | Multiple fixed spectral peaks | 6–22 |
| **CIS (Continuous Interleaved Sampling)** | 1991 | Simultaneous spectral decomposition, interleaved pulsatile | 6–22 |
| **ACE (Advanced Combination Encoder)** | 1999 | Hybrid: n-of-m maxima selection | Up to 22 |
| **HiRes / Fidelity 120** | 2000s | Current steering, virtual channels | 16 → 120 |
| **FSP / FS4 / FS4-p** | 2010s | Fine Structure, place-pitch coding | Up to 24 |

> [!NOTE]
> **CIS** is universally taught because of its clinical dominance and mathematical elegance. Each channel stimulates **non-overlapping in time** — this prevents **channel interaction** (a key KTU exam concept).

**Block 5 — Encoder / Modulator**
- Encodes stimulation parameters (amplitude, pulse width, channel) into a serial bitstream.
- Modulates the bitstream onto the **5 MHz RF carrier** using **OOK (On-Off Keying)** or **FSK**.
- Typical data rate: **~1 Mbit/s** (uplink) + **~5 Mbit/s (downlink)**.

**Block 6 — Power & Data Transmitter Coil**
- Outer diameter: ~25–30 mm.
- ~10–20 turns of Litz wire.
- Held in place by **magnet pair** (one external, one implanted) aligned across the skin.

### 2.1.2 Internal Sub-system (Implanted Receiver-Stimulator)

**Block 7 — Receiver Coil + Magnets**
- Inner coil diameter: ~15–22 mm.
- Demodulates incoming data and harvests RF power simultaneously.

**Block 8 — Power Recovery & Regulation**
- RF → AC → full-wave rectifier → DC-DC regulator.
- Supplies regulated voltages (1.8 V, 3.3 V, 8 V compliance) to internal logic and current sources.

**Block 9 — Demodulator & Decoder ASIC**
- Recovers clock, bit-synchronizes, and unpacks frames.
- Each frame contains: **channel ID, pulse amplitude, pulse width, polarity**.

**Block 10 — Current Source / DAC Array**
- One **monopolar or bipolar current source** per electrode channel.
- Output current range: **0 to ~2 mA** in steps of ~1 µA (typical 8-bit DAC).

**Block 11 — Electrode Selection Switch Matrix (MUX)**
- Routes one or more current sources to any of the **N electrodes**.
- Allows **current steering** (simultaneous stimulation of two adjacent electrodes at fractional amplitudes creates a "virtual" pitch percept between them — basis of Fidelity 120).

**Block 12 — Hermetic Titanium Case**
- Laser-welded titanium enclosure.
- Feedthroughs: ceramic-to-metal seals carrying wires to the electrode array.

**Block 13 — Electrode Array (Intra-cochlear)**
- **Length**: 16–31 mm.
- **Material**: Platinum-Iridium (90/10), often with silicone carrier.
- **Geometry**: Straight (perimodiolar) or pre-curled (modiolus-hugging).
- **Contacts**: Band electrodes, surface area typically **0.2–0.6 mm²**.

## 2.2 The Transcutaneous Link — Wireless Power & Data Transfer

The transcutaneous inductive link is the **most critical safety-critical** subsystem. It must:
1. Deliver **≥ 40 mW** of regulated power.
2. Provide **bit-error-rate < 10⁻⁹** on data.
3. Operate safely with **< 41°C temperature rise** (ISO 14708 limit: ΔT ≤ 2 °C).

The link is modeled as a **loosely-coupled air-core transformer**:

$$k = \frac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$$

where **k** is the coupling coefficient (typically 0.05–0.3 for skin-coupled CIs), **M** is mutual inductance, and **L₁, L₂** are coil self-inductances.

The induced voltage in the secondary (implanted) coil:

$$V_2 = j\omega M I_1 = j\omega k \sqrt{L_1 L_2} \, I_1$$

where **ω = 2πf** and **f ≈ 5 MHz**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cochlear Implant Engineering Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every formula** a KTU student may need for Module 2 derivations and numerical problems. **Memorise this entire table.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Boundary Condition |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|-------------------|
| 1 | $Q = I \cdot t$ | Charge per phase (Shannon limit basis) | Coulombs (C) | $Q \le Q_{max} = 0.24\ \mu\text{C/phase}$ |
| 2 | $D = \dfrac{I \cdot t}{A} = \dfrac{Q}{A}$ | Charge density (safety metric) | $\mu\text{C/cm}^2$ | $D \le 25\ \mu\text{C/cm}^2$ (geometric) |
| 3 | $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | Current density | $\mu\text{A/cm}^2$ | $J \le 30\ \mu\text{A/cm}^2$ (some electrodes) |
| 4 | $V_c = I \cdot Z_{elec} + V_{polarization}$ | Compliance voltage requirement | Volts | $V_c \le V_{supply}$ (typically 8 V) |
| 5 | $Z_{elec} \approx R_{access} + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{dl}}$ | Electrode-tissue impedance | Ohms | 1–10 kΩ @ 1 kHz |
| 6 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{delivered}}{P_{transmitted}} \approx k^2 Q_{coil}$ | Inductive link efficiency | Unitless | $\eta = 0.05$–$0.30$ |
| 7 | $f_{car} = 5\ \text{MHz}$ | Telemetry carrier | Hz | ISM medical band |
| 8 | $P_{stim} = I^2 \cdot Z_{elec} \cdot t_{phase}$ | Power dissipated per pulse | Watts | $P_{avg} \le 0.5$ mW electrode |
| 9 | $V_{induced} = \omega k \sqrt{L_1 L_2} I_1$ | Secondary coil voltage | Volts | Drives rectifier |
| 10 | $\text{DR}_{elec} = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{I_{max}}{I_{thr}}\right)$ | Electrical dynamic range | dB | 10–20 dB (vs 100 dB acoustic) |
| 11 | $\Delta f_{channel} = \dfrac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{N_{channels}}$ | Channel bandwidth | Hz | 250–500 Hz typical |
| 12 | $\text{DR}_{acoustic} = 20\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{min}}\right)$ | Acoustic dynamic range | dB | ~120 dB normal hearing |
| 13 | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\omega L}{R}\right)$ | Phase of electrode impedance | Radians | Used for channel separation |
| 14 | $E = \int V(t) \cdot I(t)\, dt$ | Energy per stimulation pulse | Joules | Used in safety calc |
| 15 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{P \cdot t}{m \cdot c}$ | Tissue heating | °C | $\Delta T \le 2$ °C (ISO 14708) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use `|` (vertical bar) inside table cells for absolute value or magnitude.** Use $\lvert x \rvert$ in LaTeX inline mode instead — Markdown tables will break otherwise. This is a strict KTU Board examination typographical rule.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Modern cochlear implants are **the most commercially successful neural implant in history**, with over **1 million recipients worldwide** as of 2024. Engineering principles derived from CIs are now applied to:
- **Retinal prostheses (Argus II, Alpha-IMS)** — uses identical current-steering architecture.
- **Deep brain stimulators (DBS)** — shares charge-balance and telemetry protocols.
- **Vagal nerve stimulators** — for epilepsy and depression.
- **Spinal cord stimulators** — for chronic pain management.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** Cochlear implants are the **proof-of-concept** that bidirectional, safe, long-term (>30 years) electrical neural interfaces are possible. They remain the **gold-standard benchmark** against which all new neural prosthetics are validated.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Charge and Charge-Density Limits (Shannon Limit Foundation)

**Problem Statement:**
A cochlear implant delivers biphasic current pulses of amplitude **I = 1.2 mA** and pulse width **t = 200 µs/phase** through an electrode of geometric surface area **A = 0.4 mm²**. Determine the charge per phase, the charge density, and assess against the **Shannon safety envelope**.

### Step 1 — Compute Charge per Phase

$$Q = I \times t$$

Substituting values:

$$Q = (1.2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A}) \times (200 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s})$$

$$Q = 1.2 \times 200 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{C}$$

$$Q = 240 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{C} = 0.24\ \mu\text{C/phase}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Charge Density

$$D = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.24\ \mu\text{C}}{0.4\ \text{mm}^2}$$

Converting mm² → cm² (since the standard unit is µC/cm²):

$$0.4\ \text{mm}^2 = 0.4 \times 10^{-2}\ \text{cm}^2 = 0.004\ \text{cm}^2$$

Therefore:

$$D = \frac{0.24\ \mu\text{C}}{0.004\ \text{cm}^2} = 60\ \mu\text{C/cm}^2$$

### Step 3 — Compare Against Shannon / ISO 14708 Limits

| Metric | Calculated | Limit | Verdict |
|--------|-----------|-------|---------|
| Charge per phase Q | 0.24 µC | $\le 0.24\ \mu\text{C}$ | **AT LIMIT** |
| Charge density D | 60 µC/cm² | $\le 25\ \mu\text{C/cm}^2$ (geometric for Pt-Ir) | **VIOLATION** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineering conclusion:** Although Q is within Shannon's per-phase limit, the charge density **D = 60 µC/cm² exceeds 25 µC/cm²**. The electrode area must be enlarged to **at least** $A_{min} = \dfrac{0.24\ \mu\text{C}}{25\ \mu\text{C/cm}^2} = 0.0096\ \text{cm}^2 = 0.96\ \text{mm}^2$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Inductive Power Link Voltage

**Problem Statement:**
An external transmitter coil has self-inductance **L₁ = 5 µH**, carries current **I₁ = 200 mA (peak)** at **f = 5 MHz**, and is coupled to the implanted coil (L₂ = 5 µH) with coupling coefficient **k = 0.15**. Find the induced secondary voltage.

### Step 1 — Compute Angular Frequency

$$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 5 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz}$$

$$\omega = 31.416 \times 10^6\ \text{rad/s}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Mutual Inductance

$$M = k \sqrt{L_1 L_2} = 0.15 \times \sqrt{(5 \times 10^{-6})^2}$$

$$M = 0.15 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 0.75\ \mu\text{H}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Induced RMS Voltage

$$V_2 = \omega M I_1$$

$$V_2 = (31.416 \times 10^6) \times (0.75 \times 10^{-6}) \times (0.200)$$

$$V_2 = 31.416 \times 0.75 \times 0.200 = 4.71\ \text{V (peak)}$$

Converting to RMS (assuming sinusoidal):

$$V_{2,RMS} = \frac{V_{2,peak}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{4.71}{1.414} = 3.33\ \text{V RMS}$$

### Step 4 — Verify Compliance Sufficiency

The induced voltage must be **greater than the sum of**:
- Rectifier drop (~0.7 V × 2 = 1.4 V for full-wave bridge).
- Compliance voltage required by stimulation circuitry (~8 V peak).

> [!WARNING]
> **Result is insufficient!** At 3.33 V RMS, the implant cannot drive an 8 V compliance voltage. **Conclusion:** A **resonant tank circuit** on the secondary (parallel LC tuned to 5 MHz) is **essential** to step up the voltage through Q-amplification by a factor of **20–40×**, yielding usable DC rails.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Channel Frequency Mapping (Tonotopic Place Theory)

**Problem Statement:**
A CI with N = 16 channels allocates frequency range **300 Hz to 6000 Hz** linearly along the basilar membrane. Find the centre frequency of channel 8 and the bandwidth per channel.

### Step 1 — Compute Per-Channel Bandwidth

$$\Delta f = \frac{f_{max} - f_{min}}{N} = \frac{6000 - 300}{16} = \frac{5700}{16}$$

$$\Delta f = 356.25\ \text{Hz per channel}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Centre Frequency of Channel k

For linear (Greenwood-like) allocation, the centre of channel k:

$$f_{c,k} = f_{min} + \left(k - \frac{1}{2}\right) \Delta f$$

For k = 8:

$$f_{c,8} = 300 + \left(8 - 0.5\right) \times 356.25$$

$$f_{c,8} = 300 + 7.5 \times 356.25 = 300 + 2671.875$$

$$f_{c,8} = 2971.875\ \text{Hz} \approx 2.97\ \text{kHz}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In real CIs, **logarithmic (Greenwood) mapping** is preferred because the cochlea resolves frequency logarithmically. The bandwidth therefore grows with frequency.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Stimulation Strategy Selection

| Criterion | CIS | ACE | HiRes / Fidelity 120 | FSP / FS4 |
|-----------|-----|-----|---------------------|-----------|
| **Temporal Resolution** | High | High | Medium | Highest (uses zero crossings) |
| **Spectral Resolution** | Medium (N channels) | High (n-of-m) | Very High (virtual channels) | Medium |
| **Channel Interaction** | Low (interleaved) | Low | Medium (simultaneous) | Low |
| **Power Consumption** | Medium | Medium-High | High | Medium |
| **Music Appreciation** | Poor | Poor-Fair | Fair | Better |
| **Speech in Quiet** | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| **Speech in Noise** | Good | Very Good | Very Good | Good |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |

## 3.5 Stimulation Modes — Comparative Matrix

| Mode | Anode | Cathode | Impedance | Use Case |
|------|-------|---------|-----------|----------|
| **Monopolar (MP1)** | External (case) | Intra-cochlear | Low (~1 kΩ) | High current efficiency, most common |
| **Bipolar (BP)** | Adjacent intra-cochlear | Intra-cochlear | High (~5–10 kΩ) | Better channel selectivity, less spread |
| **Common Ground (CG)** | All others | One intra-cochlear | Medium | Selectivity, power efficiency |
| **Tripolar / Quadrupolar** | Multiple | Multiple (with current steering) | Tunable | Used for current steering (virtual channels) |

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Signal-Flow Timeline for a Single CIS Frame

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant MIC as Microphone
    participant DSP as DSP
    participant TX as Transmitter
    participant RX as Receiver
    participant CS as Current Source
    participant EL as Electrode

    MIC->>DSP: Acoustic waveform (continuous)
    DSP->>DSP: AGC compress, FFT to N bands
    DSP->>DSP: Extract envelope from band k
    Note over DSP: Map envelope to amplitude a_k
    DSP->>TX: Frame (channel ID, amplitude, pulse width)
    TX->>RX: RF transmission 5 MHz
    RX->>RX: Demodulate, decode
    RX->>CS: Activate channel k source
    CS->>EL: Biphasic current pulse I, t_phase
    EL->>EL: Charge balanced cathodic then anodic
    Note over EL: Refractory period then next channel
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Charge-Balanced Biphasic Pulse Structure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[T = 0 Cathodic Phase Begins] --> B[I negative during t_cathode]

    C --> D[I positive during t_anode equal charge]
    D --> E[T = 2t_phase Pulse Ends]
    E --> F[Inter Pulse Gap IPG]

    classDef gap fill:#90EE90,stroke:#006400,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering insight:** In modern CIs, the **anodic (positive) phase** is delivered first for most strategies because cathodic-first pulses preferentially recruit **central** (low-spontaneous-rate) fibers, producing more natural loudness growth.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: Differentiate between a hearing aid and a cochlear implant. Why is a cochlear implant necessary in cases of severe sensorineural hearing loss?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- A **hearing aid** is an acoustic amplifier that increases sound pressure level delivered to the external auditory canal; it requires functional outer hair cells in the cochlea to transduce the amplified signal (1 mark).
- A **cochlear implant** is a neural prosthetic that bypasses the damaged cochlear hair cells entirely, converting acoustic signals into electrical pulse trains delivered directly to the **spiral ganglion neurons** via an intra-cochlear electrode array (1 mark).
- Severe sensorineural hearing loss implies widespread loss of inner hair cells; amplification alone cannot restore transduction. Direct electrical stimulation of auditory neurons is the only clinically validated intervention, making the cochlear implant necessary (1 mark).

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]
**Q: List the major functional blocks of the external sound processor in a modern cochlear implant.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Microphone (often directional array) (½ mark).
2. Analog front-end: preamplifier, AGC, anti-aliasing filter (½ mark).
3. Analog-to-digital converter (ADC) (½ mark).
4. Digital signal processor implementing the speech processing strategy (e.g., CIS, ACE) (1 mark).
5. Encoder/modulator and RF transmitter coil (½ mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: With the help of a neat functional block diagram, describe the architecture of a modern multi-channel cochlear implant. Discuss in detail the role of the (a) external sound processor, and (b) the implanted receiver-stimulator unit. Include the transcutaneous telemetry link in your discussion. (14 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) External Sound Processor (7 marks)**

The external sound processor is worn behind the ear or off-the-ear and performs all the acoustic-to-electrical conversion as well as the bulk of the signal processing. Its functional blocks are:

1. **Microphone Array** — converts acoustic pressure waves into an analog electrical signal. Modern CIs use **dual- or tri-microphone directional arrays** for beamforming, improving the signal-to-noise ratio in noisy environments by **6–10 dB** (1 mark).

2. **Analog Front-End (AFE)** — comprises a low-noise preamplifier, an **Automatic Gain Control (AGC)** that compresses the 100+ dB acoustic dynamic range to the 10–20 dB electrical dynamic range, and an anti-aliasing low-pass filter with a cut-off near **8 kHz** to band-limit speech (1 mark).
