3. **ADC** — typically a **16-bit sigma-delta converter** sampling at **16–48 kHz**, providing sufficient resolution (≥ 12-bit) to satisfy the loudness just-noticeable-difference (1 mark).

4. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** — the computational heart of the device. It implements a **filter bank** (FFT or band-pass filter bank) to decompose the audio into **N = 12–22 frequency bands**, extracts the **envelope** of each band, applies the **strategy engine** (CIS, ACE, etc.), performs the **T → L loudness mapping**, and constructs stimulation frames (1 mark).

5. **Encoder / Modulator** — converts each frame (channel ID, pulse amplitude, pulse width) into a serial bitstream, modulates it onto a **5 MHz carrier** using **OOK or FSK**, and routes it to the transmitter coil (1 mark).

6. **RF Transmitter Coil** — typically 10–20 turns of Litz wire with an outer diameter of 25–30 mm; magnetically coupled to the implanted coil across the intact skin (1 mark).

7. **Power Source** — a rechargeable Li-ion or Zn-air battery providing ≥ 40 mW continuous power (1 mark).

**(b) Implanted Receiver-Stimulator Unit (7 marks)**

The implanted unit is enclosed in a hermetically sealed titanium case placed in the mastoid bone. It contains:

1. **Receiver Coil + Magnet** — receives the 5 MHz RF signal and harvests both data and power inductively (1 mark).

2. **Power Recovery Block** — full-wave rectifier + DC-DC regulators providing **1.8 V, 3.3 V, and 8 V compliance rails** (1 mark).

3. **Demodulator / Decoder** — recovers clock, performs bit-synchronization, decodes each stimulation frame (channel ID, amplitude, pulse width, polarity) (1 mark).

4. **Control ASIC** — orchestrates the timing of stimulation events, enforces safety interlocks (e.g., charge balance verification), and handles telemetry back to the external unit (impedance measurement, neural response telemetry) (1 mark).

5. **Programmable Current Source Array** — **N** independent current sources (typically 8-bit DAC, range 0–2 mA, step ~1 µA); sources are linear and constant-current by design so that electrode impedance variation does not affect delivered charge (1 mark).

6. **Electrode Selection MUX** — routes each current source to any of the **N** electrode contacts; supports monopolar, bipolar, tripolar, and current-steering configurations (1 mark).

7. **Electrode Array** — **16–31 mm** Platinum-Iridium carrier with **12–22 band electrodes**, inserted into the scala tympani to lie close to the spiral ganglion; each contact has surface area ~0.2–0.6 mm² (1 mark).

**Transcutaneous Telemetry Link (1 mark bonus):** The inductive link operates at **5 MHz** with coupling coefficient **k ≈ 0.05–0.30**, providing simultaneous bidirectional power + data transfer across the intact skin. A resonant LC tank on the secondary side provides Q-amplification (×20–40) to step up the received voltage to a level sufficient to drive the compliance-voltage rail (8 V). ISO 14708 mandates a **ΔT ≤ 2 °C** temperature rise, requiring careful SAR and power management.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

**Q: Explain the CIS (Continuous Interleaved Sampling) speech processing strategy used in cochlear implants. With reference to the architecture, derive expressions for (a) charge per phase and charge density, and (b) the induced voltage in the transcutaneous inductive link. Comment on charge safety limits. (14 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — CIS Strategy and Charge Equations (7 marks)**

**CIS — Overview (3 marks):**
CIS, introduced by Wilson et al. in 1991, decomposes the audio signal into N band-pass filter outputs (typically 6–22 channels). For each band, the **envelope** is extracted via full-wave rectification + low-pass filtering. Each envelope is mapped to an amplitude via the T→L function, and a sequence of **non-overlapping, charge-balanced biphasic pulses** is generated — one channel at a time, in rapid succession. This **interleaved** temporal structure eliminates **simultaneous channel interaction** (channel cross-talk), a key advantage over compressed-analog strategies.

**Charge per Phase (2 marks):**

$$Q = I \cdot t_{phase}$$

where I is the cathodic current amplitude (typically 0.1–2 mA) and t_phase is the pulse width (typically 10–400 µs).

**Charge Density (2 marks):**

$$D = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{I \cdot t_{phase}}{A}$$

where A is the geometric surface area of the stimulating electrode contact (typically 0.2–0.6 mm²).

**Part (b) — Inductive Link Voltage (5 marks)**

The transcutaneous link is modeled as a loosely-coupled air-core transformer. The mutual inductance between the external and implanted coils is:

$$M = k \sqrt{L_1 L_2}$$

where k is the coupling coefficient (typically 0.05–0.30 for skin-coupled CIs).

The induced RMS voltage in the implanted secondary coil is:

$$V_2 = \omega M I_1 = 2\pi f \, k \sqrt{L_1 L_2} \, I_1$$

Substituting typical values: f = 5 MHz, k = 0.15, L₁ = L₂ = 5 µH, I₁ = 200 mA, we obtain V₂ ≈ 3.33 V RMS. A **resonant LC tank** on the secondary with quality factor Q ≈ 30 provides voltage amplification:

$$V_{boost} = Q \cdot V_2 \approx 100\ \text{V (no load)}$$

which is sufficient to drive the 8 V compliance rail after rectification and regulation.

**Charge Safety Comment (2 marks):**
The **Shannon safety envelope** (1992) gives a charge per phase limit of approximately **0.24 µC/phase**. Modern Pt-Ir micro-electrodes further limit charge density to **D ≤ 25 µC/cm² (geometric)** to prevent irreversible electrochemical reactions (water hydrolysis, platinum dissolution). Failure to maintain charge balance (i.e., residual DC > 0.1 µC) can cause electrode corrosion and tissue damage. Modern CIs enforce charge balance using **capacitor-coupled** stages or active **DC-blocking** circuits, with **< 0.1 µC residual DC** per pulse.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing hearing aid with cochlear implant** (loses 1–2 marks easily). Use the exact phrase "**bypasses damaged hair cells; directly stimulates spiral ganglion neurons**" — examiners award marks for this specific terminology.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the transcutaneous link.** Many students draw the external and internal blocks but omit the inductive coupling and 5 MHz carrier. **Always mention the telemetry link explicitly** — it carries up to 2 marks on its own.
>
> 3. **Wrong direction of current steering.** If asked about "virtual channels" or "Fidelity 120", students often confuse the anode/cathode role. **Rule: cathode is the stimulating electrode; anode is the return.** Current flows from anode → tissue → cathode externally; inside the lead, from cathode lead wire outward.
>
> 4. **Numerical errors in charge density.** Always convert mm² → cm². **1 mm² = 0.01 cm²**. Failure to do this is the single most common calculation error in KTU board exams.
>
> 5. **Writing "Shannon limit = 0.24 µC"** without specifying "per phase" — examiners will deduct marks. Always state **"per phase"** explicitly.
>
> 6. **Not labelling diagrams.** KTU examiners require **NEAT, LABELLED block diagrams** with arrows indicating signal/power flow direction. Unlabelled arrows cost 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 2: Cochlear Implant Architecture**
>
> - ☐ **CI = Neural prosthetic**, not a hearing aid. **Bypasses hair cells**, stimulates spiral ganglion directly.
> - ☐ Two sub-systems: **External Sound Processor (SP)** + **Internal Receiver-Stimulator (IRS)**.
> - ☐ **Transcutaneous link**: 5 MHz inductive, k ≈ 0.05–0.30, simultaneous power + data.
> - ☐ **AGC** compresses **120 dB acoustic → 10–20 dB electrical** dynamic range.
> - ☐ **N = 12–22 channels** mapped tonotopically (apex = low freq, base = high freq).
> - ☐ **CIS strategy**: non-overlapping interleaved pulses; eliminates channel interaction.
> - ☐ **ACE strategy**: n-of-m maxima selection; balances speed + spectral resolution.
> - ☐ **Charge per phase Q = I·t**; limit **0.24 µC/phase** (Shannon).
> - ☐ **Charge density D = Q/A**; limit **25 µC/cm²** geometric on Pt-Ir.
> - ☐ **Biphasic, charge-balanced pulses** are **MANDATORY** to prevent DC corrosion.
> - ☐ **Compliance voltage** ≈ 8 V; limits max I × Z_product deliverable.
> - ☐ **Electrode modes**: Monopolar (most common), Bipolar (selective), Tripolar (current steering).
> - ☐ **Current steering** creates **virtual channels** between physical electrodes (basis of Fidelity 120).
> - ☐ **Electrode material**: Pt-Ir (90/10); area 0.2–0.6 mm².
> - ☐ **ISO 14708** mandates ΔT ≤ 2 °C; **P_avg ≤ 0.5 mW per electrode**.
> - ☐ **Hermetic Ti case** with ceramic feedthroughs; biostable > 30 years in vivo.
> - ☐ **Filter bank**: FFT or band-pass; envelope extraction; T→L loudness mapping.
> - ☐ **AGC attack ~ 5 ms, release ~ 50–200 ms**.
> - ☐ **ADC**: 16–24-bit sigma-delta, 16–48 kHz.
> - ☐ **Receiver coil diameter**: 15–22 mm; **Tx coil diameter**: 25–30 mm.
> - ☐ **Resonant tank Q ≈ 20–40** amplifies secondary voltage to compliance rail.
> - ☐ **AGC**: Acoustic Dynamic Range = 120 dB; Electrical DR = 10–20 dB.
> - ☐ **1 million+ users worldwide** (2024); **most successful neural implant** commercially.
> - ☐ **Totally implantable CI** is the current research frontier.
> - ☐ **Bilateral CI** improves localization; **EAS** (Electric-Acoustic Stimulation) preserves low-frequency residual hearing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: External Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT753 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS AND IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** External Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

In the architecture of any **Neuroprosthetic System**, the **External Unit** (also called the *External Controller*, *Wearable Processor*, or *External Speech Processor* in auditory implants) refers to the complete set of **non-implanted, body-worn or table-top electronic subsystems** that perform sensor acquisition, signal processing, command generation, transcutaneous power delivery, bidirectional telemetry, and user-interface functions for the implanted stimulator or recorder.

Per the KTU 2024 Scheme definition aligned with international biomedical-device standards (ISO 14708, IEC 60601-1), the External Unit is the portion of the prosthesis residing **outside the hermetic envelope** of the body. It typically includes:

1. **Power Source** (battery or inductive primary)
2. **Signal Acquisition Module** (sensors, microphones, electrodes, accelerometers)
3. **Digital Signal Processor (DSP)** and **Microcontroller Unit (MCU)**
4. **Telemetry Transceiver** (transcutaneous link)
5. **Transcutaneous Power Coupling Coil / Antenna**
6. **User Interface** (buttons, LEDs, mobile app, display)
7. **Galvanic Isolation & Safety Barrier**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The External Unit is the *master* of the implant. Even the most sophisticated implanted electrode array is useless without a properly engineered external counterpart that can safely deliver power and bi-directional data across the skin barrier.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at a hotel, and you want to control the lights, TV, and air conditioning of your room from the lobby.

- **The Implanted Device** = Your hotel room's appliances.
- **The Skin** = The hotel room's locked door.
- **The External Unit** = The remote control and the front-desk operator combined.

The remote (External Unit) *sends commands* through the door (via an invisible infrared/radio signal = **telemetry**), and the operator (External Unit power supply) *feeds the appliances* through a hole in the wall (= **transcutaneous energy transfer**). You press a button (User Interface) → the remote thinks (DSP) → sends a wireless command → the in-room device obeys.

Just as a hotel guest cannot reach the room's wiring without the desk's help, the body's internal neural tissue cannot be stimulated without an **External Unit** safely orchestrating the energy and information transfer across the **skin barrier**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value | Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **MICS Band (Medical Implant Communication Service)** | **402 – 405 MHz** | FCC/ETSI reserved for implant ↔ external telemetry |
| **WMTS (Wireless Medical Telemetry Service)** | **608 – 614 MHz, 1395 – 1400 MHz, 1427 – 1432 MHz** | Used in wearable patient monitoring |
| **Inductive coupling frequency (TET)** | **100 kHz – 1 MHz** typical | Skin-safe power transfer |
| **Specific Absorption Rate (SAR) safety limit** | **≤ 1.6 W/kg** (FCC), **≤ 2.0 W/kg** (ICNIRP) | RF exposure to body tissue |
| **Skin thickness (epidermis + dermis)** | **1.5 – 3.0 mm** | Determines inductive link efficiency |
| **Galvanic isolation breakdown** | **≥ 1500 V RMS** (per IEC 60601-1) | Patient safety barrier |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The External Unit is *not* just "the box with the battery." It is a complete **biomedical-grade electronic ecosystem** responsible for safe, reliable, and precise communication with the implant.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Functional Block Decomposition of the External Unit

A typical External Unit of a neural prosthesis is built from **seven functional sub-blocks**, each performing a critical, KTU-examinable task:

### 2.1.1 Power Source Module
- **Primary cells:** Lithium-iodine (Li/I₂) — non-rechargeable, long shelf life (~10 years), used in early pacemakers.
- **Secondary cells:** Lithium-ion (Li-ion), Lithium-polymer (LiPo), and Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH) — rechargeable, higher energy density.
- **Supercapacitors:** Used in modern implants for burst power delivery (e.g., cochlear implants' high-current stimulation pulses).
- **Energy harvesting:** Body-heat (thermoelectric), limb-motion (piezoelectric), and RF energy scavenging (research stage).

### 2.1.2 Transcutaneous Energy Transfer (TET) System
- Two coupled **LC tanks** (primary coil outside, secondary coil inside) transfer energy via near-field magnetic induction.
- Operating principle: Faraday's Law of electromagnetic induction.

$$\mathcal{E} = -N \frac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$$

- The coupling coefficient $k$ is a measure of how efficiently flux from the primary coil reaches the secondary coil.

$$k = \frac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}} \quad \text{where } M \text{ is mutual inductance}$$

- Typical TET operating frequency: **100 kHz – 1 MHz**.

### 2.1.3 Telemetry Transceiver
- **Forward telemetry** (External → Implant): command data, stimulation parameters.
- **Backward telemetry** (Implant → External): neural recordings, impedance measurements, battery status.
- Modulation schemes used: **ASK (Amplitude Shift Keying)**, **FSK (Frequency Shift Keying)**, **PSK (Phase Shift Keying)**.
- MICS band is standard: **402–405 MHz**.

### 2.1.4 Signal Acquisition & Pre-processing
- For *recording* prostheses (e.g., BCI): external unit amplifies and digitizes signals from scalp electrodes (EEG) or from telemetry of implanted ECoG arrays.
- For *stimulating* prostheses (e.g., cochlear): external microphone → pre-amplifier → bandpass filter (typically **100 Hz – 8 kHz**) → ADC.

### 2.1.5 Digital Signal Processor (DSP)
- The "brain" of the External Unit.
- Runs algorithms such as:
  - **Speech processing** (cochlear implant: CIS, ACE, HiRes strategies)
  - **Feature extraction** (BCI: common spatial patterns, spectral power bands)
  - **Closed-loop control** (DBS, motor prostheses)

### 2.1.6 User Interface
- Physical controls (push buttons, rotary knobs).
- LEDs and LCDs for status display.
- Modern devices integrate **Bluetooth** to a smartphone app for parameter tuning (e.g., Medtronic Activa PC, Cochlear Nucleus 7).

### 2.1.7 Safety & Isolation Barrier
- **Optocouplers** and **isolation transformers** provide galvanic isolation between the mains-powered charger and the implant-side electronics.
- Prevents any single-fault leakage current from reaching the patient (**microshock** hazard, < **10 µA** allowed, per IEC 60601-1).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Formula / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Faraday's Law (TET) | $\mathcal{E} = -N \dfrac{d\Phi_B}{dt}$ | Volts; $N$ = turns, $\Phi_B$ = Wb |
| 2 | Coupling coefficient | $k = \dfrac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \le k \le 1$ |
| 3 | Inductive link efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{k^2 \, Q_1 \, Q_2}{(1+\sqrt{1+k^2 Q_1 Q_2})^2}$ | where $Q = \omega L / R$ is quality factor |
| 4 | Resonant frequency (LC tank) | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz |
| 5 | Skin depth (EM wave) | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2\rho}{\omega \mu}}$ | m; $\rho$ = resistivity, $\mu$ = permeability |
| 6 | Power transmitted (Friis eqn) | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | For far-field RF telemetry |
| 7 | Bit-error rate (FSK) | $P_e = \dfrac{1}{2} \mathrm{erfc}\left(\sqrt{\tfrac{E_b}{N_0}}\right)$ | Coherent FSK |
| 8 | Battery energy density | $E = V \cdot Q$ | Wh; Li-ion ≈ 250 Wh/kg |
| 9 | SAR limit (safety) | $\mathrm{SAR} = \dfrac{\sigma \vert E \vert^2}{\rho}$ | W/kg; limit 1.6 (FCC), 2.0 (ICNIRP) |
| 10 | Microshock threshold | $I_{\mathrm{leak}} < 10 \, \mu\mathrm{A}$ | Direct cardiac contact |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important:** In LaTeX tables, absolute-value bars inside formulas are written using `\vert` (as shown in row 9 above) to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. Never use raw `|` inside a table row.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The External Unit is the engineering **centerpiece** of any clinically viable neural prosthesis because:

- It allows **upgradability** of algorithms without re-implantation (a major commercial advantage of e.g., cochlear implant processors vs. the permanently implanted receiver-stimulator).
- It enables **regulatory compliance** — only the implanted portion must undergo the most stringent Class III biocompatibility testing.
- It is the **only place** where high-bandwidth, high-power computation (like deep-learning neural decoders) can be run without thermal/battery constraints on the implant.
- It serves as the **safety interlock** — the surgeon/clinician can switch off the entire prosthesis by simply removing the external processor (e.g., taking off the cochlear implant headpiece).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Transfer Efficiency of an Inductive TET Link

A classic KTU-style problem: derive the maximum achievable efficiency $\eta$ of a two-coil inductive link given quality factors $Q_1$, $Q_2$ and coupling $k$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

We model the two-coil system as two weakly-coupled series RLC resonators. The primary side has source $V_s$, resistance $R_1$, inductance $L_1$, and capacitance $C_1$; the secondary has $R_2$, $L_2$, $C_2$, and delivers power to a load $R_L$.

**Step 1:** At the resonance frequency $\omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{L_1 C_1} = 1/\sqrt{L_2 C_2}$, the reactive parts cancel, leaving only the resistances. The reflected impedance from secondary to primary is:

$$Z_{\mathrm{ref}} = \frac{(\omega M)^2}{R_2 + R_L}$$

**Step 2:** Define the loaded quality factors:

$$Q_1 = \frac{\omega L_1}{R_1}, \quad Q_{2L} = \frac{\omega L_2}{R_2 + R_L}$$

**Step 3:** The total impedance seen by the source is $R_1 + Z_{\mathrm{ref}}$. The power delivered to the load is the power dissipated in $R_L$, which equals the source power times the ratio of $Z_{\mathrm{ref}}$ to the total, *projected* onto $R_L$:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{\mathrm{load}}}{P_{\mathrm{source}}} = \frac{Z_{\mathrm{ref}}/R_L}{(R_1 + Z_{\mathrm{ref}})/R_1}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting $M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ and using $Q_1 Q_{2L} = k^2 \omega^2 L_1 L_2 / [R_1 (R_2+R_L)]$, we get $Z_{\mathrm{ref}} = k^2 Q_1 Q_{2L} R_1$. Let $u = k^2 Q_1 Q_{2L}$:

$$\eta = \frac{u \cdot R_1/R_L}{R_1(1+u)} = \frac{u/Q_{2L}'}{1+u}$$

where $Q_{2L}' = (R_2+R_L)/\omega L_2 \cdot Q_{2L}/Q_{2L} = $ the unloaded/loaded ratio.

**Step 5:** Maximizing $\eta$ with respect to load $R_L$ (i.e., choosing $Q_{2L} = 1/\sqrt{1+u}$ for matched load), we obtain the famous KTU formula:

$$\boxed{\eta_{\max} = \frac{k^2 Q_1 Q_2}{\left(1 + \sqrt{1 + k^2 Q_1 Q_2}\right)^2}}$$

**Step 6:** Physical interpretation. When $k^2 Q_1 Q_2 \gg 1$ (strong coupling or high-Q coils), $\eta_{\max} \to 1$. When $k^2 Q_1 Q_2 \ll 1$ (weak link, e.g., across thick skin with misaligned coils), $\eta_{\max} \approx k^2 Q_1 Q_2 / 4$ — a strong design driver for high-$Q$ coils.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** This formula appears frequently in KTU Module 2 questions on transcutaneous energy transfer. Memorize the boxed form, and remember that efficiency depends on the **product** $k^2 Q_1 Q_2$, not on $k$ alone.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Resonant Frequency and Coil Selection

**Problem:** A cochlear-implant external headpiece coil has $L_1 = 4.7 \,\mu H$ and is paired with an internal coil $L_2 = 5.2 \,\mu H$ across a 2 mm skin layer. Mutual inductance $M = 0.5 \,\mu H$. Find the coupling coefficient $k$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Recall the definition of coupling coefficient:

$$k = \frac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the geometric mean of the two inductances:

$$\sqrt{L_1 L_2} = \sqrt{(4.7 \times 10^{-6})(5.2 \times 10^{-6})} = \sqrt{24.44 \times 10^{-12}}$$

$$\sqrt{L_1 L_2} = 4.944 \times 10^{-6} \;\mathrm{H} = 4.944 \,\mu\mathrm{H}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the formula:

$$k = \frac{0.5 \times 10^{-6}}{4.944 \times 10^{-6}} = 0.1011$$

**Step 4:** Express as a percentage:

$$k \approx 10.1 \%$$

This is a **typical** coupling value for a 2 mm skin gap in transcutaneous cochlear-implant links, confirming that $k$ is small and the design must rely on **high-$Q$ coils** to maintain efficiency.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Powering Methods for the External Unit

| Criterion | Primary Battery | Rechargeable Battery | Inductive TET | RF Energy Harvesting |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy delivered | High (kWh-class) | Medium (Wh-class) | Medium (W-class continuous) | Low (mW-class) |
| Replacement frequency | Every 5–10 years | Every 1–3 years | None (continuous) | None (ambient) |
| Patient convenience | Low (surgery) | Medium (daily/weekly charging) | High (wear & charge) | Very high |
| Efficiency | 100 % (on-body) | 100 % (on-body) | 30 – 80 % | < 10 % |
| Safety | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent if isolated | Concerns for SAR |
| Typical application | Pacemaker | Cochlear, DBS, SCS | Cochlear headpiece | Experimental BCI |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an External Unit

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **functional architecture** and signal/power flow of a generic External Unit for a bidirectional neural prosthesis (e.g., a closed-loop DBS or a cochlear implant).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph EXT[EXTERNAL UNIT BODY WORN]
        A1[User Interface Buttons LED Display] --> A2[Microcontroller MCU]
        A2 --> A3[Digital Signal Processor DSP]
        A3 --> A4[Stimulus Parameter Encoder]
        A3 --> A5[Forward Telemetry Modulator FSK]
        BAT[Rechargeable Li-ion Battery 3.7 V] --> REG[Voltage Regulator 3.3 V 1.8 V]
        REG --> A2
        REG --> A3
        REG --> A6[Power Amplifier Class E]
        A6 --> A7[Primary TET Coil L1 Outside Skin]
        A5 --> A8[Telemetry Antenna 402 MHz MICS]
    end

    subgraph SKIN[TRANSCUTANEOUS INTERFACE 1.5 to 3 mm]
        A7 -. inductive coupling k .-> I1[Secondary TET Coil L2 Implanted]
        A8 -. RF link 402 MHz .-> I2[Implant Telemetry Antenna]
    end

    subgraph IMP[IMPLANTED UNIT]
        I1 --> I3[Rectifier and Voltage Regulator]
        I3 --> I4[Implant MCU]
        I2 --> I5[Telemetry Demodulator]
        I5 --> I4
        I4 --> I6[Stimulus Current Generator]
        I6 --> I7[Electrode Array on Nerve or Cortex]
        I7 -. neural recording .-> I8[Implant Amplifier and ADC]
        I8 --> I9[Backward Telemetry Modulator]
        I9 --> I2
    end

    I2 -. RF back telemetry .-> A10[External Telemetry Demodulator]
    A10 --> A3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The *solid arrows* represent **wired** power or signal paths within a unit (external or implant). The *dotted arrows* crossing the "SKIN" block represent **wireless transcutaneous coupling** — inductive for power, RF for data. This is the central engineering challenge of any neural prosthesis.

## 4.2 State Transition Diagram: Power Modes of the External Unit

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> OFF
    OFF --> BOOT: User Button Press
    BOOT --> SELF_TEST: MCU initializes
    SELF_TEST --> STANDBY: All checks pass
    SELF_TEST --> FAULT: Battery low or coil disconnected
    FAULT --> OFF: User acknowledgement
    STANDBY --> LINK_ACTIVE: TET coil aligned implant detected
    LINK_ACTIVE --> STIM_RUNNING: Stimulation command issued
    STIM_RUNNING --> LINK_ACTIVE: Stimulus burst complete
    LINK_ACTIVE --> STANDBY: Implant out of range
    STANDBY --> CHARGING: Charger connected
    CHARGING --> STANDBY: Battery full
    STANDBY --> OFF: Long press power button
    OFF --> [*]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **STIM_RUNNING** state is the only state where actual neural stimulation current is delivered. KTU questions often test the *safety interlocks* — e.g., "What happens if the TET coil detaches during STIM_RUNNING?" (Answer: the system must drop back to LINK_ACTIVE and emit an audible alarm within < 100 ms, per IEC 60601-1.)

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEBT753]**
**Define the term "External Unit" in the context of a neural prosthesis. List its four major functional blocks.**

**Model Answer:**

The **External Unit** of a neural prosthesis is the **non-implanted, body-worn portion** of the device that handles user interaction, signal processing, transcutaneous power delivery, and bi-directional telemetry to the implanted stimulator/recorder.

The **four major functional blocks** are:
1. **Power Source & Regulator** (battery + voltage regulation)
2. **Signal Acquisition / Generation Block** (microphone/sensor → ADC, or DSP-generated stimulus parameters)
3. **Transcutaneous Coupling Stage** (TET primary coil for power + telemetry antenna for data)
4. **User Interface & Safety Isolation** (buttons, LEDs, optocouplers, isolation barrier)

*(Examiner expects mention of safety/isolation as a distinct block — common omission.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEBT753]**
**What is the MICS band? Why is it preferred for medical implant telemetry?**

**Model Answer:**

**MICS** stands for **Medical Implant Communication Service**, a radio-frequency band reserved by international regulators (FCC in the USA, ETSI in Europe) for communication with implanted medical devices.

- **Frequency range:** **402 – 405 MHz** (a 3 MHz wide window).
- **Maximum radiated power:** **25 µW** (EIRP), deliberately low to limit tissue heating.
- **Channel bandwidth:** 300 kHz, providing **10 channels** within the band.

**Why preferred for implant telemetry:**
1. **Low tissue absorption** at UHF (~400 MHz) — wavelength ~75 cm is large compared to body size, so waves penetrate rather than get absorbed.
2. **Low SAR** at permitted power, satisfying safety standards.
3. **Dedicated band** — minimal interference from WiFi, Bluetooth, or cellular.
4. **Globally harmonized** — same band usable worldwide, easing regulatory approval.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A 14-mark KTU question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students who write generic "essay-style" answers without diagrams, formulas, or numbered points typically lose 3–4 marks per sub-part. Always use **headings, bullet points, formulas, and at least one diagram per 7-mark sub-part**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of the External Unit** of a cochlear implant. Explain the role of the directional microphone, the speech processor, the transmitting coil, and the FM-based telemetry link.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the **maximum power-transfer efficiency** of a two-coil inductive transcutaneous energy transfer (TET) system in terms of the coupling coefficient $k$ and the unloaded quality factors $Q_1$ and $Q_2$ of the two coils. Discuss how skin thickness and coil misalignment affect the achievable efficiency.

#### **Model Solution — Q.A(a)**

**Block diagram of a Cochlear-Implant External Unit (Cochlear Nucleus-class architecture):**

```
[Directional Microphone] -> [Pre-amp + AGC] -> [Bandpass Filter 100 Hz - 8 kHz]
                                                          |
                                                          v
[ADC] -> [DSP Speech Processor: CIS/ACE Strategy]
                                                          |
                                                          v
[Stimulus Parameter Encoder: Channel Gains, Pulse Rate, Pulse Width]
                                                          |
                                                          v
[FSK/PSK Modulator @ ~5 MHz] -> [RF Transmitter Coil (Primary)]
                                                          |
                                                  (transcutaneous)
                                                          v
                                              [Implanted Receiver Coil]
```

**Role of each block (Examiner expects 4 clearly labeled points, 1.5 marks each + 1 mark for the diagram = 7 marks):**

1. **Directional Microphone:** Picks up environmental sound with emphasis on the front (reducing rear noise by ~6 dB). Two-microphone arrays (e.g., in the Nucleus 7 processor) enable beamforming.
2. **Speech Processor (DSP):** Implements the sound-coding strategy. In **CIS (Continuous Interleaved Stimulation)**, the audio is split into N (typically 16–22) frequency bands, envelopes are extracted, compressed, and used to modulate interleaved biphasic current pulses delivered to the corresponding electrode.
3. **Stimulus Parameter Encoder:** Maps the processed envelope values to stimulation parameters (current amplitude, pulse width, electrode selection) and packs them into a serial bitstream for the telemetry link.
4. **FM-based Telemetry Link + Transmitting Coil:** A carrier in the **5 MHz** range (older devices) or **49 MHz** / **2.4 GHz** (newer devices) is FSK-modulated with the stimulus data and fed to the primary coil, which inductively couples power *and* data into the implant's secondary coil.

#### **Model Solution — Q.A(b)**

**Derivation of maximum TET efficiency:**

We present the full derivation as in **Section 3.1** of these notes. The student should reproduce:

$$\boxed{\eta_{\max} = \frac{k^2 Q_1 Q_2}{\left(1 + \sqrt{1 + k^2 Q_1 Q_2}\right)^2}}$$

**Numerical example (to embed in the answer for full marks):**

Assume $Q_1 = 80$, $Q_2 = 60$, $k = 0.15$ (typical across 2 mm skin with mild misalignment):

$$k^2 Q_1 Q_2 = (0.15)^2 \times 80 \times 60 = 0.0225 \times 4800 = 108$$

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{108}{\left(1 + \sqrt{1 + 108}\right)^2} = \frac{108}{(1 + 10.44)^2} = \frac{108}{130.99} \approx 82.4\%$$

**Discussion of skin-thickness and misalignment effects:**

- **Skin thickness** directly affects $k$. Going from 1 mm to 3 mm can reduce $k$ by **a factor of 2–3**, because flux falls off with the cube of distance in the near-field.
- **Coil misalignment** (lateral offset, angular tilt) further reduces $k$. A 5 mm lateral offset can cut $k$ in half.
- The product $k^2 Q_1 Q_2$ is the **figure of merit**. To maintain efficiency in real-world wearable conditions, designers use **high-$Q$ Litz-wire coils** (Q up to 200), **ferrite cores**, and **auto-tuning** capacitors that track resonance in real time.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss:** Students often write "efficiency depends on $k$" — it actually depends on $k^2 Q_1 Q_2$. This distinction is worth 2 marks in a KTU 14-mark question.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **transcutaneous power and data telemetry** methods used in modern neural prostheses. Compare **inductive coupling** with **RF telemetry (MICS band)** in terms of range, data rate, and safety.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **safety and isolation requirements** for the External Unit as per **IEC 60601-1**. In your answer, define the terms *macroshock*, *microshock*, *galvanic isolation*, and *patient auxiliary current*.

#### **Model Solution — Q.B(a)**

**Transcutaneous Power Transfer — Inductive Coupling:**

- Two coils (primary external, secondary internal) form a loosely-coupled transformer.
- Operating frequency **100 kHz – 1 MHz** (chosen as a compromise between skin absorption and coil size).
- Power transfer range: **mm to cm**, efficient for wearable/headpiece designs.
- Typical power levels: **10 mW – 1 W** for implants.

**Transcutaneous Data Telemetry — RF (MICS band):**

- Carrier frequency **402 – 405 MHz**.
- Modulation: FSK, PSK, or proprietary schemes.
- Data rates: typically **200 – 800 kbps**.
- Range: **2 – 5 m** (sufficient for in-room communication).

**Comparison Table (essential for full marks):**

| Parameter | Inductive (TET) | RF (MICS) |
|---|---|---|
| Primary function | Power transfer | Data telemetry |
| Operating frequency | 100 kHz – 1 MHz | 402 – 405 MHz |
| Range | mm – cm | m |
| Data rate | kbps (low) | up to Mbps |
| Tissue heating | Low at kHz–MHz | Low at 400 MHz with 25 µW EIRP |
| Alignment sensitivity | High | Low |
| Regulatory class | AIMD (Active Implantable Medical Device) | MICS / Medical Data Service |

#### **Model Solution — Q.B(b)**

**Definitions (each worth ~1 mark):**

1. **Macroshock:** Electric shock from a current applied to the *skin surface* that passes through the body. Threshold of perception ≈ **1 mA**; let-go threshold ≈ **10 mA**; ventricular fibrillation ≈ **100 mA** at 50 Hz.
2. **Microshock:** Direct application of current to the *heart* via an intracardiac conductor (e.g., pacing lead). Threshold for fibrillation is **as low as 10 µA** — 10,000× more sensitive than macroshock.
3. **Galvanic isolation:** Electrical separation of two circuits such that no direct conductive path exists between them; achieved via optocouplers, transformers, or capacitive coupling. Required isolation withstand voltage: **≥ 1500 V RMS** for medical devices.
4. **Patient auxiliary current:** Any current flowing through the patient via a *non-functional* path (e.g., leakage through a cable shield). Must be limited to **≤ 100 µA** (Type B), **≤ 50 µA** (Type BF), **≤ 10 µA** (Type CF — direct cardiac contact).

**Safety requirements summary for IEC 60601-1:**

- The External Unit must be powered by an **SELV (Safety Extra-Low Voltage)** source or by an **internally powered ME equipment** (battery).
- The applied part (the part that contacts the patient — for an External Unit, this is the headpiece or skin-contact surface) must be of **Type BF** (Body Floating) or **Type CF** (Cardiac Floating) classification.
- A **Means of Protection (MOP)** — typically 2× MOPP (Means of Patient Protection) — must be provided between the mains circuit and the applied part.
- **Defibrillation-proof** applied parts must withstand a 5 kV defibrillator pulse without damage.
- An **alarm system** (audible and visual) must signal any out-of-spec condition within **< 100 ms** for life-supporting devices.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss:** Students frequently confuse *macroshock* and *microshock* thresholds. Remember the rule of thumb: **microshock is 10,000× more dangerous** because the current bypasses the skin's natural resistance and goes directly to the heart via an implanted lead.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for "External Unit"**

- ☐ **Definition:** External Unit = non-implanted, body-worn controller of the neural prosthesis; performs sensing, processing, telemetry, and powering.
- ☐ **Major blocks:** (1) Power source (battery), (2) TET primary coil, (3) Telemetry antenna, (4) DSP/MCU, (5) Sensor front-end, (6) User interface, (7) Safety/isolation barrier.
- ☐ **MICS band:** **402–405 MHz**, 25 µW EIRP, 10 channels of 300 kHz each.
- ☐ **TET frequency:** 100 kHz – 1 MHz; efficiency formula $\eta = \frac{k^2 Q_1 Q_2}{(1+\sqrt{1+k^2 Q_1 Q_2})^2}$.
- ☐ **Coupling coefficient** $k = M / \sqrt{L_1 L_2}$, typically 5–15% across skin.
- ☐ **IEC 60601-1** safety: Type BF/CF applied parts, 2× MOPP, microshock threshold 10 µA, defibrillation withstand 5 kV.
- ☐ **Inductive vs RF telemetry:** Inductive = power, mm–cm range, kHz–MHz; RF = data, m range, MICS band.
- ☐ **Power sources compared:** Primary cells (decade-life), Li-ion (rechargeable), inductive (no battery), RF harvesting (experimental).
- ☐ **Speech coding strategies** for cochlear implants: CIS, ACE, HiRes — all run in the External Unit's DSP.
- ☐ **Skin thickness** 1.5–3 mm is the dominant factor reducing $k$ in real-world TET.
- ☐ **Galvanic isolation** is non-negotiable for any device with a mains connection.

**Final Mnemonic — the "4 S's" of the External Unit:**
> **S**ense → **S**hape → **S**end → **S**ustain (power).

This captures the signal flow (sensor → DSP → telemetry → TET) in a single sentence — perfect for last-minute revision before walking into the KTU examination hall.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: RF Link
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT753 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS AND IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** RF Link

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of RF Link in Neural Prosthesis

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RF Link (Radio Frequency Telemetry Link):** In a neural prosthesis, an **RF Link** is the wireless electromagnetic coupling channel that simultaneously performs **bidirectional data telemetry** (outgoing recorded neural signals, incoming stimulation/control commands) and **transcutaneous energy transfer (TET)** between an **external transceiver (Tx/Rx coil or antenna)** and the **subcutaneous implanted module**.

The link operates either in the **near-field (inductive / magnetic coupling) regime** — typically at **13.56 MHz ISM** or **125 kHz–1 MHz** — for short-range power transfer, or in the **far-field (radiative) regime** — typically at the **MICS band 402–405 MHz** or **MedRadio 401–406 MHz, 413–457 MHz** — for high-data-rate telemetry, as defined by the FCC and ITU-R radio regulations.

The governing relationship is the **Friis Transmission Equation** for far-field and the **mutually-coupled resonant inductor model** for near-field.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Wireless Charger + Wi-Fi Hotspot" Inside the Body**

Think of a neural implant RF link as **two things happening at once**:

1. **Like a wireless phone charger (inductive coupling):** A coil of wire outside your head (or chest) and a coil of wire inside the body form two halves of a **loosely coupled transformer**. When AC current flows in the external coil, a time-varying magnetic field (B-field) crosses the skin and induces a voltage in the internal coil — this **charges the implant's battery** without any wire piercing the skin.

2. **Like a Wi-Fi signal in reverse (radiative telemetry):** The neural signals picked up by electrodes inside the brain (microvolt-level) are amplified, digitized, and **broadcast as a low-power radio signal** out through body tissue to an external receiver — exactly like a tiny Wi-Fi card, but transmitting through flesh instead of air.

The **RF link** is the *combined hardware + signal-processing pipeline* that makes both of these wireless feats reliable, safe, and energy-efficient, even though the body is mostly **salt water (a lossy dielectric)** that absorbs and reflects RF energy.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value / Band | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Speed of light in vacuum, **c** | **3 × 10⁸** | m/s |
| Permeability of free space, **μ₀** | **4π × 10⁻⁷** | H/m |
| Permittivity of free space, **ε₀** | **8.854 × 10⁻¹²** | F/m |
| Characteristic impedance of free space, **Z₀** | **377** | Ω |
| **MICS / MedRadio band** | **402 – 405** | MHz |
| **ISM band (common for TET)** | **13.56** | MHz |
| **SAR limit (IEEE C95.1, 10 g avg.)** | **2.0** | W/kg |
| **SAR limit (FCC, 1 g avg.)** | **1.6** | W/kg |
| Typical neural data rate | 1 – 10 | Mbps |
| Typical TET power | 10 – 100 | mW |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of an RF Link

The RF link in a neural implant is split into **three functional planes** working simultaneously:

### Plane 1 — Forward (Downlink) Data + Power Path
- **External controller → Implant**
- Modulation schemes: **ASK (Amplitude Shift Keying)** or **OOK (On-Off Keying)** for simplicity and low-power receiver.
- Carries: stimulation parameters, calibration values, clock synchronization.
- Often combined with **power transfer** using a single carrier (the **piggyback modulation** or **load-shift-keying** in reverse).

### Plane 2 — Reverse (Uplink) Telemetry Path
- **Implant → External receiver**
- Modulation schemes: **FSK (Frequency Shift Keying)** or **GMSK / DBPSK** for noise immunity in tissue.
- Carries: raw or compressed neural spike trains, electrode impedance measurements, battery state-of-charge.
- **Bit rate:** modern bidirectional brain-computer interfaces demand **≥ 1 Mbps** per channel; aggregated systems target **50–200 Mbps**.

### Plane 3 — Power Harvesting Path
- Continuous-wave (CW) sinusoidal carrier, usually **13.56 MHz** (10× tissue depth penetration vs. MICS).
- Receiver uses **Schottky diode rectifier + LC matching network** to convert RF → DC (the "RF-DC" stage).
- Output fed to a **low-dropout regulator (LDO)** for stable implant supply.

## 2.2 Near-Field vs Far-Field — Boundary Criterion

The **Fraunhofer distance** $d_F = \frac{2 D^2}{\lambda}$ (where D is the largest antenna/coil dimension) separates the two regimes:

- **Near-field (reactive):** $d < d_F$ → dominant **inductive / capacitive** coupling. Used for **TET**.
- **Far-field (radiative):** $d \gg d_F$ → dominant **electromagnetic wave propagation**. Used for **high-data-rate telemetry**.

For a 30 mm external coil at 13.56 MHz, $\lambda \approx 22$ m, so $d_F \approx 0.08$ mm — meaning **transcutaneous coupling is ALWAYS near-field**. For a 5 mm antenna at 403 MHz, $\lambda \approx 0.74$ m, so $d_F \approx 6.8$ cm — so MICS telemetry is near-field for shallow implants (cortical) and far-field for deeper ones.

## 2.3 The Mutually-Coupled Resonant Inductor Model (TET)

When two coils (primary L₁, secondary L₂) are placed within a few cm of each other across skin:

- **Coupling coefficient:** $k = \frac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$, where M is the mutual inductance.
- **Coupling is weak:** typically $k = 0.01$ to $0.3$ across 5–20 mm of tissue.
- **Quality factors:** $Q_1 = \frac{\omega L_1}{R_1}$, $Q_2 = \frac{\omega L_2}{R_2}$, where R₁, R₂ are coil series resistances (skin + proximity losses included).

### Resonance Tuning
Both sides are tuned to the same carrier frequency to maximize energy transfer:

$$f_0 = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_1 C_1}} = \frac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{L_2 C_2}}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Examination Critical)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the EXAM GOLD — memorize before any KTU test on this module. Render uses `\vert` to preserve markdown table syntax (no bare `|`).**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes / Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Resonant frequency | $f_0 = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | Hz | Tuning both Tx and Rx coils |
| 2 | Quality factor (series R) | $Q = \dfrac{\omega_0 L}{R}$ | — | Higher Q = sharper resonance |
| 3 | Coupling coefficient | $k = \dfrac{M}{\sqrt{L_1 L_2}}$ | — | 0 (no coupling) → 1 (ideal) |
| 4 | Mutual inductance | $M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ | H | Used in induced EMF |
| 5 | Induced secondary voltage | $V_2 = j\omega M I_1$ | V | First-order TET equation |
| 6 | Power transfer efficiency (4-coil) | $\eta = \dfrac{k^2 Q_1 Q_2}{(1+k^2 Q_1 Q_2)}$ | — | Max possible ≈ 50% with 2-coil; >80% with 4-coil |
| 7 | **Friis transmission eqn** | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | W | **Far-field only** |
| 8 | Free-space path loss (linear) | $FSPL = \left(\dfrac{4\pi d}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | — | Convert to dB: $20\log_{10}(4\pi d/\lambda)$ |
| 9 | Reflection coefficient | $\Gamma = \dfrac{Z_L - Z_0}{Z_L + Z_0}$ | — | $\vert\Gamma\vert^2$ = return loss power ratio |
| 10 | VSWR | $VSWR = \dfrac{1 + \vert\Gamma\vert}{1 - \vert\Gamma\vert}$ | — | 1 (matched) → ∞ (open) |
| 11 | Return loss (dB) | $RL = -20\log_{10}\vert\Gamma\vert$ | dB | Higher = better match |
| 12 | SNR (linear) | $SNR = \dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}$ | — | In dB: $10\log_{10}(SNR)$ |
| 13 | BER (coherent BPSK) | $P_b = Q\!\left(\sqrt{2 E_b / N_0}\right)$ | — | E_b = energy/bit, N₀ = noise PSD |
| 14 | Nyquist bit rate (noiseless) | $C = 2 BW \log_2 M$ | bps | M = signal levels (M-ary PAM) |
| 15 | Shannon-Hartley capacity | $C = BW \cdot \log_2(1+SNR)$ | bps | Hard upper bound for AWGN |
| 16 | Skin depth in tissue | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{2}{\omega \mu_0 \sigma}}$ | m | At 13.56 MHz in muscle, δ ≈ 9 cm |
| 17 | Specific Absorption Rate | $SAR = \dfrac{\sigma \vert E\vert^2}{2 \rho}$ | W/kg | $\rho$ = tissue density, σ = conductivity |
| 18 | Coil self-inductance (solenoid) | $L = \dfrac{\mu_0 N^2 A}{\ell}$ | H | N=turns, A=area, ℓ=length |

## 2.5 Why Each Piece Matters in Engineering Practice

- **Tuning to $f_0$** is what makes the weak link useful — without resonance, $kQ_1 Q_2$ collapses and < 1% of transmitted power reaches the implant.
- **Friis equation** sets the **link budget**: it tells designers whether a chosen frequency, antenna gain, and distance can meet the receiver sensitivity floor (~ −90 dBm for MedRadio).
- **BER** is the **clinical reliability metric** — dropped bits in a stimulation command could fire the wrong electrode.
- **SAR** is the **regulatory hard limit** — exceeding it triggers thermal tissue damage, so it constrains the maximum radiated power.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Example

## 3.1 Derivation #1 — Friis Transmission Equation (From Maxwell to Power Received)

We derive the received power in free space for two lossless antennas separated by distance $d$.

**Step 1 — Define the transmitter.** Consider an isotropic radiator with input power $P_t$. The power flux density (Poynting vector magnitude) at distance $d$ is:

$$S = \frac{P_t}{4\pi d^2} \quad [\text{W/m}^2]$$

**Step 2 — Apply directivity of the transmit antenna.** A real antenna has gain $G_t$ in the direction of the receiver, so the flux density becomes:

$$S = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the power captured by the receive antenna.** The effective aperture of a lossless antenna is related to its gain by:

$$A_e = \frac{\lambda^2 G_r}{4\pi}$$

So the power delivered to the matched receiver is:

$$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \frac{P_t G_t}{4\pi d^2} \cdot \frac{\lambda^2 G_r}{4\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Combine into the canonical Friis form.**

$$P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$$

This is the **free-space** equation — for tissue, we replace it with the **in-body path loss (IBPL)** model $PL(d) = PL(d_0) + 10 n \log_{10}(d/d_0)$ where $n \approx 3.5$–$5$ (much steeper than free space due to absorption).

## 3.2 Derivation #2 — Power Transfer Efficiency of a 2-Coil Resonant Link

Consider two series-resonant coils: primary (L₁, R₁, C₁) and secondary (L₂, R₂, C₂), with mutual inductance M.

**Step 1 — Write the loop equations using KVL:**

$$V_1 = (R_1 + j\omega L_1 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_1}) I_1 - j\omega M I_2$$

$$0 = -j\omega M I_1 + (R_2 + j\omega L_2 + \frac{1}{j\omega C_2}) I_2 + R_L$$

where $R_L$ is the load on the secondary.

**Step 2 — Apply resonance condition.** At $\omega = \omega_0 = 1/\sqrt{L_i C_i}$ for both sides, the reactive terms vanish, leaving:

$$V_1 = R_1 I_1 - j\omega_0 M I_2$$

$$0 = -j\omega_0 M I_1 + (R_2 + R_L) I_2$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $I_2$ from the second equation:**

$$I_2 = \frac{j\omega_0 M}{R_2 + R_L} I_1$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the first equation to get input impedance:**

$$V_1 = \left[ R_1 + \frac{(\omega_0 M)^2}{R_2 + R_L} \right] I_1$$

**Step 5 — Define the reflected resistance seen by the source:**

$$R_{ref} = \frac{(\omega_0 M)^2}{R_2 + R_L} = \frac{k^2 \omega_0^2 L_1 L_2}{R_2 + R_L} = \frac{k^2 \omega_0 L_1 \cdot \omega_0 L_2}{R_2 + R_L}$$

Using $Q_i = \omega_0 L_i / R_i$:

$$R_{ref} = k^2 Q_1 Q_2 (R_2 + R_L) \cdot \frac{R_1 R_2}{(R_2 + R_L)} = k^2 Q_1 Q_2 (R_2 \parallel R_L)$$

**Step 6 — Compute the efficiency** $\eta = P_{load}/P_{source}$:

$$\eta = \frac{R_{ref} \cdot \frac{R_L}{R_2+R_L}}{R_1 + R_{ref}} = \frac{k^2 Q_1 Q_2 R_L / R_1}{1 + k^2 Q_1 Q_2 (R_2+R_L)/R_1}$$

For matched load ($R_L = R_2$):

$$\boxed{\eta = \frac{k^2 Q_1 Q_2}{1 + k^2 Q_1 Q_2}}$$

This is the **famous KTL resonant efficiency formula** — it proves why **high Q coils + high k + low R** are essential for implant TET.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — KTU Numerical Style

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worked Problem (typical KTU Part B 7-mark style):**
> A transcutaneous energy transfer (TET) system uses two planar spiral coils across 10 mm of skin. Primary coil: $L_1 = 5 \,\mu\text{H}$, $R_1 = 0.5 \,\Omega$. Secondary: $L_2 = 6 \,\mu\text{H}$, $R_2 = 0.6 \,\Omega$. Coupling coefficient $k = 0.1$. Operating frequency $f_0 = 13.56$ MHz. Compute the link efficiency assuming matched load.

**Step 1 — Compute the quality factors.**

$$Q_1 = \frac{\omega_0 L_1}{R_1} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 13.56\times 10^6 \cdot 5 \times 10^{-6}}{0.5}$$

$$Q_1 = \frac{2\pi \cdot 13.56 \cdot 5}{0.5} = \frac{425.9}{0.5} \approx 851.8$$

$$Q_2 = \frac{2\pi \cdot 13.56 \cdot 6}{0.6} = \frac{511.1}{0.6} \approx 851.8$$

**Step 2 — Compute the product $k^2 Q_1 Q_2$.**

$$k^2 Q_1 Q_2 = (0.1)^2 \cdot 851.8 \cdot 851.8 = 0.01 \cdot 7.26 \times 10^5 \approx 7256$$

**Step 3 — Apply the efficiency formula.**

$$\eta = \frac{7256}{1 + 7256} = \frac{7256}{7257} \approx 0.99986$$

That is **99.99% efficiency** — unrealistically high because we ignored **proximity effect, skin-effect, and tissue losses**. In practice, designers use de-embedded Q values **$Q_{\text{eff}} = 30$–$100$** measured with the body present, giving realistic efficiencies of **40%–85%**.

**Step 4 — Recompute with realistic $Q_{\text{eff}} = 50$.**

$$k^2 Q_1 Q_2 = 0.01 \cdot 50 \cdot 50 = 25$$

$$\eta = \frac{25}{1 + 25} = \frac{25}{26} \approx 0.962 \;(96.2\%)$$

**Step 5 — Recompute with very low coupling $k = 0.02$ (deep implant, 20 mm depth).**

$$k^2 Q_1 Q_2 = (0.02)^2 \cdot 50 \cdot 50 = 0.0004 \cdot 2500 = 1.0$$

$$\eta = \frac{1.0}{1 + 1.0} = 0.5 \;(50\%)$$

This is the practical regime — **4-coil (relay resonator) systems** are introduced to break this barrier.

## 3.4 Derivation #3 — The 4-Coil (Two Intermediate Resonator) System

A 4-coil link inserts two high-Q **repeater resonators** (L₂, L₃) between the source (L₁) and load (L₄) coils. The total efficiency becomes the product of pairwise couplings weighted by the intermediate Q values, with the effective k boosted by a factor $\sqrt{Q_2 Q_3}$. This is the basis of **WiTricity / Witricity-style** wireless power transfer used in modern neural dust and brain stimulator implants (e.g., MIT 2017 "brain dust" paper, Johns Hopkins Neurochip-2).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Bidirectional RF Link Architecture for a Neural Prosthesis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SrcExt[External Control Unit] --> Enc1[Channel Encoder]
    Enc1 --> Mod1[ASK Modulator]
    Mod1 --> PwrAmp[Class-E Power Amplifier]
    PwrAmp --> TxCoil[Tx Coil 13.56 MHz]
    TxCoil ==>|Inductive Link| RxCoil[Rx Implant Coil]
    RxCoil --> Rect[Schottky Rectifier]
    Rect --> LDO[LDO Regulator]
    LDO --> Batt[Implant Battery]
    LDO --> StimCkt[Stimulation Circuit]
    Enc1 --> StimCkt

    El[Neural Electrodes] --> Amp[Neural Amplifier]
    Amp --> ADC[ADC]
    ADC --> Enc2[Channel Encoder Implant]
    Enc2 --> Mod2[FSK Modulator]
    Mod2 --> TxImp[Implant Tx Antenna 403 MHz]
    RxCoil -.->|Shared coil| TxImp
    TxImp ==> |Radiative Link| RxExt[External Rx Antenna]
    RxExt --> Demod[FSK Demodulator]
    Demod --> Dec[Channel Decoder]
    Dec --> SrcExt
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Mutual Coupling Model (TET Equivalent Circuit)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Vsrc[V_source 13.56 MHz] --> R1[R1 coil loss]
    R1 --> L1[L1 primary coil]
    L1 --> C1[C1 primary cap]
    L1 ---|M mutual| L2[L2 secondary coil]
    L2 --> C2[C2 secondary cap]
    L2 --> R2[R2 coil loss]
    L2 --> RL[R_load matched]
    Vsrc --> Gnd1[Ground Primary]
    RL --> Gnd2[Ground Secondary]
```

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Receiver Signal Chain (Reverse Telemetry)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Implant Antenna 403 MHz] --> B[Bandpass Filter SAW]
    B --> C[LNA Low Noise Amplifier]
    C --> D[I-Q Downconverter]
    D --> E[Baseband Amplifier]
    E --> F[ADC 10 bit 100 MSps]
    F --> G[Packet Decoder]
    G --> H[SPI to Neural DSP]
    H --> I[External PC Display]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Link-Budget Calculation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Transmit Power Pt dBm] --> Gt[+ Tx Antenna Gain Gt dBi]
    Gt --> FSPL[Minus FSPL or IBPL]
    FSPL --> Gr[+ Rx Antenna Gain Gr dBi]
    Gr --> Mf[Minus Mismatch Loss ML dB]
    Mf --> Pl[Minus Polarization Loss PL dB]
    Pl --> Pr[Received Power Pr dBm]
    Pr --> Sens{Compare to Rx Sensitivity}
    Sens -->|Above| Pass[Link Margin Positive]
    Sens -->|Below| Fail[Link Fails Add Margin]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks each) — Short Answer

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]
**Define an RF link in the context of a neural prosthesis. Name the two regulatory frequency bands commonly used for transcutaneous power and data transfer.**

**Model Answer:**
An **RF link** in a neural prosthesis is the **wireless electromagnetic coupling** between an **external transceiver** and the **subcutaneous implant** for both **transcutaneous energy transfer (TET)** and **bidirectional data telemetry**. The two standard regulatory bands are:
1. **13.56 MHz ISM band** — used for **near-field inductive power transfer** (high tissue penetration, low absorption).
2. **402–405 MHz MICS / MedRadio band** — used for **far-field data telemetry** (compact antenna, moderate data rate ≥ 1 Mbps, internationally allocated for implants).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]
**Distinguish between near-field and far-field RF coupling. Which is preferred for transcutaneous power transfer and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Near-Field | Far-Field |
|---|---|---|
| Mechanism | Inductive / capacitive coupling | EM wave radiation |
| Distance regime | $d < 2D^2/\lambda$ | $d \gg 2D^2/\lambda$ |
| Governing equation | Mutual inductance (M) | Friis equation |
| Typical use in implants | **TET (power)** | **Telemetry (data)** |

**Near-field (inductive) is preferred for TET** because: (i) it works efficiently even at very short ranges (mm–cm); (ii) it is largely unaffected by lossy tissue at low frequencies (e.g., 13.56 MHz); (iii) it does not radiate energy into the body, so SAR stays low.

## 5.2 PART B Question A (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Choice Option

### Q.A. (a) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**Explain the Friis transmission equation. Derive it from first principles. Mention its limitations when applied to in-body neural implant links. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Statement:** The Friis transmission equation gives the **received power** $P_r$ at a distance $d$ in free space for two lossless antennas:

$$P_r = P_t \, G_t \, G_r \left( \frac{\lambda}{4\pi d} \right)^2$$

**2. Derivation (5 logical steps, each carrying partial credit):**

- **Step 1:** Isotropic power flux density at distance d: $S = P_t / (4\pi d^2)$ (sphere area $4\pi d^2$).
- **Step 2:** Apply Tx directivity: $S = P_t G_t / (4\pi d^2)$.
- **Step 3:** Effective aperture of Rx antenna: $A_e = \lambda^2 G_r / (4\pi)$.
- **Step 4:** Power captured: $P_r = S \cdot A_e$.
- **Step 5:** Substitute and rearrange to canonical Friis form (full expansion shown in §3.1).

**3. Limitations for in-body neural implants:**

- **Free-space assumption violated**: tissue has $\sigma \neq 0$ and $\varepsilon_r \approx 50$–$80$, causing **strong absorption** and **dispersion** — must be replaced by **in-body path loss (IBPL)** models.
- **Polarization mismatch**: linearly polarized external antenna may not align with the implanted antenna orientation.
- **Multipath and standing waves** in layered tissue (skin → fat → muscle → bone) cause **fading** of 10–30 dB.
- **Antenna detuning**: the body shifts the resonant frequency by 5–15% — must be re-tuned post-implant.
- **Equation is valid only in far-field** $d \gg 2D^2/\lambda$ — for very compact implants, the **reactive near-field** dominates and Friis overestimates $P_r$.

### Q.A. (b) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**A neural implant uses a 2-coil TET link with $L_1 = L_2 = 10 \,\mu\text{H}$, $R_1 = R_2 = 1 \,\Omega$, $k = 0.15$, $f_0 = 13.56$ MHz, and matched load. Compute: (i) the quality factors, (ii) the product $k^2 Q_1 Q_2$, (iii) the link efficiency, and (iv) the link efficiency if the implant depth increases so that $k$ drops to 0.05. Comment on the result. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Quality factors:**

$$Q_1 = Q_2 = \frac{2\pi \cdot 13.56 \times 10^6 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-6}}{1} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 135.6}{1} \approx 851.8$$

But using **effective (in-body) Q** is more realistic. Taking $Q_{\text{eff}} = 50$ as the measured value:

$$Q_1 = Q_2 = 50$$

**(ii) Product $k^2 Q_1 Q_2$:**

$$k^2 Q_1 Q_2 = (0.15)^2 \cdot 50 \cdot 50 = 0.0225 \cdot 2500 = 56.25$$

**(iii) Link efficiency (matched load, 2-coil):**

$$\eta = \frac{k^2 Q_1 Q_2}{1 + k^2 Q_1 Q_2} = \frac{56.25}{1 + 56.25} = \frac{56.25}{57.25} \approx 0.9826 \;(98.3\%)$$

**(iv) With $k = 0.05$:**

$$k^2 Q_1 Q_2 = (0.05)^2 \cdot 50 \cdot 50 = 0.0025 \cdot 2500 = 6.25$$

$$\eta = \frac{6.25}{1 + 6.25} = \frac{6.25}{7.25} \approx 0.862 \;(86.2\%)$$

**Comment:** As coupling drops with depth, efficiency falls rapidly (from 98% to 86%) because $\eta$ is **non-linear** in $k^2$. For deep implants, a **4-coil relay-resonator** topology or **higher-Q lithographic coils** (Q > 100) must be used to recover the lost efficiency.

## 5.3 PART B Question B (14 Marks) — KTU ESE Choice Option

### Q.B. (a) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]
**With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a bidirectional RF link for a neural prosthesis. Explain the role of each block. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The bidirectional RF link has **three planes** (see diagram in §4.1). Block-by-block description:

1. **External Control Unit:** Source of stimulation commands; receives decoded neural data.
2. **Channel Encoder/Decoder:** Adds error-correction (e.g., Hamming, Reed-Solomon) and CRC.
3. **Modulator/Demodulator (ASK/FSK):** Maps digital bits to analog RF waveforms.
4. **Class-E Power Amplifier:** High-efficiency (> 90%) Tx amplifier for the 13.56 MHz carrier.
5. **Tx/Rx Coils or Antennas:** Performs the electromagnetic coupling across tissue.
6. **Schottky Rectifier + LDO:** RF-DC conversion; produces clean implant supply rail.
7. **Neural Amplifier + ADC:** Amplifies microvolt neural signals (gain ~ 60–80 dB) and digitizes them.
8. **Implant Tx Antenna:** Re-radiates digitized data on a separate carrier (e.g., 403 MHz) to avoid interference with the power link.
9. **External Rx Antenna + Demodulator:** Captures and decodes the uplink.

(Examiner expects all 8–9 blocks named and briefly justified; partial credit for each.)

### Q.B. (b) [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]
**What is Specific Absorption Rate (SAR)? Why is it critical in neural implant design? Compute the peak SAR if a 2 GHz signal produces a peak electric field of 80 V/m in brain tissue with conductivity 1.5 S/m and density 1040 kg/m³. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** SAR quantifies the rate at which RF energy is absorbed per unit mass of tissue:

$$SAR = \frac{\sigma \vert E\vert^2}{2 \rho}$$

**Why critical:** The brain has **no thermal regulation** and is encased in the skull; excessive SAR causes **thermal tissue damage, protein denaturation, and blood-brain barrier disruption**. Regulatory limits are **1.6 W/kg (FCC, 1 g)** and **2.0 W/kg (IEEE, 10 g)**. Designers must show SAR compliance before any clinical trial.

**Computation:**

$$SAR = \frac{1.5 \cdot (80)^2}{2 \cdot 1040} = \frac{1.5 \cdot 6400}{2080} = \frac{9600}{2080}$$

$$SAR \approx 4.615 \;\text{W/kg}$$

**Comment:** This **exceeds the IEEE limit (2.0 W/kg)** by 2.3×, so the device is **not compliant** at this E-field. The designer must either reduce radiated power, increase carrier frequency (shallower penetration), or use **pulsed operation** with low duty cycle (< 10%).

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in RF-Link questions:**
> 1. **Confusing near-field with far-field formulas** — Friis equation CANNOT be used for transcutaneous inductive links; examiners deduct 2–3 marks instantly.
> 2. **Forgetting the $4\pi d^2$ vs $(4\pi d/\lambda)^2$ factor** — this single error changes link budget answers by 30+ dB.
> 3. **Not mentioning in-body path loss (IBPL)** when discussing implant telemetry — credit is reserved for recognizing that **tissue is NOT free space**.
> 4. **Skipping units** in numerical answers (mW vs dBm, Ω vs kΩ) — KTU examiners deduct ½ mark per inconsistency.
> 5. **Ignoring SAR compliance** in any "design a wireless implant" question — SAR is a **mandatory 1–2 mark topic** in any 14-mark question on RF link.

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **RF Link = bidirectional wireless coupling for power + data** between external controller and implant.
- ✅ **13.56 MHz ISM** → near-field inductive TET.
- ✅ **402–405 MHz MICS / MedRadio** → far-field data telemetry.
- ✅ **Friis equation** governs **far-field**; **mutual inductance (M)** governs **near-field**.
- ✅ **Efficiency of 2-coil TET:** $\eta = \dfrac{k^2 Q_1 Q_2}{1 + k^2 Q_1 Q_2}$ (matched load, resonance).
- ✅ **Efficiency is non-linear in $k^2$** — small drops in coupling from depth cause large efficiency losses.
- ✅ **4-coil relay-resonator topology** can recover efficiency for deep implants (used in MIT "neural dust" 2017).
- ✅ **Resonance:** $f_0 = 1/(2\pi\sqrt{LC})$ — must tune **both** Tx and Rx.
- ✅ **Q-factor:** $Q = \omega_0 L / R$ — high Q is essential; use **litz wire** or **lithographic spiral** to reduce skin-effect R.
- ✅ **Impedance matching** with $\Gamma = (Z_L - Z_0)/(Z_L + Z_0)$ is needed for max power transfer.
- ✅ **SAR limit:** **1.6 W/kg (FCC) / 2.0 W/kg (IEEE)** — must show compliance.
- ✅ **Skin depth in tissue at 13.56 MHz ≈ 9 cm** — so sub-skin implants always couple via near-field.
- ✅ **Modulation choices:** **ASK/OOK for downlink (simplicity, low-power Rx); FSK/DBPSK for uplink (noise immunity).**
- ✅ **Coil design rules:** planar spiral (flexible, conformable) for implants; solenoid (high Q) for external.
- ✅ **Coupling coefficient $k$** typically **0.01–0.3** across tissue — design for the worst case.
- ✅ **Link budget** must include Tx power, gains, FSPL/IBPL, mismatch loss, polarization loss, **and** a **≥ 10 dB margin** for safety.
- ✅ **Bidirectional isolation:** use **separate coils for Tx-power and Rx-data**, or use **frequency-division duplex (FDD)** on one coil with a diplexer.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Internal Unit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT753 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS AND IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Internal Unit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Internal Unit (Implanted Unit)**: The fully implantable, hermetically sealed subsystem of a neural prosthetic device that is surgically positioned within the body (typically in a subcutaneous pocket in the chest, abdomen, or skull). It houses the **biocompatible titanium enclosure**, **rechargeable power source**, **pulse-generation / neural recording ASICs**, **telemetry coils**, and **electrode lead interfaces**, and communicates bidirectionally with an external unit through a transcutaneous inductive link.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the internal unit is the **Implanted Pulse Generator (IPG)** in stimulator-based prostheses (e.g., cochlear implant, deep brain stimulator, retinal prosthesis) or the **Implantable Neural Recording System (INRS)** in brain-computer interface (BCI) prostheses.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Submarine Inside Your Body**
> Imagine a **miniature submarine** lowered into a saltwater ocean (the human body). The submarine has:
> - A **titanium hull** (hermetic encapsulation) that prevents saltwater from flooding the electronics.
> - A **periscope** (the electrode lead) that pokes out to "see" or "talk" to the surroundings (nerves/tissue).
> - A **reactor** (battery + power-receiving coil) that fuels it.
> - A **sonar/radio room** (telemetry coil) that talks to a surface ship (the external unit) without ever needing a hatch to open.
> - **Hatches with sealed cables** (feedthroughs) where signal lines pass through the hull.
>
> That submarine is the **Internal Unit**.

### 1.3 Engineering Specifications at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics for a Clinical-Grade Internal Unit**
> - **Outer Diameter:** **30 – 55 mm**
> - **Thickness:** **6 – 10 mm**
> - **Weight:** **20 – 50 g**
> - **Material of Casing:** **Commercially Pure Titanium (Grade 1–4, ASTM F67)**
> - **Hermeticity:** **< 1 × 10⁻⁸ atm·cc/s He** (per MIL-STD-883 Method 1014)
> - **Power Source:** **Rechargeable Li-ion or wirelessly powered**
> - **Operating Frequency for Inductive Link:** **100 kHz – 13.56 MHz**
> - **In-Body Operating Temperature:** **34 °C – 42 °C**
> - **Targeted Lifetime:** **5 – 25 years** depending on battery cycles and hermetic integrity

### 1.4 Functional Block Summary

The internal unit typically performs four core functions inside the body:

1. **Stimulation / Recording** — delivers charge-balanced biphasic current pulses to neural tissue OR amplifies and digitizes extracellular neural signals.
2. **Power Reception / Storage** — receives RF energy via inductive link; stores in a rechargeable cell.
3. **Bidirectional Telemetry** — exchanges command and data packets with the external unit.
4. **Hermetic Protection** — isolates sensitive silicon from ionic body fluids for the lifetime of the device.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Subsystem Architecture (Top-Down Decomposition)

The internal unit is decomposed into **five tightly coupled sub-modules**. Each sub-module's purpose and design driver is explained below.

### A. Hermetic Titanium Enclosure
- **Why:** Body fluid is a corrosive saline electrolyte (≈ **0.9 % NaCl**, 150 mM). Any pinhole in encapsulation causes **ionic ingress → dendrite growth → silicon corrosion → device failure within weeks**.
- **How:** Two halves of Grade-1 titanium are laser-welded after electronics are loaded. Hermeticity is verified via **helium leak test**.
- **Feedthroughs:** Either **glass-to-metal seals** (Al₂O₃ ceramic pins brazed into Ti pins) or **ceramic-to-metal planar feedthrough arrays**.

### B. Power Subsystem
- **Rechargeable Li-ion cell** (~ 30 – 200 mAh) for systems that cannot be powered inductively continuously.
- **Inductive power receiver** (LC tank + Schottky rectifier + LDO regulator) for high-power systems (e.g., retinal implants, BCI).
- **Energy budget example (Cochlear IPG – Nucleus Profile Plus):** ≈ 30 mW average.

### C. Stimulation / Recording ASIC
- **Stimulator ASIC:** Constant-current source (compliance up to ± 15 V), charge-balancing switches, **DAC-driven amplitude control (resolution ≤ 1 µA)**.
- **Recorder ASIC:** Low-noise neural amplifier (input-referred noise **< 5 µVrms**), **16 – 256 channel multiplexer**, **ADC ≥ 10-bit, ≥ 30 kS/s/ch**.

### D. Telemetry Coils
- One coil for **forward telemetry** (commands from external → internal).
- Optionally a **back-telemetry coil** (implanted data, e.g., impedances, neural signals).
- Operating at **RF carrier 100 kHz – 13.56 MHz** to avoid absorption by water and meet **FCC/ICNIRP SAR** limits.

### E. Electrode Lead Assembly
- **Cuff electrode** for peripheral nerve stimulation.
- **Intracranial depth electrodes** (e.g., Utah Array, Michigan Probe) for cortical recording.
- **Intracochlear electrode array** for cochlear implants.
- **Pt-Ir or PEDOT:PSS contacts** for low-impedance chronic stimulation.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table — All Equations You Must Memorize for Internal-Unit Problems**

| # | Equation | Variable Meaning | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $\varepsilon = -N\dfrac{d\Phi_B}{dt} = -N A B \omega \cos(\omega t)$ | Induced EMF in receiver coil | V (volts) | Wireless power transfer to implant |
| 2 | $M = k\sqrt{L_1 L_2}$ | Mutual inductance of primary (ext.) and secondary (int.) coils | H (henries) | Telemetry link design |
| 3 | $k = \dfrac{r_1^2 \, r_2^2 \, N_1 N_2}{\sqrt{(r_1^2 + d^2)^3 (r_2^2 + d^2)^3}}$ | Coupling coefficient between two coaxial coils | dimensionless (0 < k ≤ 1) | Tuning distance between coils |
| 4 | $P_{load} = \dfrac{(\omega M)^2 R_L}{\left(R_1 R_2 + (\omega M)^2\right)^2} V_1^2$ | Power delivered to implant load | W (watts) | Inductive powering efficiency |
| 5 | $Q_{phase} = I_{stim} \cdot t_{phase}$ | Charge per stimulation phase | C (coulombs) | Charge-balanced stimulation |
| 6 | $D = \dfrac{Q_{phase}}{A_{electrode}}$ | Charge density at electrode | C/cm² | Safety of stimulation |
| 7 | $\log(D) + \log(Q) \le k_{Shannon}$ | Shannon's safe-stimulation boundary (k ≈ 1.85) | log(µC/cm²) + log(µC) | Tissue-damage prevention |
| 8 | $I_{leak,max} = \dfrac{V_{DC}}{R_{load}}$ | Allowed DC leakage after charge-balancing | A | Preventing net DC injection |
| 9 | $V_{noise,rms} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | Johnson-Nyquist thermal noise (recording) | V | Neural amplifier input noise |
| 10 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | Recording signal-to-noise ratio | dB | BCI signal quality |
| 11 | $f_{res} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\sqrt{LC}}$ | LC resonance for telemetry | Hz | Coil matching network |
| 12 | $\tau = RC$ | Time constant of electrode-electrolyte interface | s | Stimulation pulse shaping |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Note for Markdown:** All absolute-value bars in the table above have been written as `\vert` or with structured notation to avoid breaking the markdown table parser. Do **not** type raw `|` inside table cells.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cochlear implants** (Cochlear Ltd., MED-EL, Advanced Bionics) — IPG sits behind the ear; ≈ 22 internal stimulation channels.
- **Deep Brain Stimulators (DBS)** (Medtronic Activa, Abbott Infinity, Boston Scientific Vercise) — IPG implanted in the chest; lead routed subcutaneously to the brain.
- **Sacral Neuromodulation** (Medtronic InterStim) — for bladder/bowel control.
- **Vagus Nerve Stimulation (VNS)** (LivaNova) — for epilepsy and depression.
- **Retinal Prostheses (Argus II, PRIMA)** — internal unit powers an epiretinal/subretinal electrode array.
- **Motor BCIs (Neuralink, BrainGate, Synchron)** — fully implanted recorder ASICs sitting in the skull.

The internal unit is therefore the **engineering heart** of every clinical and research-grade neural prosthesis.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> All derivations below are deliberately exhaustive — every algebraic transition is shown. This is the level of working expected from a **full-mark KTU ESE answer script**.

## 3.1 Derivation: Inductive Power Transfer to the Internal Unit

### Setup
A circular primary coil of **N₁ turns**, radius **r₁**, carrying sinusoidal current **I₁(t) = I₀ cos(ωt)**, is placed at a perpendicular distance **d** above the secondary (internal-unit) coil of **N₂ turns**, radius **r₂**.

### Step 1 — Magnetic field on the axis of the primary coil
By the Biot–Savart law, the on-axis B-field of a circular current loop is:

$$
B_z(z) = \frac{\mu_0 N_1 I_1 r_1^2}{2 (r_1^2 + z^2)^{3/2}}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute z = d and I₁ = I₀ cos(ωt)

$$
B_z(d,t) = \frac{\mu_0 N_1 I_0 r_1^2 \cos(\omega t)}{2 (r_1^2 + d^2)^{3/2}}
$$

### Step 3 — Magnetic flux linked with one turn of the secondary coil
Assuming the secondary coil is small enough that the field is uniform across its area A₂ = πr₂²:

$$
\Phi_2 = B_z(d,t) \cdot A_2 = \frac{\mu_0 N_1 I_0 r_1^2 \pi r_2^2 \cos(\omega t)}{2 (r_1^2 + d^2)^{3/2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Induced EMF across the secondary coil (Faraday's Law)

$$
\varepsilon_2 = -N_2 \frac{d\Phi_2}{dt}
$$

Differentiating cos(ωt) gives −ω sin(ωt):

$$
\varepsilon_2 = -N_2 \cdot \frac{\mu_0 N_1 I_0 r_1^2 \pi r_2^2}{2 (r_1^2 + d^2)^{3/2}} \cdot (-\omega \sin(\omega t))
$$

$$
\varepsilon_2(t) = \frac{\mu_0 N_1 N_2 I_0 \omega \, \pi r_1^2 r_2^2}{2 (r_1^2 + d^2)^{3/2}} \sin(\omega t)
$$

### Step 5 — Express via the coupling coefficient k
Mutual inductance **M** between two coaxial coils is:

$$
M = \frac{\mu_0 N_1 N_2 \pi r_1^2 r_2^2}{2 (r_1^2 + d^2)^{3/2}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\;\varepsilon_2(t) = M \, I_0 \, \omega \, \sin(\omega t)\;}
$$

Equivalently in the time-harmonic (phasor) domain:

$$
\tilde{\varepsilon_2} = j \omega M \tilde{I_1}
$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Sanity Check
Let N₁ = N₂ = 10, r₁ = r₂ = 15 mm, d = 10 mm, I₀ = 0.5 A, ω = 2π × 100 kHz.

$$
M = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(10)(10)\pi (0.015)^2 (0.015)^2}{2 ((0.015)^2 + (0.010)^2)^{3/2}}
$$

Numerator:
$$
(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(100)(\pi)(2.25\times 10^{-7}) \approx 1.25 \times 10^{-7} \times 2.25 \times 10^{-7} \approx 8.88 \times 10^{-15}
$$

Denominator:
$$
2 \, (3.25 \times 10^{-4})^{3/2} = 2 \times 1.85 \times 10^{-6} \approx 3.7 \times 10^{-6}
$$

So:
$$
M \approx 2.4 \times 10^{-9}\ \text{H} = 2.4\ \text{nH}
$$

The induced EMF amplitude:
$$
\varepsilon_{2,pk} = M I_0 \omega = (2.4\times 10^{-9})(0.5)(2\pi \times 10^5) \approx 7.5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{V} = 0.75\ \text{mV}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **tuned LC resonance** on the secondary side is essential — multiplying the small received voltage by the loaded Q (often 30 – 80) yields several tens of millivolts, enough to forward-bias a Schottky rectifier and charge the internal battery.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Shannon's Safety Boundary for Stimulation

### Background
McHardy & Shannon (1988) experimentally determined the boundary in the **charge-per-phase (Q) vs. charge-density (D)** plane below which chronic neural stimulation does **not** produce histologically observable neural damage.

### Step 1 — Define charge per phase

$$
Q = I_{stim} \cdot t_{phase} \quad [\mu C]
$$

### Step 2 — Define charge density

$$
D = \frac{Q}{A_{electrode}} \quad [\mu C / cm^2]
$$

### Step 3 — Empirical logarithmic boundary
For safe stimulation with a charge-balanced biphasic pulse and Pt-Ir electrodes, Shannon's equation is:

$$
\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) \le k
$$

where **k ≈ 1.85** is the empirical constant.

### Step 4 — Worked example
A stimulating microelectrode has area **A = 0.01 cm² (≈ 1 mm²)**, and the clinician sets a pulse of **I = 200 µA** for a phase width **t = 100 µs**.

Compute the charge per phase:

$$
Q = (200 \times 10^{-6})(100 \times 10^{-6}) = 2 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{C} = 0.02\ \mu C
$$

Compute the charge density:

$$
D = \frac{0.02\ \mu C}{0.01\ cm^2} = 2\ \mu C / cm^2
$$

Evaluate the safety index:

$$
\log_{10}(2) + \log_{10}(0.02) = 0.301 + (-1.699) = -1.398
$$

Since **−1.398 < 1.85**, the stimulation is comfortably within the safe region. ✅

### Step 5 — Find the maximum allowable I
Set the safety index equal to 1.85 and solve for Q:

$$
\log_{10}(D) + 1.85 = k \Rightarrow \log_{10}(D Q) = 1.85
$$

$$
DQ \le 10^{1.85} \approx 70.8
$$

With D = Q/A:

$$
\frac{Q^2}{A} \le 70.8 \Rightarrow Q_{max} = \sqrt{70.8 \, A}
$$

For A = 0.01 cm²:

$$
Q_{max} = \sqrt{0.708} \approx 0.841\ \mu C
$$

Hence with t = 100 µs:

$$
I_{max} = \frac{Q_{max}}{t} = \frac{0.841\ \mu C}{100\ \mu s} = 8.41\ \text{mA}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always compute the **Shannon safety index** before approving stimulation parameters during device design. Examiners love this derivation.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Johnson–Nyquist Input Noise of the Neural Recording Front-End

### Step 1 — Thermal-noise voltage across a resistance

$$
V_{n,rms} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}
$$

where k_B = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K, T = 310 K (body temperature), Δf is the recording bandwidth.

### Step 2 — Plug in numbers for a typical electrode–tissue interface (R = 5 kΩ, Δf = 10 kHz)

$$
V_{n,rms} = \sqrt{4 (1.38 \times 10^{-23})(310)(5 \times 10^3)(10^4)}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{4 (1.38 \times 10^{-23})(310)(5 \times 10^7)}
$$

$$
= \sqrt{8.55 \times 10^{-13}} \approx 9.25 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{V} = 0.925\ \mu V_{rms}
$$

### Step 3 — Implication for neural recording
Extracellular action potentials (APs) are typically **50 – 500 µV**. With a 0.925 µV noise floor, the **SNR** is:

$$
SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{0.925 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(216) \approx 46.7\ \text{dB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A 46-dB SNR is sufficient for **threshold-based spike sorting**; this is why implant designers push for **< 5 µVrms** input-referred noise.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Two Mermaid diagrams are provided below. The first is a top-level **functional block architecture** of the internal unit; the second is a **subsystem interaction map** showing how power, data, and stimulation signals flow across the hermetic barrier.

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of the Internal Unit

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Titanium Hermetic Enclosure] --> B[Subcutaneous Pocket Implant Site]
    B --> C[Hermetic Feedthrough Array Al2O3 Ceramic]
    C --> D[Stimulation Lead Connector]
    C --> E[Recording Lead Connector]
    D --> F[Stimulator ASIC Current Source]
    E --> G[Neural Amplifier ASIC]
    F --> H[Microcontroller and DSP Core]
```
