| Selective Activation | Fiber diameter-based, Direction-based |
| Closed-loop / Open-loop | Real-time feedback-based neuromodulation |
| Safety Considerations | Charge per phase, charge density, Shannon limits |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tap on a Speaker" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a speaker playing music.** If you tap it gently, nothing happens. If you hit it very hard with a hammer, you might damage the speaker cone. If you tap it rhythmically with the right force, you can make it produce a clean tone. Similarly, an electrical stimulator must "tap" a nerve with just the right amount of electrical charge — not too little (no response), not too much (tissue damage), and in the right pattern (natural-feeling activation).

In this analogy:
- **Tap force = Pulse amplitude (V or mA)**
- **Tap duration = Pulse width (µs)**
- **Rhythm of taps = Stimulation frequency (Hz)**
- **Number of taps per second = Repetition rate**
- **Tap direction = Anodic vs Cathodic phase**

---

## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

The following are the **canonical safety and activation thresholds** a KTU student must memorize:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Numerical Benchmarks (Memorize for ESE):**
> - **Strength–Duration (SD) threshold charge:** typically **0.1 – 1 µC/phase** for peripheral nerve activation
> - **Maximum safe charge per phase:** **< 0.4 µC** (Shannon limit boundary)
> - **Maximum safe charge density:** **< 30 µC/cm² (geometric)** for safe chronic stimulation
> - **Typical neural chronaxie:** **30 – 150 µs** (most fibers)
> - **Typical rheobase:** **0.5 – 5 mA** for cuff electrodes
> - **Therapeutic DBS frequency:** **130 – 185 Hz**
> - **Standard biphasic pulse width:** **60 – 450 µs**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stimulation Waveforms — The Engineering Toolbox

### 2.1.1 Monophasic Pulse

A monophasic pulse delivers current in **one direction only** (cathodic-first or anodic-first). It is **historically used** (e.g., early cardiac pacemakers) but **not safe for chronic neural stimulation** because net DC residue accumulates, causing:

- Electrochemical corrosion of the metal electrode
- pH shift at the electrode–tissue interface (generation of toxic species like H₂O₂)
- Faradaic reactions that damage tissue

### 2.1.2 Biphasic Pulse

A biphasic pulse has **two phases of equal and opposite charge**, ensuring the net DC = 0. This is the **gold standard** for chronic neural stimulation.

**Sub-categories:**

| Type | Description | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Symmetric biphasic** | Both phases identical in amplitude and duration | Laboratory / short-term experiments |
| **Asymmetric biphasic** | One phase smaller but longer; the other sharper | Cortical prosthetics — smaller phase recruits fibers, larger phase reverses electrochemical reaction |
| **Charge-balanced biphasic** | Net charge = 0 per pulse cycle | **Clinical / chronic implants** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why biphasic?**
> The cathodic phase (negative) **depolarizes** the neuron membrane and triggers an action potential. The anodic phase (positive) **repolarizes** the membrane AND reverses electrochemical charge buildup at the electrode. This protects both the electrode (no net DC corrosion) and the tissue (no pH shock).

### 2.1.3 Stimulation Phase Polarity — Cathodic vs Anodic

| Polarity | Effect on Axon |
|---|---|
| **Cathodic (negative electrode near axon)** | Depolarizes membrane at the cathode side → **initiates action potential** (preferred for activation) |
| **Anodic (positive electrode near axon)** | Hyperpolarizes locally but **depolarizes at the anode-side virtual cathode** (used for blocking / virtual electrodes) |

---

## 2.2 Stimulation Parameters — The Five Control Knobs

### (a) Pulse Amplitude (A or V)
- Current-controlled mode: amplitude in **mA** (preferred clinically — independent of tissue impedance changes)
- Voltage-controlled mode: amplitude in **V** (simpler electronics, but impedance drift causes inconsistent charge delivery)

### (b) Pulse Width (PW, in µs)
- Determines how long charge is injected per phase
- Shorter PW → more spatially selective stimulation
- Longer PW → lower threshold but more charge consumption

### (c) Frequency (f, in Hz)
- Low frequency (10–50 Hz): tremor suppression in DBS, sensory encoding
- Medium frequency (50–130 Hz): motor recruitment, FES gait
- High frequency (>130 Hz): therapeutic DBS for Parkinson's, paresthesia-free sensory stimulation

### (d) Inter-Pulse Interval (IPI)
- Time between two consecutive pulses in a train
- Inverse of frequency: $IPI = 1/f$

### (e) Duty Cycle
- Ratio of "ON" stimulation time to total cycle time
- $Duty\ Cycle = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100\%$

---

## 2.3 Electrode Configurations — Spatial Selectivity

### 2.3.1 Monopolar
- One **active electrode** on/in tissue; one large **return (ground) electrode** placed distantly
- Widespread current spread → **broad recruitment**, low selectivity
- Used in deep brain stimulation (DBS) macroelectrodes

### 2.3.2 Bipolar
- Both active and return electrodes close together on the nerve cuff or array
- Confined current path → **sharper recruitment**
- Standard for peripheral nerve cuff stimulation

### 2.3.3 Tripolar
- Three electrodes: central cathode flanked by two anodes
- Most spatially selective → **selective fascicle activation**
- Used in intraneural electrodes (e.g., TIME, LIFE electrodes)

### 2.3.4 Current Steering (Steered Virtual Cathode)
- Multiple electrodes with weighted currents create a **virtual cathode** between physical electrodes
- Allows fine spatial control without physically moving electrodes
- Used in cochlear implants and retinal prostheses

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Stimulation Theory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print/remember this table for the ESE exam. The `|...|` notation has been intentionally avoided to preserve markdown table integrity.**

| Formula | Meaning | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| $Q = I \times PW$ | Charge per phase (Coulombs) | Threshold determination |
| $D = Q / A$ | Charge density (C/cm²) | Safety limit < 30 µC/cm² |
| $V_{thr} = V_{rh} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\tau_{ch}}{PW}\right)$ | **Weiss–Lapicque strength–duration equation** | Threshold vs PW |
| $\tau_{ch}$ | Chronaxie — PW at 2× rheobase | Fiber-type selectivity |
| $k = \log\left(\frac{Q}{Q_{thr}}\right)$ | Shannon's k-value for safe stimulation | $k < 1.85$ is safe |
| $IPI = \frac{1}{f}$ | Inter-pulse interval | Pulse train design |
| $f_{tetanic} = \frac{1}{T_{twitch}}$ | Fusion frequency (smooth tetanus) | FES muscle stimulation (~30 Hz) |
| $\lambda_{axon} \propto \sqrt{d_{axon}}$ | Length constant — larger fibers recruited first | Recruitment order |

---

## 2.5 Strength–Duration Curve — The Core Concept

The **strength–duration (S–D) curve** describes the inverse relationship between stimulus amplitude and pulse width needed to reach the activation threshold. It is governed by the **Weiss equation**:

$$I_{thr}(PW) = I_{rh}\left(1 + \frac{\tau_{ch}}{PW}\right)$$

Where:
- $I_{thr}$ = threshold current (mA)
- $I_{rh}$ = rheobase — the minimum current at infinite pulse width
- $\tau_{ch}$ = chronaxie — pulse width at $2 \times I_{rh}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight (Why Chronaxie Matters for Selectivity):**
> Larger-diameter axons have **shorter chronaxies** (often 30–70 µs) than smaller fibers (200+ µs). By tuning PW to a fiber's chronaxie, an engineer can preferentially recruit specific nerve populations — a key concept for **natural-feeling prosthetic control** and **selective muscle activation**.

---

## 2.6 Closed-Loop vs Open-Loop Stimulation

| Mode | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Open-loop** | Pre-programmed stimulation, no feedback | Conventional cardiac pacemaker |
| **Closed-loop** | Stimulation adjusted by neural/physiological feedback signal | Responsive DBS for Parkinson's (detects beta-band LFP) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Closed-loop systems reduce total charge delivered (better safety), adapt to patient state (better efficacy), and minimize side effects — a major trend in modern neuromodulation implants (NeuroPace RNS, Medtronic Percept PC).

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Dominant Strategy |
|---|---|
| Cochlear implant | Biphasic current pulses, monopolar, ~1000 Hz interleaved across channels |
| Retinal prosthesis | Biphasic sub-threshold + suprathreshold, frequency ≥ 20 Hz for phosphene persistence |
| Deep Brain Stimulation | Symmetric biphasic voltage, 130–185 Hz, monopolar |
| Functional Electrical Stimulation (FES) | Current-controlled biphasic, 20–50 Hz, asymmetric to favor cathodic recruitment |
| Cortical Visual Prosthesis | Asymmetric biphasic, high charge injection (IrO₂ / TiN coatings) |
| Vagal Nerve Stimulation | Biphasic, 20–30 Hz, low charge (< 0.5 mC/cm²) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Strength–Duration (Weiss–Lapicque) Equation

We start from the **cable equation** for a passive nerve fiber membrane modeled as an RC circuit.

### Step 1 — Membrane as RC Circuit

The nerve membrane behaves like a parallel combination of:
- Membrane capacitance $C_m$
- Membrane resistance $R_m$

A current pulse $I$ applied across the membrane will charge this RC combination to a voltage $V_m$.

### Step 2 — Capacitor Charging Equation

For a constant current step applied at $t = 0$, the membrane voltage rises according to:

$$V_m(t) = I \cdot R_m \cdot \left(1 - e^{-t / \tau_m}\right)$$

where $\tau_m = R_m C_m$ is the membrane time constant.

### Step 3 — Apply the Threshold Condition

For an action potential to be triggered, $V_m$ must reach the threshold voltage $V_{thr}$:

$$V_{thr} = I \cdot R_m \cdot \left(1 - e^{-PW / \tau_m}\right)$$

Solving for the required current $I = I_{thr}$:

$$I_{thr} = \frac{V_{thr}}{R_m \cdot \left(1 - e^{-PW / \tau_m}\right)}$$

### Step 4 — Expand the Exponential (Taylor Series)

For large $PW \gg \tau_m$, the exponential term $e^{-PW/\tau_m}$ vanishes, leaving:

$$I_{thr}(\infty) = \frac{V_{thr}}{R_m} \equiv I_{rh}$$

This is the **rheobase** — the asymptotic minimum current.

### Step 5 — Define Chronaxie

Chronaxie $\tau_{ch}$ is defined as the pulse width at which the threshold current equals **twice** the rheobase, i.e., $I_{thr} = 2 I_{rh}$.

Substituting into Step 3:

$$2 I_{rh} = \frac{V_{thr}}{R_m \cdot \left(1 - e^{-\tau_{ch}/\tau_m}\right)}$$

Since $I_{rh} = V_{thr}/R_m$, we simplify:

$$2 = \frac{1}{1 - e^{-\tau_{ch}/\tau_m}} \implies 1 - e^{-\tau_{ch}/\tau_m} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Solving:

$$e^{-\tau_{ch}/\tau_m} = \frac{1}{2} \implies \tau_{ch} = \tau_m \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \tau_m$$

### Step 6 — Final Weiss–Lapicque Equation

Substituting Step 4 into Step 3 and rearranging yields the **canonical Weiss equation**:

$$I_{thr}(PW) = I_{rh}\left(1 + \frac{\tau_{ch}}{PW}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the single most-tested formula in Module 2 of PEEBT753. Be ready to derive it AND plot it.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Charge Calculation

**Problem:** A stimulator delivers biphasic, charge-balanced current pulses. Each cathodic phase has amplitude $I = 2.5\ \text{mA}$ and pulse width $PW = 200\ \mu\text{s}$. The electrode surface area is $A = 0.05\ \text{cm}^2$.

**Find:**
1. Charge per phase
2. Charge density
3. Determine if within Shannon safety limit

### Solution:

**Step 1 — Charge per phase:**

$$Q = I \times PW = (2.5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A}) \times (200 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s})$$

$$Q = 5.0 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{C} = 0.5\ \mu\text{C}$$

**Step 2 — Charge density:**

$$D = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^{-7}}{0.05} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{C/cm}^2 = 10\ \mu\text{C/cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Shannon's k-value safety check:**

Shannon's limit defines:

$$\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) < 1.85$$

$$\log_{10}(10 \times 10^{-6}) + \log_{10}(0.5 \times 10^{-6}) = -5 + (-5.301) = -10.301$$

Since $-10.301 < 1.85$, the stimulation is **well within the safe operating envelope** — confirmed safe.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Shannon's equation is **logarithmic** in both charge AND charge density, which makes it **massively conservative**. Always cite both parameters in your ESE answers.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Stimulation Strategies

| Strategy | Pros | Cons | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Monophasic cathodic** | Simple, lowest threshold | Tissue damage, electrode corrosion | Acute lab experiments only |
| **Symmetric biphasic** | Perfect charge balance, simple | Higher threshold than cathodic alone | Most clinical implants |
| **Asymmetric biphasic** | Lower cathodic threshold + safe reversal | Complex timing circuitry | Cortical microstimulation |
| **Monopolar electrode** | Simple surgical lead | Low selectivity | DBS macroelectrodes |
| **Bipolar electrode** | Better selectivity | Limited to nearby tissue | Peripheral nerve cuffs |
| **Tripolar electrode** | High selectivity | Complex fabrication | Intraneural interfaces |
| **Current steering** | Tunable virtual electrode | Calibration drift, complex control | Cochlear/retinal implants |
| **Open-loop stimulation** | Reliable, simple | Battery drain, side effects | Conventional pacemakers |
| **Closed-loop stimulation** | Adaptive, efficient, fewer side effects | Sensor/algorithm overhead | Responsive DBS, smart prosthetics |

---

## 3.4 Derivative: Recruitment Order of Nerve Fibers

The order in which axons are recruited during stimulation is **size-dependent** (in healthy nerves). For a uniform external field, larger diameter fibers have:

- Larger membrane area
- Lower internal resistance
- Higher excitability

Hence the recruitment order is:

$$\text{A}\alpha\ (\text{large motor}) \;\rightarrow\; \text{A}\beta\ (\text{touch}) \;\rightarrow\; \text{A}\delta\ (\text{pain}) \;\rightarrow\; \text{C}\ (\text{pain, autonomic})$$

**Engineering implication:** Natural physiological recruitment follows the **opposite order** — small motor units first. This is why simple electrical stimulation produces **"reversed recruitment"**, which feels less natural than voluntary motion — a central engineering problem in FES and prosthetic control.

> [!NOTE]
> Selective stimulation can correct this by exploiting the **chronaxie difference**: short PW (≤ 50 µs) preferentially recruits smaller fibers because their shorter chronaxie matches better.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Stimulation Strategy Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Clinical Goal Defined] --> B{Chronic or Acute Use?}
    B -- Acute Lab --> C[Monophasic Allowed]
    B -- Chronic Implant --> D[Must Use Charge-Balanced Biphasic]
    D --> E{Tissue Type?}
    E -- Peripheral Nerve --> F[Bipolar Cuff or Tripolar]
    E -- Cortex --> G[Microelectrode Array with Asymmetric Biphasic]
    E -- Deep Brain --> H[Monopolar DBS Macroelectrode]
    E -- Retina --> I[Current Steering via Multielectrode Array]
    F --> J{Selectivity Needed?}
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J -- Yes --> K[Tune PW Near Target Chronaxie]
    J -- No --> L[Use Standard Parameters PW 200us f 50Hz]
    K --> M{Adaptive Response Needed?}
    L --> M
    M -- Yes --> N[Closed-Loop with Neural Feedback]
    M -- No --> O[Open-Loop Pre-Programmed]
    N --> P[Verify Shannon Limit k less than 1.85]
    O --> P
    P --> Q[Deploy Stimulator with Safety Watchdog]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stimulation Subsystem Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Power[Battery or Wireless Power Link] --> Driver
    Driver[Current or Voltage Driver] --> Switch
    Switch[Analog Switching Matrix H-Bridge] --> ElectrodeA[Electrode A]
    Switch --> ElectrodeB[Electrode B]
    MicroCtrl[Microcontroller FPGA] --> Driver
    MicroCtrl --> Switch
    Sensor[Neural Sensor LFP or ENG] --> MicroCtrl
    MicroCtrl --> Comm[Wireless Telemetry Coil]
    Comm --> External[External Programmer Clinician]
    ElectrodeA --> Tissue[Excitable Neural Tissue]
    ElectrodeB --> Tissue
    Tissue -.Return Signal.-> Sensor
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Stimulation Modes

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> Stimulating: Trigger Event
    Stimulating --> Idle: Pulse Train Complete
    Stimulating --> Safety_Abort: Charge Imbalance Detected
    Stimulating --> Safety_Abort: Impedance Out of Range
    Safety_Abort --> Alarm: Disable Output
    Alarm --> Idle: Manual Reset by Clinician
    Stimulating --> Adaptive_Mode: Closed Loop Enabled
    Adaptive_Mode --> Stimulating: Adjusted Parameters
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Strength–Duration Curve Conceptual Plot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X[PW axis] --> X1[Short PW 30us High Threshold]
    X --> X2[Medium PW 100us]
    X --> X3[Long PW 500us]
    X --> X4[Infinity Rheobase]
    Y[Threshold Current axis] --> Y1[Very High]
    Y --> Y2[Moderate]
    Y --> Y3[Low]
    Y --> Y4[Lowest Constant]
    X1 --- Y1
    X2 --- Y2
    X3 --- Y3
    X4 --- Y4
```

> [!NOTE]
> In the actual exam, draw the **S-D curve** with **rheobase** as the horizontal asymptote and **chronaxie** marked on the PW axis at the height of $2 \times I_{rh}$. This is a frequently requested 3-mark question in Part A.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — 3-Mark Questions

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`
**Define charge-balanced biphasic stimulation and explain why it is preferred for chronic neural implants.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **charge-balanced biphasic pulse** delivers a cathodic (negative) phase followed by an anodic (positive) phase of **equal and opposite charge**, ensuring net DC current per pulse cycle equals zero.

It is preferred for chronic implants because:

1. **Electrochemical safety:** Zero net DC prevents electrode corrosion and dissolution of metal ions (Pt, Ir) into surrounding tissue.
2. **Tissue safety:** Prevents pH shifts and toxic electrochemical byproducts (H₂O₂, ClO⁻) at the electrode-tissue interface.
3. **Long-term stability:** Maintains stable electrode impedance over years of operation, critical for devices like cochlear implants and DBS that operate continuously.

(Mention the cathodic phase as the **depolarizing** phase for neural recruitment and the anodic phase for **charge reversal** — full credit.)

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`
**What is chronaxie? How does it differ from rheobase?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Rheobase ($I_{rh}$):** The minimum stimulus amplitude required to excite a nerve when an infinitely long pulse width is applied. It is the **asymptotic minimum** of the strength–duration curve.
- **Chronaxie ($\tau_{ch}$):** The pulse width at which the threshold current is **exactly twice the rheobase**. It is a direct measure of the nerve's **membrane time constant**.

$$\tau_{ch} = \tau_m \cdot \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \cdot \tau_m$$

**Key difference:** Rheobase is a *current* value, while chronaxie is a *time* value. Chronaxie is **fiber-type specific** — making it the preferred metric for designing **selective stimulation** strategies.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the various components of a **closed-loop neural stimulation system**. Discuss how feedback signals such as **Local Field Potentials (LFPs)** or **Evoked Compound Action Potentials (ECAPs)** are used to adapt stimulation parameters in real time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stimulator delivers symmetric biphasic pulses with amplitude $I = 3\ \text{mA}$, pulse width $PW = 300\ \mu\text{s}$, frequency $f = 50\ \text{Hz}$. The electrode geometric surface area is $0.04\ \text{cm}^2$. Calculate:
1. Charge per phase
2. Charge density per phase
3. Total charge delivered per second
4. Verify whether stimulation is within Shannon's safe limit ($k < 1.85$). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):** Draw the architecture (refer to Section 4.2):

```
[Sensor — LFP/ECAP] → [Amplifier + Filter] → [ADC] → 
[Microcontroller — DSP] → [Decision Algorithm] → 
[DAC + Current Driver + H-Bridge Switch] → [Electrode] → [Tissue]
```

**Explanation (4 marks):**

1. **Sensing block:** Picks up neural signals (LFPs in DBS at 13–30 Hz beta band; ECAPs in cochlear implant via reverse telemetry).
2. **Processing block:** Extracts features (e.g., beta-band power, ECAP amplitude) and compares to a programmed threshold.
3. **Adaptive decision:** If feature exceeds threshold → trigger stimulation; if below → suppress.
4. **Stimulation output:** Adjusts amplitude, PW, or frequency based on need.

**Real-world examples:** Medtronic Percept PC (DBS), NeuroPace RNS (epilepsy), closed-loop cochlear implants.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**1. Charge per phase:**

$$Q = I \times PW = (3 \times 10^{-3}) \times (300 \times 10^{-6}) = 9 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{C} = 0.9\ \mu\text{C}$$

**2. Charge density per phase:**

$$D = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{9 \times 10^{-7}}{0.04} = 2.25 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{C/cm}^2 = 22.5\ \mu\text{C/cm}^2$$

**3. Total charge per second:**

Since 50 pulses are delivered per second, and each pulse has one cathodic and one anodic phase (both 0.9 µC):

$$Q_{sec} = 2 \times 0.9\ \mu\text{C} \times 50 = 90\ \mu\text{C/s}$$

**4. Shannon's safety verification:**

$$\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) = \log_{10}(22.5 \times 10^{-6}) + \log_{10}(0.9 \times 10^{-6})$$

$$= -4.648 + (-6.046) = -10.694$$

Since $-10.694 < 1.85$, the stimulation is **safe**.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare and contrast **monopolar, bipolar, and tripolar electrode configurations** for neural stimulation. Discuss their effects on spatial selectivity, current spread, and recruitment. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Weiss–Lapicque strength–duration equation** starting from the passive membrane RC model. Explain how the chronaxie of a nerve fiber determines the choice of pulse width for **selective stimulation**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Comparison table (5 marks):**

| Aspect | Monopolar | Bipolar | Tripolar |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Electrode count** | 1 active + 1 distant return | 2 close electrodes | 3 (center cathode + 2 flanking anodes) |
| **Current path** | Wide spread | Confined between two electrodes | Highly localized at center |
| **Selectivity** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Current threshold** | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| **Use case** | DBS macroelectrodes | Peripheral nerve cuffs | Intraneural, fascicle-selective |
| **Example device** | Medtronic Activa DBS | Cuff electrodes (e.g., cuff around vagus) | TIME/LIFE electrodes (Micera lab) |

**Discussion (2 marks):**
- Monopolar activates a broad volume — useful when the entire nerve or nucleus must be engaged.
- Bipolar confines current — preferred for cuff-type peripheral nerve interfaces to avoid stimulating adjacent muscles.
- Tripolar offers the sharpest recruitment contour by using two flanking anodes to "focus" the cathodic field in the middle — ideal when individual fascicles within a nerve must be activated independently (e.g., for natural-feeling hand prosthesis control).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Derivation (5 marks):** Refer to Section 3.1 above for the full derivation. The student must show:

1. Membrane as RC circuit → voltage response $V_m(t) = I R_m (1 - e^{-t/\tau_m})$
2. Apply threshold condition $V_m = V_{thr}$
3. Solve for $I_{thr}$ as a function of PW
4. Define rheobase in the $PW \to \infty$ limit
5. Define chronaxie at $I_{thr} = 2 I_{rh}$
6. Arrive at the canonical Weiss equation:

$$I_{thr}(PW) = I_{rh}\left(1 + \frac{\tau_{ch}}{PW}\right)$$

**Selective stimulation explanation (2 marks):**
- Different fiber diameters have different chronaxies.
- **Large motor fibers:** chronaxie ≈ 30–70 µs
- **Small sensory fibers:** chronaxie ≈ 100–300 µs
- By choosing a pulse width **close to a target fiber's chronaxie**, we can selectively recruit that population while sparing others.
- For example, using short PW (50 µs) preferentially recruits motor fibers in FES applications → mimics natural motor unit recruitment order.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common marking deductions in this topic:**
> 1. **Confusing chronaxie with time constant τ** — chronaxie = 0.693·τ, not τ itself. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this.
> 2. **Forgetting to specify the unit of charge density** — always write µC/cm², not just C/cm².
> 3. **Drawing the S-D curve incorrectly** — the curve approaches rheobase asymptotically; many students incorrectly show it hitting zero on the y-axis.
> 4. **Mixing up cathodic and anodic effects** — examiners want the explicit statement that **cathodic stimulation depolarizes and initiates AP**, anodic hyperpolarizes at the anode but creates virtual cathodes laterally.
> 5. **Skipping the derivation steps** — KTU values **stepwise algebra** (1 mark per correct step). Always show all 5–6 steps in Weiss equation derivation.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-density revision checklist for "Stimulation Strategies":**

- **Waveforms:** Monophasic (acute only) → Biphasic charge-balanced (clinical gold standard). Cathodic phase depolarizes, anodic phase reverses charge.
- **Polarity:** Cathode = depolarization (recruitment); Anode = hyperpolarization locally + virtual cathode laterally (used for current steering and anodal block).
- **Parameters:** Amplitude (mA) + PW (µs) + Frequency (Hz) + IPI + Duty Cycle.
- **Strength–Duration:** Weiss equation $I_{thr} = I_{rh}(1 + \tau_{ch}/PW)$. Rheobase = current at infinite PW. Chronaxie = PW at 2× rheobase.
- **Electrode configurations:** Monopolar (broad) → Bipolar (focused) → Tripolar (highly selective) → Current steering (virtual electrode).
- **Safety limits:** Charge per phase < 0.4 µC; Charge density < 30 µC/cm²; Shannon $k < 1.85$.
- **Modes:** Open-loop (fixed) vs Closed-loop (responsive feedback — e.g., beta-band LFP in PD).
- **Recruitment order:** Electrical recruitment is **reversed** vs natural (large fibers first). Selective PW tuning can correct this.
- **FES frequency:** 20–50 Hz produces smooth tetanic contraction (fusion frequency).
- **DBS frequency:** 130–185 Hz for symptomatic relief; low-frequency (60 Hz) can worsen tremor.
- **Coatings and materials:** IrO₂, TiN, PEDOT enable higher safe charge injection for chronic stimulation.
- **Remember to mention** *both* charge AND charge density in safety arguments — never just one.
- **Always end an ESE answer** with a real-world device example (Medtronic, Cochlear Ltd., Second Sight, Neuralink) to score the "application" marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Myoelectric Signal - Acquisition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT753 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS AND IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Myoelectric Signal - Acquisition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Myoelectric Signal (MES) / Electromyogram (EMG)** — A *biopotential* generated by the depolarization of skeletal muscle fibers during voluntary or electrically evoked contraction. When measured from the body surface, it is termed **Surface EMG (sEMG)**, whereas when recorded using needle/wire electrodes inserted into the muscle, it is termed **Intramuscular EMG (iEMG or needle EMG)**.

The myoelectric signal is the **summed electrical activity of numerous motor unit action potentials (MUAPs)** recorded within the pickup volume of the recording electrode. It is the **primary control input** for myoelectric prosthetics, powered orthoses, and human–machine interfaces.

### 1.2 Key Quantitative Parameters (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Myoelectric Signal Characteristics:**
> - **Amplitude range:** $\mathbf{50\ \mu V \ to\ 5\ mV}$ (peak-to-peak, surface recording)
> - **Frequency band of interest:** $\mathbf{20\ Hz\ to\ 500\ Hz}$ (dominant energy: $\mathbf{50–150\ Hz}$)
> - **Recommended sampling frequency:** $\mathbf{1\ kHz\ to\ 4\ kHz}$ (≥ 2× upper cutoff, per Nyquist)
> - **Common-mode voltage at skin:** up to $\pm 300\ mV$ (DC offset)
> - **Skin–electrode contact impedance:** $\mathbf{1\ k\Omega\ to\ 100\ k\Omega}$ (at 50 Hz)

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Listening to the Muscles Whisper"

Imagine you are standing outside a packed cricket stadium. Thousands of fans are cheering. You cannot hear any single voice, but you can clearly feel the *collective roar*. A myoelectric electrode is exactly that: it cannot "hear" a single motor neuron firing, but it captures the *collective electrical roar* of hundreds of muscle fibers contracting beneath it.

- The **stadium** = the muscle
- The **fans** = motor units (each a single nerve + the muscle fibers it innervates)
- Your **ear at the gate** = the recording electrode
- The **collective roar** = the raw EMG waveform

The louder (stronger) the contraction, the more "fans" cheer at once, increasing the **amplitude** of the signal. A **faster fatigue** or **finer motor control** shifts the spectrum toward higher **frequencies**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for viva:** EMG amplitude $\uparrow$ with **force**; EMG median frequency $\downarrow$ with **muscle fatigue** (spectral compression).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Myoelectric Acquisition Chain — Step-by-Step Logic

A clinically/engineering-acceptable myoelectric signal cannot be obtained with just an electrode and an oscilloscope. The raw signal is buried in noise, has tiny amplitude, and rides on a large DC offset. The acquisition chain systematically *rescues* it.

| Step | Block | Purpose | KTU-Standard Value |
|------|-------|---------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Electrode** | Transduces ionic current → electron current | Ag/AgCl preferred (polarization < 1 mV) |
| 2 | **Isolation / Patient Protection** | Galvanic isolation per IEC 60601 | Breakdown > 4000 V RMS |
| 3 | **Instrumentation Pre-amp (IA)** | Differential gain with high CMRR | CMRR > 90 dB, Gain 5–10 |
| 4 | **High-Pass Filter (HPF)** | Removes DC offset, motion artifact | $f_c = 10\text{–}20\ Hz$ |
| 5 | **Main Amplifier** | Brings signal to ADC range | Total gain 500–2000 |
| 6 | **Low-Pass Filter (LPF)** | Anti-aliasing + high-freq noise removal | $f_c = 450\text{–}500\ Hz$ |
| 7 | **Notch Filter** | 50/60 Hz power-line interference | Q = 5–10, narrow band |
| 8 | **ADC** | Converts analog → digital | 12–16 bit, $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why differential recording?** A single-ended electrode picks up the *sum* of wanted signal + huge common-mode noise (50 Hz mains hum, body potential). A differential pair *subtracts* what is common to both, leaving only the local bioelectric event.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities below are **board-exam favourites**. Master the unit conversions and the dB forms.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Differential amplifier gain (single op-amp) | $G_d = \dfrac{R_f}{R_i}$ | dimensionless | For IA use $G = 1 + \dfrac{2R_f}{R_g}$ |
| 2 | Instrumentation amplifier gain | $G_{IA} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_1}{R_g}\right)\dfrac{R_3}{R_2}$ | dimensionless | Symmetric layout = high CMRR |
| 3 | CMRR (ratio) | $\text{CMRR} = \dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}$ | unitless | Always > 10⁴ in EMG |
| 4 | CMRR in decibels | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | dB | KTU requires > **80 dB** |
| 5 | Common-mode rejection (voltage) | $V_{cm,out} = \dfrac{V_{cm,in}}{\text{CMRR}}$ | V | If $V_{cm}=1$ V, CMRR=10⁴ → 100 µV survives |
| 6 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | $\text{SNR} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_s}{V_n}\right)$ | dB | Minimum acceptable: **20 dB** |
| 7 | Noise figure | $NF = \text{SNR}_{in,dB} - \text{SNR}_{out,dB}$ | dB | < 4 dB is excellent for EMG pre-amp |
| 8 | Nyquist sampling criterion | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Hz | For EMG: $f_s \geq 1\ kHz$ (typically 2 kHz) |
| 9 | Anti-alias LPF cutoff (practical) | $f_c \leq 0.4\, f_s$ | Hz | Guard band for roll-off |
| 10 | RMS noise of resistor (Johnson) | $V_{n,RMS} = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | V | Drives pre-amp input noise design |
| 11 | Total thermal noise of cascaded stages | $V_{n,total}^2 = V_{n1}^2 + \dfrac{V_{n2}^2}{G_1^2} + \dfrac{V_{n3}^2}{(G_1 G_2)^2}$ | V² | Front-end dominates |
| 12 | Skin–electrode half-cell potential | $E_{hc} \approx 0.22\ V$ (Ag/AgCl) | V | DC offset to be HPF-filtered |
| 13 | Power-line interference estimate | $V_{PLI} = \dfrac{V_{cm}}{\text{CMRR}}$ | V | Drives notch filter requirement |
| 14 | ADC dynamic range | $DR_{dB} = 6.02\,N + 1.76$ | dB | N = bits; 12-bit ≈ 74 dB |
| 15 | Input impedance requirement | $Z_{in} \geq 100 \times Z_{electrode}$ | Ω | Minimum $10^{10}\ \Omega$ for sEMG |

### 2.3 Why "Why & How" of Each Stage

- **Isolation first, signal processing later:** Patient safety is non-negotiable; a fault on the "mains side" of the isolated barrier must never reach the body. Optical or transformer isolation is the KTU-recommended standard.
- **High CMRR differential first stage:** Eliminates the dominant 50 Hz mains hum *before* it can be amplified.
- **High-pass filter after pre-amp, not before:** Placing an HPF directly at the electrode would load the high-impedance source and inject filter noise into the wanted band.
- **Notch filter last in the analog chain:** Digital notch filters (after ADC) are sharper and free of component drift, but a 50 Hz analog notch is still preferred in KTU answers for real-time prosthetic controllers.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

EMG acquisition is the **front-end of every modern prosthetic arm** (e.g., Ottobock bebionic, DEKA LUKE, Coapt pattern-recognition controllers). It is also central to:
- **Exoskeletons** for gait assistance (sEMG from quadriceps/tibialis)
- **Rehabilitation robots** for stroke patients
- **Silent speech recognition** (facial EMG)
- **HCI** — gesture-based computer control
- **Sports biomechanics** — muscle activation timing studies
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Output Voltage of a Three-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier

The instrumentation amplifier (IA) is the *workhorse* of every EMG front-end. We will derive its closed-form output.

**Topology (mental picture):**
- Two input op-amps (A1, A2) form the input buffer–gain stage.
- A subtractor op-amp (A3) rejects the common-mode and produces single-ended output.
- A single resistor $R_g$ sets the differential gain.

**Step 1 — KVL around the $R_g$ branch (input stage):**

Because the two input op-amps are in negative feedback with their non-inverting inputs held at $V_1$ and $V_2$ respectively, the inverting inputs follow the same voltages: $V^-_{A1} = V_1$ and $V^-_{A2} = V_2$. The voltage across $R_g$ is therefore $V_1 - V_2$. The current through $R_g$ is:

$$
I_{R_g} = \frac{V_1 - V_2}{R_g}
$$

Since no current enters the op-amp inputs, the same current flows through both feedback resistors $R_f$ of the input stage.

**Step 2 — Output voltages of the input op-amps:**

The voltage at the inverting input of A1 is $V_1$. The drop across its feedback resistor $R_f$ is $I_{R_g}\cdot R_f$, appearing between the inverting input and the output of A1. So:

$$
V_{o1} = V_1 + I_{R_g}\,R_f = V_1 + \frac{(V_1 - V_2)\,R_f}{R_g}
$$

Similarly, for A2, the current direction reverses through its feedback resistor (because $V_2 < V_1$ in the assumed case):

$$
V_{o2} = V_2 - I_{R_g}\,R_f = V_2 - \frac{(V_1 - V_2)\,R_f}{R_g}
$$

**Step 3 — Differential swing of the input stage:**

Subtracting:

$$
V_{o1} - V_{o2} = (V_1 - V_2) + \frac{2 R_f\,(V_1 - V_2)}{R_g}
$$

$$
V_{o1} - V_{o2} = (V_1 - V_2)\!\left(1 + \frac{2 R_f}{R_g}\right)
$$

**Step 4 — Subtractor stage gain:**

For the classic difference amplifier (A3) with all four resistors equal to $R$:

$$
G_3 = \frac{R}{R} = 1
$$

So the output is simply:

$$
V_{out} = (V_{o1} - V_{o2})\cdot G_3
$$

**Step 5 — Final closed-form result:**

$$
\boxed{\,V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2 R_f}{R_g}\right)(V_1 - V_2)\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU examiner tip:** When asked to "derive" the IA gain, **always** show that the gain is set by a *single* resistor $R_g$. This is the single most important engineering advantage of the IA — the user can vary the gain without breaking the high CMRR symmetry.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Required CMRR to Limit Power-Line Interference

**Problem setup:** The body is at a common-mode potential of $V_{cm} = 1\ \mathrm{V}$ at 50 Hz (mains pickup). The wanted EMG differential signal is $V_{diff} = 500\ \mu V$. We require that the residual common-mode output be no more than 5% of the wanted signal.

**Step 1 — Maximum tolerable common-mode output:**

$$
V_{cm,out,max} = 0.05 \times V_{diff} = 0.05 \times 500\,\mu V = 25\,\mu V
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for required CMRR (ratio form):**

$$
\text{CMRR} = \frac{V_{cm,in}}{V_{cm,out,max}} = \frac{1\ \mathrm{V}}{25\,\mu V} = \frac{1}{25\times 10^{-6}} = 40{,}000
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to decibels:**

$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(40{,}000)
$$

$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \times ( \log_{10} 4 + 4 ) = 20 \times (0.602 + 4) = 20 \times 4.602
$$

$$
\boxed{\,\text{CMRR}_{dB} \approx 92.04\ \mathrm{dB}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A common-mode rejection ratio of at least **92 dB** is mandatory for a 1 V body-potential pickup to remain below 5% of a 500 µV EMG signal. KTU question-bank problems frequently set this as the target CMRR.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Sampling Frequency and Anti-Alias Filter Design

**Given:** EMG bandwidth $B = 20\ \mathrm{Hz\ to\ }500\ \mathrm{Hz}$, available ADC with maximum $f_s = 2\ \mathrm{kHz}$.

**Step 1 — Nyquist minimum:**

$$
f_{s,min} = 2 f_{max} = 2 \times 500 = 1000\ \mathrm{Hz}
$$

Since 2 kHz ≥ 1 kHz, Nyquist is satisfied.

**Step 2 — Practical anti-alias cutoff (with 25% guard band):**

$$
f_c = 0.4 \times f_s = 0.4 \times 2000 = 800\ \mathrm{Hz}
$$

> Hmm — this exceeds the wanted 500 Hz. Therefore we must **either** reduce the sampling rate to 1.25 kHz or accept a 4th-order Butterworth with a sharper roll-off.

**Step 3 — Recompute for sharper roll-off (Butterworth, 4th order, –80 dB/decade):**

Attenuation required at $f_s/2 = 1000\ \mathrm{Hz}$ to bring 500 Hz signal noise below ADC LSB (12-bit, ±5 V → 2.44 mV LSB):

$$
\text{Atten}_{req} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{V_{noise}}{V_{LSB}}\right) = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.5}{2.44\times 10^{-3}}\right) \approx 60.2\ \mathrm{dB}
$$

A 2nd-order Butterworth gives –40 dB/decade, which is insufficient. A **4th-order Sallen–Key Butterworth** gives –80 dB/decade, comfortably meeting the spec.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Electrode Trade-off

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU comparison table** for the three electrode families. Memorise this — it appears in nearly every question paper.

| Property | Surface (Ag/AgCl) | Needle (concentric) | Fine-wire (intramuscular) |
|----------|-------------------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| Invasiveness | Non-invasive | Invasive (single fiber) | Minimally invasive |
| Pickup volume | Large (many MUs) | Small (few MUs) | Smallest (single MU) |
| Amplitude | 50 µV – 5 mV | 100 µV – 2 mV | 50 µV – 1 mV |
| Bandwidth | 20–500 Hz | 20–1500 Hz | 20–5000 Hz |
| Motion artifact | High | Low | Very low |
| Pain / risk | None | Moderate pain, infection risk | Mild discomfort |
| Use in prosthetics | **Standard** | Research only | Research only |
| Cross-talk | High (adjacent muscles) | Low | Very low |

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Noise Sources & Mitigation

| Noise Source | Frequency | Amplitude | Mitigation Technique |
|--------------|-----------|-----------|----------------------|
| Power-line interference (50/60 Hz) | 50/60 Hz | Up to 1 V cm-mode | High CMRR amp + notch filter |
| Motion artifact | 0–20 Hz | Up to tens of mV | HPF @ 20 Hz, skin prep |
| Thermal (Johnson) noise of skin | White | $\sim 1\,\mu V$ RMS | Low-noise pre-amp, short cables |
| Shot noise of electrode | White | $\propto \sqrt{I_{DC}}$ | Ag/AgCl electrodes (low polarization) |
| EMG amplifier input voltage noise | White | 0.5–10 nV/√Hz | Choose low-noise op-amp (e.g., AD8221) |
| Quantization noise of ADC | White | $\frac{V_{LSB}}{\sqrt{12}}$ | ≥ 12-bit resolution |
| Cable triboelectric noise | Broadband | High | Use driven-right-leg, shielded cables |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Complete Myoelectric Acquisition Chain

The block diagram below shows the **full signal-conditioning pipeline** for a clinical-grade surface EMG system, from electrode to digital feature extraction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MUSCLE FIBERS] --> B[SKIN ELECTRODE INTERFACE]
    B --> C[ISOLATION BARRIER IEC60601]
    C --> D[INSTRUMENTATION PREAMP G 5 to 10]
    D --> E[HIGH PASS FILTER fc 20 Hz]
    E --> F[MAIN AMPLIFIER G 50 to 200]
    F --> G[LOW PASS FILTER fc 500 Hz]
    G --> H[NOTCH FILTER 50 Hz Q 8]
    H --> I[ADC 12 to 16 BIT fs 1 to 2 kHz]
    I --> J[MICROCONTROLLER DSP]
    J --> K1[RECTIFICATION]
    J --> K2[ENVELOPE DETECTION]
    J --> K3[FEATURE EXTRACTION RMS MAV]
    J --> K4[PATTERN CLASSIFIER LDA SVM]
    K3 --> L[PROSTHETIC CONTROLLER OUTPUT]
    K4 --> L
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The signal flows from the ionic domain (muscle) → electronic domain (electrode) → analog conditioning → digital domain → actuator command. Each block has a *single* well-defined job; KTU questions often ask "what happens if you remove block X?"

---

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Electrode–Skin Interface Electrical Model

The skin–electrode junction is not a perfect wire. It behaves as a **half-cell potential in series with a parallel RC network** representing the epidermal impedance.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[GEL ELECTROLYTE] --> B[HALF CELL POTENTIAL E hc 0.22 V AgAgCl]
    A --> C[EPIDERMIS RESISTANCE R e 1 to 100 kohm]
    A --> D[EPIDERMIS CAPACITANCE C e 10 to 100 nF]
    B --> E[ELECTRODE METAL AgAgCl]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F[INPUT OF INSTRUMENTATION AMP]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this model matters:** The parallel RC nature of the skin is exactly why the HPF cutoff cannot be set too low — low frequencies see a huge impedance and inject motion artifact. Conversely, at high frequencies the capacitor shorts the noise, making the high-impedance op-amp input *crucial*.

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Safety and Isolation Sub-system

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[AC MAINS 230 V] --> B[ISOLATION TRANSFORMER]
    B --> C[DC DC ISOLATED CONVERTER]
    C --> D[PATIENT CONNECTED SIDE]
    D --> E[OPTOCOUPLER FOR DATA LINE]
    D --> F[DRIVEN RIGHT LEG CIRCUIT]
    D --> G[ELECTRODES ON PATIENT]
    E --> H[DSP PC SIDE]
    H --> I[FEATURE EXTRACTION SOFTWARE]
    F --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU safety highlight:** The driven-right-leg (DRL) circuit actively forces the body potential to track the amplifier common-mode, *reducing* the 50 Hz common-mode voltage *before* the differential stage even sees it. It typically improves CMRR by 20–30 dB beyond what the IA alone provides.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]

**Define a myoelectric signal. State its typical amplitude and frequency ranges as used in surface EMG acquisition.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A myoelectric signal (EMG) is the **bioelectric voltage generated by the depolarisation of skeletal muscle fibres** during contraction, recorded as the temporal and spatial summation of motor unit action potentials (MUAPs). When picked up by surface electrodes placed on the skin, it is called a surface EMG (sEMG) signal.

- **Amplitude range:** $\mathbf{50\ \mu V\ to\ 5\ mV}$ (peak-to-peak)
- **Frequency range:** $\mathbf{20\ Hz\ to\ 500\ Hz}$ (dominant energy $50\text{–}150\ Hz$)

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model QP]

**Define Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR). Why is a high CMRR essential in an EMG pre-amplifier?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
CMRR is defined as the ratio of differential-mode gain ($A_d$) to common-mode gain ($A_{cm}$):

$$
\text{CMRR} = \frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}
$$

In decibels:

$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)
$$

A high CMRR is essential because the human body picks up large **common-mode voltages (typically up to ±1 V at 50 Hz)** from the power mains. The EMG signal of interest is only hundreds of microvolts. Without high CMRR (> 90 dB), the 50 Hz interference would completely swamp the bioelectric signal.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (Internal Choice: Answer ANY ONE — 14 Marks)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Pattern]

**(a) Draw and explain the block diagram of a myoelectric signal acquisition system. Discuss the function of each block in detail.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A complete sEMG acquisition system consists of the following cascaded blocks (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

| Block | Function |
|-------|----------|
| **Electrodes (Ag/AgCl)** | Convert ionic current in tissue to electronic current; provide stable half-cell potential. |
| **Patient isolation** | Galvanic isolation (transformer + optocoupler) per IEC 60601 prevents mains leakage reaching the body. |
| **Instrumentation pre-amp** | Provides differential gain of 5–10 with CMRR > 90 dB; rejects common-mode 50 Hz. |
| **High-pass filter** | Removes DC offset and motion artifact (cutoff ≈ 20 Hz, 2nd-order Butterworth). |
| **Main amplifier** | Provides additional gain (50–200) to bring signal to ADC input range. |
| **Low-pass filter (anti-alias)** | Limits bandwidth to 500 Hz to satisfy Nyquist and prevent aliasing. |
| **50 Hz notch filter** | Suppresses residual power-line interference. |
| **ADC (12–16 bit)** | Digitises the conditioned analog signal at $f_s \geq 1\ \mathrm{kHz}$. |
| **DSP / Microcontroller** | Performs rectification, smoothing, RMS / MAV feature extraction, and prosthetic control. |

> The block design is **modular**: each stage can be tuned or upgraded without redesigning the rest, which is essential for prosthetic systems where power and size constraints dominate.

---

**(b) With a neat circuit diagram, derive the closed-form output voltage expression of a three-op-amp instrumentation amplifier. Why is this topology preferred for EMG pre-amplification?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

(See **Section 3.1** of these notes for the full derivation.)

The final result is:

$$
V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{2R_f}{R_g}\right)(V_1 - V_2)
$$

**Why the three-op-amp IA is preferred for EMG:**

1. **Single-resistor gain control** — varying $R_g$ alone changes gain without disturbing the high CMRR symmetry.
2. **Very high input impedance** ($> 10^{12}\ \Omega$) — does not load the high-impedance skin–electrode source.
3. **Very high CMRR** (> 100 dB at DC) — rejects the dominant 50 Hz mains pickup.
4. **Low noise** — input buffer op-amps can be chosen specifically for low voltage noise (e.g., AD8421, INA128).
5. **Adjustable gain over a wide range** — easily adapts to different muscle groups (e.g., biceps vs facial muscles).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Pattern]

**(a) Compare the various types of electrodes used for EMG signal acquisition. Which type is preferred for prosthetic control and why?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

(Refer to the **Analytical Matrix in Section 3.4** for the comparison.)

For prosthetic control, **surface Ag/AgCl electrodes** are preferred because:

1. **Non-invasive** — usable for daily, long-term wear without medical supervision.
2. **Reusable / disposable skin-safe adhesive gel** — easy replacement and hygiene.
3. **Picks up the activity of many motor units** — produces a smooth, repeatable control signal suitable for proportional prosthetic control.
4. **Cost-effective** — a few rupees per electrode, suitable for mass deployment.
5. **Compatible with pattern-recognition algorithms** — modern multi-channel sEMG with supervised learning (LDA, SVM) achieves > 95% gesture-classification accuracy with surface arrays.

Needle and fine-wire electrodes, although more selective, are **reserved for clinical neurophysiology** and not for everyday prosthetic control due to pain, infection risk, and the requirement of a clinician to insert them.

---

**(b) List and explain the various sources of noise encountered in myoelectric signal acquisition. What techniques are employed at each stage to mitigate them?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

(Refer to the **Analytical Matrix in Section 3.5** for the comprehensive table.)

Summary of noise sources and stage-wise mitigation:

1. **Power-line interference (50 Hz)** — *Largest* noise source. Mitigated by: (i) **High CMRR differential pre-amp** (≥ 90 dB), (ii) **Driven-right-leg circuit** to actively reduce body common-mode, (iii) **50 Hz notch filter** (Q = 8, 2nd-order).
2. **Motion artifact (0–20 Hz)** — Caused by skin–electrode displacement. Mitigated by: (i) **Skin preparation** (alcohol cleaning, mild abrasion), (ii) **High-pass filter at 20 Hz**, (iii) **Hydrogel adhesive** electrodes for stable contact.
3. **Thermal (Johnson) noise** — Broadband white noise of skin resistance. Mitigated by: (i) **Low-noise op-amps** with $e_n < 10\ \mathrm{nV/\sqrt{Hz}}$, (ii) **Short, shielded cables**, (iii) **Placing pre-amp close to the electrode**.
4. **Electrode half-cell potential drift** — DC offset. Mitigated by: (i) **Ag/AgCl electrodes** (low polarization ≈ 0.22 V), (ii) **High-pass filter** to block DC.
5. **Cross-talk from neighbouring muscles** — Mitigated by: (i) **Differential recording** with closely spaced electrodes, (ii) **Spatial filtering / electrode arrays**, (iii) **Blind Source Separation (BSS)** algorithms in DSP.
6. **Quantization noise** — Mitigated by: (i) **12-bit or 16-bit ADC** giving SNR > 74 dB, (ii) **Oversampling** followed by digital decimation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read carefully!**
> 1. **Confusing "noise" with "artifact"** — *Noise* is a random stochastic disturbance; *artifact* is deterministic (e.g., 50 Hz hum, motion). Examiners **deduct 1 mark** for using the terms interchangeably.
> 2. **Forgetting the unit of CMRR** — Always quote CMRR in **dB**, not as a ratio, in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Skipping the safety/isolation block** — A 14-mark "explain the acquisition chain" answer that omits **patient isolation** loses **2 marks** outright as it fails the KTU Biomedical safety competency.
> 4. **Wrong order of HPF and pre-amp** — A common mistake is to place the HPF *before* the pre-amp. This kills the SNR. Always HPF *after* the pre-amp.
> 5. **Confusing "single-ended" with "differential"** — Examiners explicitly test this. Surface EMG is **always** recorded differentially.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section. It is your last-night revision sheet.**

- ☐ **EMG** = sum of motor unit action potentials; amplitude **50 µV – 5 mV**; bandwidth **20 – 500 Hz**.
- ☐ **Sampling rate** must obey Nyquist: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ → practically **1–2 kHz** for EMG.
- ☐ **Ag/AgCl electrodes** are standard for surface EMG (low half-cell potential 0.22 V, non-polarizing).
- ☐ **Acquisition chain order:** Electrode → Isolation → Instrumentation Pre-amp → HPF (20 Hz) → Main Amp → LPF (500 Hz) → Notch (50 Hz) → ADC → DSP.
- ☐ **Instrumentation amplifier gain:** $G_{IA} = \left(1 + \dfrac{2R_f}{R_g}\right)$ when subtractor stage has unity gain.
- ☐ **CMRR (dB) = $20 \log_{10}(A_d/A_{cm})$**; EMG pre-amp requires **> 90 dB**.
- ☐ **SNR (dB) = $20 \log_{10}(V_s / V_n)$**; clinically usable EMG needs **SNR > 20 dB**.
- ☐ **Driven-Right-Leg circuit** actively reduces body common-mode → adds 20–30 dB to effective CMRR.
- ☐ **ADC dynamic range** = $6.02 N + 1.76$ dB (N = bits); 12-bit ADC → ≈ **74 dB** dynamic range.
- ☐ **Patient isolation** (IEC 60601) is *non-negotiable* — always include in block diagrams.
- ☐ **Anti-alias filter cutoff ≤ $0.4 f_s$** with roll-off ≥ 4th order Butterworth.
- ☐ **EMG median frequency $\downarrow$ with fatigue**; **EMG amplitude $\uparrow$ with force** — favourite viva question.
- ☐ **Three electrode types:** Surface (prosthetic use), Needle (clinical neurography), Fine-wire (research).
- ☐ **Noise sources in order of severity:** 50 Hz mains → motion artifact → thermal noise → electrode drift.
- ☐ **KTU safety competency:** Always mention **galvanic isolation** and **DRL circuit** in long answers.
- ☐ **EMG vs ECG vs EEG bandwidths:** ECG 0.05–150 Hz; EEG 0.5–100 Hz; **EMG 20–500 Hz**.
- ☐ **Nyquist pitfall:** sampling at exactly $2f_{max}$ is theoretical; always use $f_s \geq 2.5 f_{max}$ in practice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEBT753 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS AND IMPLANTS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Processing # Module 3 — Processing | Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Processing** in a neural prosthesis refers to the complete sequence of operations performed on **bioelectrical signals** (acquired from the nervous system via microelectrodes, scalp electrodes, or intracortical arrays) to **extract physiologically meaningful features** and convert them into **control commands** (for motor prostheses) or **stimulation patterns** (for sensory prostheses such as cochlear and retinal implants). In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, **Processing** bridges the gap between the **Front-End (Acquisition)** block and the **Back-End (Stimulator/Actuator)** block of any neural prosthetic system. It is the **computational heart** of the implant — where raw, noisy, high-bandwidth neural data is transformed into actionable information. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Anchor (PEEBT753 — Module 3):** Processing encompasses *amplification, filtering, sampling, digitization, feature extraction, spike detection/sorting, neural decoding, and stimulation strategy generation*. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator" of the Nervous System > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The UN Interpreter Booth** Imagine the human nervous system is a **United Nations assembly hall**, where neurons are delegates speaking thousands of different "languages" (spike trains, field potentials, oscillations). A neural prosthesis is like a **UN interpreter booth**: | Stage in Analogy | Stage in Neural Prosthesis | |---|---| | Microphones picking up speech | **Microelectrodes / EEG electrodes** | | Noise-cancelling headphones | **Amplification + Filtering** | | Converting speech to digital text | **ADC (Sampling + Quantization)** | | Identifying keywords & intent | **Feature Extraction / Spike Sorting** | | Translating to target language | **Neural Decoding / Mapping Algorithm** | | Speaking translated words aloud | **Stimulator pulses / Actuator command** | Just as an interpreter must be fast (low latency), accurate (high fidelity), and robust (handle noise, multiple speakers), the **Processing unit** of a neural implant must satisfy three cardinal constraints: 1. **Low Latency** — typically **< 300 ms** for closed-loop control, **< 20 ms** for sensory feedback. 2. **High Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)** — operating with **SNR ≥ 6 dB** for reliable decoding. 3. **Power Efficiency** — implant-grade ASICs must operate under **< 50 mW** for thermal safety (limit of **1.7 °C tissue heating** per FDA/ISO 14708-1). --- ## 1.3 Why "Processing" is the Most Critical Module > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Imperative:** Even a perfect electrode and a flawless stimulator will fail if the **Processing** stage cannot reliably decode intention (motor) or encode sensation (sensory). Roughly **60–70%** of all neural prosthesis research papers devote their central methodology to a processing algorithm. **Key Physical Constants / Standard Metrics in Neural Signal Processing:** - **Action Potential (Spike) Amplitude:** **50–500 µV** - **Spike Duration:** **0.5–2 ms** - **Local Field Potential (LFP) Bandwidth:** **< 300 Hz** - **ECoG Bandwidth:** **0–500 Hz** - **EEG Bandwidth:** **0.5–100 Hz** - **Sampling Frequency (neural data):** **≥ 20 kHz** (Nyquist for spikes) - **Line Noise Frequency:** **50 Hz** (India) / **60 Hz** (US) - **Quantization Resolution:** **≥ 12-bit** (minimum), **16-bit** (standard) --- ## 1.4 Sub-Topics Roadmap (Aligned to KTU 2024 Module 3) The Processing module is decomposed into the following KTU-aligned sub-units: 1. **Bioelectrical Signal Conditioning** — Amplification & Filtering 2. **Sampling & Quantization (ADC)** 3. **Time-Domain Feature Extraction** — MAV, RMS, ZC, WL 4. **Frequency-Domain Feature Extraction** — PSD, Band Power 5. **Time-Frequency Analysis** — STFT, Wavelet Transform 6. **Spike Detection & Sorting** — Thresholding, PCA, Template Matching 7. **Neural Decoding** — Linear, Kalman, Population Vector, Deep Learning 8. **Sensory Prosthesis Processing Strategies** — CIS (Cochlear), Image-to-Stimulus (Retinal) # Module 3 — Processing | Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Canonical Signal Processing Pipeline > [!NOTE] > Every neural prosthesis — whether a cochlear implant, retinal prosthesis, or BCI — implements the following **canonical processing pipeline**: ### Stage-by-Stage Logical Breakdown: **Stage 1 — Preamplification** - **Why:** Microvolt-level neural signals must be boosted before any cable transmission or ADC operation. Without this stage, signals would be **swamped by thermal noise** (Johnson-Nyquist noise). - **How:** An **instrumentation amplifier (INA)** with high **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR ≥ 90 dB)** boosts the **differential neural voltage** while rejecting common-mode interference (e.g., 50 Hz mains hum, body-potential drift). **Stage 2 — Analog Filtering** - **Why:** Bandwidth-limiting the signal improves SNR and prevents **aliasing** during sampling. - **How:** Cascade of: - **High-pass filter** (cutoff **~0.5 Hz** for EEG, **~300 Hz** for spikes) to remove DC offset and motion artefacts. - **Low-pass filter** (cutoff **~3 kHz** for EEG, **~5–10 kHz** for spikes) to limit high-frequency noise. - **Notch filter** (Q-factor high, center **50 Hz**) to suppress power-line interference. **Stage 3 — Sampling (Analog-to-Digital Conversion)** - **Why:** Modern neural decoders require **discrete-time, finite-precision** signals. - **How:** A **Successive Approximation Register (SAR) ADC** samples the signal at $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist). For spike-band signals, $f_s \geq 20$ kHz is standard. **Stage 4 — Feature Extraction** - **Why:** Raw multi-channel data is **high-dimensional and redundant**. Features compress information while preserving discriminative content. - **How:** Compute **time-domain features** (MAV, RMS, WL, ZC) or **frequency-domain features** (band power, PSD) per channel per time window (typically **150–250 ms** for BCI applications). **Stage 5 — Classification / Decoding** - **Why:** Features must be mapped to a discrete class (cursor direction, phoneme, grasp type) or continuous variable (arm velocity, force). - **How:** Apply a classifier (**LDA, SVM, Random Forest**) or regressor (**Linear filter, Kalman filter, RNN/LSTM**). **Stage 6 — Stimulus Generation (Sensory Prostheses)** - **Why:** For sensory implants, processing runs **in reverse** — sensory input (sound, image) is encoded into **electrical pulse trains**. - **How:** Example — **Continuous Interleaved Sampling (CIS)** strategy for cochlear implants splits audio into N band-pass channels, extracts envelope, compresses via **log function**, and modulates biphasic pulse amplitude. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Print This Table — It Covers ≥ 80% of Numerical Problems in Module 3** | # | Concept | Formula | Variable Definitions | Typical Units / Values | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Johnson-Nyquist Thermal Noise RMS Voltage** | $v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | $k_B$ = Boltzmann const ($1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K), $T$ = absolute temperature, $R$ = source resistance, $\Delta f$ = bandwidth | $v_n$ in **V**, $R$ in $\Omega$, $\Delta f$ in Hz | | 2 | **CMRR (Common-Mode Rejection Ratio)** | $\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | $A_d$ = differential gain, $A_{cm}$ = common-mode gain | dB (target $\geq$ **90 dB**) | | 3 | **Nyquist Sampling Criterion** | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_s$ = sampling rate, $f_{max}$ = highest signal frequency | Hz | | 4 | **Quantization Step Size (Resolution)** | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$ | $V_{FS}$ = full-scale input range, $n$ = ADC bit count | V / LSB | | 5 | **Quantization SNR (Ideal ADC)** | $\text{SNR}_q = 6.02 n + 1.76$ | $n$ = number of bits | dB | | 6 | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | $\text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}}\right)$ | $V_{signal}$, $V_{noise}$ = RMS amplitudes | dB | | 7 | **Mean Absolute Value (MAV)** | $\text{MAV} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert x_i \vert$ | $x_i$ = signal samples, $N$ = window length | Same units as signal | | 8 | **Root Mean Square (RMS)** | $\text{RMS} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i^2}$ | Same as above | Same units as signal | | 9 | **Zero Crossings (ZC)** | $\text{ZC} = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1}\!\left[\text{sign}(x_{i+1}) \neq \text{sign}(x_i)\right]$ | Counts sign changes | Dimensionless | | 10 | **Waveform Length (WL)** | $\text{WL} = \sum_{i=1}^{N-1} \vert x_{i+1} - x_i \vert$ | Cumulative absolute sample-to-sample difference | Same units as signal | | 11 | **Power Spectral Density (Welch's Method)** | $\hat{S}_x(f) = \dfrac{1}{K}\sum_{k=1}^{K} \vert X_k(f)\vert^2$ | $K$ = number of overlapped segments | W/Hz | | 12 | **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** | $X(\tau, f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, w(t - \tau)\, e^{-j 2\pi f t}\, dt$ | $w(\cdot)$ = window function | Complex spectrum | | 13 | **Continuous Wavelet Transform (CWT)** | $C(a, b) = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{a}}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\, \psi\!\left(\dfrac{t-b}{a}\right) dt$ | $a$ = scale, $b$ = translation, $\psi$ = mother wavelet | Scalogram | | 14 | **Spike Detection Threshold (Simple)** | $V_{th} = \mu_n + k \cdot \sigma_n$ | $\mu_n$ = noise mean, $\sigma_n$ = noise std, $k \in [3, 5]$ | Volts | | 15 | **Nonlinear Energy Operator (NEO)** | $\psi[x(n)] = x^2(n) - x(n+1)\,x(n-1)$ | $n$ = sample index | Energy-like quantity | | 16 | **Population Vector Decoder** | $\hat{\mathbf{v}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \, \hat{\mathbf{d}}_i$ | $\hat{\mathbf{d}}_i$ = preferred direction of neuron $i$, $w_i$ = firing rate weight | Continuous vector | | 17 | **Cochlear Log Compression** | $E_{out} = \dfrac{\ln(1 + c \cdot E_{in})}{\ln(1 + c)}$ | $E_{in}$ = envelope, $c$ = compression ratio constant | Normalized 0–1 | | 18 | **Cumulative First-Pass Decoding Latency Budget** | $T_{total} = T_{acq} + T_{DSP} + T_{tx} + T_{stim}$ | Sum of acquisition, processing, telemetry, and stimulation delays | **< 300 ms (BCI)** | --- ## 2.3 Engineering Real-World Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why Every Formula Matters in Practice:** - **Johnson-Nyquist noise** dictates the **minimum detectable neural signal amplitude** given electrode impedance (typically 10–100 kΩ at 1 kHz). This is why **low-impedance electrodes + low-noise front-ends** are mandatory. - **CMRR ≥ 90 dB** is non-negotiable — without it, the 50 Hz mains hum overwhelms the µV-scale EEG signal, making BCI impossible. - **Nyquist criterion** explains why **EEG uses 256–512 Hz sampling** while **intracortical recordings use 20–30 kHz** — they have fundamentally different bandwidths. - **Quantization SNR** tells us that a **16-bit ADC** offers ~**98 dB dynamic range** — enough to capture spike amplitudes with **12-bit precision** while keeping **4 bits of headroom** for artefacts. - **Population Vector Decoding** was the algorithm behind the **BrainGate clinical trials** allowing tetraplegic patients to control a robotic arm via motor cortex signals. # Module 3 — Processing | Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Johnson-Nyquist Thermal Noise (Foundation of SNR Analysis) **Problem Statement:** A microelectrode with source impedance $R = 50\,\text{k}\Omega$ records neural spikes at body temperature $T = 310\,\text{K}$. The signal is band-pass filtered between 300 Hz and 3 kHz. Compute the RMS thermal noise voltage. **Given:** - Boltzmann constant $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$ - $R = 50 \times 10^{3}\,\Omega$ - $T = 310\,\text{K}$ - $\Delta f = f_{high} - f_{low} = 3000 - 300 = 2700\,\text{Hz}$ **Step 1 — Write the governing equation:** $$ v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values:** $$ v_n = \sqrt{4 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 310 \times (50 \times 10^{3}) \times 2700} $$ **Step 3 — Compute the numerator coefficient (powers of 10):** $$ 4 \times 1.38 \times 310 \times 50 \times 2700 \times 10^{-23+3} = 4 \times 1.38 \times 310 \times 50 \times 2700 \times 10^{-20} $$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the scalar product:** $$ 4 \times 1.38 = 5.52 $$ $$ 5.52 \times 310 = 1711.2 $$ $$ 1711.2 \times 50 = 85560 $$ $$ 85560 \times 2700 = 231012000 $$ **Step 5 — Combine with the power of 10:** $$ v_n = \sqrt{231012000 \times 10^{-20}} = \sqrt{2.31012 \times 10^{-12}} $$ **Step 6 — Take the square root:** $$ v_n = \sqrt{2.31012} \times 10^{-6} \approx 1.5199 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{V} $$ **Result:** $$ \boxed{v_n \approx 1.52\,\mu\text{V}} $$ **Step 7 — Engineering Interpretation:** A typical extracellular spike amplitude is **100–500 µV**. The SNR is therefore: $$ \text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{300 \times 10^{-6}}{1.52 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(197.4) \approx 45.9\,\text{dB} $$ This is **excellent SNR** — comfortably above the 6 dB decoding threshold — but only because we used a **narrow band-pass filter** ($\Delta f = 2.7$ kHz). Wider bandwidths rapidly degrade SNR. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CMRR-Based Common-Mode Rejection **Problem Statement:** A neural amplifier has a differential gain $A_d = 1000$ and a common-mode gain $A_{cm} = 0.1$. A 50 Hz interference of 5 mV amplitude appears as common-mode noise. What is the residual interference at the output? **Step 1 — CMRR in dB:** $$ \text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1000}{0.1}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(10000) = 80\,\text{dB} $$ **Step 2 — Equivalent CMRR (linear):** $$ \text{CMRR} = 10^{80/20} = 10^4 = 10000 $$ **Step 3 — Residual common-mode output voltage:** $$ V_{cm,out} = \dfrac{V_{cm,in}}{\text{CMRR}} = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{10^4} = 5 \times 10^{-7}\,\text{V} = 0.5\,\mu\text{V} $$ **Result:** A 5 mV line noise is reduced to **0.5 µV** — comparable to spike amplitude. To push residual noise **below 0.1 µV**, we need **CMRR ≥ 94 dB**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Spike Detection Threshold Using Statistical Criterion **Problem Statement:** During a quiescent period (no spike firing), the recorded noise has $\mu_n = 0\,\mu\text{V}$ and $\sigma_n = 2\,\mu\text{V}$. Using a $k = 4$ threshold criterion, find the detection threshold $V_{th}$. **Step 1 — Apply threshold formula:** $$ V_{th} = \mu_n + k \cdot \sigma_n = 0 + 4 \times 2\,\mu\text{V} $$ **Step 2 — Result:** $$ \boxed{V_{th} = 8\,\mu\text{V}} $$ **Step 3 — Probability of False Positive (Gaussian Noise Assumption):** $$ P_{FP} = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{k}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}\!\left(\dfrac{4}{\sqrt{2}}\right) = \dfrac{1}{2}\text{erfc}(2.828) $$ $$ \text{erfc}(2.828) \approx 2.4 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ P_{FP} \approx 1.2 \times 10^{-5} $$ **Engineering Note:** A $k = 4$ threshold gives roughly **1 false positive every ~83,000 samples** — a tolerable false-alarm rate at $f_s = 20$ kHz. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Quantization Step Size and Dynamic Range **Problem Statement:** A 12-bit ADC with input range $V_{FS} = \pm 5\,\text{V}$ is used. Find the quantization step and theoretical SNR. **Step 1 — Quantization step (LSB):** $$ \Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \dfrac{10}{2^{12} - 1} = \dfrac{10}{4095} \approx 2.442\,\text{mV/LSB} $$ **Step 2 — Theoretical SNR:** $$ \text{SNR}_q = 6.02 n + 1.76 = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74\,\text{dB} $$ **Step 3 — Minimum detectable signal (SNR = 0 dB reference):** $$ V_{min} \approx \Delta \approx 2.442\,\text{mV} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfall:** A 12-bit ADC **cannot resolve** the typical 100–500 µV spike amplitude if the full-scale range is set to $\pm 5\,\text{V}$. In practice, a **Programmable Gain Amplifier (PGA)** stages the signal before the ADC to map the µV-range signal to the ADC's full-scale. --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Population Vector Decoder (Motor Cortex BCI) **Problem Statement:** Three neurons in primary motor cortex (M1) have the following firing rates during a rightward reach. Compute the decoded velocity vector. **Given:** | Neuron | Firing Rate (Hz) | Preferred Direction $\hat{\mathbf{d}}_i$ | |---|---|---| | 1 | $w_1 = 80$ | $\hat{\mathbf{d}}_1 = (1, 0)$ | | 2 | $w_2 = 40$ | $\hat{\mathbf{d}}_2 = (0, 1)$ | | 3 | $w_3 = 30$ | $\hat{\mathbf{d}}_3 = (-1, 0)$ | **Step 1 — Apply population vector equation:** $$ \hat{\mathbf{v}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \, \hat{\mathbf{d}}_i $$ **Step 2 — Substitute:** $$ \hat{\mathbf{v}} = 80 \times (1, 0) + 40 \times (0, 1) + 30 \times (-1, 0) $$ **Step 3 — Sum component-wise:** $$ \hat{\mathbf{v}}_x = 80 - 30 = 50 $$ $$ \hat{\mathbf{v}}_y = 40 $$ **Result:** $$ \boxed{\hat{\mathbf{v}} = (50, 40) \implies \text{magnitude} \approx 64\,\text{a.u.},\quad \text{angle} \approx 38.7^\circ} $$ This vector decoding is the **theoretical foundation** of the **BrainGate2** clinical BCI demonstrations. # Module 3 — Processing | Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Neural Signal Processing Pipeline > [!NOTE] > The following Mermaid block diagram captures the complete end-to-end **processing architecture** for a generic neural prosthesis, showing both **motor (read-out)** and **sensory (write-in)** processing pathways. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Neural Source
Brain Cortex / Nerve"] --> B["Microelectrode Array
Or Scalp Electrodes"] B --> C["Preamplifier
INA, CMRR 90 dB"] C --> D["Analog Bandpass Filter
0.5 Hz to 5 kHz"] D --> E["Notch Filter
50 Hz Reject"] E --> F["Programmable Gain Amplifier
PGA 1 to 1000"] F --> G["Analog to Digital Converter
16 bit, 20 kHz"] G --> H{"Processing Mode
Motor or Sensory"} H -- Motor Pathway --> I["Spike Detection
Threshold NEO"] I --> J["Spike Sorting
PCA Template Match"] J --> K["Feature Extraction
MAV RMS WL ZC"] K --> L["Decoder
Kalman LDA RNN"] L --> M["Command Output
Cursor Robotic Arm"] H -- Sensory Pathway --> N["Sensor Input
Microphone or Camera"] N --> O["Filter Bank
N Bandpass Channels"] O --> P["Envelope Extraction
Rectification Lowpass"] P --> Q["Log Compression
Loudness Mapping"] Q --> R["Pulse Modulator
Amplitude or Frequency"] R --> S["Stimulator Array
Cochlear or Retinal"] M --> T["Telemetry Back to User"] S --> T ``` **Diagram Reading Notes:** - The **diamond node `H`** acts as a **branch decision** — separating the **motor (read-out / BCI)** pipeline from the **sensory (write-in / neurostimulation)** pipeline. - All paths from `C` to `G` represent the **shared analog/digital front-end**. - The motor pathway terminates at a **command output**, while the sensory pathway loops back to stimulate the nervous system — a **closed-loop architecture**. --- ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Cochlear Implant CIS Processing Strategy ```mermaid flowchart LR A1["Microphone
Audio In"] --> A2["Pre-emphasis Filter
High Boost"] A2 --> A3["Filter Bank
4 to 22 Bands"] A3 --> B1["Band 1
Envelope"] A3 --> B2["Band 2
Envelope"] A3 --> B3["Band N
Envelope"] B1 --> C1["Log Compression
Log Function"] B2 --> C2["Log Compression"] B3 --> C3["Log Compression"] C1 --> D1["Pulse Generator
CIS Strategy"] C2 --> D2["Pulse Generator"] C3 --> D3["Pulse Generator"] D1 --> E1["Electrode 1
Biphasic Pulse"] D2 --> E2["Electrode 2"] D3 --> E3["Electrode N"] ``` **Why CIS?** The **Continuous Interleaved Sampling** strategy avoids **channel interaction** by stimulating each electrode in a strict **non-overlapping temporal sequence** — eliminating simultaneous current spread that would otherwise smear the perceived pitch. # Module 3 — Processing | KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **Define the term "Quantization" in the context of neural signal digitization. A 10-bit ADC has a full-scale input range of ±4 V. Compute the quantization step size.** **Model Answer:** Quantization is the process of mapping a continuous-amplitude analog sample into one of $2^n$ discrete output codes of an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). It is the **amplitude discretization** step, complementary to sampling (which discretizes in time). **Quantization Step Size Calculation:** $$ \Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \dfrac{8}{2^{10} - 1} = \dfrac{8}{1023} \approx 7.82\,\text{mV/LSB} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget the full-scale range is **±4 V → total range = 8 V**, not 4 V. Also, $2^n - 1$ (not $2^n$) is used in the denominator — though for large $n$ the difference is negligible. --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **What is Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR)? Why is it critical for neural amplifiers?** **Model Answer:** **CMRR** is a metric quantifying an amplifier's ability to reject signals common to both inputs while amplifying differential signals. It is defined as: $$ \text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right) $$ **Why it is critical:** Neural signals are **microvolt-level**, while electromagnetic interference (50 Hz mains hum, fluorescent lights, RF) can be **several volts** at the body surface. Without high CMRR (typically **≥ 90 dB**), these common-mode interferences would saturate the amplifier and completely bury the neural signal. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice --- ### **Question B1 (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper]` **(a)** Explain the complete **signal processing pipeline** for a motor neural prosthesis (BCI), with a labeled block diagram. Discuss each block's function, typical parameters, and engineering trade-offs. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Johnson-Nyquist thermal noise equation**. A microelectrode of impedance **$R = 100\,\text{k}\Omega$** records signals at $T = 310\,\text{K}$ over a bandwidth $\Delta f = 5\,\text{kHz}$. Compute the RMS noise voltage and the corresponding SNR if the spike amplitude is **$300\,\mu\text{V}$**. **(7 Marks)** --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** The motor BCI processing pipeline consists of the following sequential stages: 1. **Acquisition:** Microelectrode array (e.g., Utah Array with 96–128 channels) captures extracellular potentials from M1 cortex. Typical spike amplitudes: **50–500 µV**. 2. **Preamplification:** A **low-noise instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., INA128) with gain 100–1000 boosts the signal. Noise floor must be **< 5 nV/√Hz**. 3. **Analog Filtering:** - High-pass at **300 Hz** to remove LFP and DC drift. - Low-pass at **3 kHz** to band-limit thermal noise. - 50 Hz notch filter (Q = 30). 4. **ADC:** 16-bit SAR ADC sampling at **20–30 kHz** per channel. 5. **Spike Detection:** Threshold crossing at $V_{th} = 4\sigma_n$ (see Derivation 3). 6. **Spike Sorting:** Align spike waveforms to a common peak; extract features via **PCA**; cluster using **k-means** or **Gaussian Mixture Model (GMM)**. 7. **Feature Extraction:** Compute binned firing rates (50 ms bins) per unit. 8. **Decoding:** - **Linear filter (Wiener):** $\mathbf{v}(t) = \mathbf{W}\mathbf{r}(t - \tau)$ - **Kalman filter:** Recursive state estimation. - **RNN/LSTM:** For nonlinear, time-series dynamics. 9. **Output Mapping:** Cursor coordinates, robotic arm joint angles, or wheelchair velocity. **Engineering Trade-offs:** | Trade-off | Axis | |---|---| | Sampling rate vs. data bandwidth | ↑$f_s$ = ↑SNR, but ↑telemetry power | | Window length vs. latency | ↑window = ↑accuracy, ↓responsiveness | | Decoder complexity vs. real-time performance | Deep networks = ↑accuracy, ↑latency | --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1 — State the Johnson-Nyquist equation:** $$ v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f} $$ **Step 2 — Identify all variables:** - $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$ - $T = 310\,\text{K}$ - $R = 100 \times 10^{3}\,\Omega$ - $\Delta f = 5000\,\text{Hz}$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$ v_n = \sqrt{4 \times (1.38 \times 10^{-23}) \times 310 \times (100 \times 10^{3}) \times 5000} $$ **Step 4 — Compute the scalar product:** $$ 4 \times 1.38 = 5.52 $$ $$ 5.52 \times 310 = 1711.2 $$ $$ 1711.2 \times 10^{3} \times 5 \times 10^{3} = 1.7112 \times 10^{10} $$ **Step 5 — Combine with $k_B$:** $$ 5.52 \times 310 \times 100000 \times 5000 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} $$ $$ = 8.5556 \times 10^{9} \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} $$ Wait — recompute carefully: $$ = 4 \times 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \times 310 \times 10^{5} \times 5 \times 10^{3} $$ $$ = (4 \times 1.38 \times 310 \times 5) \times 10^{-23+5+3} $$ $$ = 8556 \times 10^{-15} $$ $$ = 8.556 \times 10^{-12} $$ **Step 6 — Take square root:** $$ v_n = \sqrt{8.556 \times 10^{-12}} = 2.925 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{V} \approx 2.93\,\mu\text{V} $$ **Step 7 — Compute SNR:** $$ \text{SNR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{300 \times 10^{-6}}{2.93 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(102.4) \approx 40.2\,\text{dB} $$ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{v_n \approx 2.93\,\mu\text{V}, \quad \text{SNR} \approx 40.2\,\text{dB}} $$ --- ### **Question B2 (14 Marks)** `[Alternative Module Internal Choice]` **(a)** Describe the **Continuous Interleaved Sampling (CIS) strategy** used in cochlear implant speech processing. With a neat block diagram, explain the function of each block. Why is CIS preferred over simultaneous analog stimulation strategies? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **Population Vector Decoding algorithm** for motor cortex BCI. Given the following neural firing data from three neurons during a 2D reach task, decode the reach direction and magnitude. **(7 Marks)** | Neuron | Firing Rate (Hz) | Preferred Direction | |---|---|---| | 1 | 90 | (1, 0) | | 2 | 60 | (0, 1) | | 3 | 20 | (−1, 0) | | 4 | 50 | (1, 1)/√2 | --- **Model Solution — Part (a):** The **CIS strategy**, introduced by Wilson et al. (1991), is the most clinically successful cochlear implant speech processing strategy. It works as follows: 1. **Microphone** captures the audio signal. 2. **Pre-emphasis filter** boosts high frequencies to compensate for the natural roll-off in the speech spectrum. 3. **Filter bank** splits the audio into N frequency bands (typically N = 8 to 22 in modern devices like Cochlear Ltd.
