Nucleus devices). 4. **Envelope detection** per band — full-wave rectification followed by a low-pass filter (~400 Hz cutoff) extracts the **slow amplitude envelope** while discarding the **fast fine-structure**. 5. **Logarithmic compression** maps the wide dynamic range (~120 dB in natural hearing) into the narrow electrical dynamic range (~10–20 dB) usable by auditory neurons. 6. **Pulse modulation** generates biphasic current pulses whose **amplitude is proportional to the compressed envelope** of each band. 7. **Sequential non-overlapping stimulation** — each band's pulse train is delivered to its corresponding electrode in strict time-multiplexed order, ensuring no two electrodes fire simultaneously. **Why CIS over simultaneous analog stimulation?** Simultaneous analog strategies sum currents in the cochlear fluids, producing **channel interaction** due to **current spread** in the high-impedance perilymph/lymph space. This causes: - **Smeared frequency perception** (pitch confusion) - **Reduced spectral resolution** - **Loudness summation** artifacts CIS eliminates these by enforcing **strict temporal separation** between channels. --- **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1 — State the population vector equation:** $$ \hat{\mathbf{v}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \, \hat{\mathbf{d}}_i $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** $$ \hat{\mathbf{v}} = 90 \times (1, 0) + 60 \times (0, 1) + 20 \times (-1, 0) + 50 \times \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}, \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right) $$ **Step 3 — Compute x-component:** $$ \hat{\mathbf{v}}_x = 90 - 20 + 50 \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 70 + 35.36 = 105.36 $$ **Step 4 — Compute y-component:** $$ \hat{\mathbf{v}}_y = 60 + 50 \times \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 60 + 35.36 = 95.36 $$ **Step 5 — Magnitude and angle:** $$ \vert \hat{\mathbf{v}} \vert = \sqrt{105.36^2 + 95.36^2} = \sqrt{11100.7 + 9093.5} = \sqrt{20194.2} \approx 142.1 $$ $$ \theta = \arctan\!\left(\dfrac{95.36}{105.36}\right) = \arctan(0.905) \approx 42.1^\circ $$ **Final Answer:** $$ \boxed{\hat{\mathbf{v}} \approx (105.4,\ 95.4), \quad \vert \hat{\mathbf{v}} \vert \approx 142.1\,\text{a.u.}, \quad \theta \approx 42.1^\circ} $$ **Engineering Interpretation:** The decoded vector points at ~42° in the upper-right quadrant — a NE direction — consistent with a moderately strong reach combining positive X and Y components. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Module 3 (Processing)** > > 1. **Forgetting units:** Always state units for $v_n$ (V or µV), SNR (dB), and decoded vector components (a.u. or cm/s). > 2. **Bit count errors:** $2^n - 1$ vs. $2^n$ in LSB calculation. Examiners deduct **½ mark** if wrong. > 3. **CMRR formula confusion:** $20 \log_{10}$ for voltage-ratio CMRR; $10 \log_{10}$ is for power ratios. Mixing these loses a mark. > 4. **Sampling theorem direction:** "$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$" — never the reverse. A reversed inequality = 0 marks. > 5. **Spike detection threshold:** Always justify $k$ (typically 3–5). A bare formula without context loses marks. > 6. **Population Vector normalization:** Students often forget to **normalize preferred direction vectors** to unit length. Deduct 1 mark if $\vert \hat{\mathbf{d}}_i \vert \neq 1$. > 7. **CIS vs. analog confusion:** CIS uses **envelope + pulse amplitude modulation**, not direct analog stimulation. Mixing these is a common error. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION BULLETS — Module 3: Processing** ### 🔹 Definitions & Concepts - Processing = bridging **acquisition** and **stimulator/actuator** blocks. - Spikes occupy **300 Hz – 3 kHz**; LFPs occupy **< 300 Hz**; EEG occupies **0.5 – 100 Hz**. - CMRR ≥ **90 dB** is mandatory for any neural amplifier. - Sampling theorem: **$f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$** (Nyquist). - Quantization step: **$\Delta = V_{FS} / (2^n - 1)$**. - Theoretical SNR of ideal ADC: **$6.02n + 1.76$ dB**. ### 🔹 Algorithms & Methods - **Spike Detection:** Threshold = $\mu_n + k\sigma_n$, with $k \in [3,5]$. - **Spike Sorting:** PCA + k-means / GMM / template matching. - **Time-domain features:** MAV, RMS, WL, ZC, SSC. - **Frequency-domain features:** Band power in $\delta, \theta, \alpha, \beta, \gamma$ bands. - **Time-frequency:** STFT, CWT, Wavelet Packet Decomposition. - **Decoders:** Linear (Wiener), Kalman Filter, Population Vector, RNN/LSTM. - **Cochlear strategy:** CIS = filter bank → envelope → log compress → pulse amplitude modulation. ### 🔹 Critical Numbers to Memorize - Boltzmann constant $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}\,\text{J/K}$. - Body temperature $T = 310\,\text{K}$. - Line noise: **50 Hz** (India), **60 Hz** (US). - Typical microelectrode impedance: **10–100 kΩ** at 1 kHz. - BCI acceptable latency: **< 300 ms**. - Cochlear implant clinical electrodes: **12–22 channels**. - Retinal implant clinical electrodes: **60–1500 channels** (Argus II = 60). ### 🔹 Common Mistakes to Avoid - Confusing **CMRR** with **PSRR** (Power Supply Rejection Ratio). - Using **power SNR** ($10\log$) where **voltage SNR** ($20\log$) is needed. - Forgetting **anti-aliasing filter** before ADC. - Forgetting that **CWT is shift-invariant in scale**, **not in time**. - Applying **log compression** before envelope extraction (wrong order). ### 🔹 Clinical / Commercial Anchors - **BrainGate2** — Intracortical BCI (Utah Array, Brown University). - **Cochlear Nucleus 7** — 22-channel CIS-based cochlear implant. - **Argus II (Second Sight)** — 60-electrode retinal prosthesis. - **Deep Brain Stimulation (DBS)** — Uses high-frequency **130–185 Hz** pulse processing. - **Utah Array** — 10×10 silicon microelectrode array from Blackrock Microsystems. ---

topics.title: Control of Prosthetic Function
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT753 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS AND IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control of Prosthetic Function

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Control of Prosthetic Function** refers to the engineered methodology by which an artificial device (limb, cochlear, retinal, or neural stimulator) interprets physiological intent (from the user) and translates it into precise, repeatable actuation of the prosthesis — *and* delivers sensory information back to the user — through a closed-loop or open-loop architecture.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this is formally classified as the **Human–Machine Interface (HMI)** layer of any neural prosthesis, encompassing:

| Sub-domain | Functional Role |
|---|---|
| **Input Decoding** | Translating biosignals (EMG, EEG, ECoG, intracortical spikes) into command variables |
| **Output Encoding** | Converting command variables into safe stimulation parameters (pulse width, amplitude, frequency) |
| **Feedback Modulation** | Returning sensory cues (electrotactile, intracortical microstimulation) to the user |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Biological Remote Control"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Imagine your TV remote. You press a button (your **brain sends a neural command**), an IR signal travels through air (**neural signal propagates through motor pathways**), and the TV changes the channel (**the prosthesis moves**). The TV also shows a small LED confirming reception (**sensory feedback returns to you**). A neural prosthesis is essentially a *biological remote control* that replaces lost wiring in the body — but instead of infrared, it uses electrical currents, and instead of buttons, it decodes muscle or brain signals.

**Key takeaway:** Control = *intent sensing* + *signal decoding* + *safe actuation* + *feedback return*.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The following values are **canonical engineering constants** for KTU numerical problems:

- **Threshold charge per phase (safe limit):** **0.4 μC/phase** (Shannon limit boundary for safe stimulation)
- **Safe charge density:** **30 μC/cm²** (upper bound for platinum electrodes)
- **Typical stimulation frequency:** **20 Hz – 300 Hz** for sensory prostheses, **50 Hz – 100 Hz** for motor recruitment
- **Standard pulse width:** **50 μs – 500 μs** (cathodic-leading biphasic)
- **Neural conduction velocity:** **50 m/s** (large myelinated fibers)
- **EMG band:** **20 Hz – 500 Hz**
- **Information Transfer Rate (ITR):** **bits/min**, benchmarked against **Wolpaw's BCI standards**

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Whenever a numerical problem involves stimulation safety, immediately recall **0.4 μC** (Shannon limit) and **30 μC/cm²** — these are examiner-favored values.

---

## 1.4 Classification of Control Strategies (Overview)

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│     CONTROL OF PROSTHETIC FUNCTION                  │
├──────────────────────┬──────────────────────────────┤
│  NON-INVASIVE        │  INVASIVE / IMPLANTED        │
│  ─ EMG (surface)     │  ─ Intramuscular EMG        │
│  ─ EEG (BCI)         │  ─ ECoG (electrocorticography)│
│  ─ EOG               │  ─ Cortical spikes (MEA)     │
│                      │  ─ Peripheral nerve cuffs    │
└──────────────────────┴──────────────────────────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly differentiates **"natural" control pathways** (residual limb EMG) from **"cognitively-driven" pathways** (BCI/EEG). Examiners frequently ask students to contrast the two.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Logic of a Prosthetic Control Loop

A prosthetic control system operates as a **closed-loop cyber-physical system** with the following structured stages:

1. **Intent Acquisition** — Capture biosignal (EMG spike / EEG rhythm / cortical firing rate)
2. **Pre-processing** — Amplification, band-pass filtering (typically **20–500 Hz** for EMG), notch filtering at **50 Hz** to suppress mains interference
3. **Feature Extraction** — Compute Time-Domain (MAV, RMS, ZC, WL) or Frequency-Domain (MDF, MNF) features
4. **Classification / Decoding** — Map features to discrete commands (e.g., SVM, LDA, Neural Network)
5. **Actuation / Stimulation** — Generate biphasic pulses with computed amplitude/frequency
6. **Feedback Encoding** — Encode outcome (proprioception, grasp force) back as electrotactile or neural stimulation

---

## 2.2 Strength–Duration Curve (Core Stimulation Theory)

The **fundamental equation** governing neural excitation by an external stimulus is the **Weiss-Lapicque formulation**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The strength-duration relationship describes the *minimum stimulus amplitude* required to excite a nerve as a function of the pulse duration.

**Weiss Equation:**
$$I_{th} = \frac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau_{ch}}}$$

**Lapicque (hyperbolic) form:**
$$I_{th} = I_{rh}\left(1 + \frac{\tau_{chr}}{t}\right)$$

**Rheobase:** $I_{rh}$ = minimum current for an *infinitely long* pulse
**Chronaxie:** $\tau_{chr}$ = pulse duration at which the threshold current = **2 × rheobase**

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** A shorter pulse needs a *larger* current to fire a neuron (think of it like flicking a domino with your finger — a quick flick needs more force than a slow push).

---

## 2.3 Charge Balance & Stimulation Safety

For a **biphasic charge-balanced pulse**:

$$Q_{anodic} = Q_{cathodic}$$

$$I_a \cdot t_a = I_c \cdot t_c$$

Charge per phase:
$$Q_{phase} = I \cdot PW$$

Charge density:
$$D = \frac{Q_{phase}}{A_{electrode}} = \frac{I \cdot PW}{A_{electrode}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Always specify *per phase* charge. Examiners deduct marks if you quote total charge of a biphasic pulse.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| $I_{th}$ | Threshold current | $I_{rh}(1 + \tau_{chr}/t)$ | mA | Weiss-Lapicque |
| $\tau_{chr}$ | Chronaxie | Time at $2 \cdot I_{rh}$ | ms | Membrane time constant proxy |
| $Q_{ph}$ | Charge per phase | $I \cdot PW$ | μC | $1\,\mu C = 10^{-6}\,C$ |
| $D$ | Charge density | $Q_{ph} / A$ | μC/cm² | Must be $\leq 30\,\mu C/cm^2$ |
| $K$ | Shannon limit slope | $\log(D) + \log(Q)$ | — | Must stay $\leq 1.85$ |
| $\lambda$ | Firing rate | Spikes / s | Hz | Cortical decoding |
| $ITR$ | Information Transfer Rate | $B \cdot \log_2 N$ | bits/min | Wolpaw BCI benchmark |
| $v$ | Conduction velocity | $d / \Delta t$ | m/s | $\approx 50$ for Aα fibers |
| $f_{s}$ | Stimulation freq. | $1/T_{stim}$ | Hz | 20–300 Hz sensory |
| $R_m$ | Membrane resistance | $V_m / I_m$ | Ω·cm² | Resistive membrane model |
| $C_m$ | Membrane capacitance | $Q / V$ | μF/cm² | $\approx 1$ for axon |

---

## 2.5 Pattern Recognition Pipeline (EMG-Based Control)

Modern myoelectric prostheses use **pattern recognition**, not simple threshold switching:

$$\vec{x}(t) = [MAV, RMS, WL, ZC, SSC]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{n}$$

$$\hat{y} = \arg\max_{i} \, P(C_i \mid \vec{x})$$

where $C_i$ is class $i$ (e.g., "hand open", "grasp", "pinch").

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** Pattern recognition allows *multiple degrees of freedom* (DoF) from limited EMG channels — essential for the KTU case study on multifunction prosthetic hands.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Strength–Duration Relationship

**Starting premise:** The nerve membrane behaves as a parallel **RC circuit** with time constant $\tau_m = R_m \cdot C_m$.

### Step 1 — Membrane Model Setup

Apply a rectangular current pulse $I$ of duration $t$. The transmembrane potential change $V_m(t)$ evolves as:

$$V_m(t) = I \cdot R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$

**Explanation:** At $t=0$, capacitor is uncharged (membrane at rest). At $t \rightarrow \infty$, the capacitor saturates and the entire $I \cdot R_m$ drop appears across the membrane.

### Step 2 — Threshold Condition

Neural firing occurs when $V_m(t) \geq V_{th}$. Solve for the minimum current $I_{th}$:

$$V_{th} = I_{th} \cdot R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)$$

Rearranging:

$$I_{th} = \frac{V_{th}}{R_m \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}\right)}$$

### Step 3 — Define Rheobase

As $t \rightarrow \infty$, $e^{-t/\tau_m} \rightarrow 0$:

$$I_{rh} = \frac{V_{th}}{R_m}$$

This is the *rheobase* — minimum current for infinitely long pulse.

### Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify

Substituting $V_{th}/R_m = I_{rh}$ back into Step 2:

$$I_{th} = \frac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-t/\tau_m}}$$

This is the **Weiss equation**. We can simplify using $1 - e^{-x} \approx x$ for small $x$:

$$I_{th} \approx I_{rh} \cdot \frac{\tau_m}{t} \quad \text{for } t \ll \tau_m$$

### Step 5 — Identify Chronaxie

Setting $I_{th} = 2 I_{rh}$:

$$2 I_{rh} = \frac{I_{rh}}{1 - e^{-\tau_{chr}/\tau_m}}$$

$$2(1 - e^{-\tau_{chr}/\tau_m}) = 1$$

$$e^{-\tau_{chr}/\tau_m} = \frac{1}{2}$$

Taking natural logarithm:

$$\frac{\tau_{chr}}{\tau_m} = \ln 2$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{chr} = \tau_m \cdot \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \, \tau_m}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Shannon Safety Limit

For an electrode to remain in the **non-damaging regime**, Shannon (1992) proposed:

$$K = \log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q)$$

with the safety criterion $K \leq 1.85$.

**Derivation logic:**

- Start with charge density $D = Q/A$ in $\mu C/cm^2$
- Take $\log_{10}$ of both quantities (in consistent units)
- Empirical data shows that $K > 1.85$ produces histological damage in cortical tissue

**Worked numerical:**

Given: $I = 200\,\mu A$, $PW = 100\,\mu s$, $A = 0.01\,cm^2$.

$$Q = I \cdot PW = 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 100 \times 10^{-6} = 2 \times 10^{-8}\,C = 0.02\,\mu C$$

$$D = Q/A = 0.02 / 0.01 = 2\,\mu C/cm^2$$

$$K = \log_{10}(2) + \log_{10}(0.02) = 0.301 + (-1.699) = -1.398$$

Since $-1.398 \ll 1.85$, the regime is **safe**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Information Transfer Rate (ITR) for BCI Control

The ITR quantifies how fast a control channel transmits commands:

$$B = \frac{1}{T}\left[\log_2 N + P \log_2 P + (1-P)\log_2\frac{1-P}{N-1}\right]$$

where:
- $N$ = number of possible classes (target choices)
- $P$ = classifier accuracy
- $T$ = average trial duration

**Step-by-step substitution for a 4-class motor imagery BCI:**

- $N = 4$
- $P = 0.90$
- $T = 4\,s$

$$B = \frac{1}{4}\left[\log_2 4 + 0.9 \log_2 0.9 + 0.1 \log_2 \frac{0.1}{3}\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{4}\left[2 + 0.9(-0.152) + 0.1(-4.907)\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{4}\left[2 - 0.137 - 0.491\right]$$

$$= \frac{1}{4}\left[1.372\right]$$

$$= 0.343 \text{ bits/trial}$$

Converting to bits/min:

$$ITR = 0.343 \times \frac{60}{4} = 5.14 \text{ bits/min}$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Hint:** This ITR value (~5 bits/min) is *low* — typical EEG-BCIs achieve 10–25 bits/min, while intracortical BCIs exceed 100 bits/min.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed-Loop Prosthetic Control Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeUser[User Brain Intent] --> nodeAcq[Biosignal Acquisition]
    nodeAcq --> nodePreProc[Preprocessing Amplify Filter]
    nodePreProc --> nodeFeat[Feature Extraction MAV RMS MDF]
    nodeFeat --> nodeDec[Decoder SVM LDA Neural Network]
    nodeDec --> nodeCmd[Command Output Joint Angle Velocity]
    nodeCmd --> nodeAct[Actuator Prosthetic Limb Motor]
    nodeAct --> nodeTask[Task Execution Grasping Walking]
    nodeTask --> nodeSense[Sensory Outcome Tactile Proprioception]
    nodeSense --> nodeFB[Feedback Encoder Electrotactile Neural]
    nodeFB --> nodeUser
    nodeDec -. safety .-> nodeSafe[Safety Supervisor Charge Limit Watchdog]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Solid arrows = forward signal flow. Dashed arrow = supervisory safety link that *interrupts* stimulation if Shannon limit is exceeded. The loop closes via sensory feedback returning to the user's brain.

---

## 4.2 Stimulation Pulse Generator Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA[Microcontroller Command Input] --> nodeB[Pulse Width Generator]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Current Amplifier DAC Controlled]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Output Stage Biphasic H Bridge]
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE[Electrode Tissue Interface]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Nerve Fiber Recruitment]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Neural Response Encoded]
    nodeG -. monitor .-> nodeH[Charge Balance Monitor]
    nodeH -. feedback .-> nodeB
    nodeH -. fault .-> nodeI[Abort Stimulation]
```

---

## 4.3 Control Strategy Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart{Choose Control Strategy}
    nodeStart --> nodeResidual[Residual Limb EMG Present]
    nodeStart --> nodeNoResidual[No Residual Limb / High Amputation]
    nodeResidual --> nodePattern[Pattern Recognition Myoelectric]
    nodeNoResidual --> nodeTarget{Target Application}
    nodeTarget --> nodeCochlear[Cochlear Implant Auditory]
    nodeTarget --> nodeRetinal[Retinal Implant Visual]
    nodeTarget --> nodeMotor[Motor Prosthetic Arm Leg]
    nodeMotor --> nodeBCI[EEG or ECoG BCI]
    nodeBCI --> nodeMotorIm[Intracortical Microelectrode Array]
    nodePattern --> nodeOut1[Direct Muscle Control via Skin Electrodes]
    nodeCochlear --> nodeOut2[Sound to Electrode Mapping Bandpass Filters]
    nodeRetinal --> nodeOut3[Image to Phosphene Mapping Retinal Ganglion]
    nodeMotorIm --> nodeOut4[Decoded Spike Trains to Actuator]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked *"compare control strategies,"* always structure your answer as a **table** comparing invasiveness, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), DoF achievable, training time, and clinical maturity.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define the rheobase and chronaxie of a nerve membrane. Why is chronaxie clinically important for prosthetic stimulation? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

- **Rheobase** ($I_{rh}$): The minimum stimulus current amplitude that can excite a nerve when applied for an infinitely long pulse duration.
- **Chronaxie** ($\tau_{chr}$): The minimum pulse duration required to excite a nerve when the stimulus amplitude is exactly **twice the rheobase** ($\approx 0.693 \tau_m$).
- **Clinical importance:** Chronaxie is *patient-specific* (50–200 μs typically) and serves as a baseline for setting safe, energy-efficient stimulation parameters in implanted pulse generators (IPGs). Stimulation at pulse width ≈ chronaxie is the *most charge-efficient* operating point. **(3 marks)**

---

### Q2. List any three signal sources used to control an upper-limb myoelectric prosthesis. Compare their SNR. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **Surface EMG** — SNR ≈ 10–20 dB (non-invasive, noisy)
2. **Intramuscular EMG** — SNR ≈ 25–35 dB (invasive, stable)
3. **ECoG** — SNR ≈ 30–40 dB (invasive, high frequency content, stable long-term)
4. **(Bonus) Intracortical spikes** — SNR ≈ 40–50 dB (single-unit resolution)

Intracortical signals offer the highest SNR but require craniotomy; surface EMG is clinically preferred for low-cost prosthetics. **(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A: Prosthetic Stimulation Control `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q(a). With neat diagrams, explain the closed-loop control architecture of a myoelectric prosthetic hand. Discuss the role of feature extraction and pattern recognition in decoding user intent.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Architecture (3 marks):** A myoelectric prosthetic hand uses a **closed-loop control architecture** comprising:

1. **Biosignal acquisition** — Surface EMG electrodes on residual forearm muscles (typically 4–8 channels).
2. **Signal conditioning** — Amplification (gain 1000×), bandpass filtering (20–500 Hz), 50 Hz notch.
3. **Feature extraction** — Time-domain features (MAV, RMS, Zero Crossings, Waveform Length) or frequency-domain features (Mean/Median Frequency).
4. **Classification** — Machine learning classifier (LDA, SVM, or ANN) maps features to discrete grasp patterns (open, power grip, pinch, lateral pinch).
5. **Actuation** — DC motors or servo-actuators drive finger joints through cable-driven linkages.
6. **Feedback** — Force sensors in fingertips → electrotactile or vibrotactile stimulation on residual limb.

**Step 2 — Feature Extraction (2 marks):** Time-domain features are computationally cheap:

$$MAV = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\vert x_i \vert$$

$$RMS = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}x_i^2}$$

These features capture *muscle activation intensity*, which correlates with intended grip force.

**Step 3 — Pattern Recognition Role (2 marks):** Pattern recognition transforms *continuous, noisy* EMG into *discrete, reliable* commands. With 4 channels, classification accuracy exceeds 90% for 4–6 grasp types, enabling multifunction control without mode-switching.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q(b). Derive the Weiss strength–duration equation from the RC membrane model. For a nerve with $R_m = 10\,k\Omega\cdot cm^2$, $C_m = 1\,\mu F/cm^2$, and threshold $V_{th} = 15\,mV$, calculate the rheobase and chronaxie.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Membrane Time Constant (1 mark):**

$$\tau_m = R_m \cdot C_m = 10{,}000 \times 1 \times 10^{-6} = 0.01\,s = 10\,ms$$

**Step 2 — Rheobase Calculation (2 marks):**

$$I_{rh} = \frac{V_{th}}{R_m} = \frac{15 \times 10^{-3}}{10{,}000} = 1.5 \times 10^{-6}\,A = 1.5\,\mu A/cm^2$$

**Step 3 — Chronaxie Calculation (2 marks):**

$$\tau_{chr} = \tau_m \cdot \ln 2 = 10 \times 0.693 = 6.93\,ms$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (2 marks):** Any pulse width shorter than 6.93 ms will require current *greater than* 3 μA/cm² to fire the nerve. For an implanted stimulator running at 100 Hz, choosing pulse width at chronaxie ensures minimum charge per pulse, preserving battery life and reducing tissue damage risk.

$$\boxed{I_{rh} = 1.5\,\mu A/cm^2, \quad \tau_{chr} = 6.93\,ms}$$

---

### Question B: BCI-Based Control `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q(a). Explain how motor imagery-based EEG signals can be used to control a prosthetic limb. What are the limitations of EEG-based control compared to intracortical BCIs?**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Motor Imagery Phenomenon (2 marks):** When a user *imagines* moving a limb, sensorimotor cortex exhibits **event-related desynchronization (ERD)** in mu (8–13 Hz) and beta (13–30 Hz) bands over the corresponding cortical area. This is detectable non-invasively via scalp EEG.

**Step 2 — Decoding Pipeline (3 marks):**

1. EEG acquisition (16–64 channels, 250 Hz sampling)
2. Spatial filtering (Common Spatial Patterns — CSP)
3. Band-power feature extraction in mu/beta bands
4. Classification (LDA or SVM) → 2–4 discrete commands
5. Command mapped to prosthetic actions (open/close, left/right)

**Step 3 — Limitations vs Intracortical (2 marks):**

| Aspect | EEG | Intracortical |
|---|---|---|
| Spatial resolution | Centimeter | ~50 μm |
| SNR | Low (10 dB) | High (40 dB) |
| DoF achievable | 2–4 | 7+ |
| Invasiveness | None | Craniotomy |
| Long-term stability | Good | Electrode degradation |
| ITR | 10–25 bits/min | >100 bits/min |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q(b). Calculate the Shannon safety index K for a stimulating electrode with: $I = 500\,\mu A$, pulse width $PW = 200\,\mu s$, electrode area $A = 0.005\,cm^2$. Is this within safe limits? Justify.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Charge per Phase (2 marks):**

$$Q = I \cdot PW = 500 \times 10^{-6} \times 200 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$Q = 1 \times 10^{-7}\,C = 0.1\,\mu C$$

**Step 2 — Charge Density (2 marks):**

$$D = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.1}{0.005} = 20\,\mu C/cm^2$$

**Step 3 — Shannon Index (2 marks):**

$$K = \log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) = \log_{10}(20) + \log_{10}(0.1)$$

$$K = 1.301 + (-1) = 0.301$$

**Step 4 — Verdict (1 mark):** Since $K = 0.301 \leq 1.85$, the regime is **SAFE** for cortical tissue. Note: $D = 20\,\mu C/cm^2$ is also below the $30\,\mu C/cm^2$ geometric limit.

$$\boxed{K = 0.301, \text{ SAFE regime}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing μA with A or μs with s costs 1 mark minimum. Always convert to base units first.
> 2. **Direction of current** — A cathodic-leading biphasic pulse should always have the cathodic phase cited for charge calculation.
> 3. **Rheobase vs threshold** — Confusing $I_{rh}$ (theoretical minimum) with operational threshold $I_{th}$ (at finite pulse width) is a top-failure point.
> 4. **"Per phase" charge** — For a biphasic pulse, total charge is *zero* (balanced); the *damage-relevant* charge is *per phase*. Quote it correctly.
> 5. **Closed-loop vs open-loop** — Examiners frequently test this distinction: open-loop = no feedback (legacy); closed-loop = feedback-modulated (modern).

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Board Revision Sheet — Control of Prosthetic Function**

- [ ] **Rheobase** $I_{rh}$ = current for infinite pulse width; **Chronaxie** $\tau_{chr} = \tau_m \ln 2 \approx 0.693 \tau_m$
- [ ] **Weiss equation:** $I_{th} = I_{rh}/(1 - e^{-t/\tau_m})$
- [ ] **Charge per phase:** $Q = I \cdot PW$
- [ ] **Shannon safety limit:** $\log D + \log Q \leq 1.85$
- [ ] **Charge density limit:** $\leq 30\,\mu C/cm^2$ (Pt electrodes)
- [ ] **Standard pulse parameters:** PW = 50–500 μs, f = 20–300 Hz
- [ ] **EMG band:** 20–500 Hz; notch at 50 Hz
- [ ] **Cortical BCI bands:** mu (8–13 Hz), beta (13–30 Hz)
- [ ] **Pattern recognition features:** MAV, RMS, ZC, WL, MDF, MNF
- [ ] **Closed-loop = feedback present** (modern); **Open-loop = no feedback** (legacy)
- [ ] **Invasive control ranks:** Intracortical spikes > ECoG > intramuscular EMG > surface EMG > EEG
- [ ] **ITR units:** bits/min — intracortical > 100; EEG ~10–25
- [ ] **Bidirectional prostheses:** Motor output **AND** sensory feedback (electrotactile / ICMS)
- [ ] **Always cite pulse parameters in:** A (or mA/μA), μs, Hz, μC/cm²
- [ ] **Mnemonic for safe limits:** "**30 / 0.4 / 1.85**" — Density / Charge / Shannon K

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Terminal Device Electronics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT753 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS AND IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Terminal Device Electronics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Terminal Device Electronics (TDE)** refers to the **entire suite of active and passive electronic subsystems housed within or directly attached to the distal (terminal) end of a neural prosthetic or implant**, which mediates bidirectional signal exchange between the host's biological neural tissue (nerves, muscles, brain) and the external prosthetic controller or environment.

In strict KTU terminology, this is the **"front-end electronics"** of a neuroprosthetic chain. It encompasses:

- **Stimulus generation circuits** (current-mode or voltage-mode stimulators)
- **Signal acquisition / recording front-ends** (low-noise neural amplifiers)
- **Electrode-tissue interface management** circuitry (charge balancing, DC blocking)
- **Multiplexing and channel-selection switches**
- **Hermetic packaging electronics and interconnects**
- **Wireless power and data telemetry modules** (in modern leadless designs)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Universal Translator at the Embassy"**
> Imagine a diplomat (the prosthetic limb or cochlear implant) who only speaks a digital, electrical "language," and the host country citizens (your nerves and brain cells) who only communicate using tiny ionic currents and chemical signals. The **Terminal Device Electronics acts as the simultaneous interpreter and security guard** at the embassy. It must:
> 1. **Translate** the digital commands from the controller into safe ionic-language pulses the nerves understand (stimulation),
> 2. **Listen** to the whispers of the nerves and amplify them so the controller can hear (recording),
> 3. **Prevent diplomatic incidents** (tissue damage) by ensuring no dangerous, unbalanced electrical charge ever crosses the border.

**Standard Metrics in the Field (Industry/Biomedical Constants):**

- **Charge Per Phase (Q):** typically limited to **< 0.4 µC per phase** for safe cortical stimulation.
- **Charge Density (D):** Shannon's safe-limit is **< 30 µC/cm² per phase** (with caveats).
- **Stimulation Frequency:** typically **20–200 Hz** for functional neuromuscular stimulation.
- **Pulse Width:** typically **50–500 µs** for clinical applications.
- **Power Budget:** chronic implants target **< 50 mW** total dissipation.

---

### 1.3 Why TDE is a Distinct Engineering Discipline

Terminal device electronics is **not** simply "small electronics." It must satisfy a unique, brutally restrictive set of constraints that ordinary consumer electronics never face:

| Constraint | Typical Consumer IC | TDE Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| Power source | Battery (replaceable) | Inductively coupled / harvested |
| Lifetime | 2–5 years | **10–30+ years** (chronic implant) |
| Operating environment | Room temperature | **37 °C, saline, hermetically sealed** |
| Failure cost | Inconvenient | **Patient harm or death** |
| Volume constraint | Flexible | **< 1 cm³** for many implants |
| Communication | Wired (USB, etc.) | **Wireless / IR / RF telemetry** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield:** Whenever a question references *safe stimulation*, your answer **must** invoke **charge-balanced biphasic waveforms** and cite **Shannon's limits** on charge (Q) and charge density (D).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-Level Functional Decomposition of TDE

The terminal device electronics, regardless of the specific implant (cochlear, retinal, FES, DBS, BCI), can be decomposed into the following functional blocks:

**Block 1 — Stimulus Waveform Generator (SWG)**
- Produces the biphasic, charge-balanced current/voltage pulse.
- Typical waveform: rectangular, square, or exponentially decaying.
- Parameters programmable: amplitude (I or V), pulse width (PW), interphase gap (IPG), frequency.

**Block 2 — Stimulus Isolator / Output Stage**
- Provides **galvanic isolation** between the digital controller and the tissue electrode.
- Two principal topologies:
  - **Capacitively-coupled (capacitor in series):** naturally blocks DC, easy to balance charge.
  - **Optically-coupled (opto-isolator):** used in some external trial stimulators.

**Block 3 — Electrode-Tissue Interface (ETI) Model**
- Modeled using the **Randles circuit**: solution resistance ($R_s$) in series with the **Warburg impedance** ($Z_w$) in parallel with the **double-layer capacitance** ($C_{dl}$).
- Critical for understanding why **constant-current** stimulation is preferred over constant-voltage (it is independent of changing electrode impedance).

**Block 4 — Recording / Sensing Front-End (when bidirectional)**
- Low-noise amplifier (LNA) with input-referred noise typically **< 5 µV RMS**.
- High input impedance (> **1 GΩ** at 1 kHz) to avoid loading the small neural signals (µV–mV range).
- Band-pass filtering: typically **300 Hz – 5 kHz** for spike activity, **0.5 – 200 Hz** for LFP/ECoG.

**Block 5 — Charge Balance Monitor (Safety Subsystem)**
- Hardware-level watchdog that ensures **the integral of current over a full biphasic cycle is zero**.
- Required by **ISO 14708** and **FDA Class III** implant guidelines.

**Block 6 — Power Management & Telemetry**
- Inductively coupled coils, RF harvesting, or lithium-iodine primary cells.
- Bidirectional telemetry allows the controller to reprogram stimulus parameters and read back electrode impedance.

### 2.2 The Four Critical "Why" Questions

**Why biphasic and not monophasic?**
- Monophasic pulses inject net DC charge → electrolysis of water → generation of toxic $H^+$ and $OH^-$ ions → gas bubbles, pH shift, tissue necrosis.
- Biphasic pulses return the charge in the second phase, keeping net DC = 0.

**Why current-controlled and not voltage-controlled?**
- The electrode-tissue impedance ($Z_{ETI}$) varies over time (fibrosis changes it).
- With constant voltage, the current — and therefore the recruited neural population — would drift.
- With constant current, recruitment is stable regardless of $Z_{ETI}$.

**Why a passive charge-balance capacitor?**
- Active charge balancing is complex and can fail.
- A series DC-blocking capacitor in the output stage is a **passive, fail-safe** way to guarantee zero net DC.
- Limitation: large capacitor required for low-frequency stimulation, which is bulky.

**Why an interphase gap (IPG)?**
- A short pause (typically **30–100 µs**) between the cathodic and anodic phases.
- Allows the **first phase to depolarize the membrane fully** before the reversing (hyperpolarizing) phase arrives.
- Improves selectivity and lowers threshold.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE:** The vertical pipe `\vert` symbol has been used in all math expressions below to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. This is a mandatory substitution for the reserved `|` character.

| # | Formula / Concept | LaTeX Expression | Variables & Units | Notes / KTU Context |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Charge per phase** | $Q = I \cdot t$ | $I$: current (A), $t$: pulse width (s) | $Q$ in Coulombs; clinical limit $< 0.4\,\mu C$ |
| 2 | **Charge density** | $D = Q / A$ | $A$: electrode geometric area ($cm^2$) | $D$ in $\mu C / cm^2$; Shannon limit $< 30$ |
| 3 | **Shannon limit (safe stimulation boundary)** | $\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) < k$ | $k \approx 1.85$ empirically | Linear: $D \cdot Q < 10^{k} \approx 70$ |
| 4 | **Stimulation threshold (Weiss / Lapicque strength-duration)** | $I_{th} = I_{rh} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{\tau_{ch}}{PW}\right)$ | $I_{rh}$: rheobase, $\tau_{ch}$: chronaxie, $PW$: pulse width | Hyperbolic strength-duration curve |
| 5 | **Chronaxie relation** | $\tau_{ch} = 0.693 \cdot R_m \cdot C_m$ | $R_m$: membrane resistance, $C_m$: capacitance | Determines optimum PW (~$ \tau_{ch}$) |
| 6 | **Energy per phase** | $E = I^2 \cdot R \cdot t = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | $R$: access resistance ($\Omega$) | Determines battery / wireless power budget |
| 7 | **Biphasic balance condition** | $\int_{0}^{PW_c} i_c(t)\,dt + \int_{0}^{PW_a} i_a(t)\,dt = 0$ | $c$: cathodic, $a$: anodic | **Mandatory** for FDA / ISO compliance |
| 8 | **Neural amplifier noise (thermal)** | $v_n = \sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | $k_B$: Boltzmann const., $T$: temp, $\Delta f$: bandwidth | Lower $R$ or $\Delta f$ reduces noise |
| 9 | **CMRR of recording front-end** | $CMRR = 20 \log_{10}\left(\vert A_{dm} / A_{cm} \vert\right)$ | $A_{dm}$: diff. gain, $A_{cm}$: common-mode gain | Should be $> 80$ dB |
| 10 | **Telemetry link (Friis equation)** | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$ | $P$: power, $G$: gain, $d$: distance, $\lambda$: wavelength | Used for RF power budget |
| 11 | **Stimulus power dissipation** | $P = I^2 \cdot R_{ETI}$ | $R_{ETI}$: electrode-tissue resistance | Drives heat-budget of hermetic package |
| 12 | **Time constant of tissue** | $\tau_{tissue} = R_s \cdot C_{dl}$ | $R_s$: solution R, $C_{dl}$: double-layer C | Sets lower limit on usable PW |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cochlear implants (Cochlear Nucleus, Advanced Bionics):** use **current-steering** terminal electronics to virtually "steer" the stimulus current between adjacent electrodes, creating up to **120+ virtual channels** from 16 physical electrodes.
- **Deep Brain Stimulators (Medtronic Activa, Abbott Infinity):** use **constant-current biphasic** TDE with **impedance monitoring** telemetry.
- **Retinal prostheses (Argus II):** use a **video-processing front-end + inductively powered TDE coil** to drive a 60-electrode epiretinal array.
- **Cardiac pacemakers:** pioneering example of TDE — pure **voltage-controlled, capacitor-discharge** through a biphasic H-bridge.
- **Brain-Computer Interfaces (Neuralink, Utah Array):** modern TDE integrates **on-chip ADC + digital compression** directly at the electrode.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Biphasic Charge-Balance Condition

**Starting Premise:** A safe neural stimulator must deposit **zero net DC charge** across the electrode-tissue interface over one full stimulation cycle to prevent electrolysis.

**Step 1: Define the cathodic phase.**
The cathodic (depolarizing) phase is a constant-current pulse of amplitude $I_c$ for a duration $PW_c$.

$$
Q_{cathode} = \int_{0}^{PW_c} I_c \, dt = I_c \cdot PW_c
$$

**Step 2: Define the anodic phase.**
The anodic (charge-recovery) phase immediately follows, with amplitude $I_a$ and duration $PW_a$ (often identical to $PW_c$).

$$
Q_{anode} = \int_{0}^{PW_a} I_a \, dt = I_a \cdot PW_a
$$

**Step 3: Apply the charge balance condition.**

$$
Q_{cathode} + Q_{anode} = 0
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the integrals.**

$$
I_c \cdot PW_c + I_a \cdot PW_a = 0
$$

**Step 5: Solve for the anodic current required.**

$$
I_a = -I_c \cdot \frac{PW_c}{PW_a}
$$

**Step 6: Special case (symmetric biphasic).**
If $PW_c = PW_a = PW$, then $I_a = -I_c$. The pulse is **symmetric** — equally positive and negative.

**Step 7: Engineering interpretation.**
> [!NOTE]
> The "–" sign indicates the **second phase must reverse polarity** relative to the first. This is the mathematical basis for the H-bridge output stage used in every clinical-grade stimulator.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Charge Density and the Shannon Safety Limit

**Step 1: Recall the definition of charge per phase.**

$$
Q = I \cdot t \quad [\text{Coulombs}]
$$

**Step 2: Define charge density** as charge per unit geometric electrode area.

$$
D = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{I \cdot t}{A} \quad [\mu C / cm^2]
$$

**Step 3: Shannon (1992) empirically observed** that for platinum electrodes, the boundary between safe stimulation and tissue-damaging stimulation satisfies:

$$
\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) \le k
$$

where $D$ is in $\mu C / cm^2$ and $Q$ is in $\mu C$.

**Step 4: Use the logarithm property** $\log a + \log b = \log(ab)$:

$$
\log_{10}(D \cdot Q) \le k
$$

**Step 5: Exponentiate both sides.**

$$
D \cdot Q \le 10^{k}
$$

**Step 6: Plug in the empirical constant** $k \approx 1.85$ (for k = log(70)):

$$
D \cdot Q \le 10^{1.85} \approx 70 \;\; \mu C^2 / cm^2
$$

**Step 7: Design interpretation.**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> A safe stimulator must operate **below the k = 1.85 line** on a log–log plot of $D$ vs $Q$. Crossing the boundary means an unacceptable risk of tissue damage, regardless of whether $D$ or $Q$ individually appears "small."

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Strength-Duration Curve (Lapicque / Weiss Equation)

**Step 1: Empirical starting point —** the threshold current $I_{th}$ required to depolarize a nerve follows a hyperbolic relationship with pulse width $PW$.

**Step 2: Define rheobase ($I_{rh}$).** This is the **minimum current** that will excite the nerve given an infinitely long pulse.

$$
\lim_{PW \to \infty} I_{th}(PW) = I_{rh}
$$

**Step 3: Define chronaxie ($\tau_{ch}$).** This is the pulse width at which the threshold current is exactly **twice the rheobase** — a key metric for choosing the operating $PW$.

$$
\tau_{ch} = PW \big|_{I_{th} = 2 I_{rh}}
$$

**Step 4: The Weiss (1901) / Lapicque equation:**

$$
I_{th}(PW) = I_{rh} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{\tau_{ch}}{PW} \right)
$$

**Step 5: Verify the limit.**

$$
\lim_{PW \to \infty} I_{th} = I_{rh} \cdot (1 + 0) = I_{rh} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 6: Verify chronaxie definition** by setting $I_{th} = 2 I_{rh}$:

$$
2 I_{rh} = I_{rh} \left(1 + \frac{\tau_{ch}}{PW}\right) \implies 2 = 1 + \frac{\tau_{ch}}{PW} \implies PW = \tau_{ch} \;\;\checkmark
$$

**Step 7: Engineering interpretation.**
- Operating at $PW = \tau_{ch}$ is the **energy-optimum point** for stimulation.
- Typical chronaxie values: **30–200 µs** for peripheral nerve, **100–500 µs** for cortical neurons.
- A TDE designer chooses $PW \approx \tau_{ch}$ to **minimize charge and power** for a given threshold.

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Energy and Power Dissipated per Stimulus Pulse

**Step 1: The instantaneous power into the tissue** is $P(t) = I^2(t) \cdot R_{ETI}$ for a current-controlled pulse (assuming $R_{ETI}$ is constant for the short pulse).

**Step 2: Energy per cathodic phase.**

$$
E_c = \int_{0}^{PW_c} I_c^2 \cdot R_{ETI} \, dt = I_c^2 \cdot R_{ETI} \cdot PW_c
$$

**Step 3: Energy per anodic phase (assuming symmetric).**

$$
E_a = I_a^2 \cdot R_{ETI} \cdot PW_a = I_c^2 \cdot R_{ETI} \cdot PW_c = E_c
$$

**Step 4: Total energy per stimulation cycle.**

$$
E_{cycle} = E_c + E_a = 2 I_c^2 \cdot R_{ETI} \cdot PW_c
$$

**Step 5: Average power with stimulation frequency $f_s$ (pulses per second per channel).**

$$
P_{avg} = E_{cycle} \cdot f_s = 2 I_c^2 \cdot R_{ETI} \cdot PW_c \cdot f_s
$$

**Step 6: Numerical sanity check.**
For $I_c = 1$ mA, $R_{ETI} = 1$ k$\Omega$, $PW_c = 100\,\mu s$, $f_s = 50$ Hz:

$$
P_{avg} = 2 \cdot (10^{-3})^2 \cdot 10^3 \cdot 10^{-4} \cdot 50 = 10^{-5}\,\text{W} = 10\,\mu\text{W per channel}
$$

**Step 7: For a 100-channel implant, total power = 1 mW** — well within the inductive power budget of typical TDE.

### 3.5 Analytical Summary Table

| Quantity | Formula | Typical Magnitude (Clinical) | Driving Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| Charge per phase, $Q$ | $I \cdot t$ | 0.05 – 0.4 µC | Shannon limit |
| Charge density, $D$ | $Q/A$ | 5 – 30 µC/cm² | Tissue safety |
| Threshold current, $I_{th}$ | Weiss equation | 0.1 – 5 mA | Recruitment level |
| Optimum PW | $\approx \tau_{ch}$ | 50 – 300 µs | Power minimization |
| Power per channel | $2 I^2 R PW f_s$ | 1 – 50 µW | Battery / link budget |
| Recording noise | $\sqrt{4 k_B T R \Delta f}$ | 1 – 5 µV RMS | Spike detection SNR |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Terminal Device Electronics

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[External Controller / Host Computer] --> B[Inductive Telemetry Link RX]
    B --> C[Power Recovery and Regulation Block]

    G --> K[Charge Balance Monitor Watchdog]
    K --> L[Safety Interlock Logic]
    L -.-> E
    I --> M[Neural Recording Amplifier LNA]
    M --> N[Band Pass Filter 300 Hz to 5 kHz]
    N --> O[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    O --> P[Telemetry Link TX]

    C --> Q[Voltage Reference and Bias Network]

    Q --> E

```

### 4.2 State Machine — Stimulus Output Cycle (KTU Favourite Diagram)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle
    Idle --> ChargingCap : Command Received
    ChargingCap --> CathodicPulse : Vcap reaches threshold
    CathodicPulse --> InterphaseGap : PW timer expires
    InterphaseGap --> AnodicPulse : IPG timer expires
    AnodicPulse --> ChargeBalanceCheck : PW timer expires
    ChargeBalanceCheck --> Idle : Net DC equals zero
    ChargeBalanceCheck --> SafeShutdown : Net DC exceeds epsilon
    SafeShutdown --> [*]
    Idle --> RecordingMode : Mode bit set
    RecordingMode --> Idle : Mode bit cleared
```

### 4.3 Signal Flow — Biphasic Pulse Generation (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Clock Generator] --> B[Monostable Cathodic Timer]
    A --> C[Monostable Interphase Gap Timer]
    A --> D[Monostable Anodic Timer]
    B --> E[H Bridge Driver Positive Leg]
    D --> F[H Bridge Driver Negative Leg]
    E --> G[Electrode Pair]
    F --> G
    C --> H[Dead Time Interlock]
    H --> E
    H --> F
    G --> I[Tissue]
```

### 4.4 Hierarchical System Map — Where TDE Sits in a Full Prosthetic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Application Layer: Patient Intent and Environment] --> B[Signal Processing Layer: Decoder and Classifier]
    B --> C[Controller Layer: Stimulation Strategy Engine]
    C --> D[Terminal Device Electronics TDE]
    D --> E[Neural Tissue and Electrode Interface]
    E --> F[Biomechanical Output: Limb, Cochlea, Retina, etc]
    F --> A
    style D fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#990000,color:#000,stroke-width:3px
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block highlighted in red is the **Terminal Device Electronics** — the *physical* layer that bridges abstract control strategy with wet, living tissue.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper Set B]**

> Define the term **"Charge Density"** in the context of neural stimulation. Why is it considered a critical safety parameter in terminal device electronics? State the commonly used clinical limit.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Charge density $D$ is defined as the **charge delivered per phase of a stimulation pulse divided by the geometric surface area of the stimulating electrode**.

$$
D = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{I \cdot t}{A} \quad [\mu C / cm^2]
$$

**Why critical (1 Mark):**
It is critical because **high charge density** leads to electrochemical reactions at the electrode surface, including **water electrolysis, generation of toxic species, and pH shifts**, all of which cause irreversible tissue damage. Shannon's empirical studies established charge density as one of the two axes defining the safe-stimulation window.

**Clinical limit (1 Mark):**
The commonly cited safe upper limit is **30 µC/cm² per phase** for platinum electrodes, in conjunction with charge-per-phase limits (Shannon boundary: $\log D + \log Q \le 1.85$).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

> Why are **biphasic** stimulation pulses universally used in clinical neural prostheses instead of monophasic pulses?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Charge Balance (1 Mark):** A biphasic pulse consists of a cathodic (depolarizing) phase followed by an anodic (charge-recovery) phase, ensuring **zero net DC charge** transfer across the electrode-tissue interface.

2. **Prevention of Electrolysis (1 Mark):** Monophasic pulses deliver net DC, causing **electrolysis of water** in tissue ($2H_2O \to H_2 + 2OH^-$ and $2H_2O \to O_2 + 4H^+$), producing gas bubbles, pH shifts, and tissue necrosis. Biphasic pulses eliminate this.

3. **Long-term Implant Safety (1 Mark):** Biphasic operation enables **chronic stimulation** over years without electrode corrosion or neural damage, satisfying **ISO 14708** and **FDA Class III** safety standards for implantable devices.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (with Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Suggested Module 3 Choice]`

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a typical terminal device electronics (TDE) module** in an implantable neural stimulator. Clearly label all major sub-blocks. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **biphasic charge-balance condition** mathematically. What is the role of a **DC-blocking capacitor** in this context, and what is its principal design limitation? **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-A:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The functional architecture of a typical TDE consists of the following sub-blocks (each 0.5 mark, with 0.5 additional for diagram and integration):

1. **Stimulus Waveform Generator (SWG):** Digital block that produces timing and amplitude references for the pulse. (0.5)

2. **Current-Mode Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC):** Converts the digital amplitude word into a precise analog current. (0.5)

3. **H-Bridge Output Stage:** A four-transistor switching network that **reverses polarity** between the cathodic and anodic phases. (0.5)

4. **DC-Blocking Capacitor:** A series capacitor between the H-bridge and the electrode, providing **passive DC isolation**. (0.5)

5. **Charge-Balance Monitor:** Compares integrated current of the two phases and asserts a fault signal if imbalance is detected. (0.5)

6. **Electrode-Tissue Interface:** Modeled as a Randles equivalent circuit — $R_s$ in series with $C_{dl} \parallel Z_w$. (0.5)

7. **Recording Front-End (LNA + BPF + ADC):** For bidirectional implants, captures neural activity in the 300 Hz – 5 kHz band. (0.5)

8. **Inductive Telemetry RX/TX & Power Recovery:** Provides wireless power and bidirectional data. (1.0 for overall system integration explanation + diagram presentation)

**Neat Block Diagram (already shown in Section 4.1 above).**

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Define the charge-balance condition. Net DC over a full cycle must be zero:

$$
\int_{cycle} i(t) \, dt = 0
$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Evaluate for a rectangular biphasic waveform with $I_c$, $PW_c$, $I_a$, $PW_a$:

$$
I_c \cdot PW_c + I_a \cdot PW_a = 0
$$

$$
I_a = -I_c \cdot \frac{PW_c}{PW_a}
$$

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** Show the special case for symmetric biphasic ($PW_c = PW_a$): $I_a = -I_c$.

**Step 4 (1 Mark):** Discuss the role of the DC-blocking capacitor: it acts as a **high-pass filter** that cannot pass DC, so any drift in the H-bridge mismatch is automatically removed.

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Design limitation: for a low-frequency stimulation waveform, the blocking capacitor must be **very large** to avoid re-shaping the pulse. For $f_s = 50$ Hz and pulse amplitude 1 mA, $C$ may need to be in the µF range — too large for monolithic integration. This is why **active charge-balancing circuits** are used in modern TDE.

**Step 6 (1 Mark):** Conclusion — a passive DC-blocking capacitor is **simple and fail-safe**, but its **size penalty** is the main reason modern designs use **active charge balancing** with calibration DACs.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Suggested Module 3 Choice]`

> **(a)** Explain the **Lapicque / Weiss strength-duration relationship** for neural stimulation. Define **rheobase** and **chronaxie**, and explain why designing $PW$ close to $\tau_{ch}$ is energy-optimal. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A stimulating electrode has a geometric surface area of **0.05 cm²** and the tissue interface resistance is **1.2 kΩ**. The stimulator delivers a **constant-current cathodic pulse** of amplitude **2 mA** for **200 µs**, immediately followed by a symmetric anodic recharge phase. Stimulation frequency is **50 Hz**.
>   (i) Calculate the **charge per phase** and the **charge density**.
>   (ii) Check whether the operating point lies within Shannon's safe stimulation window ($k = 1.85$).
>   (iii) Compute the **average power dissipation** per channel. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Q-B:**

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** The Weiss (1901) equation relates threshold current $I_{th}$ to pulse width $PW$:

$$
I_{th}(PW) = I_{rh} \cdot \left( 1 + \frac{\tau_{ch}}{PW} \right)
$$

**Step 2 (1 Mark):** **Rheobase** ($I_{rh}$) is the minimum current that will excite the nerve as $PW \to \infty$. (1)

**Step 3 (1 Mark):** **Chronaxie** ($\tau_{ch}$) is the pulse width at which $I_{th} = 2 I_{rh}$, reflecting the **membrane time constant** of the nerve. (1)

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Energy-optimal design: at $PW = \tau_{ch}$, the threshold current is exactly twice the rheobase. Multiplying charge ($I \cdot t$) gives the **minimum charge** needed for excitation. The chronaxie is biologically determined as $\tau_{ch} \approx 0.693 R_m C_m$. Choosing $PW \approx \tau_{ch}$ minimizes both charge and energy per pulse. (2)

**Step 5 (1 Mark):** Practical implication: for peripheral nerve stimulation, $\tau_{ch} \approx 30 - 200\,\mu s$, so PW is chosen in that range. (1)

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $I = 2$ mA, $PW = 200$ µs, $A = 0.05$ cm², $R = 1.2$ kΩ, $f_s = 50$ Hz.

**(i) Charge per phase and charge density (2 Marks):**

$$
Q = I \cdot t = 2 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 200 \times 10^{-6} = 4 \times 10^{-7}\,C = 0.4\,\mu C
$$

$$
D = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.4\,\mu C}{0.05\,cm^2} = 8\,\mu C / cm^2
$$

**(ii) Shannon safety check (3 Marks):**

$$
\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) = \log_{10}(8) + \log_{10}(0.4)
$$

$$
= 0.903 + (-0.398) = 0.505
$$

Since $0.505 \le 1.85$, the operating point is **well within the safe stimulation window**. (3)

**(iii) Average power per channel (2 Marks):**

$$
P_{avg} = 2 \cdot I^2 \cdot R \cdot PW \cdot f_s
$$

$$
= 2 \cdot (2 \times 10^{-3})^2 \cdot 1200 \cdot 200 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 50
$$

$$
= 2 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1200 \cdot 10^{-4} \cdot 50
$$

$$
= 2 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 6 = 48 \times 10^{-6}\,W = 48\,\mu W
$$

**Conclusion:** The design is safe (Shannon-compliant) and dissipates only ~48 µW per channel. (2)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose 1–2 Marks Each):**
> 1. **Forgetting units in calculations** — Always state µC, µC/cm², µW, etc. A numerical answer without units is awarded **zero** in KTU strict valuation.
> 2. **Confusing *charge per phase* with *charge density*** — they are NOT the same. $Q$ is in Coulombs, $D$ is in µC/cm². Examiners will deduct 0.5 mark if you mix these up.
> 3. **Using constant-voltage assumptions when the problem says constant-current** — read the stem carefully. A constant-current assumption is the *default* in modern TDE problems.
> 4. **Skipping the biphasic second phase in power calculations** — remember to multiply by 2 (or by the duty ratio) for the full cycle.
> 5. **Stating the Shannon constant as exactly 1.85 without citing the underlying condition** — write it as $\log D + \log Q \le k$, with $k = 1.85$, not just "the limit is 70."
> 6. **Writing "monophasic" in a clinical context** — this is an automatic **0** in any safety-related question. Monophasic = unsafe in 99% of clinical scenarios.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist before entering the exam hall:**

- [ ] **Terminal Device Electronics (TDE)** = front-end electronics that physically interface with neural tissue.
- [ ] **Biphasic stimulation is mandatory** for chronic implants — never monophasic.
- [ ] **Charge per phase:** $Q = I \cdot t$. **Limit:** $< 0.4\,\mu C$ typically.
- [ ] **Charge density:** $D = Q / A$. **Limit:** $< 30\,\mu C / cm^2$ (Shannon).
- [ ] **Shannon limit:** $\log_{10}(D) + \log_{10}(Q) \le 1.85$ — memorize the form, not the number.
- [ ] **Constant-current** > constant-voltage (impedance-independent recruitment).
- [ ] **Lapicque / Weiss equation:** $I_{th} = I_{rh}(1 + \tau_{ch}/PW)$.
- [ ] **Rheobase** = min current for infinite PW; **Chronaxie** = PW at $I_{th} = 2 I_{rh}$.
- [ ] **Interphase gap (IPG)** improves selectivity; typical 30–100 µs.
- [ ] **H-bridge** provides polarity reversal for biphasic pulse.
- [ ] **DC-blocking capacitor** provides fail-safe charge balance; size penalty at low $f_s$.
- [ ] **Active charge balance monitor** is a hardware safety watchdog (ISO/FDA requirement).
- [ ] **Electrode-tissue interface** is modeled as **Randles circuit**: $R_s$ + ($C_{dl} \parallel Z_w$).
- [ ] **Recording front-end** needs $> 80$ dB CMRR, $< 5\,\mu V_{RMS}$ noise, G$\Omega$ input impedance.
- [ ] **Power budget** for chronic implant: $< 50$ mW total, often tens of µW per channel.
- [ ] **Telemetry** is wireless — inductive coupling (cochlear) or RF (BCI).
- [ ] **Power dissipation per pulse:** $P_{avg} = 2 I^2 R_{ETI} PW f_s$.
- [ ] **Block diagram** of TDE: SWG → DAC → H-bridge → DC-blocking cap → ETI → Tissue.
- [ ] **Bidirectional implants** add: Tissue → LNA → BPF → ADC → Telemetry TX.
- [ ] **Hermetic packaging** (titanium / ceramic) is required for 10–30 year implant life.
- [ ] **Safety standards:** ISO 14708, FDA Class III, IEC 60601-1.
- [ ] **Common clinical examples to cite:** Cochlear (Nucleus), DBS (Activa), Retinal (Argus II), Pacemaker.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *"Biphasic, constant current, charge-balanced, Shannon-compliant, hermetically sealed, wirelessly powered."* — If you can write this from memory in 30 seconds, you are exam-ready for Module 3 — Terminal Device Electronics.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pre-Prosthetic Assessment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEBT753 - NEURAL PROSTHESIS AND IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Pre-Prosthetic Assessment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Prosthetic Assessment** is the systematic, multidisciplinary clinical evaluation performed **before** the fabrication and fitting of a prosthetic device, aimed at determining the patient's candidacy, functional baseline, residual limb status, psychological readiness, and rehabilitation goals. It establishes the engineering and clinical blueprint that guides socket design, component selection, and the subsequent prosthetic training protocol.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEBT753 – Neural Prosthesis and Implants), pre-prosthetic assessment is a **gatekeeper process** — no prosthesis is prescribed without a thorough assessment. It is the clinical foundation upon which all downstream steps (casting, socket fit, alignment, training) depend.

### Sub-Components of Pre-Prosthetic Assessment

The pre-prosthetic assessment is conventionally divided into the following sequential and parallel evaluations:

1. **Medical / Surgical Assessment** – wound healing, amputation etiology, comorbidities.
2. **Residual Limb (Stump) Evaluation** – length, shape, skin condition, scars, edema.
3. **Musculoskeletal Assessment** – joint range of motion (ROM), muscle strength, contractures.
4. **Neurological Assessment** – sensation, phantom limb pain, nerve integrity.
5. **Functional / Mobility Assessment** – balance, gait, transfers, activities of daily living (ADL).
6. **Psychological / Cognitive Assessment** – motivation, body image, cognitive capacity.
7. **Vocational / Lifestyle Assessment** – occupation, environment, recreational needs.
8. **Goal Setting & Prosthetic Prescription** – K-level determination, component matching.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Tailoring a Custom Suit**
>
> Imagine buying a custom suit. The tailor does **not** start cutting fabric on day one. Instead, he first:
> - Measures your shoulders, chest, arms, posture (physical assessment),
> - Asks about the occasion — wedding, daily office, sports (lifestyle/goal assessment),
> - Checks for any physical limitations — one shoulder higher, a stiff elbow (musculoskeletal),
> - Confirms you actually *want* and are *ready* to wear a suit (psychological readiness).
>
> Only after this "pre-tailoring assessment" does he cut the fabric (fabricate the socket) and stitch (assemble) the suit. The prosthetic process is identical — the **stump is the patient's "body"** and the **prosthesis is the "suit"**. A poor assessment leads to a poor fit, discomfort, and rejection of the device.

## 1.3 Standard Clinical Metrics Used

The following standardized metrics are universally applied during pre-prosthetic assessment:

| Metric | Standard Value / Tool | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **K-Level** | **0 to 4** (Medicare/Medicaid classification) | Defines mobility potential and prosthetic component eligibility |
| **ROM (Range of Motion)** | Hip: 0°–120° flexion; Knee: 0°–135° | Determines joint function preservation |
| **Residual Limb Length** | Measured in **cm** from bony landmark | Socket fitting & leverage |
| **Preamputation Mobility Score** | **0–46** (Houghton Scale) | Predicts prosthetic use success |
| **Phantom Pain Score** | **0–10** (Visual Analog Scale, VAS) | Quantifies neuropathic pain |
| **Barthel Index (ADL)** | **0–100** | Functional independence measure |
| **Timed Up and Go (TUG)** | **< 10 s normal; > 20 s high fall risk** | Dynamic balance assessment |
| **Berg Balance Scale** | **0–56** | Static and dynamic balance |

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Six-Phase Pre-Prosthetic Workflow

The complete pre-prosthetic assessment follows a structured **six-phase clinical pathway**. Each phase builds on the previous, and skipping a phase increases the risk of prosthesis rejection.

### Phase 1 — Patient History & Amputation Etiology

The clinician documents:
- **Cause of amputation**: trauma, vascular disease (PVD), diabetes, tumor, congenital.
- **Date of amputation** and healing timeline.
- **Previous prosthetic use** (if any) and reasons for failure.
- **Co-morbidities**: diabetes, peripheral neuropathy, cardiac disease, stroke.
- **Dominant limb** and hand preference (for upper-limb cases).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners frequently ask why diabetic patients with PVD have a *lower* K-level ceiling. The answer lies in the *vascular* compromise of the contralateral limb and reduced cardiopulmonary reserve.

### Phase 2 — Residual Limb (Stump) Assessment

Key parameters evaluated:

- **Shape**: cylindrical, conical, bulbous, irregular.
- **Length**: measured from the medial joint line (for TF — Transfemoral) or tibial tubercle (for TT — Transtibial).
- **Skin integrity**: scar adhesion, ulceration, grafting status, hypersensitivity.
- **Soft tissue**: padding, muscle atrophy, redundant tissue.
- **Bone**: sharp edges, spurs, myodesis/myoplasty status.
- **Neuroma presence**: Tinel's sign, palpation tenderness.

### Phase 3 — Musculoskeletal Evaluation

ROM is measured using a **universal goniometer**:

$$\theta_{measured} = \theta_{fulcrum\ axis} - \theta_{baseline}$$

Muscle strength is graded using the **Medical Research Council (MRC) Scale**:

| Grade | Symbol | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | Zero | No contraction |
| 1 | Trace | Flicker |
| 2 | Poor | Movement with gravity eliminated |
| 3 | Fair | Movement against gravity |
| 4 | Good | Movement against resistance |
| 5 | Normal | Full power |

### Phase 4 — Neurological & Phantom Pain Evaluation

- **Dermatomal mapping** for sensation.
- **Phantom limb sensation** vs **phantom limb pain** vs **residual limb pain** — distinguished clinically.
- **Tinel's sign** for neuroma localization.
- VAS scoring (0–10) for pain intensity.

### Phase 5 — Functional Assessment

- Transfers (bed-to-chair, sit-to-stand).
- Static balance (single-leg stance time).
- Dynamic balance (TUG test).
- Gait analysis (with or without assistive device).
- ADL scoring (Barthel Index).

### Phase 6 — Psychological & Vocational Profiling

- Motivation, coping style.
- Body image and self-esteem.
- Cognitive screening (MMSE if indicated).
- Vocational goals — return to work, sports, ADL independence.

## 2.2 K-Level Classification (Functional Levels)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **K-Level** is a Medicare-defined, five-tier (K0–K4) functional classification system that **directly determines** the prosthetic components reimbursed and prescribed. It is the single most important decision-output of pre-prosthetic assessment.

| K-Level | Description | Typical Component Class |
|---|---|---|
| **K0** | No ability to ambulate | Cosmetic / no prosthesis |
| **K1** | Household ambulator | Basic mechanical knee, SACH foot |
| **K2** | Limited community ambulator | Single-axis knee, multi-axial foot |
| **K3** | Community ambulator, variable cadence | Hydraulic/pneumatic knee, energy-storing foot |
| **K4** | High-level athlete / vigorous activity | Microprocessor knee, running blade |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** K-level is **not** a measure of current ability but of **potential/expected** ability after rehabilitation. Prescribing a K4 component to a K2 patient (or vice versa) is a common board-exam trap.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula, index, and classification used in pre-prosthetic assessment. This is the **single source of truth** for numerical problem-solving in KTU exams.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Standard Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Body Mass Index (BMI) | $\text{BMI} = \dfrac{m}{h^2}$ | kg/m² | $m$ = mass, $h$ = height |
| 2 | Ideal Stump Length (TT) | $L_{ideal} \approx 12.5\text{–}17.5$ | cm | From medial tibial plateau |
| 3 | Ideal Stump Length (TF) | $L_{ideal} \approx 20\text{–}25$ | cm (from greater trochanter) | Preserves lever arm |
| 4 | ROM deficit trigger | $\Delta\theta < 90°$ flexion (hip) | degrees | Hip flexion contracture risk |
| 5 | TUG test threshold | $t_{TUG} > 20\,\text{s}$ | seconds | High fall risk |
| 6 | Barthel Index | $I_{Barthel} = \sum_{i=1}^{10} s_i$ | score 0–100 | Sum of 10 ADL subscores |
| 7 | VAS pain | $V_{pain} \in [0, 10]$ | dimensionless | 0 = no pain, 10 = worst |
| 8 | K-Level index | $K \in \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4\}$ | ordinal | Determines component tier |
| 9 | Stump Volume (cylindrical) | $V = \pi r^{2} h$ | cm³ | Used for shrinker socks |
| 10 | Single-leg stance time | $t_{SLS} < 5\,\text{s}$ (elderly) | seconds | Fall risk indicator |
| 11 | Berg Balance cutoff | $B_{Berg} < 45$ | score /56 | High fall risk |
| 12 | Stump circumference change | $\Delta C_{stump} = \dfrac{C_{final} - C_{initial}}{C_{initial}} \times 100$ | % | Volumetric stability check |
| 13 | Energy Expenditure (TT) | $E_{TT} \approx 1.25 \times E_{normal}$ | relative cost | Compared to able-bodied |
| 14 | Energy Expenditure (TF) | $E_{TF} \approx 1.62 \times E_{normal}$ | relative cost | Higher metabolic cost |
| 15 | Suspension force balance | $F_{susp} = m \cdot g + F_{swing}$ | Newtons | $g = 9.81\,$m/s² |

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimum Residual Limb Length Ratio

The ideal stump length ensures the **leverage** required for socket control while preserving **joint function**. For a transtibial (TT) amputation, the optimum is derived from the lever-arm principle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Principle:** The longer the residual lever, the lower the force required at the distal end to generate a given torque about the proximal joint.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the torque about the knee joint.**

The quadriceps force $F_Q$ acting on the stump must generate a knee extension torque $\tau_{ext}$ to support body weight during stance:

$$\tau_{ext} = F_Q \cdot d_Q$$

where $d_Q$ is the moment arm of the quadriceps (≈ 5 cm in an average adult).

**Step 2 — The body weight reaction force acts at the center of mass.**

The ground reaction force creates an external flexion moment about the knee:

$$\tau_{flex} = W \cdot d_{GRF}$$

where $W = m g$ is body weight and $d_{GRF}$ is the horizontal distance from the knee joint axis to the line of action of the ground reaction force (typically $\approx 5$–$10$ cm during single-limb stance).

**Step 3 — Equilibrium of moments.**

For static standing on the prosthetic side:

$$F_Q \cdot d_Q = W \cdot d_{GRF}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the quadriceps force.**

$$F_Q = \frac{W \cdot d_{GRF}}{d_Q}$$

**Step 5 — Introduce the stump length $L$ as the effective lever.**

The socket must transmit this force from the soft tissue envelope. The contact pressure $p$ on the stump is distributed over the residual limb's surface area. For a cylindrical approximation:

$$p = \frac{F_Q}{A_{stump}} = \frac{F_Q}{2\pi r L}$$

**Step 6 — Apply the comfort constraint (skin tolerance).**

Human skin tolerates sustained pressure up to approximately $p_{max} \approx 35$ kPa (this is a clinically accepted soft-tissue tolerance value used in socket design):

$$p \leq p_{max} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \frac{F_Q}{2\pi r L} \leq p_{max}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the minimum stump length.**

$$L \geq \frac{F_Q}{2\pi r \, p_{max}}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute typical values for a 70 kg adult.**

Let:
- $W = 70 \times 9.81 = 686.7$ N
- $d_{GRF} = 0.07$ m
- $d_Q = 0.05$ m
- $r = 0.05$ m (≈ 10 cm stump circumference)
- $p_{max} = 35{,}000$ Pa

**Step 9 — Compute $F_Q$.**

$$F_Q = \frac{686.7 \times 0.07}{0.05} = \frac{48.07}{0.05} = 961.4 \,\text{N}$$

**Step 10 — Compute $L_{min}$.**

$$L_{min} = \frac{961.4}{2 \pi \times 0.05 \times 35{,}000}$$

$$L_{min} = \frac{961.4}{10{,}995.6} \approx 0.0874 \,\text{m} \approx 8.7 \,\text{cm}$$

**Step 11 — Clinical reconciliation.**

The derived minimum of $\approx 8.7$ cm is below the clinically preferred 12.5–17.5 cm range, which means comfort constraints (not just strength) dominate. The clinical window balances **leverage**, **socket fit**, and **joint preservation** — yielding the textbook optimum:

$$L_{TT,\,optimal} \approx 12.5\text{–}17.5 \,\text{cm}$$

$$L_{TF,\,optimal} \approx 20\text{–}25 \,\text{cm}$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Relative Energy Expenditure Index

Patients with amputation expend more energy during walking. The **Relative Cost (RC)** is defined as:

$$RC = \frac{\dot{V}O_{2,\,amputee}}{\dot{V}O_{2,\,normal}}$$

where $\dot{V}O_2$ is the rate of oxygen consumption (mL O₂/kg·min).

**Step 1 — Normal walking RC for TT amputee.**

$$RC_{TT} \approx 1.25 \quad \text{(25% more energy than able-bodied)}$$

**Step 2 — Normal walking RC for TF amputee.**

$$RC_{TF} \approx 1.62 \quad \text{(62% more energy)}$$

**Step 3 — Bilateral TF amputee.**

$$RC_{bilateral\,TF} \approx 2.5\text{–}3.0 \quad \text{(up to 200% more energy)}$$

**Step 4 — Clinical implication for K-level.**

Higher energy cost → patient fatigues faster → cannot ambulate at variable cadence → **capped at a lower K-level**. This is a frequently-tested KTU concept.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pre-Prosthetic Findings → Prosthetic Decision

> [!NOTE]
> The matrix below maps **clinical findings** (rows) to **prosthetic decisions** (columns). It is a high-yield KTU board-exam summary.

| Clinical Finding | Socket Implication | Component Implication | K-Level Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| Short TT stump (< 8 cm) | Need PTB supracondylar design | Use locking pin suspension | K1–K2 ceiling |
| Hip flexion contracture (≥ 15°) | Adjust socket flexion angle | Choose locked or stance-control knee | Lowers K to 1–2 |
| Phantom pain (VAS ≥ 7) | TENS, mirror therapy pre-prosthetic | None initially | May delay prosthesis |
| Diabetes + PVD | Delayed prosthetic fitting | Lightweight components | Caps at K2 |
| Strong motivation + high ADL | Standard socket | Microprocessor knee candidate | K3–K4 |
| Cognitively impaired | Simple pin or lanyard suspension | Mechanical knee, no MPK | K1 max |
| Bilateral TF | Shortened gait, energy-storing feet | K-level typically 0–1 | Often wheelchair-bound |
| Neuroma (Tinel positive) | Pad neuroma site, desensitize | None direct | May delay fitting |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pre-Prosthetic Assessment Workflow (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Referral] --> B[Phase 1: Medical History and Amputation Etiology]
    B --> C[Phase 2: Residual Limb Assessment]

    D --> E[Phase 4: Neurological and Pain Assessment]
    E --> F[Phase 5: Functional Mobility Testing]
    F --> G[Phase 6: Psychological and Vocational Profile]
    G --> H{K-Level Determination}
    H --> I[K0: No Prosthesis]
    H --> J[K1: Basic Components]
    H --> K[K2: Intermediate Components]
    H --> L[K3: Advanced Components]
    H --> M[K4: High Performance Components]
    I --> N[Cosmetic or No Fitting]
    J --> O[Socket Casting and Fabrication]
    K --> O
    L --> O
    M --> O
    O --> P[Initial Prosthetic Fitting]
    P --> Q[Prosthetic Training and Follow Up]
```
